topics.title: Power Diodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Diodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Diode** is a two-terminal, unidirectional solid-state semiconductor device specifically engineered to handle **high current** (from a few amperes to several kiloamperes) and **high voltage** (from a few hundred volts to several kilovolts) in power electronic switching and rectification circuits. Structurally, it is a heavily doped PŌü║-I-NŌü╗-NŌü║ sandwiched junction with a wide intrinsic (drift) region, designed for low forward voltage drop and high reverse blocking capability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** Power Diodes ŌĆö Static & Dynamic characteristics, switching characteristics, types, reverse recovery phenomenon, and ratings.

### Key Distinction from Signal Diode

| Parameter | Signal Diode | Power Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Current Rating | < 1 A | 1 A ŌĆō 5000 A |
| Voltage Rating | < 200 V | 200 V ŌĆō 10 kV |
| Junction Area | Very small | Large (for heat dissipation) |
| Switching Speed | Very fast (ns) | Slower (╬╝s) |
| Application | Logic, small signal | Rectifiers, inverters, choppers |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy ŌĆö The One-Way Floodgate:**
> Imagine a heavy steel floodgate in a river. In one direction (forward bias), it opens wide enough to let massive volumes of water through (high current), but a thick rubber seal around the edges prevents any leakage. In the reverse direction (reverse bias), the gate slams shut with a heavy hydraulic lock ŌĆö no water can pass. However, when the gate is suddenly told to close after a flood, some residual water sloshes back before it settles. This "sloshing back" is exactly what we call **reverse recovery** in a power diode ŌĆö a tiny reverse current flows momentarily before the diode truly turns OFF.

## 1.3 Symbol and Physical Construction

The circuit symbol is identical to a normal diode (an arrow with a bar), but internally a power diode has:

- **PŌü║ layer** (heavily doped P-region) ŌĆö anode contact side
- **NŌü╗ drift region** (lightly doped, wide) ŌĆö supports the reverse voltage
- **NŌü║ substrate** (heavily doped) ŌĆö cathode contact side
- The wide **NŌü╗ intrinsic-like layer** is what gives it the high voltage blocking capability.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:** The threshold forward voltage drop is approximately **0.7 V** for silicon power diodes at room temperature (**300 K**). This drops by about **2 mV/┬░C** as temperature rises.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics (V-I Curve)

The static V-I characteristic is studied in two quadrants:

### 2.1.1 Forward Conduction Region
- When **V > V_F(0)** (knee voltage, Ōēł **0.7 V** for Si), the diode conducts heavily.
- The forward current $I_F$ is related to forward voltage $V_F$ by the **Shockley equation**, modified for high injection:
$$
I_F = I_S \left( e^{V_F / (\eta V_T)} - 1 \right)
$$
- For power diodes, the on-state voltage drop is typically **1.0 V to 2.5 V** at rated current (higher than signal diodes).

### 2.1.2 Reverse Blocking Region
- When reverse biased, a small leakage current $I_R$ (in **mA** range) flows.
- Breakdown occurs at the **Reverse Repetitive Peak Voltage (VRRM)**.

## 2.2 Dynamic / Switching Characteristics

This is the **most important topic for KTU** ŌĆö students lose maximum marks here.

### Step 1: ON-State (Diode is conducting forward current $I_F$)
- Diode has stored minority carriers in the drift region.

### Step 2: Turn-OFF initiated
- Forward current begins to decrease at rate $\frac{di}{dt} = -\frac{di_F}{dt}$ as the external circuit reverses polarity.

### Step 3: Zero Crossing
- At $i = 0$, the diode is supposed to be OFF, but stored charge still remains.

### Step 4: Reverse Recovery Phase
- A large **reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$** flows in the reverse direction (carrying away the stored charge $Q_{rr}$).

### Step 5: Recovery complete
- Reverse current decays to zero, diode regains blocking capability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Reverse Recovery:**
> The diode cannot turn off instantly because the **NŌü╗ drift region** is filled with stored minority carriers (holes and electrons). These carriers must either **recombine** or be **swept out** by the reverse current before the junction can support a reverse voltage. This delay is the primary cause of switching losses in power electronics.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_F$ | Forward voltage drop | $V_F = V_0 + I_F \cdot r_d$ | V |
| $r_d$ | Dynamic (on-state) resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{\Delta V_F}{\Delta I_F}$ | $\Omega$ |
| $t_{rr}$ | Reverse recovery time | $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$ | s |
| $t_a$ | Storage time (current crosses zero to peak reverse) | from waveform | s |
| $t_b$ | Transition time (peak reverse to 25% of $I_{RR}$) | from waveform | s |
| $Q_{rr}$ | Reverse recovery charge | $Q_{rr} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, t_{rr} \cdot I_{RR}$ (approx) | C |
| $S$ | Softness Factor | $S = t_b / t_a$ | dimensionless |
| $I_{RR}$ | Peak reverse recovery current | from waveform | A |
| $\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Rate of fall of forward current | circuit dependent | A/s |
| $P_{on}$ | Power loss during turn-on | small, often neglected | W |
| $P_{off}$ | Power loss during turn-off | $P_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f$ | W |
| $V_{RRM}$ | Reverse Repetitive Peak Voltage | rating | V |
| $I_{F(AV)}$ | Average forward current | rating | A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Softness Factor (S) ŌĆö Exam Favourite:**
> - If **S > 1** ŌåÆ **Soft recovery** (gentle decay, low EMI, preferred)
> - If **S = 1** ŌåÆ **Abrupt recovery** (snappy, high EMI, causes voltage spikes)

## 2.4 Classification of Power Diodes (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these four types ŌĆö KTU questions frequently ask for comparison.

### 1. General Purpose Diodes
- $t_{rr} \approx 25$ ╬╝s, used up to **1 kHz**
- Example: **1N4007** series (1 A, 1000 V)

### 2. Fast Recovery Diodes
- $t_{rr} < 5$ ╬╝s, used at **1 kHz ŌĆō 10 kHz**
- Used in choppers, SMPS

### 3. Schottky Diodes
- **Metal-semiconductor junction** (no P-N junction!)
- $V_F \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ V (low drop)
- **No reverse recovery** (no minority carrier storage)
- Used in high-frequency, low-voltage applications (e.g., laptop adapters)

### 4. Silicon Carbide (SiC) Diodes
- $t_{rr} \approx$ few ns, $V_F \approx 1.5$ V
- Can operate at **very high temperatures** (> 200┬░C) and **high voltages** (> 10 kV)
- Premium application: EV drivetrains, solar inverters

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rectifiers** in HVDC transmission lines
- **Freewheeling diodes** in chopper circuits to protect switches from inductive kickback
- **Snubber circuits** to limit $dv/dt$ across thyristors
- **Solar PV bypass diodes** to prevent hot-spotting in shaded panels
- **Battery polarity protection** in EVs and laptops
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Loss in a Power Diode During Reverse Recovery

> **Problem (Model KTU):** A power diode has a reverse recovery time $t_{rr} = 5 \,\mu s$ and peak reverse current $I_{RR} = 20$ A. The reverse voltage during turn-off is $V_R = 200$ V and switching frequency is $f = 2$ kHz. Compute the turn-off switching loss.

### Given Data
- $t_{rr} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $I_{RR} = 20$ A
- $V_R = 200$ V
- $f = 2 \times 10^{3}$ Hz

### Step 1 ŌĆö Express the energy dissipated per switching event
During the reverse recovery interval, the voltage across the diode is approximately $V_R$ (reverse blocking voltage), and the current is approximately $I_{RR}$ decaying from its peak. The instantaneous power is $p(t) = V_R \cdot i(t)$.

We approximate the current shape as linear (worst-case):
$$
i(t) = I_{RR} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_{rr}} \right) \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le t \le t_{rr}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the energy
$$
E_{off} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} V_R \cdot i(t) \, dt
$$

Substitute the linear current expression:
$$
E_{off} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} V_R \cdot I_{RR} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_{rr}} \right) dt
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral
Factor out the constants:
$$
E_{off} = V_R \cdot I_{RR} \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_{rr}} \right) dt
$$

Split the integral:
$$
E_{off} = V_R \cdot I_{RR} \left[ \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} 1 \, dt \; - \; \frac{1}{t_{rr}} \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} t \, dt \right]
$$

Evaluate each piece:
$$
\int_{0}^{t_{rr}} 1 \, dt = t_{rr}
$$
$$
\int_{0}^{t_{rr}} t \, dt = \frac{t_{rr}^{\,2}}{2}
$$

Substitute back:
$$
E_{off} = V_R \cdot I_{RR} \left[ t_{rr} - \frac{1}{t_{rr}} \cdot \frac{t_{rr}^{\,2}}{2} \right]
$$

Simplify inside the bracket:
$$
E_{off} = V_R \cdot I_{RR} \left[ t_{rr} - \frac{t_{rr}}{2} \right] = V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot \frac{t_{rr}}{2}
$$

Thus the **standard textbook result**:
$$
E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \, V_R \, I_{RR} \, t_{rr}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the average power loss
Energy per cycle = $E_{off}$ joules. Number of cycles per second = $f$.
$$
P_{off} = E_{off} \cdot f = \frac{1}{2} \, V_R \, I_{RR} \, t_{rr} \cdot f
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute numerical values
$$
P_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 20 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{3}
$$

Compute step-by-step:
$$
\frac{1}{2} \times 200 = 100
$$
$$
100 \times 20 = 2000
$$
$$
2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01
$$
$$
0.01 \times 2 \times 10^{3} = 20
$$

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{P_{off} = 20 \text{ W}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 20 W loss may seem small, but in a compact power module switching at tens of kHz with multiple diodes, this **scales up rapidly** and demands a heatsink. This is why high-frequency converters prefer **Schottky or SiC diodes** with $t_{rr} \to 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reverse Recovery Charge $Q_{rr}$ from the Triangle Approximation

The reverse recovery current waveform (from zero crossing to recovery completion) is approximated as a **triangle** of base $t_{rr}$ and height $I_{RR}$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Area of a triangle
$$
\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Identify base and height
- Base = $t_{rr}$
- Height = $I_{RR}$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Area represents the charge
Charge is the integral of current over time:
$$
Q_{rr} = \int i(t) \, dt \approx \frac{1}{2} \, t_{rr} \cdot I_{RR}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Softness factor relation (extra derivation)
Define:
$$
S = \frac{t_b}{t_a} \quad ; \quad t_{rr} = t_a + t_b = t_a(1 + S)
$$

Solve for $t_a$:
$$
t_a = \frac{t_{rr}}{1 + S}
$$

Solve for $t_b$:
$$
t_b = \frac{S \cdot t_{rr}}{1 + S}
$$

The peak reverse current $I_{RR}$ occurs at the junction of $t_a$ and $t_b$, and is approximately:
$$
I_{RR} \approx t_a \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = \frac{t_{rr}}{1 + S} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

This makes it explicit why a **soft recovery** (high S) reduces $I_{RR}$ and hence reduces switching loss.

> [!TIP]
> **Derivative Wrap-up:** For a KTU 14-mark question, always draw the labelled **reverse recovery waveform** showing $I_F$, $I_{RR}$, $t_a$, $t_b$, $t_{rr}$, and the slopes $\frac{di}{dt}$ on both sides. Marks are awarded for the diagram alone!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reverse Recovery Waveform (Block-Level Architecture)

Below is a Mermaid rendering of the **functional signal flow during a diode turn-off event**, including the electrical events and corresponding physical states inside the device.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State A: Steady ON ŌĆö Diode conducts IF in forward direction"] --> B["State B: External circuit reverses ŌĆö Forward current begins to fall"]
    B --> C["State C: Current reaches zero ŌĆö Stored charge Qrr still present in drift region"]
    C --> D["State D: Reverse recovery phase ŌĆö Peak reverse current IRR flows for time ta"]
    D --> E["State E: Transition phase ŌĆö Current decays from IRR to zero over time tb"]
    E --> F["State F: Reverse blocking recovered ŌĆö Diode holds full reverse voltage VR"]
    F --> G["State G: Ready for next cycle at switching frequency f"]

    subgraph KTU_LABELS
        H["ta = Storage time"] --> I["tb = Transition time"]
        I --> J["trr = ta + tb (total reverse recovery time)"]
        J --> K["S = tb/ta (softness factor)"]
        K --> L["Qrr = 0.5 x trr x IRR (recovery charge)"]
    end

    C -.links to.-> H
    E -.links to.-> I
    F -.links to.-> J
    G -.links to.-> L
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Diode Operating Quadrants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD_BIAS ["FORWARD BIAS ŌĆö Quadrant I"]
        VF["Vf approximately 0.7 to 2.0 V for Si"] --> IF["If flows from anode to cathode"]
    end

    subgraph REVERSE_BLOCK ["REVERSE BIAS ŌĆö Quadrant III"]
        VR["VR applied up to VRRM rating"] --> IR["Small leakage current IR in microamps to milliamps"]
    end

    subgraph BREAKDOWN ["BREAKDOWN ŌĆö Beyond VRRM"]
        BR["Zener or Avalanche breakdown ŌĆö DESTRUCTIVE if not in rated mode"]
    end

    VF --> SWITCH_CTRL["Diode Operating Point Controller"]
    IF --> SWITCH_CTRL
    VR --> SWITCH_CTRL
    IR --> SWITCH_CTRL
    SWITCH_CTRL --> BR

    style FORWARD_BIAS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style REVERSE_BLOCK fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style BREAKDOWN fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart ŌĆö Selecting the Right Power Diode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start ŌĆö Diode Selection for Application"] --> Q1{"Operating frequency?"}

    Q1 --|Less than 1 kHz| Q2{"Voltage above 100 V?"}
    Q1 --|1 to 10 kHz| Q3{"Need low VF drop?"}
    Q1 --|More than 10 kHz| Q4{"High temperature operation?"}

    Q2 --|Yes| GP["Use General Purpose Diode, e.g., 1N4007 series"]
    Q2 --|No| GP2["Use General Purpose Diode, low voltage variant"]

    Q3 --|Yes| SCH["Use Schottky Diode, VF approx 0.3 to 0.5 V, no reverse recovery"]
    Q3 --|No| FR["Use Fast Recovery Diode, trr less than 5 microseconds"]

    Q4 --|Yes| SIC["Use SiC Diode, trr few ns, operates above 200 degrees C"]
    Q4 --|No| UFR["Use Ultrafast Recovery Diode, trr approx 50 to 100 ns"]

    GP --> END["Diode Selected"]
    GP2 --> END
    SCH --> END
    FR --> END
    SIC --> END
    UFR --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** When KTU asks to "explain reverse recovery with a neat sketch," always label all six critical quantities: $I_F$, $I_{RR}$, $t_a$, $t_b$, $t_{rr}$, and the $\frac{di}{dt}$ slopes on both sides of the zero crossing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Reverse Recovery Time" of a power diode. Why is it significant in high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$)** is the time interval between the instant the forward current of the diode passes through zero (during turn-off) and the instant the reverse current decays to **25% of its peak reverse value $I_{RR}$**. It consists of two parts: the storage time $t_a$ and the transition time $t_b$, such that $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$.

**Significance in high-frequency applications (2 marks):** At high switching frequencies, the diode is turned ON and OFF many thousands of times per second. A long $t_{rr}$ causes overlapping of voltage and current during switching transitions, leading to high **switching losses**, heating, and reduced efficiency. Hence, fast-recovery and Schottky diodes with very low $t_{rr}$ are preferred above **1 kHz**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]`
**Compare a Schottky diode with a PN-junction power diode in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Schottky Diode | PN-Junction Power Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Junction type | Metal-semiconductor (majority carrier device) | P-N junction (minority carrier device) |
| Forward voltage drop $V_F$ | Low (**0.3 ŌĆō 0.5 V**) | Higher (**0.7 ŌĆō 2.0 V**) |
| Reverse recovery | **Negligible** (no stored minority carriers) | Significant ($t_{rr}$ in microseconds) |
| Application | High-frequency, low-voltage SMPS | Line-frequency rectifiers, HVDC |

---

## Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Full Descriptive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module 1 Part B questions offer an **internal choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B completely.

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **reverse recovery characteristics** of a power diode. Define softness factor and discuss how it affects the switching loss. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sketch (2 marks):** Draw the standard reverse recovery waveform with $I_F$, $I_{RR}$, $t_a$, $t_b$, $t_{rr}$, and the $\frac{di}{dt}$ slope labelled on both sides of the zero crossing.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Explanation of phases (3 marks):**
- During steady ON-state, the diode carries forward current $I_F$ and the drift region is flooded with stored minority carriers.
- When the external circuit forces the current to fall at rate $\frac{di}{dt}$, the current passes through zero at $t_1$, but the diode is still filled with stored charge.
- A reverse current $I_{RR}$ flows during time $t_a$ (storage phase) to remove this charge.
- During time $t_b$ (transition phase), the current decays to zero and the diode regains reverse blocking capability.
- Total: $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Softness factor and switching loss (2 marks):**
- **Softness factor** $S = t_b / t_a$.
- $S > 1$ (soft recovery) ŌåÆ gentle decay ŌåÆ low $di/dt$ at the tail ŌåÆ lower EMI and lower switching loss.
- $S \approx 1$ (snappy recovery) ŌåÆ abrupt decay ŌåÆ high $di/dt$ ŌåÆ large voltage spikes ($V = L \frac{di}{dt}$) and high switching loss.

---

**(b)** A power diode is used in a chopper circuit. The forward current falls from **100 A** to zero at a rate of **40 A/╬╝s**. The reverse recovery charge is **30 ╬╝C**. Determine the **peak reverse recovery current** and the **reverse recovery time** (assuming a linear approximation). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $I_F = 100$ A
- $\frac{di}{dt} = 40 \text{ A/╬╝s} = 40 \times 10^{6}$ A/s
- $Q_{rr} = 30 \text{ ╬╝C} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ C

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find storage time $t_a$**
During the storage phase, the current falls linearly from 0 to $-I_{RR}$:
$$
I_{RR} = t_a \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the charge equation**
For a triangular current waveform, the charge removed during reverse recovery is:
$$
Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot I_{RR}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express $I_{RR}$ in terms of $t_{rr}$**
From Step 1, and noting that for a typical abrupt-recovery assumption $t_a \approx t_{rr}/2$ for triangular shape, or directly use the relation $I_{RR} = t_{rr} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} / 2$ when $t_a = t_{rr}/2$.

We use the simple relation:
$$
I_{RR} = \sqrt{Q_{rr} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}}
$$

**Derivation of this shortcut:**
$$
Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot I_{RR} \quad \text{and} \quad I_{RR} = t_a \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = \frac{t_{rr}}{2} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

Substituting:
$$
Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot \frac{t_{rr}}{2} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = \frac{t_{rr}^{\,2}}{4} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

Solving for $t_{rr}$:
$$
t_{rr} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{Q_{rr}}{di/dt}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute values**
$$
t_{rr} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{40 \times 10^{6}}} = 2 \sqrt{7.5 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
t_{rr} = 2 \times 8.66 \times 10^{-7} = 1.732 \text{ ╬╝s}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Find $I_{RR}$**
$$
I_{RR} = \frac{t_{rr}}{2} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = \frac{1.732 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times 40 \times 10^{6}
$$
$$
I_{RR} = 0.866 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 10^{6} = 34.64 \text{ A}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{t_{rr} \approx 1.732 \,\mu s \quad ; \quad I_{RR} \approx 34.64 \text{ A}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Students frequently write $40$ A/╬╝s as $40$ A/s, producing a wildly wrong answer. Always convert: $1 \text{ ╬╝s} = 10^{-6}$ s.
> 2. **Formula confusion:** Don't write $Q_{rr} = t_{rr} \cdot I_{RR}$ ŌĆö that is a **rectangle**, not a triangle. Always use the **half** factor: $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} t_{rr} I_{RR}$.
> 3. **Missing the sketch:** A 7-mark question without a labelled diagram typically loses **2 marks** outright.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **static V-I characteristics** of a power diode. Discuss the significance of the wide intrinsic drift region in its construction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö V-I Curve Description (3 marks):**
- **Forward region (Quadrant I):** Below the knee voltage (~0.7 V for Si), only a small leakage current flows. Above the knee, the diode enters heavy conduction and the current rises rapidly with small increase in $V_F$. The relationship follows the exponential Shockley law at low current, but at high injection, the curve becomes approximately linear.
- **Reverse region (Quadrant III):** A small reverse saturation current $I_S$ flows. This current remains nearly constant until the breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$ is reached.
- **Breakdown region:** Beyond $V_{BR}$, the reverse current increases sharply (avalanche multiplication). Operation here is destructive unless the diode is rated for it (as in an avalanche diode).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Significance of the wide drift region (4 marks):**
- The **NŌü╗ drift region** is lightly doped and physically wide.
- It supports the **entire reverse blocking voltage** ŌĆö the wider it is, the higher the breakdown voltage.
- It provides a long path for current conduction when forward biased, reducing the **on-state resistance** $r_d$.
- However, a wide drift region also stores more minority charge, **increasing $t_{rr}$** and switching loss ŌĆö this is the fundamental trade-off in power diode design.

---

**(b)** Compare **General Purpose**, **Fast Recovery**, **Schottky**, and **SiC diodes** in terms of construction, switching speed, forward drop, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Type | Construction | Switching Speed | Forward Drop $V_F$ | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| General Purpose | P-I-N with thick drift region | Slow ($t_{rr} \approx 25$ ╬╝s) | 1.0 ŌĆō 1.5 V | Line-frequency rectifiers, battery chargers |
| Fast Recovery | P-I-N with thin drift + lifetime killers (Au, Pt doping) | Fast ($t_{rr} \approx 1$ ŌĆō 5 ╬╝s) | 1.0 ŌĆō 1.5 V | Choppers, SMPS, induction heating |
| Schottky | Metal-semiconductor (no P-N junction, majority carrier) | Very fast ($t_{rr} \approx$ ns, effectively zero) | **0.3 ŌĆō 0.5 V** | High-frequency SMPS, freewheeling in low-voltage converters |
| SiC | Silicon Carbide wide bandgap P-N | Ultrafast ($t_{rr} \approx$ few ns) | 1.5 ŌĆō 2.0 V | EV drivetrains, solar inverters, high-temp aerospace |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often say "Schottky diodes have **zero** forward drop" ŌĆö wrong! They have $V_F \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ V, which is *low* but not zero. Also remember that Schottky diodes have **high reverse leakage current** ŌĆö they are not suitable for high-voltage applications.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- Ō£ģ A power diode is a **PŌü║-I-NŌü╗-NŌü║** structure with a wide drift region.
- Ō£ģ Forward drop $V_F \approx 0.7 - 2.5$ V (higher than signal diode).
- Ō£ģ **Reverse recovery time** $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$ is the **single most important** switching parameter.
- Ō£ģ **Softness factor** $S = t_b / t_a$ ŌĆö soft recovery is preferred for low EMI.
- Ō£ģ Switching loss formula: $P_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr} f$.
- Ō£ģ Reverse recovery charge (triangle approx): $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} t_{rr} I_{RR}$.
- Ō£ģ **Schottky** = metal-semiconductor, no minority carriers, no reverse recovery, low $V_F$, low voltage only.
- Ō£ģ **SiC** = wide bandgap, high temperature, high voltage, ultrafast switching.
- Ō£ģ For high-frequency circuits: prefer **Schottky or SiC**.
- Ō£ģ For high-voltage DC: prefer **general purpose or fast recovery**.
- Ō£ģ Always convert units carefully: **╬╝s = 10Ōü╗ŌüČ s**, **╬╝C = 10Ōü╗ŌüČ C**, **kHz = 10┬│ Hz**.
- Ō£ģ In numericals, always draw the **labelled reverse recovery waveform** to fetch full marks.
- Ō£ģ The peak reverse recovery current is related to $di/dt$ by $I_{RR} = t_a \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$.
- Ō£ģ Reverse leakage current **increases** with temperature, while forward drop **decreases** with temperature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Transistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Transistor?

A **Power Transistor** is a high-power semiconductor switching device capable of controlling large currents (amperes) and high voltages (hundreds of volts) when used in power electronic converters, motor drives, and switched-mode power supplies (SMPS). Unlike small-signal transistors used in amplifier circuits, power transistors are designed and operated primarily as **electronic switches** in either the **cut-off (OFF)** state or the **saturation (ON)** state ŌĆö not in the active (linear) region.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT741 ŌĆō Module 1):**
> *Power Transistors ŌĆö Construction, steady-state and switching characteristics, and safe operating area of Power BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, and Static Induction Transistor (SIT).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Heavy-Duty Tap"

Imagine a **household water tap** that controls a small flow of water for a kitchen sink. Now imagine an **industrial giant gate-valve** that controls a torrent of water flowing into a hydroelectric turbine. The principle is identical ŌĆö *open it to let flow pass, close it to block it* ŌĆö but the **mechanical strength, electrical drive, and ruggedness** are vastly different.

A **Power Transistor is the electronic equivalent of that industrial gate-valve**:
- The **base/gate terminal** is the small hand-wheel that commands a huge flow with very little effort.
- The **collector-emitter / drain-source channel** is the high-power pipe carrying the load current.
- The **OFF state** corresponds to a fully closed valve (blocking voltage, negligible leakage current).
- The **ON state** corresponds to a fully open valve (load current flows with minimum voltage drop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Engineering Metric:** Modern power transistors handle voltages from **100 V to 6500 V** and currents from **1 A to 1500 A**, with switching frequencies from a few hundred Hz (BJT/IGBT at high power) to several MHz (low-power MOSFET).

### 1.3 The Three Primary Power Transistor Families

| Family | Acronym | Full Form | Controlling Terminal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current-Controlled | **BJT** | Bipolar Junction Transistor | Base current $I_B$ |
| Voltage-Controlled | **MOSFET** | Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor FET | GateŌĆōSource voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| Hybrid | **IGBT** | Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor | GateŌĆōEmitter voltage $V_{GE}$ |
| Specialised | **SIT** | Static Induction Transistor | Gate voltage $V_G$ |

### 1.4 Why Multiple Types Exist ŌĆö The Trade-off Triangle

> [!NOTE]
> **The Power Electronics Trade-off Triangle:**
> Three conflicting demands ŌĆö *High Power Handling*, *High Switching Speed*, *Easy Drive* ŌĆö cannot be maximised simultaneously. Different transistor families optimise for different corners of this triangle.

- **BJT**: Optimises *power* (high current/voltage), weak in *speed* and *drive* (current-driven).
- **Power MOSFET**: Optimises *speed* and *drive* (voltage-driven), weaker in *power* (high $R_{DS(on)}$ at high voltage).
- **IGBT**: Hybrid ŌĆö combines MOSFET's *easy drive* with BJT's *high-power handling*.
- **SIT**: Specialised ŌĆö *very high switching speed* and *very high power*, but expensive and niche (audio amplifiers, induction heating).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Architecture of Each Power Transistor

### 2.1.1 Power BJT ŌĆö The Vertical NPN Triple-Diffused Device

Unlike the small-signal BJT (a planar horizontal structure), the power BJT is built as a **vertical NPN triple-diffused structure** with the following regions (from top to bottom):

1. **Emitter** ŌĆö Heavily doped NŌü║ region at the top (small lateral area).
2. **Base** ŌĆö Thin P-region sandwiched between emitter and collector.
3. **Collector** ŌĆö Moderately doped N-region (drift layer) to support high voltage.
4. **Sub-collector** ŌĆö Heavily doped NŌü║ at the bottom (for low ohmic contact resistance).

The **vertical current flow** allows large currents to flow uniformly across the entire die area, and the **lightly-doped N-drift layer** provides the high breakdown voltage $V_{CEO}$.

### 2.1.2 Power MOSFET ŌĆö The VDMOS (Vertical Diffused MOS)

The power MOSFET uses a **vertical structure** (VDMOS or VMOS) to overcome the current-handling limitations of lateral MOSFETs. The N-channel enhancement-mode VDMOS has:

- **Source (NŌü║)**: Heavily doped regions diffused into P-body wells.
- **P-body / Channel region**: Channel forms on the surface of the P-body.
- **NŌü╗ drift region**: Lightly doped epitaxial layer to support high voltage.
- **Substrate (NŌü║ drain)**: At the bottom ŌĆö current flows **vertically** from drain to source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MOSFET has **no minority carrier storage** because conduction occurs through an **inversion layer** of majority carriers (electrons in N-channel). This is why MOSFETs are inherently faster than BJTs.

### 2.1.3 IGBT ŌĆö The Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor

The IGBT structure is a clever hybrid:
- **Input stage** is a **MOSFET** (gate insulated ŌåÆ high input impedance, voltage-controlled).
- **Output stage** is a **PNP BJT** (conductivity-modulated drift region ŌåÆ low on-state voltage drop).

The four layers from top: **PŌü║ emitter (anode) ŌĆö NŌü╗ drift ŌĆö P-body (with NŌü║ source shorts) ŌĆö Gate/Emitter metal.**

The NŌü║ short through the P-body prevents **latch-up** (parasitic thyristor activation), a critical IGBT failure mode.

### 2.1.4 SIT ŌĆö The Static Induction Transistor

The SIT is essentially a **short-channel JFET** with buried-gate structure. Two key features:
- **Normally-ON** device (current flows when $V_{GS} = 0$; requires negative gate voltage to turn off).
- **Non-saturating** I-V characteristic (triode-like, similar to a vacuum triode).

## 2.2 Static (Steady-State) Characteristics

### 2.2.1 Power BJT ŌĆö Output Characteristics

In the **active region**, the collector current is given by:

$$I_C = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B + I_{CEO}$$

where $\beta_{DC}$ is the **DC current gain** and $I_{CEO}$ is the **collector-emitter leakage current with base open**.

In the **saturation region**, the on-state voltage drop $V_{CE(sat)}$ is typically **0.7 V to 2.5 V** for high-voltage BJTs.

### 2.2.2 Power MOSFET ŌĆö Output Characteristics

The drain current in the **ohmic (triode) region** is:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

In the **saturation (active) region**:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \cdot (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

For power switching, MOSFETs operate in the **ohmic region** when fully ON, characterised by the on-resistance:

$$R_{DS(on)} = \frac{V_{DS(sat)}}{I_D}$$

### 2.2.3 IGBT ŌĆö Output Characteristics

IGBT behaviour in conduction is analogous to a PNP transistor with base current supplied by the **MOSFET channel** (driven by $V_{GE}$). The on-state voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$ is typically **1.5 V to 3.5 V** ŌĆö much lower than a high-voltage MOSFET with comparable rating.

### 2.2.4 SIT ŌĆö Output Characteristics

The SIT has a **non-saturating, triode-like** characteristic. Drain current is given empirically by:

$$I_D = I_{D0} \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{q \cdot V_{GS}}{kT} \right) \cdot \tanh\left( \frac{q \cdot V_{DS}}{2kT} \right)$$

## 2.3 Switching Characteristics ŌĆö The Universal Power Transistor Model

Every power transistor (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT) exhibits **four switching intervals** during turn-on and turn-off. These intervals generate **switching losses** that limit the operating frequency.

### 2.3.1 Turn-ON Sequence (Four Intervals)

| Interval | Duration | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Turn-on delay $t_d$** | $t_{d(on)}$ | Driver signal applied; device current still zero |
| **Current rise $t_r$** | $t_r$ | Current rises from 0 to load current $I_L$ |
| **Voltage fall $t_{fv}$** | $t_{fv}$ | Voltage falls from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)}$ |

### 2.3.2 Turn-OFF Sequence (Four Intervals)

| Interval | Duration | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Turn-off delay $t_s$ (storage)** | $t_{d(off)}$ | Driver signal removed; device still conducting |
| **Voltage rise $t_{rv}$** | $t_{rv}$ | Voltage rises from $V_{CE(sat)}$ to $V_{CC}$ |
| **Current fall $t_f$** | $t_f$ | Current falls from $I_L$ to leakage level |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU ESE ŌĆö Memorise These!**

| Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_C$ | $\beta_{DC} \cdot I_B + I_{CEO}$ | BJT collector current | A |
| $\beta_{DC}$ | $I_C / I_B$ | DC current gain | dimensionless |
| $I_D$ (saturation) | $\tfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | MOSFET drain current | A |
| $R_{DS(on)}$ | $V_{DS(sat)} / I_D$ | On-state resistance of MOSFET | $\Omega$ |
| $g_m$ | $\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS} = \mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ | Transconductance | A/V or S |
| $P_{cond}$ | $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D$ | Conduction loss (duty ratio $D$) | W |
| $P_{sw(on)}$ | $\tfrac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_L (t_r + t_{fv}) f_s$ | Turn-on switching loss | W |
| $P_{sw(off)}$ | $\tfrac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_L (t_{rv} + t_f) f_s$ | Turn-off switching loss | W |
| $P_{sw,total}$ | $(P_{sw(on)} + P_{sw(off)})$ | Total switching loss | W |
| $P_{gate}$ | $Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_s$ | Gate-drive power (MOSFET/IGBT) | W |
| $E_{ts(on)}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_L (t_r + t_{fv})$ | Energy lost per turn-on | J |
| $E_{ts(off)}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_L (t_{rv} + t_f)$ | Energy lost per turn-off | J |
| $V_{th}$ | Gate threshold voltage | Turn-on voltage | V |
| $Q_g$ | Total gate charge | Charge needed to drive gate | C |

**Where:**
- $V_{CC}$ = Supply voltage (V)
- $I_L$ = Load current (A)
- $f_s$ = Switching frequency (Hz)
- $D$ = Duty cycle (dimensionless, $0 \le D \le 1$)
- $q$ = Electron charge = $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Typical Transistor Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supply) | Power MOSFET | High frequency, low power |
| Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) | IGBT | Medium power, medium frequency |
| High-voltage DC transmission (HVDC) | IGBT (press-pack) | Very high voltage and current |
| Audio Power Amplifiers | SIT (or BJT) | Non-saturating linear characteristic |
| Induction Heating | SIT / IGBT | High-frequency, high-power |
| Electric Vehicle Traction | IGBT / SiC MOSFET | High efficiency, fast switching |
| Solar Inverters | IGBT / MOSFET | High efficiency, medium power |
| Welding Inverters | IGBT | Robust, handles short circuits |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conduction Loss of a Power BJT in a Chopper Circuit

> **Problem Context:** A power BJT operates as a switch in a chopper circuit feeding a load. The chopper has supply $V_S$, load current $I_L$, switching frequency $f_s$, and duty ratio $D = t_{on} / T_s$. Derive the **average conduction loss** in the BJT.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the conduction period
The BJT is ON for a fraction $D$ of each switching period $T_s$:

$$t_{on} = D \cdot T_s = \frac{D}{f_s}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Instantaneous conduction loss
When the BJT is ON, it carries the full load current $I_L$ and drops a saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$. The instantaneous power dissipated is:

$$p_{cond}(t) = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot i_C(t)$$

For a constant load current $i_C(t) = I_L$ (assuming highly inductive load):

$$p_{cond}(t) = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_L$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Average conduction loss over one period
The average power over one switching period is the time integral divided by the period:

$$P_{cond} = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{T_s} p_{cond}(t) \, dt$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the duty cycle
Since the BJT conducts only during $t_{on}$:

$$P_{cond} = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{D \cdot T_s} V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_L \, dt$$

Since $V_{CE(sat)}$ and $I_L$ are constant during conduction:

$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_L \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot D \cdot T_s$$

$$\boxed{P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_L \cdot D}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Switching Loss of a Power BJT (Turn-Off Interval)

> **Problem Context:** During turn-off, both voltage across and current through the BJT vary approximately **linearly** with time. The collectorŌĆōemitter voltage rises from $V_{CE(sat)}$ to $V_{CC}$ in time $t_{rv}$ (voltage-rise time), while the collector current falls from $I_L$ to 0 in time $t_f$ (current-fall time). Both intervals overlap. Derive the **turn-off switching energy loss**.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Assume linear transitions
Let the voltage rise and current fall both be linear:

$$v_{CE}(t) = V_{CE(sat)} + \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{t_{rv}} \cdot t \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{t_{rv}} \cdot t$$

For simplicity, neglect $V_{CE(sat)}$ compared to $V_{CC}$:

$$v_{CE}(t) \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{t_{rv}} \cdot t$$

$$i_C(t) \approx I_L \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_f} \right)$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Instantaneous switching power

$$p_{sw}(t) = v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t)$$

$$p_{sw}(t) = \frac{V_{CC}}{t_{rv}} \cdot t \cdot I_L \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_f} \right)$$

$$p_{sw}(t) = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_L}{t_{rv}} \left( t - \frac{t^2}{t_f} \right)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Energy lost in the turn-off interval
Integrate instantaneous power over the active switching interval (0 to $t_f$, assuming $t_{rv} \approx t_f$ for simplicity):

$$E_{ts(off)} = \int_{0}^{t_f} p_{sw}(t) \, dt$$

$$E_{ts(off)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_L}{t_{rv}} \int_{0}^{t_f} \left( t - \frac{t^2}{t_f} \right) dt$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral
Compute the integral term by term:

$$\int_{0}^{t_f} t \, dt = \frac{t_f^2}{2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{t_f} \frac{t^2}{t_f} \, dt = \frac{1}{t_f} \cdot \frac{t_f^3}{3} = \frac{t_f^2}{3}$$

So the integral equals:

$$\int_{0}^{t_f} \left( t - \frac{t^2}{t_f} \right) dt = \frac{t_f^2}{2} - \frac{t_f^2}{3} = \frac{t_f^2}{6}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Final expression for turn-off energy
With $t_{rv} = t_f$:

$$E_{ts(off)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_L}{t_f} \cdot \frac{t_f^2}{6}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ts(off)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_f}{6}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Turn-off switching power loss
Multiplying by switching frequency $f_s$:

$$\boxed{P_{sw(off)} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot V_{CC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_f \cdot f_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A similar derivation gives $P_{sw(on)} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_L (t_r + t_{fv}) f_s$. The **total switching loss** scales **linearly with $f_s$** ŌĆö this is why high-frequency operation is limited.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SOA Boundary Equations (BJT)

The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power BJT is bounded by four constraints. The power dissipation inside the SOA is $P_{max} = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$.

### 3.3.1 The Four SOA Boundaries

| Boundary | Equation | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Collector current limit** | $I_C = I_{C(max)}$ | Bond-wire / die current limit |
| **Secondary breakdown limit** | $V_{CE} \cdot I_C^n = \text{const}$ (n Ōēł 4 for BJT) | Hot-spot thermal instability |
| **Power dissipation limit** | $P_D = (T_{j,max} - T_{c}) / R_{th(j-c)}$ | Thermal limit |
| **Voltage breakdown limit** | $V_{CE} = V_{CEO(SUS)}$ | Avalanche breakdown |

### 3.3.2 Derivation of Thermal Power Limit
The junction-to-case thermal resistance $R_{th(j-c)}$ relates junction temperature rise to power:

$$\Delta T = T_j - T_c = P_D \cdot R_{th(j-c)}$$

Solving for the maximum allowable dissipation:

$$\boxed{P_{D,max} = \frac{T_{j,max} - T_c}{R_{th(j-c)}} = \frac{\Delta T_{max}}{R_{th(j-c)}}}$$

For the BJT, $T_{j,max} = 150┬░C$ (or 175┬░C for some modern devices) and $R_{th(j-c)}$ is specified in the datasheet (typically 0.1 to 1 ┬░C/W).

### 3.3.3 Derivation of Secondary Breakdown Limit
The power during secondary breakdown is approximately constant:

$$P_{SB} = V_{CE} \cdot I_C = \text{const}$$

Expressed as the boundary:

$$I_{C(SB)} = \frac{P_{SB}}{V_{CE}}$$

This creates a **hyperbolic** boundary on the $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ plot (the "Reverse-Bias SOA" or **RBSOA**).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: MOSFET Switching Loss with Non-Linear Gate Drive

For a MOSFET driven from gate-source voltage $V_{GG}$ (off-state) to $V_{GG(on)}$ (on-state), the **Miller plateau** duration is critical. The plateau voltage is:

$$V_{gp} = V_{th} + \frac{I_L}{g_m}$$

The Miller plateau duration (turn-on):

$$t_{gp} = \frac{Q_{gd} \cdot R_g}{V_{GG(on)} - V_{gp}}$$

where $Q_{gd}$ is the gate-drain (Miller) charge and $R_g$ is the gate-loop resistance.

The turn-on switching energy (approximate, with Miller plateau dominant):

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_L \left( t_{gv2} + t_{gp} \right)$$

The turn-off switching energy:

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_L \left( t_{gv2} + t_{gp} \right) \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_g + R_{g,int}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing the **gate drive resistance $R_g$** slows switching, increasing losses but reducing EMI. Decreasing $R_g$ sharpens the switching edge, reducing losses but creating severe $dv/dt$ and $di/dt$ noise.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö BJT vs MOSFET vs IGBT vs SIT

| Parameter | Power BJT | Power MOSFET | IGBT | SIT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Drive** | Current ($I_B$) | Voltage ($V_{GS}$) | Voltage ($V_{GE}$) | Voltage ($V_{GS}$, negative) |
| **Input Impedance** | Low | Very high | Very high | High |
| **On-state voltage** | $V_{CE(sat)}$ Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 V | $I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | $V_{CE(sat)}$ Ōēł 1.5ŌĆō3.5 V | $V_{DS(on)}$ low |
| **Switching speed** | Medium (┬Ąs) | Very fast (ns) | Fast (hundreds of ns) | Very fast (ns) |
| **Switching frequency** | Ōēż 5 kHz | Ōēż 1 MHz | Ōēż 20 kHz | Ōēż 100 kHz |
| **Voltage rating** | Up to 1200 V | Up to 1000 V | Up to 6500 V | Up to 1500 V |
| **Current rating** | Up to 500 A | Up to 200 A | Up to 3600 A | Up to 250 A |
| **Conduction mechanism** | Minority carrier | Majority carrier | Conductivity-modulated | Majority carrier |
| **Second breakdown** | Yes (critical) | No | Yes (latch-up) | No |
| **SOA shape** | Narrower | Wider (square) | Wider (rectangular) | Limited by voltage |
| **Cost per ampere** | Low | High at high V | Low | High |
| **Typical application** | Obsolete in new designs | SMPS, low V drives | Motor drives, HVDC | HF induction, audio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Power Transistor Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Application Requirements] --> B{Rated Power Level}
    B -->|Below 1 kW| C{High Frequency > 100 kHz?}
    B -->|1 kW to 100 kW| D{Voltage > 600 V?}
    B -->|Above 100 kW| E{DC or AC Switching?}

    C -->|Yes| F[Select Power MOSFET]
    C -->|No| G[Select Power BJT or IGBT]

    D -->|Yes| H[Select IGBT]
    D -->|No| I[Select Power MOSFET]

    E -->|DC| J[Press-Pack IGBT or IGCT]
    E -->|AC| K[Module IGBT with snubber]

    F --> L[Validate: R_DS on, Q_g, E_oss]
    G --> M[Validate: V_CE sat, t_f, SOA]
    H --> N[Validate: V_CE sat, t_off, short-circuit]
    I --> O[Validate: R_DS on, gate charge]
    J --> P[Validate: thermal stack, spring contact]
    K --> Q[Validate: dv/dt, di/dt, snubber loss]

    L --> R[Final: Thermal Design, Gate Drive, Protection]
    M --> R
    N --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R

    R --> S[End: Transistor Selection Complete]
```

## 4.2 Switching Waveform Functional Block

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Driver as Gate/Base Driver
    participant Device as Power Transistor
    participant Load as Inductive Load

    Note over Driver,Load: Turn-ON sequence
    Driver->>Device: Apply V_GG on (or I_B pulse)
    activate Device
    Device->>Device: Delay t_d on
    Device->>Load: I_C rises to I_L during t_r
    Device->>Device: V_CE falls to V_CE sat during t_fv
    Note over Device: Turn-ON complete, ON-state loss = V_CE sat times I_L

    Note over Driver,Load: Turn-OFF sequence
    Driver->>Device: Remove drive signal
    Device->>Device: Storage time t_s (BJT only)
    Device->>Device: V_CE rises to V_CC during t_rv
    Device->>Load: I_C falls to zero during t_f
    deactivate Device
    Note over Device: Turn-OFF complete, switching loss integrated
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Architecture of Each Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BJT[X: Power BJT Vertical NPN]
    A1[Metal Contact] --> A2[Emitter N plus]
    A2 --> A3[Base P region]
    A3 --> A4[Collector N drift]
    A4 --> A5[Sub Collector N plus]
    A5 --> A6[Bottom Metal]
    end

    subgraph MOSFET[Y: Power MOSFET VDMOS]
    B1[Source Metal] --> B2[Source N plus]
    B2 --> B3[P Body Channel]
    B3 --> B4[N minus Drift Epitaxy]
    B4 --> B5[Drain N plus Substrate]
    B5 --> B6[Drain Metal]
    end

    subgraph IGBT[Z: IGBT Hybrid]
    C1[Emitter Metal] --> C2[P plus Emitter]
    C2 --> C3[N minus Drift]
    C3 --> C4[P Body with N plus short]
    C4 --> C5[Gate Oxide]
    C5 --> C6[Gate Metal]
    end
```

## 4.4 Safe Operating Area (SOA) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Device Operating Point V_CE, I_C] --> Check1{I_C less than I_C max?}
    Check1 -- No --> Fail1[Failure: Bond wire fusing]
    Check1 -- Yes --> Check2{V_CE less than V_CEO sus?}
    Check2 -- No --> Fail2[Failure: Avalanche breakdown]
    Check2 -- Yes --> Check3{Inside secondary breakdown curve?}
    Check3 -- No --> Fail3[Failure: Hot spot thermal runaway]
    Check3 -- Yes --> Check4{P_D less than thermal limit?}
    Check4 -- No --> Fail4[Failure: Junction over temperature]
    Check4 -- Yes --> Pass[Safe Operation Confirmed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The flowchart above maps directly onto the four SOA boundaries derived in Section 3.3. The **secondary breakdown boundary** is the most restrictive constraint for power BJTs and is **less severe for IGBTs** (which are nearly immune due to their vertical structure with cell-level design).

## 4.5 Gate Drive Functional Architecture (MOSFET/IGBT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Logic Level PWM Signal] --> B[Level Shifter]
    B --> C[Gate Driver IC e.g. IR2110]
    C --> D[Bootstrap / Isolated Supply]
    D --> E[Gate Resistor R_g]
    E --> F[Gate of Power Transistor]
    F --> G[Source or Emitter Return Path]
    G --> C

    F --> H[Power Stage: V_CC and Load]
    H --> F

    subgraph Protection
    I[Desaturation Detection] --> J[Fault Latch]
    J --> C
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Safe Operating Area" (SOA) of a power BJT. List the four constraints that bound the SOA on the $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ plane. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power BJT is the locus of points in the $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ plane within which the transistor can be operated safely without permanent damage during both switching transients and steady-state conduction.
>
> The four SOA boundaries are:
> 1. **Maximum collector current limit** ($I_C \le I_{C,max}$) ŌĆö set by the bond-wire and die current capacity.
> 2. **Secondary breakdown (RBSOA) limit** ŌĆö a hyperbolic curve $V_{CE} \cdot I_C^n = \text{const}$ due to localised hot-spot thermal runaway.
> 3. **Maximum power dissipation limit** ŌĆö set by thermal resistance: $P_{D,max} = (T_{j,max} - T_c)/R_{th(j-c)}$.
> 4. **Maximum collectorŌĆōemitter voltage limit** ŌĆö set by avalanche breakdown: $V_{CE} \le V_{CEO(SUS)}$.

---

### Question 2: Compare the gate drive requirements of a power BJT and a power MOSFET. State one advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Power BJT (current-driven):** Requires a continuous base current $I_B = I_C/\beta$ to remain in saturation. Typical $\beta = 5$ŌĆō$20$ for high-voltage BJTs, hence the base drive circuit must supply 1ŌĆō2 A continuously. **Advantage:** very low on-state voltage drop $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1$ŌĆō$2$ V even at high currents.
>
> **Power MOSFET (voltage-driven):** Requires only a charge $Q_g$ applied to the gate capacitance to switch. The steady-state gate current is essentially zero (input impedance $> 10^9 \ \Omega$). **Advantage:** very high switching speed (nanosecond range) and simple, low-power gate drive circuit.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a power BJT. Compare its structure with a small-signal BJT. **\[7 marks\]** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The **power BJT** is built as a **vertical NPN triple-diffused** structure to allow large currents to flow uniformly through the die area and to support high voltages.

**Construction layers (top to bottom):**
1. **Emitter (NŌü║):** Heavily doped, small lateral area, with metal contact.
2. **Base (P):** Thin layer (~5ŌĆō10 ┬Ąm) diffused below the emitter.
3. **Collector (NŌü╗):** Lightly doped drift region to support the high blocking voltage.
4. **Sub-collector (NŌü║):** Heavily doped layer at the bottom for low-resistance ohmic contact.
5. **Metal contacts:** Emitter on top, collector on bottom ŌĆö current flows **vertically**.

**Comparison with small-signal BJT:**

| Feature | Small-signal BJT | Power BJT |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Planar / horizontal | Vertical / triple-diffused |
| Current flow | Lateral | Vertical |
| Current handling | ~100 mA | Up to 500 A |
| Voltage rating | Ōēż 100 V | Up to 1200 V |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 100 ŌĆō 300 | 5 ŌĆō 20 |
| Base drive | Low current | High current (due to low $\beta$) |
| Thermal stability | Not critical | Critical ŌĆö needs heatsink |

The **vertical structure** of the power BJT allows a much larger die area to conduct current in parallel, hence higher current capability. The **lightly doped NŌü╗ drift layer** acts as a voltage-blocking region.

#### (b) Explain the static output characteristics of a power BJT. Define DC current gain $\beta_{DC}$ and discuss why power BJT has low $\beta$. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

The static output characteristics of a power BJT plot $I_C$ versus $V_{CE}$ for a fixed $I_B$. Three regions are identified:

1. **Cut-off region:** $I_B = 0$, $I_C \approx I_{CEO}$ (leakage), $V_{CE}$ can be high.
2. **Active (forward active) region:** $I_C = \beta_{DC} I_B + I_{CEO}$, with $V_{CE}$ in the range $V_{CE(sat)} < V_{CE} < V_{CEO}$.
3. **Saturation region:** Both junctions forward-biased; $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ŌĆō$2$ V.

**DC current gain:**

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \bigg\vert_{V_{CE} = \text{const}}$$

**Why power BJT has low $\beta$ (5ŌĆō20):**
- The **base region is thicker** (~5ŌĆō10 ┬Ąm) in a power BJT to support high voltage; thicker base reduces emitter injection efficiency.
- The **emitter area is large** but base current must be distributed uniformly, requiring high base current.
- The lightly doped **collector drift region** introduces additional recombination.
- Low $\beta$ necessitates **high base drive current** (1ŌĆō2 A for a 50 A device), which is the main drawback of the power BJT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (BJT Comparison Question):**
> Students frequently forget to mention that the **lightly-doped NŌü╗ drift region** in the power BJT is what allows the high breakdown voltage. Marks are awarded for the link between *thick drift region* ŌåÆ *high voltage* but *low $\beta$* ŌåÆ *high base current*. Without this trade-off explanation, 2 marks are lost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the construction and principle of operation of an IGBT. Show its equivalent circuit and discuss its input and output characteristics. **\[7 marks\]** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** The IGBT combines a **MOSFET input stage** and a **BJT output stage** on the same silicon die. The four-layer structure from top to bottom:

1. **PŌü║ emitter (anode)** with metal contact.
2. **NŌü╗ drift region** (conductivity-modulated for low on-state drop).
3. **P-body region** with embedded **NŌü║ source** (shorts to prevent latch-up).
4. **Gate oxide** with **metal gate electrode**.
5. **Cathode (collector)** contact at the bottom.

**Principle of operation:**
- When $V_{GE} > V_{th}$, an inversion channel forms in the P-body (MOSFET action).
- Electrons flow through the channel into the NŌü╗ drift region.
- These excess carriers **inject minority carriers (holes) from the PŌü║ emitter** into the drift region (BJT action ŌĆö the PNP formed by PŌü║/NŌü╗/P-body).
- The drift region becomes **conductivity-modulated** ŌåÆ $R_{drift}$ collapses ŌåÆ low $V_{CE(sat)}$.
- To turn off: $V_{GE} < V_{th}$ ŌåÆ channel disappears ŌåÆ injection stops.

**Equivalent circuit:** MOSFET driving the base of a PNP BJT, with a parasitic NPN (the latch-up thyristor) across the terminals.

**Input characteristic:** $I_G$ vs $V_{GE}$ ŌĆö capacitive charging curve; gate behaves as a nonlinear capacitor (like MOSFET).

**Output characteristic:** $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ ŌĆö like a BJT with $I_C$ controlled by $V_{GE}$; saturation region exists with $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$ŌĆō$3.5$ V.

#### (b) Compare the switching characteristics of power MOSFET and IGBT. Derive the expression for total switching loss in a power MOSFET. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison of switching characteristics:**

| Aspect | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|
| Turn-on speed | Very fast (ns) | Moderate (hundreds of ns) |
| Turn-off speed | Very fast (no tail current) | Slower (tail current due to minority carriers) |
| Switching frequency | Up to ~1 MHz | Up to ~20 kHz |
| Tail current | None | Yes (during turn-off due to stored minority carriers in NŌü╗ drift) |
| Switching loss | Lower at high $f_s$ | Higher due to tail |

The IGBT has a **tail current** during turn-off because stored minority carriers in the conductivity-modulated NŌü╗ drift region must recombine before the device fully blocks. This **increases turn-off energy loss**.

**Derivation of total switching loss (power MOSFET):**

**Turn-on loss:** During turn-on, $V_{DS}$ falls from $V_{CC}$ to $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_L$ and $I_D$ rises from 0 to $I_L$. Assuming **linear transitions** with rise time $t_r$ and fall time $t_{fv}$, the turn-on energy is:

$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_r} v_{DS}(t) \cdot i_D(t) \, dt$$

With linear approximations $v_{DS}(t) = V_{CC}(1 - t/t_{fv})$ and $i_D(t) = I_L (t/t_r)$:

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{6} V_{CC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_r + t_{fv})$$

**Turn-off loss:** Similarly, voltage rises from $V_{CE(sat)}$ to $V_{CC}$ in $t_{rv}$ and current falls in $t_f$:

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{6} V_{CC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{rv} + t_f)$$

**Total switching loss per cycle:**

$$E_{total} = E_{on} + E_{off} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_L}{6} (t_r + t_{fv} + t_{rv} + t_f)$$

**Average switching power:**

$$\boxed{P_{sw,total} = E_{total} \cdot f_s = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_L \cdot f_s}{6} (t_r + t_{fv} + t_{rv} + t_f)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Switching Loss Derivation):**
> Two common mistakes: (1) Students forget to **include both turn-on and turn-off energies** ŌĆö only the turn-off is derived in many textbooks, but the KTU question typically asks for **total** switching loss. (2) Many students write the integral as $\tfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_L t$ ŌĆö this is the **maximum** instantaneous switching power, **not** the energy. The correct factor is $\tfrac{1}{6}$ when both voltage and current vary linearly between 0 and full values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Power Transistors**

### Core Concepts (must remember)

- ŌśÉ **Power BJT** is **current-driven** (base current), has **low $\beta$ (5ŌĆō20)**, and is prone to **secondary breakdown**.
- ŌśÉ **Power MOSFET** is **voltage-driven** (gate voltage), has **majority-carrier conduction** (no minority carrier storage), and is **free from second breakdown**.
- ŌśÉ **IGBT** is a **hybrid** ŌĆö MOSFET input + BJT output, has **conductivity-modulated drift region** for low $V_{CE(sat)}$, but suffers from **tail current** at turn-off.
- ŌśÉ **SIT** is a **normally-ON** device (current flows at zero gate voltage) with **non-saturating** triode-like characteristics.
- ŌśÉ The **Power Electronics Trade-off Triangle**: high power + high speed + easy drive ŌĆö you can only pick **two**.

### Critical Formulas (memorise for ESE)

- ŌśÉ BJT: $I_C = \beta_{DC} I_B + I_{CEO}$
- ŌśÉ MOSFET: $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$
- ŌśÉ Conduction loss: $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_L \cdot D$
- ŌśÉ Switching loss: $P_{sw,total} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_L f_s (t_r + t_{fv} + t_{rv} + t_f)$
- ŌśÉ Gate drive power: $P_{gate} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_s$
- ŌśÉ Thermal limit: $P_{D,max} = (T_{j,max} - T_c)/R_{th(j-c)}$

### SOA Boundaries (4 limits ŌĆö memorise all)

- ŌśÉ **Maximum current** $I_{C,max}$ ŌĆö bond-wire limit
- ŌśÉ **Maximum voltage** $V_{CEO(SUS)}$ ŌĆö avalanche limit
- ŌśÉ **Maximum power** $P_{D,max}$ ŌĆö thermal limit
- ŌśÉ **Secondary breakdown** $V_{CE} I_C^n = $ const ŌĆö hot-spot limit (most restrictive for BJT)

### Exam Favourites (Killer Questions)

- ŌśÉ Compare **BJT vs MOSFET vs IGBT** ŌĆö a 14-mark favourite.
- ŌśÉ Derive **conduction and switching losses** ŌĆö appears almost every semester.
- ŌśÉ Draw and explain the **SOA** of a power BJT with all four boundaries.
- ŌśÉ Explain the **IGBT equivalent circuit** and the **latch-up** failure mode.
- ŌśÉ Discuss the **static induction transistor (SIT)** construction and characteristics.
- ŌśÉ Sketch **switching waveforms** of a power BJT showing all four intervals (turn-on: $t_d, t_r, t_{fv}$; turn-off: $t_s, t_{rv}, t_f$).

### Common Examiner Traps

- ŌśÉ Don't confuse **SIT (Static Induction Transistor)** with **SITH (Static Induction Thyristor)**.
- ŌśÉ **Power BJT** has vertical structure, NOT planar ŌĆö many students draw the wrong cross-section.
- ŌśÉ The factor in switching loss is **1/6**, not 1/2 ŌĆö derived assuming **linear** transitions.
- ŌśÉ IGBT **tail current** is the main reason IGBTs cannot switch as fast as MOSFETs.

> **End of Module 1 ŌĆö Power Transistors Notes**
> *Next module preview: Thyristors ŌĆö Concept, characteristics, gate triggering, and commutation techniques.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFETs and IGBTs: Structure, Fabrication, Regions of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOSFETs and IGBTs: Structure, Fabrication, Regions of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power MOSFET ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Power MOSFET (MetalŌĆōOxideŌĆōSemiconductor Field Effect Transistor)** is a majority-carrier, voltage-controlled unipolar semiconductor device that uses a metalŌĆōoxideŌĆōgate structure to control current flow between the drain and source terminals through a *vertically oriented conduction channel*. Unlike signal-level MOSFETs, power MOSFETs are fabricated using a **VDMOS (Vertical Double-diffused MOS)** architecture so that the device can sustain high blocking voltages (typically **500 V ŌĆō 1000 V**) while still offering very fast switching speeds (in the range of **20 ns ŌĆō 200 ns**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power MOSFETs are the workhorse of low-to-medium voltage, high-frequency power conversion (SMPS, motor drives < 5 kW, LED drivers). The absence of minority-carrier storage makes them the *fastest* power switch available.

## 1.2 IGBT ŌĆö Formal Definition

An **IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)** is a hybrid three-terminal, voltage-controlled *bipolar* power semiconductor device that combines the high-input-impedance, easy-drive characteristics of a MOSFET with the low conduction (saturation) loss of a BJT. It has a **MOSFET input stage** driving a **wide-base PNP bipolar output stage**, giving it excellent voltage and current handling capability (typically **600 V ŌĆō 6500 V** and **10 A ŌĆō 3600 A**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (Board-Exam Favourite):** MOSFET = *Unipolar* (only electrons carry current ŌåÆ fast). IGBT = *Bipolar* (electrons + holes ŌåÆ slow but low loss). IGBT has a **tail current** at turn-off due to stored minority carriers in the nŌü╗ drift region.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Smart Water Tap"

Imagine a high-pressure water pipe carrying **100 bar** of fluid:

- The **gate** of a MOSFET/IGBT is like a *solenoid-operated valve handle*. You only need to *touch* it (apply voltage, no continuous current) to open/close the pipe ŌĆö that is the **voltage-controlled** nature.
- The **drainŌĆōsource (or collectorŌĆōemitter) channel** is the actual pipe carrying the high-pressure flow.
- The **body diode** (intrinsic anti-parallel diode in MOSFETs) acts as a *check-valve* that lets current sneak back when the inductive load (like a motor) tries to push back ŌĆö protecting the switch.
- A **MOSFET** is a *fast-acting tap* ŌĆö you can open/close it thousands of times per second, but heavy flow wastes pressure (high R<sub>DS(on)</sub> loss).
- An **IGBT** is a *slower, chunkier industrial tap* ŌĆö it cannot switch as fast, but for the same pipe size, it loses much less pressure (low V<sub>CE(sat)</sub>) when fully open.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Metric | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier type | Unipolar (electrons) | Bipolar (eŌü╗ + holes) |
| Typical blocking voltage | **50 V ŌĆō 1000 V** | **600 V ŌĆō 6500 V** |
| Switching frequency | **50 kHz ŌĆō 1 MHz** | **1 kHz ŌĆō 50 kHz** |
| Conduction loss driver | R<sub>DS(on)</sub> (resistive) | V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> (constant) |
| Input impedance | **> 10Ōü╣ ╬®** (gate) | **> 10Ōü╣ ╬®** (gate) |
| Tail current | **None** | **Present (┬Ąs order)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power MOSFET ŌĆö Structure (VDMOS Cell)

The vertical structure is built on an **nŌü║ silicon substrate** (the drain contact at the bottom). The layers from bottom to top are:

1. **nŌü║ Drain substrate** ŌĆö heavily doped, provides low-resistance contact to drain.
2. **nŌü╗ Epitaxial (drift) layer** ŌĆö lightly doped; thickness of this layer sets the **breakdown voltage (BV<sub>DSS</sub>)**.
3. **p-body (well)** ŌĆö formed by *double diffusion*; this defines the channel length precisely.
4. **nŌü║ source** ŌĆö diffused inside the p-body, shorted to the p-body via source metallization (this eliminates the parasitic BJT).
5. **SiOŌéé gate oxide** ŌĆö very thin (~ 50 nm); must not exceed ~ 10 V to avoid rupture.
6. **Polysilicon gate** ŌĆö covers the channel region between the two nŌü║ source diffusions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why double diffusion?** The channel length L = depth of p-body ŌłÆ depth of nŌü║ source. Because diffusion lengths can be controlled to fractions of a micrometre, V<sub>th</sub> and transconductance become uniform across millions of cells on a single die.

### 2.1.1 Regions of Operation of Power MOSFET

The standard V<sub>GS</sub>ŌĆōV<sub>DS</sub> operating map has **three regions** (with I<sub>D</sub> vs V<sub>DS</sub> characteristics analogous to a small-signal MOSFET):

| Region | Condition | Conduction State | Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cut-off** | V<sub>GS</sub> < V<sub>th</sub> | Channel not formed, I<sub>D</sub> = 0 | I<sub>D</sub> = 0 |
| **Triode / Ohmic** | V<sub>GS</sub> > V<sub>th</sub> AND V<sub>DS</sub> < (V<sub>GS</sub> ŌłÆ V<sub>th</sub>) | Channel pinched at drain end; acts as variable resistor | I<sub>D</sub> = ┬Ą<sub>n</sub>C<sub>ox</sub>(W/L)┬Ę[(V<sub>GS</sub>ŌłÆV<sub>th</sub>)V<sub>DS</sub> ŌłÆ V<sub>DS</sub>┬▓/2] |
| **Saturation (Active)** | V<sub>GS</sub> > V<sub>th</sub> AND V<sub>DS</sub> Ōēź (V<sub>GS</sub> ŌłÆ V<sub>th</sub>) | Channel pinched-off near drain; I<sub>D</sub> constant with V<sub>DS</sub> | I<sub>D</sub> = (1/2)┬Ę┬Ą<sub>n</sub>C<sub>ox</sub>(W/L)┬Ę(V<sub>GS</sub>ŌłÆV<sub>th</sub>)┬▓ |

**Key operating note for power applications:** A power MOSFET is *used as a switch*, so it spends most of its time in **Cut-off (OFF state)** or in the **Ohmic/Triode region (fully ON state)**. The saturation region is only *transiently* traversed during switching transitions.

## 2.2 IGBT ŌĆö Structure (Punch-Through vs Non-Punch-Through)

The IGBT is a *four-layer PNPN* device but it is driven like a MOSFET. The layers from top (emitter) to bottom (collector) are:

1. **nŌü║ Emitter (source) region** ŌĆö equivalent to MOSFET source.
2. **p-body (well)** ŌĆö MOSFET channel is formed on its surface.
3. **nŌü╗ drift region** ŌĆö long, lightly doped region; supports the high blocking voltage.
4. **nŌü║ buffer layer** ŌĆö present in **PT (Punch-Through)** IGBTs; *absent* in **NPT (Non-Punch-Through)** IGBTs.
5. **pŌü║ Collector (substrate)** ŌĆö this is the layer that *injects minority holes* into the nŌü╗ drift region, causing **conductivity modulation** and hence the low V<sub>CE(sat)</sub>.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conductivity Modulation:** The pŌü║ collector injects holes into the nŌü╗ region, flooding it with carriers and *drastically reducing* the on-state resistance ŌĆö this is why IGBTs beat high-voltage MOSFETs by an order of magnitude in conduction loss.

### 2.2.1 Regions of Operation of IGBT

| Region | V<sub>GE</sub> | V<sub>CE</sub> | Equivalent Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Blocking** | V<sub>GE</sub> < V<sub>th</sub> | Positive | IGBT is OFF; J1 (collectorŌĆōdrift) junction blocks |
| **Forward Conduction (Active)** | V<sub>GE</sub> > V<sub>th</sub> | V<sub>CE</sub> > V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> | Acts as a PNP transistor; I<sub>C</sub> controlled by V<sub>GE</sub> |
| **Saturation (Used as a switch ON)** | V<sub>GE</sub> > V<sub>th</sub> | V<sub>CE</sub> Ōēż V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> | Fully ON; minimum conduction loss (~ 1.5 ŌĆō 2.5 V drop) |
| **Reverse Blocking** | Any | V<sub>CE</sub> < 0 | Symmetric IGBTs block ~ a few hundred V; asymmetric IGBTs conduct via diode |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU ESE ŌĆö memorize and practise.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{D(sat)} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}$ | MOSFET saturation current | A |
| 2 | $I_{D(triode)} = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$ | MOSFET triode current | A |
| 3 | $r_{DS(on)} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})}$ | ON-state resistance of MOSFET | ╬® |
| 4 | $V_{CE(sat)} = V_{BE(on)} + I_{C}\cdot R_{mod}$ | IGBT on-state drop (Ōēł 1.5 ŌĆō 2.5 V) | V |
| 5 | $BV_{DSS} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{s}\cdot E_{crit}^{2}}{2\,q\,N_{D}}$ | Parallel-plane breakdown of nŌü╗ drift | V |
| 6 | $R_{drift} = \dfrac{\rho_{n}\cdot t_{drift}}{A}$ | Drift-region resistance (sets R<sub>DS(on)</sub> for HV) | ╬® |
| 7 | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^{2}\cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | MOSFET conduction loss | W |
| 8 | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)}\cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE}\cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ | IGBT conduction loss | W |
| 9 | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V_{dc}\,I_{d}\,(t_{on}+t_{off})\cdot f_{sw}$ | Switching loss (per switch) | W |
| 10 | $E_{tail} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V_{CE}\cdot I_{tail}\cdot t_{tail}$ | IGBT turn-off tail-energy loss | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering use:** The R<sub>DS(on)</sub> vs BV<sub>DSS</sub> trade-off (the *silicon limit*, sometimes called the "Si limit line") is plotted on a logŌĆōlog graph: $R_{DS(on)}\cdot A \propto BV^{2.5}$. Above ~ 200 V blocking, IGBTs *always* win on conduction loss, which is precisely why traction drives, HVDC converters and industrial inverters all use IGBTs above that rating.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of MOSFET Saturation Current (I<sub>D(sat)</sub>)

The derivation begins from the **gradual channel approximation (GCA)**, which assumes the lateral field along the channel is much weaker than the transverse field across the gate oxide.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inversion-layer charge density at position y along the channel:**

The local gate-to-channel voltage is $V_{GS} - V(y)$, where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential (0 at source, V<sub>DS</sub> at drain). The induced mobile charge per unit area in the channel is:

$$
Q_{inv}(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{th}]
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Drift current at position y:**

The drain current is the integration of drift velocity ├Ś charge over the channel width W:

$$
I_{D} = -\mu_{n}\,W\,Q_{inv}(y)\,\dfrac{dV(y)}{dy}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute Q<sub>inv</sub> and integrate over the channel length L from y = 0 (source) to y = L (drain):**

$$
I_{D}\,dy = \mu_{n}\,W\,C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{th}]\,dV(y)
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{L} I_{D}\,dy = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,W\,\int_{0}^{V_{DS}}\![V_{GS}-V_{th}-V(y)]\,dV(y)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integrals:**

$$
I_{D}\cdot L = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,W\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for I<sub>D</sub> (the triode-region equation):**

$$
I_{D} = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the saturation boundary condition** $V_{DS} = V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ (channel just pinched off at the drain end):

$$
I_{D(sat)} = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2} - \dfrac{(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_{D(sat)} = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,\dfrac{(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;I_{D(sat)} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The W/L ratio is the geometric *aspect ratio* of the channel. Power MOSFETs achieve very low R<sub>DS(on)</sub> by paralleling **millions of identical cells** with large effective W/L, all sharing one V<sub>GS</sub>.

## 3.2 Derivation of IGBT On-State Voltage Drop

The IGBT in conduction is modelled as a **wide-base PNP BJT** with the MOSFET injecting base current.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Emitter (collector-side) injection:**

The pŌü║ collector injects holes into the nŌü╗ drift. The total on-state voltage is the sum of three drops:

$$
V_{CE(on)} = V_{BE(pnp)} + V_{drift} + I_{C}\cdot R_{mod}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Modulated drift-region resistance:**

Conductivity modulation by holes reduces the nŌü╗ drift resistance from its un-modulated value $R_{drift}$ to:

$$
R_{mod} = \dfrac{R_{drift}}{(1 + \beta_{pnp})}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final expression used in datasheets:**

$$
V_{CE(sat)} = V_{T}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{C}}{I_{SAT}}+1\right) + \dfrac{I_{C}\cdot W_{drift}}{q\,A\,(\mu_{n}\,n + \mu_{p}\,p)}
$$

where the second term is the **ambipolar transport** drop across the modulated drift region. For typical 1200 V IGBTs, V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> Ōēł **1.7 V to 2.3 V**, almost *independent* of current ŌĆö this is the secret of the IGBT's efficiency at high current.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (MOSFET vs IGBT trade-off)

| Parameter | Power MOSFET | IGBT | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction loss at 600 V, 100 A | High (R<sub>DS(on)</sub> ~ 0.1 ╬® ŌćÆ 10 W/V) | Low (V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> ~ 2 V ŌćÆ 200 W) | **IGBT** |
| Switching loss at 20 kHz | Very low | Higher (tail current) | **MOSFET** |
| Switching loss at 200 kHz | Low | Catastrophic | **MOSFET** |
| Voltage rating achievable economically | Up to ~ 1000 V | Up to 6500 V | **IGBT** |
| Ease of gate drive (gate charge) | Higher Q<sub>g</sub> at high V | Comparable | Tie |
| Reverse blocking capability | None (only body diode) | Limited (or via co-pack diode) | **MOSFET** (with body diode) |
| Short-circuit ruggedness | Poor | Excellent (~ 10 ┬Ąs) | **IGBT** |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exam:** Use **MOSFET** when V<sub>DC</sub> < 250 V and f<sub>sw</sub> > 50 kHz. Use **IGBT** when V<sub>DC</sub> > 600 V and f<sub>sw</sub> < 20 kHz. The 250 V ŌĆō 600 V range is the "sweet spot" where both compete.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power MOSFET Cross-Section (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the *vertical functional architecture* of a single VDMOS cell ŌĆö showing current flow, gate control, and the intrinsic body diode.

```mermaid
graph TD
    termG[Gate Terminal G] --> polyGate[Polysilicon Gate]
    termS[Source Terminal S] --> metalS[Source Metal Shorted to p-body]
    termD[Drain Terminal D] --> subDrain[n+ Drain Substrate]
    termB[Body Terminal B] --> bodyContact[p-body Contact]

    subDrain --> driftN[n- Epitaxial Drift Region]
    driftN --> bodyP[p-body Well]
    bodyP --> sourceN[n+ Source Diffusion]
    driftN --> fieldPlate[Depletion Region at p-n Junction]

    polyGate --> gateOx[SiO2 Gate Oxide 50nm]
    gateOx --> invChannel[Inversion Channel forms when VGS greater than Vth]
    invChannel --> currentFlow[Lateral Electron Flow Source to Drain]

    bodyP --> bodyDiode[Intrinsic Body Diode p-body to n- drift]
    bodyDiode --> freewheel[Provides Free-wheeling Path for Inductive Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as if you are looking through the silicon die from source-side.** The current path during conduction is: **Source metal ŌåÆ nŌü║ source ŌåÆ inversion channel (lateral) ŌåÆ n-body ŌåÆ nŌü╗ drift ŌåÆ nŌü║ substrate ŌåÆ Drain metal**. The gate oxide separates the polysilicon gate from this entire conduction path.

## 4.2 IGBT Cross-Section & Functional Equivalence

The IGBT is electrically equivalent to a **low-gain PNP BJT driven by a MOSFET**. The Mermaid diagram below maps the layer structure to its *BJT + MOSFET* functional equivalent.

```mermaid
graph TD
    mosfetInput[MOSFET Input Stage n-channel VDMOS] --> bjtOutput[Wide-base PNP BJT Output Stage]

    mosfetInput --> gTerm[Gate G]
    mosfetInput --> eTerm[Emitter E - n+ source plus p-body]
    mosfetInput --> channelMOS[Inversion Channel forms when VGE greater than Vth]

    bjtOutput --> cTerm[Collector C - p+ Substrate]
    bjtOutput --> baseRegion[n- Drift Region - acts as PNP base]
    bjtOutput --> bufferLayer[n+ Buffer Layer - present in PT IGBT only]
    bjtOutput --> holes[Hole Injection from p+ into n- Drift]

    channelMOS --> baseCurrent[Drives Base Current of PNP]
    baseCurrent --> modulatedDrift[Conductivity Modulation of n- Drift]
    modulatedDrift --> lowDrop[Low VCE sat drop 1.5 to 2.5 V]

    holes --> tailCurrent[Tail Current at Turn-off - major switching loss]
```

## 4.3 Switching Waveform Comparison

The timing diagram below contrasts turn-off behaviour of MOSFET vs IGBT ŌĆö a *favourite* KTU viva question.

```mermaid
graph LR
    t0[Time t0 Gate Pulse Removed] --> t1[t0 to t1 VGS falls below Vth]
    t1 --> t2MOS[MOSFET ID falls to zero in nanoseconds]
    t1 --> t2IGBT[IGBT ID falls quickly then exhibits tail current lasting microseconds]
    t2MOS --> t3M[MOSFET VDS rises to Vdc with small overshoot]
    t2IGBT --> t3I[IGBT VCE rises to Vdc with larger overshoot due to tail]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 / Model]**
*Compare the switching characteristics of a power MOSFET and an IGBT. Why is an IGBT preferred for high-voltage applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **MOSFET** is a unipolar device with switching times in the range of **20ŌĆō200 ns**, governed by charging/discharging of gate and junction capacitances. It exhibits *no tail current* at turn-off.
2. **IGBT** is a bipolar-conductivity-modulated device. Its turn-on is fast (~ 0.5 ┬Ąs) but turn-off shows a **tail current** lasting 0.2 ŌĆō 2 ┬Ąs due to stored minority carriers in the nŌü╗ drift region.
3. Because of **conductivity modulation**, the IGBT's on-state drop V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> is ~ 1.5 ŌĆō 2.5 V regardless of voltage rating, whereas a power MOSFET's R<sub>DS(on)</sub> rises **with the 2.5th power of breakdown voltage**. Hence for **V<sub>DC</sub> > 600 V**, IGBTs dissipate far less conduction power and are preferred.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 / Model]**
*Explain the role of the nŌü╗ drift region in a power MOSFET and in an IGBT. Why is its thickness different in the two devices?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. In a **power MOSFET**, the nŌü╗ drift region is *lightly doped and thick* to support the full blocking voltage V<sub>DS(max)</sub>. Its resistance is the *dominant* contributor to R<sub>DS(on)</sub>, so conduction loss rises sharply with voltage rating.
2. In an **IGBT**, the nŌü╗ drift region is similarly thick to support blocking voltage, but during conduction the **pŌü║ collector injects holes** that *modulate* its conductivity, reducing its resistance by a factor of (1 + ╬▓<sub>PNP</sub>).
3. The **thickness** is set by the *avalanche-breakdown requirement* $BV = E_{crit}\cdot W_{drift}/2$ ŌĆö both devices follow the same physical relationship, so for the same voltage rating the drift-region thicknesses are *comparable*, but the IGBT's lower effective resistance makes the trade-off much more favourable.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice: A or B)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "Saturation" of a MOSFET (where I<sub>D</sub> is constant) with "Saturation" of a BJT (where V<sub>CE</sub> is minimum). They are *different physical states*.
> - **Do NOT claim** IGBTs have no tail current. The tail current is the *defining* switching loss mechanism.
> - **Always draw** the V<sub>GS</sub>/V<sub>DS</sub> axes and *label* the three regions in any sketch.
> - **Forgetting to mention** the body diode in MOSFET diagrams costs 1 ŌĆō 2 marks.
> - **Avoid ambiguity:** "MOSFET is faster than IGBT" without numerical switching-time values loses marks.

### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 / Model]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional sketch, explain the **structure and operating principle of a Power MOSFET (VDMOS)**.
