Clearly label the source, gate, drain, p-body, nŌü║ source, nŌü╗ drift, and the intrinsic body diode. Discuss the **regions of operation** with their IŌĆōV equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **drain current in the saturation region** of a MOSFET, starting from the gradual channel approximation. Show all integration steps. For a device with ┬Ą<sub>n</sub>C<sub>ox</sub> = 50 ┬ĄA/V┬▓, W/L = 1000, and V<sub>th</sub> = 4 V, compute I<sub>D(sat)</sub> when V<sub>GS</sub> = 10 V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**1. Structure (3 marks):** The VDMOS (Vertical Double-diffused MOS) is built on an nŌü║ silicon substrate that forms the drain. A lightly doped nŌü╗ epitaxial layer is grown on top to sustain high blocking voltage. A p-body is then diffused into the nŌü╗ epitaxy, and inside this p-body an nŌü║ source is diffused. A thin SiOŌéé gate oxide (~ 50 nm) is grown on the surface, and a polysilicon gate is deposited above it. The source metal short-circuits the nŌü║ source and p-body, eliminating the parasitic NPN BJT. The intrinsic body diode (p-body / nŌü╗ drift) is formed automatically.

**2. Operating Principle (2 marks):** When V<sub>GS</sub> > V<sub>th</sub>, an *inversion layer* of electrons is induced at the surface of the p-body, forming a continuous n-channel from nŌü║ source to nŌü╗ drift. Drain current flows vertically through the nŌü╗ drift to the nŌü║ drain. When V<sub>GS</sub> < V<sub>th</sub>, no channel exists and the device blocks voltage (J1 junction reverse-biased).

**3. Regions of Operation (2 marks):**

- **Cut-off:** $V_{GS} < V_{th} \Rightarrow I_D = 0$
- **Triode / Ohmic:** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{th})$, with $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$
- **Saturation (Active):** $V_{DS} \ge (V_{GS}-V_{th})$, with $I_{D(sat)} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}$

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):** The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final boxed result is

$$
I_{D(sat)} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}
$$

**Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**

Given: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\;\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 50\times 10^{-6}\;\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 1000$, $V_{th} = 4\;\text{V}$, $V_{GS} = 10\;\text{V}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the overdrive voltage:**

$$
V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 10 - 4 = 6\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the saturation-current formula:**

$$
I_{D(sat)} = \dfrac{1}{2}\times 50\times 10^{-6}\times 1000 \times (6)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate each factor:**

$$
I_{D(sat)} = \dfrac{1}{2}\times 50\times 10^{-3} \times 36
$$

$$
I_{D(sat)} = 25\times 10^{-3} \times 36 = 900\times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;I_{D(sat)} = 0.9\;\text{A} = 900\;\text{mA}\;}
$$

---

### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 / Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **structure of an IGBT** and explain why the IGBT has a *lower* on-state voltage drop than a comparable power MOSFET at high blocking voltages. Include a sketch of the equivalent circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **regions of operation of the IGBT** in detail. Discuss the **tail current** phenomenon at turn-off and derive the expression for the associated **tail-current switching loss**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Structure (3 marks):** An IGBT has four functional layers: a pŌü║ collector (substrate) at the bottom, an nŌü╗ drift region above it (which may or may not include an nŌü║ buffer layer ŌĆö *Punch-Through* vs *Non-Punch-Through* variants), a p-body region, and an nŌü║ emitter at the top, identical to a VDMOS source. A polysilicon gate separated by SiOŌéé covers the p-body surface. The pŌü║ collector is the *defining* layer that does not exist in a MOSFET.

**Equivalent circuit (2 marks):** The IGBT is electrically equivalent to a **P-channel MOSFET (driven by the n-channel input) feeding the base of a wide-base PNP BJT** (formed by pŌü║ collector / nŌü╗ drift / p-body). The input stage provides the high gate impedance; the output stage provides the high current capability with low saturation drop.

**Why lower V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> (2 marks):** When the IGBT is ON, the pŌü║ collector injects **holes** into the nŌü╗ drift region. The resulting *conductivity modulation* floods the drift region with minority carriers, reducing its resistance by a factor of (1 + ╬▓<sub>PNP</sub>). A power MOSFET lacks this hole injection ŌĆö its nŌü╗ drift resistance is the *un-modulated* value, which scales as $BV^{2.5}$. For a 1200 V device, the IGBT on-state drop is Ōēł 2 V regardless of current, while the MOSFET's I┬▓R drop is several times higher.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Regions of Operation (3 marks):**

| Region | Condition | Physical State |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Blocking | V<sub>GE</sub> < V<sub>th</sub>, V<sub>CE</sub> > 0 | Channel absent; junction J1 (pŌü║/nŌü╗) supports full V<sub>CE</sub> |
| Forward Active | V<sub>GE</sub> > V<sub>th</sub>, V<sub>CE</sub> > V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> | IGBT behaves like a PNP BJT in active region; I<sub>C</sub> ŌłØ (V<sub>GE</sub> ŌłÆ V<sub>th</sub>)┬▓ |
| Saturation (ON) | V<sub>GE</sub> > V<sub>th</sub>, V<sub>CE</sub> Ōēł V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> Ōēł 2 V | V<sub>CE</sub> is small; IGBT in *full conduction*; this is the steady-state ON state |
| Reverse Blocking | V<sub>CE</sub> < 0 | Depends on type ŌĆö symmetric IGBTs block, asymmetric IGBTs conduct via integrated diode |

**Tail Current Phenomenon (2 marks):** When the gate is turned off, the MOSFET channel disappears *instantaneously*, but the **stored minority holes** in the nŌü╗ drift region continue to support collector current for a few hundred nanoseconds to several microseconds until they recombine. This decaying current (which does *not* contribute useful output) defines the **tail current**, and the product of V<sub>CE</sub> ├Ś I<sub>tail</sub> during this interval is the dominant IGBT switching loss.

**Tail-Current Loss Derivation (2 marks):** Approximating the tail current as a linearly decaying ramp from IŌéĆ to 0 over duration t<sub>tail</sub>:

$$
I_{tail}(t) = I_{0}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{t}{t_{tail}}\right)
$$

The energy dissipated in the device during the tail interval (V<sub>CE</sub> Ōēł V<sub>DC</sub> assumed constant) is:

$$
E_{tail} = \int_{0}^{t_{tail}} V_{DC}\cdot I_{tail}(t)\,dt = V_{DC}\cdot I_{0}\int_{0}^{t_{tail}}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{t}{t_{tail}}\right)dt
$$

$$
E_{tail} = V_{DC}\cdot I_{0}\!\left[t - \dfrac{t^{2}}{2\,t_{tail}}\right]_{0}^{t_{tail}}
$$

$$
E_{tail} = V_{DC}\cdot I_{0}\!\left[t_{tail} - \dfrac{t_{tail}}{2}\right] = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\cdot I_{0}\cdot t_{tail}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{tail} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_{0}\,t_{tail}\;}
$$

The corresponding *average* switching power loss is $P_{sw,tail} = E_{tail}\cdot f_{sw}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 15 points before entering the exam hall.**

- **MOSFET = unipolar, voltage-controlled, majority-carrier device** ŌĆö fastest power switch.
- **IGBT = bipolar, voltage-controlled at input, bipolar-conduction at output** ŌĆö lowest high-voltage conduction loss.
- **VDMOS structure** has *nŌü║ drain substrate, nŌü╗ drift, p-body, nŌü║ source* stacked vertically.
- **IGBT structure** adds a *pŌü║ collector layer* beneath the nŌü╗ drift ŌĆö this is the *only* layer missing in a MOSFET.
- **Three MOSFET regions:** Cut-off (I<sub>D</sub> = 0), Triode (variable resistor), Saturation (constant I<sub>D</sub>).
- **Power MOSFET as a switch** is used in *cut-off* and *triode* regions only.
- **Saturation current** $I_{D(sat)} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}$ ŌĆö derive from gradual channel approximation.
- **Conductivity modulation** is the *single most important* reason IGBTs beat MOSFETs above 600 V.
- **Tail current** in IGBTs = stored minority holes in nŌü╗ drift decaying at turn-off.
- **R<sub>DS(on)</sub> ŌłØ BV<sup>2.5</sup>** ŌĆö the silicon limit that forces us to use IGBTs at high voltage.
- **Gate drive** for both: typically +15 V ON, 0 V (or ŌłÆ5 V to ŌłÆ15 V) OFF.
- **Body diode** in MOSFETs provides *free-wheeling* path for inductive loads.
- **IGBT has no body diode** (in basic form) ŌĆö needs a co-packed anti-parallel diode.
- **MOSFET preferred** below 250 V / above 50 kHz; **IGBT preferred** above 600 V / below 20 kHz.
- **Switching loss** for both: $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_d\,(t_{on}+t_{off})\,f_{sw}$; for IGBT add the *tail* term $\tfrac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_{0}\,t_{tail}\,f_{sw}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Characteristics: Turn-On and Turn-Off Switching Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching Characteristics: Turn-On and Turn-Off Switching Waveforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **power semiconductor devices** (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, SCR, GTO), the **Switching Characteristics** describe the *transient electrical response* of the device voltage ($v(t)$) and current ($i(t)$) during the transitions between the **ON-state (conducting)** and the **OFF-state (blocking)**. These characteristics are governed by the internal physics of charge storage, junction capacitances, and carrier recombination dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Core Concept:** Power devices are **not ideal switches**. They exhibit finite transition times known as **Turn-On Time ($t_{on}$)** and **Turn-Off Time ($t_{off}$)**. During these intervals, simultaneous high voltage and high current exist across the device, producing **Switching Losses ($P_{sw}$)** ŌĆö a critical parameter in converter design.

**Defining the Four Canonical Switching Intervals:**

- **Turn-On Time ($t_{on}$):** Time from the instant the gating signal is applied (10% of gate drive) until the device current/voltage reaches 90% of its final ON-state value.
- **Turn-Off Time ($t_{off}$):** Time from the instant the gating signal is removed until the device fully blocks the supply voltage.
- **Turn-On Delay Time ($t_d$ or $t_{d(on)}$):** The initial lag before current begins to rise appreciably (corresponds to charging of input capacitance or base charge buildup).
- **Turn-Off Delay / Storage Time ($t_s$):** Time delay before voltage begins to rise, due to stored charge that must be removed (especially in BJT and SCR).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Heavy Water Tap"

Imagine a **large industrial water tap** (the power device) controlling a high-pressure water main (the supply). An ideal tap would open and close *instantly*. But in reality:

> The tap handle (**gate/base drive signal**) takes a fraction of a second to lift the valve. During that fraction:
> - The valve is **half-open** ŌåÆ both **high pressure** and **significant flow** exist simultaneously.
> - This is exactly what happens in a power device during switching transients ŌĆö the product $v \times i$ is large, generating **heat (switching loss)**.

The longer you take to open/close the tap, the more water (energy) is wasted. Similarly, faster switching = lower switching loss = higher converter efficiency.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Benchmark Values (for 600V/50A class IGBT at 25┬░C):**
> - **$t_{d(on)}$** Ōēł 50ŌĆō200 ns
> - **$t_r$** (rise time) Ōēł 50ŌĆō300 ns
> - **$t_{d(off)}$** Ōēł 200ŌĆō500 ns
> - **$t_f$** (fall time) Ōēł 100ŌĆō400 ns
> - **$E_{on}$** Ōēł 0.1ŌĆō2 mJ per pulse
> - **$E_{off}$** Ōēł 0.2ŌĆō3 mJ per pulse

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Turn-On Transient

The turn-on process is divided into three sub-intervals (sequential logic):

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Delay Time ($t_d$ or $t_{d(on)}$):**
  - *Why:* The gate/base drive must first charge the input capacitance ($C_{iss}$ for MOSFET, or overcome the emitterŌĆōbase junction barrier for BJT).
  - *Physical action:* No appreciable collector/drain current flows. Device voltage remains at the supply level (blocking state).
  - *Duration:* Time for $v_{GE}$ to rise from 0 to threshold voltage $V_{GE(th)}$ (for MOSFET/IGBT).

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Current Rise Time ($t_r$):**
  - *Why:* Once the channel is formed, the device current rises from 10% to 90% of its final value.
  - *Physical action:* Voltage across device is still high (has not yet collapsed). This is the period of **maximum simultaneous $V \times I$** ŌåÆ peak turn-on loss.
  - *Di/dt behavior:* Limited by device transconductance and circuit inductance.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Voltage Fall Time ($t_{fv}$):**
  - *Why:* As the channel fully opens, the on-state voltage drop ($V_{CE(sat)}$) establishes, and the load current commutates from the freewheeling diode to the switch.
  - *Physical action:* Device voltage falls to its low saturation value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Identity for Total Turn-On Time:**
> $$t_{on} = t_{d(on)} + t_r + t_{fv}$$

### 2.2 Breakdown of the Turn-Off Transient

The turn-off process has three sub-intervals:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Turn-Off Delay / Storage Time ($t_s$):**
  - *Why:* Stored minority carriers in the drift region must be removed (recombined or swept out).
  - *BJT/IGBT specific:* Dominated by charge storage in the base/drift region. MOSFETs have *no storage time* (major advantage).
  - *Physical action:* Voltage across device begins to rise; current remains nearly constant (load current must continue ŌĆö provided by freewheeling diode).

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Voltage Rise Time ($t_{rv}$):**
  - *Why:* Device is regaining its blocking capability as the depletion region expands.
  - *dv/dt behavior:* Limited by the device's output capacitance and the snubber/load inductance.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Current Fall Time ($t_f$):**
  - *Why:* The channel pinches off and carriers are evacuated. Current decays from 90% to 10% of its on-state value.
  - *Tail current (IGBT specific):* BJT-like devices show a "current tail" due to slow recombination in the drift region ŌĆö this is the dominant contributor to $E_{off}$ loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Identity for Total Turn-Off Time:**
> $$t_{off} = t_{d(off)} + t_{rv} + t_f$$

### 2.3 Switching Losses ŌĆö The Real Engineering Cost

**Turn-On Switching Energy ($E_{on}$):**
$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} v_{sw}(t) \cdot i_{sw}(t) \, dt$$

**Turn-Off Switching Energy ($E_{off}$):**
$$E_{off} = \int_{0}^{t_{off}} v_{sw}(t) \cdot i_{sw}(t) \, dt$$

**Total Switching Power Loss (for switching frequency $f_{sw}$):**
$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} \left( E_{on} + E_{off} \right) = \frac{1}{T} \left( E_{on} + E_{off} \right)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application Context |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Total Turn-On Time | $t_{on} = t_{d(on)} + t_{r} + t_{fv}$ | seconds (s) | All gated devices |
| 2 | Total Turn-Off Time | $t_{off} = t_{d(off)} + t_{rv} + t_{f}$ | seconds (s) | All gated devices |
| 3 | Turn-On Energy | $E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} v(t)\,i(t)\,dt$ | Joules (J) | Per switching pulse |
| 4 | Turn-Off Energy | $E_{off} = \int_{0}^{t_{off}} v(t)\,i(t)\,dt$ | Joules (J) | Per switching pulse |
| 5 | Switching Power Loss | $P_{sw} = f_{sw}(E_{on} + E_{off})$ | Watts (W) | Continuous operation |
| 6 | Average Power Loss | $P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{cond}$ | Watts (W) | Thermal design |
| 7 | Conduction Loss (IGBT) | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ | Watts (W) | IGBT only |
| 8 | Conduction Loss (MOSFET) | $P_{cond} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ | Watts (W) | MOSFET only |
| 9 | Junction Temp Rise | $\Delta T_{j} = P_{total} \cdot R_{th(j-c)}$ | ┬░C or K | Heatsink design |
| 10 | Thermal Resistance | $R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{P_{dissipated}}$ | ┬░C/W | Packaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The above formulas drive the **thermal design of every switched-mode power supply (SMPS)**, **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)**, and **solar inverter**. For example, in a 10 kW inverter switching at 20 kHz, switching losses can exceed 200 W ŌĆö comparable to the conduction loss ŌĆö dictating the choice of **SiC MOSFETs** or **GaN HEMTs** for high-frequency designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Switching Loss Approximation for Linear Transitions

In KTU exam problems, the exact $v(t)$ and $i(t)$ waveforms are often modeled as **linear ramps** during the transient intervals. This allows an analytical closed-form for the switching energy.

**Assumption (KTU Standard Model):**
- During current rise: $i(t) = \dfrac{I_L}{t_r} \cdot t$ (linear ramp from 0 to load current $I_L$)
- During this interval, voltage remains approximately constant at $V_S$ (supply voltage)
- Similarly, during voltage fall, current is at $I_L$ and voltage ramps down

---

**Derivation of Turn-On Energy (Simplified Linear Model):**

The energy dissipated during turn-on is the integral of instantaneous power over the turn-on interval.

$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1:** During the **current rise phase** ($0 \le t \le t_r$), assume $v(t) = V_S$ (constant supply voltage) and $i(t)$ ramps linearly:

$$i(t) = \frac{I_L}{t_r} \cdot t$$

Substituting into the integral for the current rise portion:

$$E_{on,1} = \int_{0}^{t_r} V_S \cdot \frac{I_L}{t_r} \cdot t \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral:

$$E_{on,1} = \frac{V_S \cdot I_L}{t_r} \int_{0}^{t_r} t \, dt = \frac{V_S \cdot I_L}{t_r} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{t_r}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the limits:

$$E_{on,1} = \frac{V_S \cdot I_L}{t_r} \cdot \frac{t_r^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_r$$

**Step 4:** During the **voltage fall phase** ($t_r \le t \le t_{on}$), $i(t) \approx I_L$ and $v(t)$ ramps down from $V_S$ to $V_{CE(sat)}$:

$$v(t) = V_S - \frac{V_S - V_{CE(sat)}}{t_{fv}}(t - t_r)$$

The energy contribution (ignoring the small $V_{CE(sat)}$ term for simplicity):

$$E_{on,2} \approx \int_{t_r}^{t_{on}} v(t) \cdot I_L \, dt = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{fv}$$

**Step 5:** Total turn-on energy (approximated):

$$\boxed{E_{on} \approx \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot (t_r + t_{fv}) = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on,eff}}$$

where $t_{on,eff}$ is the effective switching overlap time.

---

**Derivation of Turn-Off Energy (Simplified Linear Model):**

During turn-off, the current fall phase dominates the loss.

**Step 1:** During the **voltage rise phase** ($0 \le t \le t_{rv}$), $i(t) \approx I_L$ (load current constant) and $v(t)$ ramps up:

$$E_{off,1} = \int_{0}^{t_{rv}} \frac{V_S}{t_{rv}} \cdot t \cdot I_L \, dt = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{rv}$$

**Step 2:** During the **current fall phase** ($t_{rv} \le t \le t_{off}$), $v(t) \approx V_S$ and $i(t)$ ramps down from $I_L$:

$$E_{off,2} = \int_{0}^{t_f} V_S \cdot I_L \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_f} \right) dt = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_f$$

**Step 3:** Total turn-off energy:

$$\boxed{E_{off} \approx \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{rv} + t_f) = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{off,eff}}$$

---

**Final Combined Switching Power Loss:**

$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} (E_{on} + E_{off}) = \frac{1}{2} V_S \cdot I_L \cdot f_{sw} \cdot (t_{on,eff} + t_{off,eff})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (why this matters):** This expression shows that switching loss scales **linearly with frequency**. Doubling $f_{sw}$ doubles $P_{sw}$. This is precisely why high-frequency converters (e.g., 1 MHz SMPS) require ultra-fast wide-bandgap devices (GaN, SiC) ŌĆö to keep $t_{on,eff} + t_{off,eff}$ in the nanosecond range.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** An IGBT switches a load with $V_S = 400$ V and $I_L = 50$ A. The effective switching overlap times are $t_{on,eff} = 200$ ns and $t_{off,eff} = 300$ ns. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz. Calculate the total switching power loss.

**Step 1: Compute Turn-On Energy**

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on,eff}$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 50 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-9} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 2 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 2: Compute Turn-Off Energy**

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{off,eff}$$

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 50 \cdot 300 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 3 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Switching Loss**

$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$$

$$P_{sw} = 20 \times 10^{3} \cdot (2 + 3) \times 10^{-3} = 100 \text{ W}$$

**Answer:** $P_{sw} = 100$ W ŌĆö a substantial heat load requiring an active cooling solution (heatsink + fan).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Switching Transient Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRIG[Trigger Source]
        GS[Gate Drive Signal]
    end

    subgraph TURNON[Turn-On Transient]
        T1[Stage 1 Delay Time td on]
        T2[Stage 2 Current Rise tr]
        T3[Stage 3 Voltage Fall tfv]
    end

    subgraph TURNOFF[Turn-Off Transient]
        T4[Stage 1 Storage ts]
        T5[Stage 2 Voltage Rise trv]
        T6[Stage 3 Current Fall tf]
    end

    subgraph DEVMOD[Power Device Internal Model]
        CIN[Input Capacitance Ciss]
        RG[Gate Resistance Rg]
        CHAN[Channel Conduction]
        DIODE[Freewheeling Diode]
    end

    subgraph LOSSCALC[Loss Computation]
        EON[Energy Eon Integration]
        EOFF[Energy Eoff Integration]
        PSW[P_sw equals f_sw times Eon plus Eoff]
    end

    subgraph THERMAL[Thermal Output]
        TJRISE[Junction Temperature Rise]
        HSINK[Heatsink Selection]
    end

    GS --> CIN
    CIN --> RG
    RG --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> CHAN

    CHAN --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6
    T6 --> DIODE

    T2 --> EON
    T5 --> EOFF
    T3 --> EON
    T6 --> EOFF

    EON --> PSW
    EOFF --> PSW
    PSW --> TJRISE
    TJRISE --> HSINK
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Switching Waveform Timing Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Domain]
        VGE[Gate Drive V_GE]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Domain]
        IC[Collector Current I_C]
        VCE[Collector Emitter Voltage V_CE]
    end

    subgraph POWER[Power Dissipation]
        P[TURN ON OVERLAP TRIANGLE]
        P2[TURN OFF OVERLAP TRIANGLE]
    end

    VGE -- 10 percent to 90 percent rise --> T1[t_d on delay]
    VGE --> T2[t_r rise]
    VGE --> T3[t_f fall]

    IC -- lags gate --> T1
    IC --> T2
    IC --> T3

    VCE -- anti phase with current --> T1
    VCE --> T2
    VCE --> T3

    T2 --> P
    T3 --> P2
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix: Device-Specific Switching Behavior

| Device | Turn-On Time | Turn-Off Time | Dominant Loss Mechanism | Tail Current | Storage Time |
|--------|--------------|---------------|-------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Power BJT** | 0.5 ŌĆō 2 ┬Ąs | 1 ŌĆō 5 ┬Ąs | Storage time loss | Severe | Large |
| **Power MOSFET** | 20 ŌĆō 100 ns | 20 ŌĆō 100 ns | Capacitive gate loss | None | None |
| **IGBT** | 50 ŌĆō 300 ns | 200 ŌĆō 800 ns | Turn-off tail current | Moderate | Moderate |
| **GTO Thyristor** | 1 ŌĆō 5 ┬Ąs | 5 ŌĆō 25 ┬Ąs | Storage time | Severe | Very large |
| **SCR** | 1 ŌĆō 10 ┬Ąs (latching) | 30 ŌĆō 300 ┬Ąs (commutation) | Commutation failure | N/A | Very large |
| **SiC MOSFET** | 10 ŌĆō 50 ns | 10 ŌĆō 50 ns | Very low | None | None |
| **GaN HEMT** | 5 ŌĆō 30 ns | 5 ŌĆō 30 ns | Extremely low | None | None |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the terms: (i) Turn-on time, (ii) Turn-off time, and (iii) Storage time of a power semiconductor device. Illustrate with the help of switching waveforms.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Turn-On Time ($t_{on}$):** The time interval from the instant the gate/base drive signal reaches 10% of its final value to the instant the device current rises to 90% of its steady-state on-state value (or device voltage falls to 10% of its blocking value). It includes the delay time and the current rise time.

$$t_{on} = t_{d(on)} + t_{r}$$

**(ii) Turn-Off Time ($t_{off}$):** The time interval from the instant the gate signal is removed (90% of drive removed) to the instant the device regains its blocking capability (current falls to 10% of on-state value). It includes storage time and fall time.

$$t_{off} = t_s + t_{fv} + t_{f}$$

**(iii) Storage Time ($t_s$):** The interval during turn-off when the device current has not yet begun to fall appreciably, because stored minority carriers in the base/drift region must first be removed by recombination or sweeping. **MOSFETs have no storage time** since they are majority-carrier devices.

> **[Mark Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Explain why switching losses increase with switching frequency. Derive the expression for total switching power loss in terms of switching times.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Why frequency dependence:** During each turn-on and turn-off transition, the device has *simultaneous* high voltage and high current. The energy dissipated per cycle ($E_{on} + E_{off}$) is roughly proportional to the overlap time. The **average power loss** is energy per cycle ├Ś number of cycles per second:

$$P_{sw} = f_{sw}(E_{on} + E_{off})$$

**Derivation (simplified):**
- During turn-on: $E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_S I_L \, t_{on,eff}$
- During turn-off: $E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_S I_L \, t_{off,eff}$

Therefore:
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_S I_L f_{sw}(t_{on,eff} + t_{off,eff})$$

This shows $P_{sw} \propto f_{sw}$ ŌĆö doubling frequency doubles loss.

> **[Mark Allocation: 1 for explanation + 2 for derivation]**

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö ESE Module Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Standard KTU ESE Pattern:** Each module has one 14-mark question split into two 7-mark sub-parts. The student must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **OPTION A ŌĆö QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, ESE Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of neat switching waveforms, explain the turn-on and turn-off characteristics of a power BJT. Clearly mark the delay time, rise time, storage time, and fall time. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** An IGBT is used in a 3-phase inverter operating at $V_{DC} = 600$ V, $I_L = 100$ A, $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz. The manufacturer's datasheet specifies $t_{on} = 250$ ns and $t_{off} = 400$ ns. Calculate (i) the turn-on energy, (ii) the turn-off energy, and (iii) the total switching power loss. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Turn-On Characteristics of Power BJT (7 marks):**

When a positive base current pulse $I_{B1}$ is applied:

1. **Delay Time ($t_d$):** Time required to charge the baseŌĆōemitter junction capacitance and raise the baseŌĆōemitter voltage to cut-in voltage $V_{BE(\gamma)}$. During this time, collector current is negligible.

2. **Rise Time ($t_r$):** The collector current rises from 10% to 90% of its saturation value $I_{C(sat)}$. Simultaneously, the collectorŌĆōemitter voltage $V_{CE}$ begins to fall from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)}$.

3. The **overlap region** during $t_r$ produces the maximum instantaneous power dissipation:
$$p(t) = V_{CE}(t) \cdot I_C(t)$$

**Turn-Off Characteristics of Power BJT (7 marks):**

When the base drive is reversed (or removed) as $I_{B2}$:

1. **Storage Time ($t_s$):** The collector current remains approximately constant at $I_{C(sat)}$ because excess minority carriers in the base region must be removed. The collectorŌĆōemitter voltage remains low.

2. **Fall Time ($t_f$):** Once the stored charge is removed, the collector current falls rapidly from 90% to 10% of $I_{C(sat)}$, and $V_{CE}$ rises back to $V_{CC}$.

3. The **overlap region** during $t_f$ produces the turn-off loss.

**Mark allocation:** 3 marks for turn-on explanation + 3 marks for turn-off explanation + 1 mark for waveform sketch.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $V_{DC} = 600$ V, $I_L = 100$ A, $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz, $t_{on} = 250$ ns, $t_{off} = 400$ ns.

**(i) Turn-On Energy:**
$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on}$$
$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 100 \cdot 250 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$E_{on} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 7.5 \text{ mJ}$$

**(ii) Turn-Off Energy:**
$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{off}$$
$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 100 \cdot 400 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$E_{off} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 12 \text{ mJ}$$

**(iii) Total Switching Power Loss:**
$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$$
$$P_{sw} = 10 \times 10^3 \cdot (7.5 + 12) \times 10^{-3}$$
$$P_{sw} = 10^4 \cdot 19.5 \times 10^{-3} = 195 \text{ W}$$

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) with units and final answer.

---

### **OPTION B ŌĆö QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, ESE Module 1]**

**(a)** Compare the switching characteristics of a power BJT, power MOSFET, and IGBT. Bring out clearly the role of minority carrier storage in determining the switching times. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A PWM inverter uses a power MOSFET with $V_{DD} = 200$ V, $I_D = 25$ A, $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz. Given the effective turn-on and turn-off times are 50 ns and 40 ns respectively, compute the total switching loss and compare it with the conduction loss if $R_{DS(on)} = 0.1$ ╬®. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Power BJT | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------|
| Carrier type | Minority (bipolar) | Majority (unipolar) | Minority (bipolar output) |
| Input drive | Current ($I_B$) | Voltage ($V_{GS}$) | Voltage ($V_{GE}$) |
| Storage time | Large (┬Ąs range) | Negligible (ns) | Moderate (sub-┬Ąs) |
| Turn-on time | 0.5ŌĆō2 ┬Ąs | 20ŌĆō100 ns | 50ŌĆō300 ns |
| Turn-off time | 1ŌĆō5 ┬Ąs | 20ŌĆō100 ns | 200ŌĆō800 ns |
| Tail current | Yes (severe) | No | Yes (moderate) |
| Conduction drop | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1$ŌĆō2 V | $I^2 R_{DS(on)}$ | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$ŌĆō3 V |

**Role of minority carrier storage:**
- BJT and IGBT suffer from **charge storage** in the base/drift region ŌåÆ longer storage time ŌåÆ higher turn-off loss.
- MOSFET is a **majority carrier** device ŌåÆ no storage time ŌåÆ ultra-fast switching ŌåÆ preferred for very high $f_{sw}$.
- IGBT is a **hybrid** (MOSFET input + BJT output) ŌĆö combines ease of drive with low conduction drop, but pays a penalty in turn-off tail current.

**Mark allocation:** 4 marks for table + 3 marks for the explanation of storage.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 200$ V, $I_D = 25$ A, $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz, $t_{on,eff} = 50$ ns, $t_{off,eff} = 40$ ns, $R_{DS(on)} = 0.1$ ╬®.

**Step 1: Switching Power Loss**

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 25 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 125 \text{ ╬╝J}$$

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 25 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 100 \text{ ╬╝J}$$

$$P_{sw} = 50 \times 10^3 \cdot (125 + 100) \times 10^{-6} = 11.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Conduction Power Loss** (assuming continuous sinusoidal current, $I_{rms} = I_D / \sqrt{2} \approx 17.68$ A):

$$P_{cond} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^{2} = 0.1 \cdot (17.68)^2 \approx 31.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

$$\frac{P_{cond}}{P_{sw}} = \frac{31.25}{11.25} \approx 2.78$$

Conduction loss dominates by a factor of ~2.78. This is typical of MOSFETs at moderate frequencies ŌĆö their low switching loss is a key advantage.

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks for switching loss calculation, 2 marks for conduction loss, 3 marks for comparison and engineering insight.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Mixing nanoseconds and seconds without $10^{-9}$ conversion ŌĆö results in a factor of $10^9$ error. Always check: $t$ in **seconds** in all formulas.
> 2. **Wrong Form of Formula:** Using $E_{on} = V_S \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on}$ (rectangular) instead of the correct **triangular overlap** $\frac{1}{2} V_S I_L t_{on}$. The $\frac{1}{2}$ is the key differentiator in valuation.
> 3. **Confusing $t_{on}$ with $t_{on,eff}$:** The datasheet $t_{on}$ may include delay + rise + voltage fall. Be careful when applying the half-power formula.
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Frequency:** $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz $= 20 \times 10^3$ Hz, not 20 Hz.
> 5. **Misidentifying Turn-Off Tail Current (IGBT):** Examiners love to test whether students recognize that the IGBT turn-off waveform has a *current tail* (unlike MOSFET). Mentioning this earns bonus marks.
> 6. **Not Drawing the Inductor/Diode in the Test Circuit:** A 14-mark switching waveform question without a proper circuit diagram loses 1ŌĆō2 marks automatically.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Switching = non-instantaneous transitions** between ON and OFF states; the device exhibits finite $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$.
- [x] **Turn-on** = Delay time ($t_{d(on)}$) + Current rise time ($t_r$) + Voltage fall time ($t_{fv}$).
- [x] **Turn-off** = Storage time ($t_s$) + Voltage rise time ($t_{rv}$) + Current fall time ($t_f$).
- [x] **Storage time** exists only in **minority carrier** devices (BJT, IGBT, SCR) ŌĆö **MOSFET has none**.
- [x] **Switching loss** arises from simultaneous $V \cdot I$ overlap during transitions.
- [x] **Key formula:** $P_{sw} = f_{sw}(E_{on} + E_{off}) = \frac{1}{2} V_S I_L f_{sw}(t_{on,eff} + t_{off,eff})$.
- [x] **Switching loss is proportional to frequency** ŌĆö limits maximum $f_{sw}$ in converter design.
- [x] **MOSFET** = fastest, lowest switching loss, but higher conduction loss (high $R_{DS(on)}$).
- [x] **IGBT** = balance of speed and low conduction drop, but has turn-off tail current.
- [x] **BJT** = slowest, highest switching loss ŌĆö mostly obsolete in modern designs.
- [x] **Wide-bandgap (SiC, GaN)** devices achieve $t_{on}$, $t_{off}$ in the **sub-50 ns** range, enabling MHz switching.
- [x] **Total power loss** = Switching loss + Conduction loss ŌåÆ drives **thermal design** (heatsink, $R_{th(j-c)}$).
- [x] **$dv/dt$ and $di/dt$** during switching can cause **EMI, ringing, and false triggering** ŌĆö mitigated by **snubbers**.
- [x] **Freewheeling diode** provides the path for inductive load current during turn-off ŌĆö must be considered in waveforms.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power semiconductor device is defined as the continuous region of voltageŌĆōcurrent $(V \text{ŌĆō} I)$ coordinates within the output characteristics over which the device can be safely operated without suffering self-destructing thermal runaway, secondary breakdown, or permanent degradation of its junction structure. The SOA is graphically represented on the $I_C$ (collector/drain current) versus $V_{CE}$ (collectorŌĆōemitter/drainŌĆōsource voltage) plane and is bounded by four fundamental limits:

1. **Maximum continuous collector/drain current limit** $(I_{C,\text{max}})$
2. **Maximum permissible collectorŌĆōemitter voltage limit** $(V_{CE,\text{max}})$
3. **Maximum power dissipation hyperbola** $(P_D = I_C \cdot V_{CE})$
4. **Secondary breakdown limit** (a thermal/avalanche instability boundary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SOA is the single most important reliability specification cited in manufacturer datasheets (ON Semiconductor, Infineon, STMicroelectronics) and forms the basis of all snubber and heatsink design calculations under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework mapped to **CO1** of PEEBT741.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rectangular swimming pool** with four distinct walls:
- The **water-level wall** (current limit) ŌĆö beyond which the swimmer (current carriers) would be swept out.
- The **depth wall** (voltage limit) ŌĆö beyond which the water pressure (electric field) would crack the pool floor.
- A **diagonal "thermal fence"** (power limit) ŌĆö beyond which the pool water (heat) boils.
- A **hidden "sandy ledge"** (secondary breakdown) ŌĆö a sneaky slope that collapses under concentrated foot pressure (localized hotspots).

A power device can be operated *anywhere* inside this four-walled pool. The instant the operating point $(V, I)$ crosses any wall, the device dies. This is exactly the SOA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** The SOA is a **manufacturer-supplied "envelope"** drawn on the $V_{CE}$ŌĆō$I_C$ chart that tells the design engineer: *"For how much voltage, and how much current, and for how long, may I push this transistor before it goes up in smoke?"*

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

The fundamental metrics explicitly stamped on every SOA curve include:
- $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$ ŌĆö **Susustaining voltage** ($\approx \mathbf{80\%\ \text{to}\ 90\%\ of\ BV_{CEO}}$)
- $I_{C,\text{max}}$ ŌĆö **Continuous collector current rating**
- $P_D$ ŌĆö **Maximum power dissipation at $T_C = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$** (typically **$\mathbf{100\ \text{W}\ \text{to}\ 500\ \text{W}}$** for power BJTs)
- $R_{\theta JC}$ ŌĆö **Junction-to-case thermal resistance** (in $\mathbf{^{\circ}\text{C/W}}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Boundaries of the SOA ŌĆö Structured Logic

The SOA envelope is constructed by four boundaries acting simultaneously:

**Boundary 1 ŌĆö The Current Limit (Horizontal Wall, Top)**
- A vertical line at $I_C = I_{C,\text{max}}$.
- Exceeding this saturates the bond-wire and silicon die cross-section with current, causing **fusing of metallization**.
- This is a **hard, instantaneous limit** ŌĆö no thermal time constant is involved.

**Boundary 2 ŌĆö The Voltage Limit (Vertical Wall, Right)**
- A vertical line at $V_{CE} = V_{CE,\text{max}}$.
- Crossing this triggers **avalanche breakdown** of the collectorŌĆōbase junction.
- Bounded by $BV_{CEO}$ (with base open) or $BV_{CBO}$ (with base shorted to emitter).

**Boundary 3 ŌĆö The Power Hyperbola (Curved Wall)**
- A hyperbolic locus defined by $P_D = I_C \cdot V_{CE} = \text{constant}$.
- Shaped by thermal equilibrium: $P_D = \dfrac{T_{J,\text{max}} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}$.
- It is the **primary thermal limit**.

**Boundary 4 ŌĆö The Secondary Breakdown Limit (Insidious Curve)**
- The most subtle and dangerous limit. Occurs due to **current crowding** in the die, where a local hotspot conducts more current, gets hotter, conducts even more ŌĆö a positive feedback loop.
- Dominant in **Forward Bias SOA (FBSOA)** at high $V_{CE}$ and in **Reverse Bias SOA (RBSOA)** during turn-off transients.

### 2.2 FBSOA vs. RBSOA ŌĆö Two Distinct Curves

| Parameter | FBSOA (Forward Bias SOA) | RBSOA (Reverse Bias SOA) |
|---|---|---|
| Bias condition | BaseŌĆōemitter forward biased (ON state) | BaseŌĆōemitter reverse biased (turn-off transient) |
| Time duration | DC / steady-state | Pulsed (turn-off transient, typically $\le 10\ \mu\text{s}$) |
| Boundary nature | Limited by current, voltage, power, *and* secondary breakdown | Limited primarily by **dynamic secondary breakdown** (latch-up of parasitic BJT) |
| Application context | Continuous conduction in linear regulators, motor drives | Switching converter turn-off, inductive load commutation |
| Pulse capability | Lower (sustained heating) | Much higher (brief thermal stress) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Curves Exist:** When a power device turns *off* an inductive load, the current falls rapidly while the voltage spikes up. The simultaneous high $di/dt$ and high $dV/dt$ forward-biases the *parasitic substrate BJT* inside the MOSFET/IGBT, leading to a latching failure. The RBSOA curve is the manufacturer's guarantee against this dynamic failure.

### 2.3 The $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$ Sustain Voltage Phenomenon

During turn-off, the device experiences a **voltage overshoot** above the DC bus. If this exceeds $V_{CEO}$ but remains below $BV_{CBO}$, the device enters a **negative resistance avalanche mode** where it is unable to sustain the current ŌĆö the current collapses to zero abruptly. The maximum voltage the device can sustain *while still carrying current* is $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$, which is **always less than** $BV_{CEO}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** Snubber circuits must clamp the turn-off voltage spike to **$\le 80\%$ of $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$** to leave a safety margin.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Boundary | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | Power dissipation hyperbola | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $P_D = \dfrac{T_{J,\text{max}} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}$ | Power limit from thermal model | W |
| 3 | $R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$ | Total junction-to-ambient resistance | ┬░C/W |
| 4 | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot R_{\theta JA}$ | Junction temperature at ambient | ┬░C |
| 5 | $V_{CE(\text{sus})} \approx 0.8\ \text{to}\ 0.9 \cdot BV_{CEO}$ | Sustaining voltage approximation | V |
| 6 | $I_{C,\text{pulse}} = I_{C,\text{cont}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{t_{p,\text{cont}}}{t_{p,\text{actual}}}}$ | Pulse current derating rule | A |
| 7 | $Z_{\theta}(t) = R_{\theta JC} \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}}{1}$ | Transient thermal impedance | ┬░C/W |
| 8 | $E_{\text{SOA}} = \int_{0}^{t} V_{CE}(\tau)\cdot I_C(\tau)\ d\tau$ | Energy dissipated during a pulse | J (Joules) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Notice:** All vertical bars `$\vert \cdot \vert$` in the table above have been replaced with the inline syntax `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Never use raw `|` inside table rows.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SOA analysis is the cornerstone of:
- **Snubber design** in IGBT-based motor drives (Mitsubishi, Semikron modules).
- **Linear regulator design** where a BJT/MOSFET operates in the active region continuously.
- **Inrush limiting** in power supplies, where SOA dictates the safe duration of surge currents.
- **Reliability qualification** in aerospace and EV traction inverters per **AEC-Q101** and **MIL-STD-750** standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö The Power Hyperbola

The third SOA boundary (the power limit) is derived directly from the **first law of thermodynamics** applied to the silicon die.

**Step 1:** Energy balance at the junction.
The heat generated in the junction per unit time equals the electrical power dissipated in the device, since the device has no other energy storage (in the ideal switching case).

$$P_{\text{gen}} = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$$

**Step 2:** Heat flow through the thermal stack.
The heat flows from the junction through the case to the ambient (or heatsink), characterized by the thermal resistance $R_{\theta JC}$.

$$P_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{T_J - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions.
The maximum power the device can dissipate without exceeding $T_{J,\text{max}}$ is:

$$P_{D,\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_{J,\text{max}} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}$$

**Step 4:** Express the constant-power curve.
Setting $P_{D,\text{max}}$ as a constant and solving for $I_C$ as a function of $V_{CE}$:

$$\boxed{I_C = \dfrac{P_{D,\text{max}}}{V_{CE}} = \dfrac{T_{J,\text{max}} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC} \cdot V_{CE}}}$$

This is the equation of a rectangular hyperbola in the $V_{CE}$ŌĆō$I_C$ plane. As $V_{CE} \to 0$, the curve asymptotically allows infinite current ŌĆö but in practice, it is *truncated* by the current limit $I_{C,\text{max}}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Pulse Current Derating

A device rated for $I_{C,\text{cont}}$ at $t_{p,\text{cont}} = \infty$ (continuous operation) can handle a higher peak current for a brief pulse. The rule of thumb derivation:

**Step 1:** Energy balance during a single pulse.
$$E_{\text{pulse}} = P_{\text{peak}} \cdot t_p = V_{CE} \cdot I_{C,\text{peak}} \cdot t_p$$

**Step 2:** Compare with continuous rating.
The same energy, if delivered continuously, would correspond to the rated current:

$$E_{\text{cont}} = P_{\text{cont}} \cdot t_{p,\text{cont}} = V_{CE} \cdot I_{C,\text{cont}} \cdot t_{p,\text{cont}}$$

**Step 3:** Equate (assume all energy goes to heating the junction).
$$I_{C,\text{peak}} \cdot t_p = I_{C,\text{cont}} \cdot t_{p,\text{cont}}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for peak current.

$$\boxed{I_{C,\text{peak}} = I_{C,\text{cont}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{p,\text{cont}}}{t_p}}$$

This is the basis of the **pulse derating curves** in datasheets, which show that a $1\ \text{ms}$ pulse allows $\approx 3\times$ the rated current, while a $10\ \mu\text{s}$ pulse allows $\approx 10\times$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Transient Thermal Impedance

The thermal model is more accurately described by a **Cauer RC ladder network** (analogous to transmission line theory). For a single-pole approximation:

**Step 1:** Write the heat-conduction equation.
The junction temperature responds to a step in dissipated power as a first-order lag:

$$T_J(t) = T_A + P_D \cdot R_{\theta JC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)$$

where $\tau_{th} = R_{\theta JC} \cdot C_{\theta JC}$ is the thermal time constant.

**Step 2:** Define transient thermal impedance.
$$Z_{\theta}(t) = R_{\theta JC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Verify limiting cases.
- As $t \to 0$: $Z_{\theta}(t) \to 0$ (no time to heat up).
- As $t \to \infty$: $Z_{\theta}(t) \to R_{\theta JC}$ (steady-state DC thermal resistance).

**Step 4:** Practical usage ŌĆö square-wave pulse train.
For a periodic pulse train with duty cycle $D$ and period $T$:

$$\boxed{Z_{\theta,\text{avg}} = D \cdot R_{\theta JC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_p/\tau_{th}}\right) + (1-D) \cdot R_{\theta JC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T/\tau_{th}}\right)}$$

In most KTU problems, the simpler approximation $Z_{\theta,\text{avg}} \approx D \cdot R_{\theta JC}$ is acceptable for $t_p \gg \tau_{th}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Boundary Contributions

| Boundary | Physical Cause | Time-Scale | Mitigation in Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current Limit $I_{C,\text{max}}$ | Bond-wire fusing, die cross-section saturation | Microseconds (instantaneous) | Use larger die / parallel devices |
| Voltage Limit $V_{CE,\text{max}}$ | Avalanche breakdown of CB junction | Nanoseconds | Snubber capacitor, TVS clamp |
| Power Hyperbola $P_D$ | Average heating of die | Milliseconds to seconds | Heatsink, forced-air cooling |
| Secondary Breakdown | Current crowding + thermal runaway | Microseconds | Matched emitter ballast resistors, RBSOA-aware switching |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö The SOA Envelope Construction

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how the four boundaries interact to form the composite SOA envelope, starting from device characterization and ending in the designer's safe-operating region.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Under Test Characterization] --> B[Measure BV_CEO and V_CEsus]
    A --> C[Measure I_Cmax at T_C 25C]
    A --> D[Measure P_Dmax from R_thetaJC and T_Jmax]
    A --> E[Measure Secondary Breakdown Threshold]

    B --> F[Plot Vertical Wall at V_CEmax]
    C --> G[Plot Horizontal Wall at I_Cmax]
    D --> H[Plot Power Hyperbola I_C equals P_D / V_CE]
    E --> I[Plot SBTL Curve at High V_CE Region]

    F --> J[Superimpose All Four Boundaries]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K[Composite SOA Envelope on V_CE vs I_C Plane]
    K --> L[FBSOA Curve]
    K --> M[RBSOA Curve at Turn Off Transient]

    L --> N[Design Operating Point Selection]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Safe Operating Point with 20% Derating]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Power Device Thermal Stack

The following diagram models the heat flow path from the silicon junction to ambient, showing each thermal resistance element that influences the power-hyperbola boundary of the SOA.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Junction T_J] --> RJ1[R_theta_JC - Junction to Case]
    RJ1 --> C[Case T_C]
    C --> RJ2[R_theta_CS - Case to Heatsink]
    RJ2 --> HS[Heatsink T_S]
    HS --> RJ3[R_theta_SA - Heatsink to Ambient]
    RJ3 --> A[Ambient T_A]

    P_D[Power Dissipated P_D] --> J
    P_D -. Heat Flow Path .-> A
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö FBSOA vs RBSOA Switching Cycle

This block diagram maps the temporal sequence of a power device transitioning through the two SOA regimes during a switching cycle in a typical chopper circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Switch ON - Forward Conduction] --> S2[Device in FBSOA Region]
    S2 --> S3[Gate Turn Off Command Issued]
    S3 --> S4[Base Emitter Reverse Biased]
    S4 --> S5[Device Operates in RBSOA Region]
    S5 --> S6[Voltage Spike Clamped by Snubber]
    S6 --> S7[Device Fully OFF - Blocking Voltage]
    S7 --> S1

    S2 -. V_CE low, I_C high .-> X[FBSOA: Bounded by I_Cmax and SBTL]
    S5 -. V_CE high, I_C falling .-> Y[RBSOA: Bounded by Dynamic SBTL]
```

### 4.4 Schematic ŌĆö Generic Snubber Clamp Topology (Functional Block)

A physical schematic adapted as a block-level functional architecture, showing how an RCD snubber clamps the turn-off voltage to keep the operating point inside the RBSOA envelope.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VBUS[DC Bus V_dc] --> L[Load Inductor L]
    L --> D[Freewheel Diode]
    D --> GND1[Ground]

    L --> SW[Power Switch T1]
    SW --> GND2[Ground]

    SW -. Parallel RCD Snubber .-> SNB
    SNB[RCD Snubber Network] --> GND3[Ground]

    VBUS --> C1[DC Link Capacitor] --> GND1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Adaptation Rationale:** Complex physical schematics with discrete components are converted to **functional block architectures** to maintain Mermaid compatibility while preserving the signal-flow and circuit-topology information critical for KTU board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define the term Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power semiconductor device. List any two of its boundary limits.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power semiconductor device is the region on the $V_{CE}$ŌĆō$I_C$ characteristic plane within which the device can be operated without suffering thermal runaway, secondary breakdown, or permanent damage.

The four boundary limits are:
1. Maximum continuous collector current $(I_{C,\text{max}})$
2. Maximum collectorŌĆōemitter voltage $(V_{CE,\text{max}})$
3. Maximum power dissipation hyperbola $(P_{D,\text{max}})$
4. Secondary breakdown limit (SBTL)

*Examiner's Note:* Award **1 mark** for the definition, **1 mark** each for any two boundaries. Total 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Forward Bias SOA (FBSOA) and Reverse Bias SOA (RBSOA).*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FBSOA | RBSOA |
|---|---|---|
| Base bias | Forward biased (ON) | Reverse biased (turn-off) |
| Duration | Continuous / steady | Pulsed (transient) |
| Primary limit | Power + secondary breakdown | Dynamic secondary breakdown (latch-up) |
| Pulse capability | Lower | Higher (shorter duration) |

*Examiner's Note:* Award **1.5 marks** per row, or **proportionate** credit for any two distinguishing features. Total 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Compulsory Attempt

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain the construction of the Safe Operating Area curve for a power BJT, with the help of a neatly labelled sketch. Discuss the role of the secondary breakdown limit in limiting the FBSOA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power BJT has $T_{J,\text{max}} = 150^{\circ}\text{C}$, $R_{\theta JC} = 1^{\circ}\text{C/W}$, and operates at $T_C = 75^{\circ}\text{C}$. Calculate the maximum power dissipation. If the device is operated at $V_{CE} = 50\ \text{V}$, determine the maximum allowable collector current. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The SOA curve is constructed by superimposing four boundaries on the $V_{CE}$ŌĆō$I_C$ characteristic plane:

1. **Horizontal current limit** at $I_C = I_{C,\text{max}}$ ŌĆö set by bond wire fusing.
2. **Vertical voltage limit** at $V_{CE} = V_{CE,\text{max}}$ ŌĆö set by avalanche breakdown.
3. **Power hyperbola** $I_C = P_{D,\text{max}} / V_{CE}$ ŌĆö set by thermal equilibrium.
4. **Secondary breakdown limit** ŌĆö a curve that *cuts in* at high $V_{CE}$ (above $\approx 50\%$ of $V_{CE,\text{max}}$) and reduces the allowable current well below the DC power limit.

**Role of secondary breakdown in FBSOA:**
Secondary breakdown is triggered by **current crowding** in the die. Localized hotspots conduct more, heat up further, and create a thermal positive feedback that punctures the junction. The SBTL is the dominant FBSOA limit at high $V_{CE}$ because the power hyperbola alone would *theoretically* allow higher current. The SBTL curve **shaves off the upper-right corner** of the hyperbolic envelope, making the FBSOA *non-rectangular*.

*Examiner's Note:* Award **3 marks** for the labelled sketch, **2 marks** for boundary explanations, **2 marks** for the SBTL discussion. Total 7 marks.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Maximum power dissipation.**
Using the thermal model:

$$P_{D,\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_{J,\text{max}} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{D,\text{max}} = \dfrac{150 - 75}{1} = 75\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Maximum collector current at $V_{CE} = 50\ \text{V}$.**
Using $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$:

$$I_{C,\text{max}} = \dfrac{P_{D,\text{max}}}{V_{CE}} = \dfrac{75}{50} = 1.5\ \text{A}$$

*Examiner's Note:* Award **3 marks** for the thermal-model formula, **2 marks** for the numerical substitution, **2 marks** for the current calculation. Total 7 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the RBSOA of an IGBT during turn-off. Why is RBSOA larger than FBSOA at short pulse durations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power MOSFET is rated for $I_D = 10\ \text{A}$ continuous, and is operated in a pulse mode with $t_p = 1\ \text{ms}$. The continuous pulse reference time is $t_{p,\text{cont}} = 10\ \text{ms}$. Calculate the peak pulse current. If the SOA power hyperbola allows $P_D = 50\ \text{W}$ and the drainŌĆōsource voltage during the pulse is $V_{DS} = 20\ \text{V}$, verify whether this peak current is within the SOA. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**RBSOA during IGBT turn-off:**
When the gate is turned off, the collector current falls rapidly while the collectorŌĆōemitter voltage rises sharply (limited by the snubber). The IGBT operates in the RBSOA during this transient. The RBSOA is bounded primarily by the **dynamic avalanche** of the parasitic NPN BJT inside the IGBT structure, which can latch the device permanently in the ON state.

**Why RBSOA > FBSOA at short pulses:**
Because RBSOA pulses are short (typically $\le 10\ \mu\text{s}$), there is insufficient time for thermal runaway or for the parasitic BJT to fully latch. The thermal mass of the die absorbs the transient energy without significant temperature rise. Thus, the manufacturer allows a much higher $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ product in RBSOA than in FBSOA.

*Examiner's Note:* Award **2 marks** for the sketch, **3 marks** for the IGBT turn-off narrative, **2 marks** for the pulse-duration reasoning. Total 7 marks.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Peak pulse current.**
Using the pulse derating formula:

$$I_{C,\text{peak}} = I_{C,\text{cont}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{p,\text{cont}}}{t_p} = 10 \cdot \dfrac{10\ \text{ms}}{1\ \text{ms}} = 100\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2: Check against the SOA power limit.**
The maximum current allowed by the power hyperbola at $V_{DS} = 20\ \text{V}$:

$$I_{D,\text{soa}} = \dfrac{P_D}{V_{DS}} = \dfrac{50}{20} = 2.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3: Compare and conclude.**

$$\boxed{I_{C,\text{peak}} = 100\ \text{A} \gg I_{D,\text{soa}} = 2.5\ \text{A}}$$

The peak pulse current **violates the SOA**. The device will fail. The designer must either:
- Reduce the pulse current to $\le 2.5\ \text{A}$, or
- Increase the allowable $P_D$ (better cooling / heatsink), or
- Lower the operating $V_{DS}$ to $\le 0.5\ \text{V}$ (impractical for a power switch).

*Examiner's Note:* Award **2 marks** for derating calculation, **2 marks** for the SOA current calculation, **3 marks** for the comparison and the engineering conclusion. Total 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the thermal model units:** $R_{\theta JC}$ is in **┬░C/W**, not K/W. A factor-of-273 error here costs **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$ with $BV_{CEO}$:** $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$ is **always lower**. Using them interchangeably is a hallmark of weak answers.
> 3. **Ignoring the SBTL:** In FBSOA sketches, the secondary breakdown curve *must* appear ŌĆö drawing only the rectangular envelope loses **2 to 3 marks**.
> 4. **No derating margin:** KTU examiners expect a **20% safety margin** discussion on the final operating point. Omitting it is considered incomplete design.
> 5. **Mixing up FBSOA and RBSOA:** Many students treat them as identical. They are **not** ŌĆö RBSOA applies to the *turn-off transient*, FBSOA applies to *steady-state conduction*. Confusing them costs the **2-mark comparison** in Part A.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SOA = Four walls** on the $V_{CE}$ŌĆō$I_C$ plane: current, voltage, power, secondary breakdown.
- **FBSOA** = steady-state / forward conduction; **RBSOA** = turn-off transient.
- **Power hyperbola** equation: $P_{D,\text{max}} = (T_{J,\text{max}} - T_C) / R_{\theta JC}$.
- **Pulse derating rule:** $I_{\text{peak}} \cdot t_p = I_{\text{cont}} \cdot t_{p,\text{cont}}$ (energy equivalence).
- **$V_{CE(\text{sus})} \approx 0.8$ to $0.9 \times BV_{CEO}$** ŌĆö never equal.
- **Snubber design** must clamp turn-off spikes to **$\le 80\%$ of $V_{CE(\text{sus})}$**.
- **Secondary breakdown** is a **thermal positive feedback** (current crowding) ŌĆö distinct from avalanche breakdown.
- **Always apply a 20% derating** in the final design operating point per KTU board examiner expectations.
- **MOSFETs, BJTs, and IGBTs** all have SOA curves ŌĆö IGBT SOA is generally the most generous; BJT SOA is the most restrictive.
- **RBSOA > FBSOA at short pulses** because the die's thermal mass absorbs transient energy.
- **Thermal resistance stack:** $R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Paralleling of Transistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paralleling of Transistors (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition):**
> **Paralleling of Transistors** is a power-handling extension technique in which two or more power semiconductor switching devices (BJTs, MOSFETs, IGBTs, or GTOs) are electrically connected in **parallel** so that they collectively share a single, high-magnitude load current that exceeds the rated continuous current capacity of any single device.

In a parallel connection, all collectors (or drains for MOSFETs/IGBTs) are tied together to a common positive rail, all emitters (or sources) are tied to a common negative rail, and **all base (gate) terminals are driven by the same control signal**. The total load current $I_L$ is theoretically divided equally among the $n$ parallel devices, so each device carries a current of $I_T = I_L / n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Parallel Instead of One Bigger Device?**
> - **Availability:** Discrete power devices above ~1000 A are rare, expensive, and have slower switching speeds.
> - **Switching Performance:** Smaller devices have **lower switching losses** ($E_{on} + E_{off}$) and better $dv/dt$, $di/dt$ characteristics.
> - **Redundancy & Reliability (n+1 redundancy):** If one device fails short in an $n+1$ configuration, the system continues to operate.
> - **Thermal Distribution:** Heat is spread over a larger physical area, simplifying heatsink design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Multiple Taps on a Water Pipe**
> Imagine a single kitchen tap that can only deliver **10 liters per minute**, but your industrial tank needs **40 liters per minute**. You cannot manufacture a bigger tap easily. Instead, you **install four identical taps side-by-side** and connect their inlets to a common supply pipe. Each tap delivers ~10 L/min, and together they meet the 40 L/min demand. This is exactly what **paralleling of transistors** achieves for electrons.

If two identical water taps are connected, they share equally. However, if one tap has a slightly wider opening (lower resistance), it carries more flow (more current), and the other tap sits idle. This **uneven sharing** is the central engineering challenge of paralleling transistors.

---

## 1.3 Key Terms Highlighted

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Static Current Sharing** | Current division among parallel devices in the **ON-state** (steady conduction). |
| **Dynamic Current Sharing** | Current division during **switching transients** (turn-on and turn-off). |
| **Derating Factor (DF)** | Ratio of the actual usable current per device to its rated current, when used in parallel. |
| **Ballast Resistor** | A small emitter/source resistance added **externally** to force equal current sharing. |
| **Thermal Runaway** | Cascading failure where the hotter device draws more current, becomes even hotter, and destroys itself. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric for KTU:**
> For $n$ identical devices in parallel, the **ideal derating factor** is **$1/n$**, meaning each device is used at only $1/n^{th}$ of its individual rating. Real systems derate further to **$0.8/n$** to handle mismatches.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Paralleling Necessary? ŌĆö The Engineering Drivers

1. **Current Demand Exceeds Single-Device Rating:** Modern motor drives, induction heaters, and traction inverters demand currents of **1000 A ŌĆō 5000 A DC-bus equivalents**. A single TO-247 BJT or TO-264 IGBT typically handles only **50 A ŌĆō 200 A**.
2. **Switching Frequency vs. Current Trade-off:** Higher current-rated devices have **larger die areas**, longer minority-carrier transit times, and therefore **slower switching**. Paralleling smaller, faster devices preserves switching performance.
3. **Cost and Modularity:** Standardized half-bridge or six-pack IGBT modules (e.g., 600 A / 1200 V) can be paralleled to build a custom high-current stack.

---

## 2.2 The Core Problem ŌĆö Unequal Current Sharing

Even when devices are manufactured from the same wafer lot, small parameter mismatches cause **uneven current sharing**. The two main culprits are:

### A. Static (ON-State) Mismatch ŌĆö $V_{BE}$ or $V_{CE(sat)}$ Spread
- The output characteristic of a BJT/IeBT is a positive-slope curve: $V_{CE}$ rises as $I_C$ rises.
- If two BJTs share the same $V_{CE}$ drop (which they must, because they are in parallel), the device with the **lower $V_{BE}$ (or lower $V_{CE(sat)}$ curve)** hogs more current.
- MOSFETs are slightly better because their $R_{DS(on)}$ has a *positive temperature coefficient*, providing natural self-balancing.

### B. Dynamic (Switching) Mismatch ŌĆö $t_{on}$, $t_{off}$ Spread
- During turn-on and turn-off, devices with **faster switching** carry the entire transient current spike.
- This dynamic imbalance causes **localized heating** even if steady-state currents are balanced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) Problem in BJTs:**
> For BJTs, $V_{BE}$ has a **negative temperature coefficient** of approximately **$-2$ mV/┬░C**. This means a hotter transistor has a lower $V_{BE}$, conducts *more* current, heats up *further*, and leads to **thermal runaway** ŌĆö a self-reinforcing destructive loop.

---

## 2.3 Methods to Force Equal Current Sharing

| S.No. | Method | How It Works | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Ballast Emitter Resistors** | Add a small $R_E$ (e.g., 0.1 ╬® ŌĆō 1 ╬®) in series with each emitter. The negative feedback of $R_E$ opposes current hogging. | BJTs, Darlington |
| **2** | **Gate Resistor Matching** | Use individual gate resistors $R_G$ to slow down faster devices, equalizing $t_{on}$/$t_{off}$. | MOSFETs, IGBTs |
| **3** | **Matched / Binned Devices** | Select devices from the same wafer with $\Delta V_{BE} < 5$ mV at rated $I_C$. | High-reliability systems |
| **4** | **Current Sharing Reactors (CSRs)** | Coupled inductors on the emitters couple devices magnetically to enforce equal di/dt. | High-power industrial drives |
| **5** | **Forced Current Sharing (Active Control)** | Sense each device current with a shunt; feedback loop adjusts gate drive. | Advanced modular converters |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU board-exam essentials. Memorize the boundary conditions, units, and the *meaning* of each variable.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_L = n \cdot I_T$ | Total load current equals $n$ parallel devices each carrying $I_T$ | A |
| 2 | $I_T = \dfrac{I_L}{n}$ | Current per parallel device (ideal case) | A |
| 3 | $DF = \dfrac{I_T}{I_{rated}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Ideal Derating Factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $DF_{actual} = \dfrac{0.8}{n}$ to $\dfrac{0.9}{n}$ | Practical Derating Factor accounting for mismatch | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{total} = n \cdot V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_T$ | Total conduction loss in parallel string | W |
| 6 | $P_{loss,per} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_T + I_T^2 \cdot r_{CE}$ | Conduction loss per device (using $r_{CE}$ slope resistance) | W |
| 7 | $R_{th,total} = \dfrac{R_{th,j-c}}{n} + R_{th,c-s} + R_{th,s-a}$ | Equivalent thermal resistance of $n$ parallel devices on a common heatsink | ┬░C/W |
| 8 | $\Delta I_T = \dfrac{\Delta V_{BE}}{R_E + r_{CE}}$ | Current mismatch between two devices, where $\Delta V_{BE}$ is the $V_{BE}$ spread | A |
| 9 | $R_E \geq \dfrac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_{T,max}}$ | Minimum ballast emitter resistor required to limit mismatch to $\Delta I_{T,max}$ | ╬® |
| 10 | $V_{BE}(T) = V_{BE}(T_0) + \alpha \cdot (T - T_0)$, $\alpha \approx -2$ mV/┬░C | Temperature dependence of $V_{BE}$ (NTC) | V |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR ANSWER SCRIPTS:** In your exam paper, write absolute values as $\vert V_{BE} \vert$ or $\vert \Delta I \vert$ ŌĆö **never** as `|V_BE|` or `|╬öI|` ŌĆö because the pipe symbol clashes with markdown table cells in KTU's digital evaluation portal.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Traction Inverters** (Indian Railways 3-phase VVVF drives): Parallel IGBTs in 1700 V / 2400 A modules.
- **HVDC Converters** in power transmission: Series-parallel stacks of thyristors.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Drivetrains**: Tesla Model S inverter uses **paralleled IGBTs/SiC MOSFETs** to handle 1500 A peak.
- **Welding Power Supplies**: Parallel MOSFETs at 100 kHz for high-current pulsed output.
- **Solar String Inverters**: Paralleled IGBTs in 3-level NPC (Neutral Point Clamped) topologies.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Current Sharing in Two Parallel BJTs Without Ballast Resistor

Consider **two identical BJTs** $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ connected in parallel, sharing a common $V_{CE}$ and a common base drive.

**Step 1:** Write the collector current equation for each device using the Ebers-Moll model.

$$I_{C1} = I_{S1} \cdot e^{V_{BE1}/V_T}$$

$$I_{C2} = I_{S2} \cdot e^{V_{BE2}/V_T}$$

Here, $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at room temperature, and $I_{S1}, I_{S2}$ are the saturation currents.

**Step 2:** Because both transistors share the same collector and emitter nodes, the constraint is:

$$V_{BE1} = V_{BE2} = V_{BE}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the *ratio* of currents equals the ratio of saturation currents:

$$\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}} = \frac{I_{S1}}{I_{S2}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $I_S$ depends on the active base-emitter junction area $A$ and the device temperature. A 5% device mismatch typically produces an $I_S$ mismatch of ┬▒10%, meaning **one device carries ~55% of the total current**.

**Step 5:** If a load current of $I_L = 200$ A is shared between two parallel BJTs, the unhappier device may carry:

$$I_{C,hot} = 0.55 \times 200 = 110 \text{ A}$$

while the cooler one carries only $90$ A. This **20 A imbalance** leads to localized heating and eventual failure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Current Sharing With Ballast Emitter Resistor

To overcome the NTC thermal runaway, we add a small **ballast emitter resistor** $R_E$ to *each* emitter.

**Step 1:** With $R_E$ added, the new KVL constraint across the base-emitter loop becomes:

$$V_{BE1} + I_{C1} R_E = V_{BE2} + I_{C2} R_E = V_{drive}$$

**Step 2:** Taking the difference of the two equations:

$$V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = (I_{C2} - I_{C1}) R_E$$

**Step 3:** Express the $V_{BE}$ mismatch in terms of saturation current mismatch using the Ebers-Moll model:

$$V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{S1}}\right) - V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C2}}{I_{S2}}\right)$$

$$V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1} \cdot I_{S2}}{I_{C2} \cdot I_{S1}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1} \cdot I_{S2}}{I_{C2} \cdot I_{S1}}\right) = (I_{C2} - I_{C1}) R_E$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the current difference, we get the **maximum tolerable current mismatch**:

$$\Delta I_C = \vert I_{C1} - I_{C2} \vert = \frac{V_T}{\,R_E\,} \ln\left(\frac{I_{S,\max}}{I_{S,\min}}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the **minimum required ballast resistor**:

$$R_{E,\min} = \frac{V_T \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{I_{S,\max}}{I_{S,\min}}\right)}{\Delta I_{C,\max}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):** Two BJTs have $I_{S,\max}/I_{S,\min} = 1.10$ (10% saturation current spread). Find the minimum $R_E$ to keep $\Delta I_C \le 5$ A at $V_T = 26$ mV.

$$R_{E,\min} = \frac{0.026 \cdot \ln(1.10)}{5}$$

$$\ln(1.10) \approx 0.0953$$

$$R_{E,\min} = \frac{0.026 \times 0.0953}{5} = \frac{0.002478}{5} = 0.000496 \text{ ╬®} \approx 0.5 \text{ m╬®}$$

So an $R_E$ as small as **0.5 m╬®** is sufficient to clamp the mismatch to 5 A. In practice, designers use **50 m╬® to 500 m╬®** to allow margin for thermal gradients and aging.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derating Factor in a Parallel String

**Step 1:** Let the rated current of a single device be $I_{rated}$. The total load current that $n$ devices can collectively handle *ideally* is:

$$I_{L,\text{ideal}} = n \cdot I_{rated}$$

**Step 2:** The **derating factor** is the fraction of rated current at which each device actually operates:

$$DF = \frac{I_T}{I_{rated}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 3:** With mismatches, an *unequal sharing coefficient* $k$ is introduced, where the worst-case device carries $k \cdot I_T$ with $k > 1$. The total current is then:

$$I_L = n \cdot \frac{I_{rated}}{k}$$

**Step 4:** Hence the **practical derating factor**:

$$DF_{actual} = \frac{1}{n \cdot k}$$

**Step 5:** For a typical mismatch of $k = 1.2$ (one device carries 20% more than average) and $n = 4$ parallel BJTs:

$$DF_{actual} = \frac{1}{4 \times 1.2} = \frac{1}{4.8} \approx 0.208$$

This means each of the four BJTs is used at only **~20.8%** of its individual rating ŌĆö a heavy penalty, but necessary for reliability.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Resistance of $n$ Parallel Devices

**Step 1:** Each device has a junction-to-case thermal resistance $R_{th,j-c}$. When $n$ devices are mounted on a *common* heatsink, the case-to-sink resistance $R_{th,c-s}$ is shared.

**Step 2:** Equivalent thermal resistance seen by *one* device:

$$R_{th,device} = R_{th,j-c} + n \cdot R_{th,c-s} + R_{th,s-a}$$

Wait ŌĆö this is the series interpretation. In a true parallel thermal configuration, the *combined* thermal resistance from the $n$ junctions to ambient is:

$$R_{th,\text{parallel}} = R_{th,j-c} + \frac{R_{th,c-s}}{n} + R_{th,s-a}$$

**Step 3:** Reasoning: the case-to-sink interface is shared, so the *heat flux* per device is $1/n$ of total, effectively reducing the heatsink-side resistance by factor $n$.

**Step 4:** Final expression:

$$R_{th,\text{parallel}} = R_{th,j-c} + \frac{R_{th,c-s}}{n} + R_{th,s-a}$$

This is why **paralleling improves thermal performance** ŌĆö the heatsink resistance effectively shrinks, allowing more total current for the same junction temperature.

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **PWM drive signal** feeds all base resistors in parallel.
- Each transistor $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_n$ has its **own ballast emitter resistor** $R_E$ to enforce current balance.
- All collectors merge at $+V_{DC}$, all emitters merge at the common return rail, and the **load current $I_L$** is the sum of all branch currents.
- The dashed arrows to the **Common Heatsink** indicate the shared thermal path.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
*Why are power transistors paralleled? Mention any two problems encountered during paralleling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reasons for paralleling (1.5 marks):** When the load current demand exceeds the rated current of a single power transistor, multiple transistors are connected in parallel to **share the load current**. It also improves **switching speed** (smaller devices switch faster) and provides **redundancy** for reliability.
- **Two problems (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Static current mismatch** due to $V_{BE}$ or $V_{CE(sat)}$ spread between devices.
  2. **Thermal runaway** caused by the negative temperature coefficient of $V_{BE}$ in BJTs ŌĆö the hotter device hogs more current, heats up further, and fails.
  3. *(Optional third)* Dynamic current sharing mismatch during turn-on/turn-off transients.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Expected Pattern, Module 1]**
*What is a ballast resistor and why is it used in parallel transistor circuits?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **ballast resistor** is a small resistor (typically 0.1 ╬® ŌĆō 1 ╬®) connected in series with the **emitter** of each BJT in a parallel string. It introduces **negative current feedback** ŌĆö if one device begins to hog current, the voltage drop across its $R_E$ increases, reducing its $V_{BE}$ drive and forcing the current back to the average value. It **counteracts the negative temperature coefficient of $V_{BE}$** and prevents **thermal runaway**, thereby enforcing **equal static current sharing** among parallel devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Paralleling of BJTs

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 1, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of **two BJTs connected in parallel** to share a load current of 80 A. Derive the expression for the **minimum ballast emitter resistance** required to limit the current mismatch to within 4 A, given that the saturation current ratio $I_{S,\max}/I_{S,\min} = 1.12$ and $V_T = 26$ mV. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **derating factor** in a parallel transistor configuration. For a system with **four BJTs** in parallel, each rated 50 A, with an unequal current sharing coefficient of $k = 1.25$, calculate the **maximum safe load current** and the **practical derating factor**. Also derive the **equivalent thermal resistance** expression for $n$ devices mounted on a common heatsink. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Ballast Resistor Derivation (7 marks)

**Circuit Operation (2 marks):**
Two BJTs $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are connected with collectors tied to $+V_{DC}$, emitters each passing through their own ballast resistor $R_E$ before joining the common return rail. Bases are driven from a common driver through individual base resistors $R_B$. The total load current $I_L = 80$ A ideally splits into 40 A per device.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the Ebers-Moll collector current:

$$I_{C1} = I_{S1} \cdot e^{V_{BE1}/V_T}, \quad I_{C2} = I_{S2} \cdot e^{V_{BE2}/V_T}$$

With ballast resistors, the KVL constraint is:

$$V_{BE1} + I_{C1} R_E = V_{BE2} + I_{C2} R_E$$

Subtracting and rearranging:

$$V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = (I_{C2} - I_{C1}) R_E = -\Delta I_C \cdot R_E$$

Expressing the $V_{BE}$ difference via logarithms:

$$V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1} \cdot I_{S2}}{I_{C2} \cdot I_{S1}}\right) = -\Delta I_C \cdot R_E$$

Solving for $R_E$:

$$R_{E,\min} = \frac{V_T \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{I_{S,\max}}{I_{S,\min}}\right)}{\Delta I_{C,\max}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**

$$R_{E,\min} = \frac{0.026 \cdot \ln(1.12)}{4} = \frac{0.026 \times 0.1133}{4} = \frac{0.002946}{4} \approx 0.000737 \text{ ╬®}$$

$$\boxed{R_{E,\min} \approx 0.74 \text{ m╬®}}$$

**Conclusion:** A ballast resistor of at least **0.74 m╬®** in each emitter leg keeps current mismatch within 4 A.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Derating Factor and Thermal Resistance (7 marks)

**Derating Factor Concept (2 marks):**
The derating factor is the ratio of the **actual operating current per device** to its **rated current** when devices are used in parallel. It is **always less than 1** because mismatches force us to operate below rating. Ideal derating factor is $1/n$; practical derating factor is $1/(n \cdot k)$ where $k \ge 1$ is the unequal sharing coefficient.

**Calculation of Maximum Load Current (2 marks):**

The worst-case device carries:

$$I_{T,\max} = k \cdot \frac{I_L}{n} \le I_{rated}$$

$$\frac{I_L}{n} \le \frac{I_{rated}}{k}$$

$$I_{L,\max} = \frac{n \cdot I_{rated}}{k} = \frac{4 \times 50}{1.25} = \frac{200}{1.25} = 160 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L,\max} = 160 \text{ A}}$$

**Practical Derating Factor:**

$$DF_{actual} = \frac{1}{n \cdot k} = \frac{1}{4 \times 1.25} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ or } 20\%$$

**Thermal Resistance Derivation (3 marks):**
Each device has junction-to-case resistance $R_{th,j-c}$, and all $n$ cases share a common case-to-sink interface resistance $R_{th,c-s}$ (effectively divided by $n$ because heat flux is shared), followed by the sink-to-ambient resistance $R_{th,s-a}$:

$$R_{th,\text{parallel}} = R_{th,j-c} + \frac{R_{th,c-s}}{n} + R_{th,s-a}$$

This shows that **paralleling reduces the heatsink-side thermal resistance** by factor $n$, enabling higher total current for the same junction temperature rise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Paralleling of MOSFETs / IGBTs (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Expected Pattern, Module 1]**
