**(a)** Compare the **current sharing behavior of BJTs and MOSFETs** when connected in parallel. Why are MOSFETs considered naturally self-balancing, while BJTs require ballast resistors? Derive the **static current sharing ratio** for two parallel MOSFETs with $R_{DS(on)}$ mismatch. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss any **three methods to improve current sharing** in a parallel string of four IGBTs. Calculate the **total conduction loss** for four parallel MOSFETs, each carrying 30 A with $R_{DS(on)} = 20$ m╬®. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö BJT vs MOSFET Current Sharing (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BJT | MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| Output characteristic slope | Positive ($V_{CE}$ rises with $I_C$) | Positive ($V_{DS}$ rises with $I_D$) |
| Temperature coefficient of $V_{BE}$ / $V_{GS(th)}$ | **Negative** (~ $-2$ mV/┬░C) | **Positive** for $R_{DS(on)}$ (+0.4 %/┬░C) |
| Natural current sharing | Poor (requires ballast) | **Good (self-balancing)** |
| Switching speed | Slower (minority carrier) | Faster (majority carrier) |
| Drive requirement | Continuous base current | Voltage-driven (low gate current) |
| Need for ballast | **Yes ŌĆö essential** | Usually not needed |

**Why MOSFETs are self-balancing (2 marks):**
For MOSFETs, $R_{DS(on)}$ has a **positive temperature coefficient** (PTC). If one MOSFET starts hogging current, it heats up, its $R_{DS(on)}$ increases, and its current naturally *decreases* ŌĆö pushing current back to the cooler parallel device. This is **inherently stable** and is the opposite of the BJT's thermal runaway behavior.

**Derivation of static current sharing ratio (2 marks):**
For two parallel MOSFETs sharing total current $I_L$:

$$I_{D1} = \frac{R_{DS2}}{R_{DS1} + R_{DS2}} \cdot I_L$$

$$I_{D2} = \frac{R_{DS1}}{R_{DS1} + R_{DS2}} \cdot I_L$$

$$\frac{I_{D1}}{I_{D2}} = \frac{R_{DS2}}{R_{DS1}}$$

The device with the *lower* $R_{DS(on)}$ carries the *higher* current. As temperature rises, $R_{DS(on)}$ rises, balancing the currents automatically.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Three Methods + Loss Calculation (7 marks)

**Three methods to improve current sharing (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):**

1. **Individual Gate Resistors ($R_G$):** Place a small resistor (10ŌĆō100 ╬®) at each gate. The $R_G C_{iss}$ time constant slows faster devices, equalizing $t_{on}$/$t_{off}$ and improving *dynamic* current sharing.
2. **Current Sharing Reactor (CSR):** A coupled inductor in the source legs of adjacent MOSFETs. The mutual inductance forces equal $di/dt$, naturally equalizing transient currents.
3. **Source-Side Kelvin Connection + Active Gate Drive Control:** Use a 4-pin package (Kelvin source) to eliminate source-inductance-induced gate-voltage errors, and optionally use a feedback controller that senses each device's current and adjusts the gate voltage.

**Conduction Loss Calculation (4 marks):**

For one MOSFET:

$$P_{cond,1} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} = (30)^2 \times 0.020 = 900 \times 0.020 = 18 \text{ W}$$

For four MOSFETs (all identical):

$$P_{cond,total} = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cond,total} = 72 \text{ W}}$$

Note: This is the **conduction loss only**. Total losses must also include switching losses $P_{sw} = f_{sw}(E_{on} + E_{off})$, which depend on the switching frequency $f_{sw}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Derating Factor with Efficiency** ŌĆö DF is dimensionless and applies to current per device, not to overall efficiency.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\ln()$ in the ballast resistor formula** ŌĆö Many students write $R_E = V_T / \Delta I$ without the $\ln(I_{S,max}/I_{S,min})$ term, losing 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up thermal resistance addition rules** ŌĆö Series resistances add directly; parallel thermal resistances follow the reciprocal rule, but only on the heatsink side. Junction-to-case is *always* in series per device.
> 4. **Stating "MOSFETs need ballast resistors"** ŌĆö This is wrong. MOSFETs have PTC behavior and are self-balancing. BJT is the one needing ballast.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** ŌĆö KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 mark for missing "╬®", "A", "W", or "┬░C/W" units.
> 6. **Not mentioning negative temperature coefficient (NTC) of BJT's $V_{BE}$ explicitly** ŌĆö This is the *conceptual core* of why paralleling BJTs is challenging. Examiners expect this phrase.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Paralleling of Transistors (Module 1):**
> - **Definition:** Paralleling connects $n$ transistors in parallel to share a load current exceeding a single device's rating.
> - **Ideal current per device:** $I_T = I_L / n$.
> - **Ideal derating factor:** $DF = 1/n$.
> - **Practical derating factor:** $DF_{actual} = 1/(n \cdot k)$ where $k \ge 1$ accounts for mismatch.
> - **BJT problem:** NTC of $V_{BE}$ ($-2$ mV/┬░C) causes thermal runaway.
> - **BJT solution:** Ballast emitter resistor $R_E$ with $R_{E,\min} = \dfrac{V_T \ln(I_{S,\max}/I_{S,\min})}{\Delta I_{C,\max}}$.
> - **MOSFET advantage:** PTC of $R_{DS(on)}$ makes them **self-balancing** ŌĆö no ballast needed.
> - **IGBT solution:** Individual gate resistors + Kelvin source + matched $V_{GE(th)}$.
> - **Static sharing mismatch source:** $V_{BE}$ / $V_{CE(sat)}$ / $R_{DS(on)}$ spread.
> - **Dynamic sharing mismatch source:** $t_{on}$, $t_{off}$ spread.
> - **Thermal resistance of $n$ parallel devices:** $R_{th} = R_{th,j-c} + R_{th,c-s}/n + R_{th,s-a}$.
> - **Standard mismatches:** BJT $\Delta V_{BE} \le 5$ mV; MOSFET $\Delta R_{DS(on)} \le 10$%; IGBT $\Delta V_{GE(th)} \le 0.5$ V.
> - **Current sharing reactors (CSRs):** Coupled inductors for high-power applications (>500 A).
> - **Total conduction loss in parallel:** $P_{cond,total} = n \cdot I_T^2 \cdot R_{on}$ (resistive devices) or $n \cdot V_{sat} \cdot I_T$ (BJT/IGBT).
> - **Industrial applications:** Traction drives, HVDC, EV inverters, welding supplies, solar inverters.
> - **Rule of thumb:** Always operate parallel devices at **$\le 80\%$** of $I_L/n$ for safe margin.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thyristor Family: SCRs, DIAC, TRIAC and GTOs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thyristor Family: SCRs, DIAC, TRIAC and GTOs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Thyristor Family ŌĆö A Foundational Introduction

The **thyristor family** constitutes the cornerstone of classical power electronics. It is a class of bistable, regenerative semiconductor devices fabricated from four alternating p-n-p-n layers (or two inverse-paralleled structures in the case of the TRIAC). Members of this family share a common lineage ŌĆö the Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) ŌĆö and include the **SCR, DIAC, TRIAC, and GTO**. All of them are *controllable latching switches*: once turned ON by a control signal, they remain ON until the principal current is forced below a minimum holding threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to clearly differentiate between the *symmetrical* triggering behaviour of the DIAC/TRIAC and the *unidirectional* switching behaviour of the SCR and GTO. Mere descriptive knowledge is insufficient ŌĆö the V-I characteristics, latching/holding current relations, and dv/dt triggering phenomena are *frequently tested* concepts.

---

## 1.2 The Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** The SCR is a four-layer (P-N-P-N), three-junction, three-terminal bistable power semiconductor device that conducts only in the forward direction when triggered by a gate pulse, and continues to conduct as long as the anode current remains above the holding current.

**The Three Terminals Are:**
- **Anode (A):** Positive terminal during forward conduction.
- **Cathode (K):** Negative terminal (heavily doped N+).
- **Gate (G):** Control terminal used to initiate conduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Latching Door" Analogy**
> Imagine a heavy, spring-loaded door that is normally *closed* (OFF state). Pushing the door lightly does nothing. But if someone (the *gate signal*) applies a small force at a specific point (the *gate*), the door *swings open* and *stays open* by itself ŌĆö it has latched. The only way to close it is for someone on the other side (the *anode current* going to zero ŌĆö the supply AC cycle passing through zero) to push it shut. That is exactly how an SCR works in an AC circuit.

---

## 1.3 The DIAC (Diode for Alternating Current)

**Definition:** A DIAC is a two-terminal, three-layer, bidirectional triggering device whose structure is essentially an antiparallel combination of two Shockley diodes. It exhibits a *negative resistance* region and is primarily used to fire TRIACs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Pressure Relief Valve"**
> A DIAC is like a pressure-relief valve that stays shut in either direction until the voltage across it exceeds a *breakover voltage* (typically 30ŌĆō40 V). Once this threshold is reached, the valve "pops open," the voltage across it collapses, and current flows freely. It conducts equally well in both polarities ŌĆö that is the essence of *bidirectional* behaviour.

---

## 1.4 The TRIAC (Triode for Alternating Current)

**Definition:** A TRIAC is a bidirectional, three-terminal power semiconductor device equivalent to two SCRs connected in anti-parallel. It can conduct in *both* quadrants I and III of the V-I plane and is triggered by a gate signal of either polarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Two-Way Latching Door"**
> If an SCR is a latching door that only opens from one side, a TRIAC is a *double latching door* that opens from either side. Whichever direction the main current wants to flow, the gate signal can initiate conduction in that direction, and it stays on until the current naturally reverses (in AC) or falls below the holding value.

---

## 1.5 The Gate Turn-Off Thyristor (GTO)

**Definition:** A GTO is a four-layer, three-terminal thyristor that, unlike the conventional SCR, can be *turned OFF* by applying a sufficiently large *negative* gate-cathode current pulse. It is thus a *fully controllable* latching switch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Latchable Door with a Release Button"**
> If the SCR is a door that can only be pushed open (gate ON) and must be slammed shut by the AC cycle, the GTO is a door that has a *release button* on the gate side. You can push it open with a positive gate pulse, *and* you can pull the latch back with a strong negative gate pulse ŌĆö full remote control in both directions.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Device Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$) of DIAC | **30 ŌĆō 40** | V |
| Holding current ($I_H$) of SCR | **5 ŌĆō 50** | mA |
| Latching current ($I_L$) of SCR | **20 ŌĆō 200** | mA |
| Critical dv/dt (typical) | **50 ŌĆō 200** | V/┬Ąs |
| Gate trigger current ($I_{GT}$) | **1 ŌĆō 50** | mA |
| Turn-off time ($t_q$) of GTO | **5 ŌĆō 25** | ┬Ąs |
| Forward breakover voltage ($V_{BO,SCR}$) | **200 ŌĆō 4500+** | V |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Structure and Two-Transistor Model of the SCR

The four-layer P-N-P-N structure of the SCR can be conceptually split into **two coupled complementary transistors**:

- The upper **P-N-P** layer acts as a PNP transistor ($Q_1$).
- The lower **N-P-N** layer acts as an NPN transistor ($Q_2$).
- The middle N-layer (Gate) and P-layer (Anode) are *common* to both.

This is the classical **two-transistor analogue model** (Gentry, 1964 paradigm), which is still used in KTU textbooks to explain regenerative action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Regenerative Latch Condition:** Conduction is self-sustaining when $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$, where $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are the common-base current gains of the two equivalent transistors. The gate current artificially boosts $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ above unity, triggering the *breakover*.

---

## 2.2 Modes of SCR Turn-ON (Triggering Methods)

The SCR can be turned ON through several distinct physical mechanisms ŌĆö KTU frequently asks for the *classification*:

1. **Forward voltage triggering** ŌĆö Anode-to-cathode voltage exceeds the forward breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$).
2. **Gate triggering** ŌĆö Application of a forward gate current $I_G \geq I_{GT}$.
3. **dv/dt triggering** ŌĆö A rapid rate of rise of anode voltage charges the depletion-layer capacitance and acts as a *displacement current* equivalent to a gate signal.
4. **Temperature triggering** ŌĆö At high junction temperatures, leakage current increases, raising $\alpha$'s and eventually causing thermal breakover.
5. **Light triggering (LTT)** ŌĆö Photon-generated carriers reduce the breakover voltage (used in HVDC valves).

---

## 2.3 Latching Current vs. Holding Current ŌĆö A Critical Distinction

This is one of the **most tested KTU distinctions**:

- **Latching Current ($I_L$):** The *minimum* anode current that must flow *immediately after turn-on* to guarantee that the SCR remains in the ON state when the gate signal is removed. It is the threshold that *latches* the regenerative action.
- **Holding Current ($I_H$):** The *minimum* anode current required to *keep* the SCR in conduction *after* it has already latched. It is always **less than** the latching current: $I_H < I_L$.

The physical reason: After latching, the internal current gain $\alpha$ of the two-transistor model has saturated, so less anode current is required to maintain $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$.

---

## 2.4 dv/dt Triggering ŌĆö The Unintended Turn-ON

The junction capacitance $C_j$ between the anode and cathode acts as a path for a *displacement current*:

$$i_{disp} = C_j \frac{dv}{dt}$$

If this displacement current is large enough to act as a *virtual gate current*, it can falsely trigger the SCR. The maximum permissible rate of rise of forward voltage is therefore specified by the manufacturer as $\left(\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right)_{critical}$.

---

## 2.5 DIAC V-I Characteristics

The DIAC exhibits a sharp *breakover* in both polarities. The relevant parameters are:

- **Breakover voltage $V_{BO}$:** Typically 30ŌĆō40 V in either polarity.
- **Dynamic breakback voltage $V_{BB}$:** The voltage at which the device *returns* to its high-impedance state.
- **Negative resistance region:** Between $V_{BO}$ and $V_{BB}$, the DIAC has *negative differential resistance*, enabling relaxation-oscillator firing of TRIACs.

---

## 2.6 TRIAC Operating Modes (Four Quadrants)

Unlike the SCR, a TRIAC can be triggered in *any* of four modes, classified by the polarity of the gate signal relative to MT1 (Main Terminal 1):

| Mode | MT2 Polarity | Gate Polarity | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** ($MT2+, G+$) | + | + | High |
| **II** ($MT2+, G-$) | + | ŌłÆ | Low |
| **III** ($MT2-, G-$) | ŌłÆ | ŌłÆ | High |
| **IV** ($MT2-, G+$) | ŌłÆ | + | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Modes I and III are the *most sensitive* and are the preferred triggering modes in 90% of dimmer/heat-control circuit designs.

---

## 2.7 GTO ŌĆö Turn-OFF Mechanism and Turn-Off Gain

The GTO is turned OFF by applying a *negative* gate-cathode voltage that sinks a current $I_{GQ}$ from the gate. The **turn-off gain** is defined as:

$$G_{TQ} = \frac{I_A}{I_{GQ}}$$

where $I_A$ is the anode current being commutated. Typical $G_{TQ}$ values range from **3 to 5**, indicating that the gate drive must supply roughly 20ŌĆō30% of the anode current to achieve turn-off ŌĆö a major disadvantage of the GTO compared to modern IGBTs.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ | Regenerative turn-on condition | Two-transistor model |
| 2 | $I_H < I_L$ | Holding < Latching | Conduction thresholds |
| 3 | $I_{GT} = \dfrac{V_{GT}}{R_G}$ | Gate trigger current | Trigger circuit design |
| 4 | $i_{disp} = C_j \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | dv/dt displacement current | Snubber design |
| 5 | $P_{gate} = I_{GT} \cdot V_{GT}$ | Average gate dissipation | Heatsink calc. |
| 6 | $G_{TQ} = \dfrac{I_A}{\vert I_{GQ} \vert}$ | GTO turn-off gain | Commutation design |
| 7 | $V_{AK} = V_{BO} \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$ (approx.) | Voltage collapse after triggering | Switching transient |
| 8 | $t_q = \dfrac{Q_{rr}}{di/dt}$ | Turn-off time (approximate) | GTO commutation |
| 9 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ | Max operating frequency | Switching loss |
| 10 | $P_{loss,cond} = V_T \cdot I_{T(avg)} + r_T \cdot I^2_{T(rms)}$ | Conduction loss | Thermal design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulae are used by power electronics design engineers at **ABB, Siemens, Schneider Electric** to design HVDC valves, traction converters, STATCOMs, and industrial motor drives. The same thyristor family that KTU asks about is what runs India's 800 kV HVDC RaigarhŌĆōPugalur line.

---

## 2.9 Comparison Table: SCR vs. TRIAC vs. GTO

| Feature | SCR | DIAC | TRIAC | GTO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Layers | P-N-P-N | ŌĆö | Equivalent to 2 SCRs | P-N-P-N |
| Terminals | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| Direction of conduction | Unidirectional | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| Turn-off by gate | No | N/A | No | **Yes** |
| Holding current | Yes | N/A | Yes | Yes |
| Typical voltage | up to 6 kV+ | 30ŌĆō40 V | up to 1.2 kV | up to 4.5 kV |
| Typical current | up to 5 kA | < 2 A | up to 200 A | up to 4 kA |
| dv/dt sensitivity | High | N/A | High | High |
| Typical application | HVDC, cycloconverters | TRIAC firing | Lamp dimmers, heaters | Traction, large drives |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Regenerative Turn-On Condition ($\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$)

**Problem:** Starting from the two-transistor equivalent model, derive the condition under which the SCR latches into conduction.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose the four-layer structure

The P-N-P-N stack is split into:
- Transistor $Q_1$ (PNP): Emitter = Anode (P), Base = N-layer, Collector = inner P-layer.
- Transistor $Q_2$ (NPN): Emitter = Cathode (N+), Base = inner P-layer, Collector = N-layer.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Write the emitter currents

Let $I_A$ = anode current, $I_K$ = cathode current, $I_G$ = gate current. From the BJT model:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_{E1} + I_{CBO1}$$

$$I_K = \alpha_2 I_{E2} + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply KCL at the inner P-layer (collector of $Q_1$ = base of $Q_2$)

The collector current of $Q_1$ equals the base current of $Q_2$:

$$I_{C1} = I_{B2}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Express anode current in terms of $\alpha$'s

By KCL: $I_A = I_K + I_G$ (the gate shunts the base current of $Q_1$).

$$I_A = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1) I_K + (I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}) + \alpha_2 I_G$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Identify the latching condition

For the device to *regeneratively* turn ON, $I_A$ must become large for any small perturbation. The coefficient of $I_K$ must be positive:

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1 \geq 0$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Physical interpretation

When the gate current $I_G$ is applied, the base drive of $Q_2$ increases, raising $\alpha_2$. The collector current of $Q_2$ feeds the base of $Q_1$, raising $\alpha_1$. The positive feedback forces $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ above 1 ŌĆö the device latches.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Relationship Between Latching and Holding Currents

**Claim:** $I_L > I_H$ for the same device at the same temperature.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Current gain dependence on collector current

In a BJT, $\alpha$ is a function of the collector current. For low injection, $\alpha \approx \alpha_0 (1 - e^{-I_C/I_{sat}})$. As $I_C$ increases from a small value, $\alpha$ *rises* from $\alpha_0$ toward unity.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Define latching point

At the instant of triggering (just after the gate pulse is applied), the anode current is rising from zero. The minimum anode current $I_L$ required to *establish* latching satisfies:

$$\alpha_1(I_L) + \alpha_2(I_L) = 1$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Define holding point

Once latched, the anode current is well above $I_L$ and the $\alpha$'s are saturated near unity. To *unlatch*, the anode must drop to a level $I_H$ where the $\alpha$'s have decayed back such that:

$$\alpha_1(I_H) + \alpha_2(I_H) = 1$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compare the two

Because $\alpha$ is a monotonically *increasing* function of $I_C$ in the active region, the threshold $I_C$ required to reach $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ is **higher when starting from zero** (latching) than **when starting from saturation** (holding). Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_L > I_H}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical example

For a typical 25 A SCR at 25┬░C: $I_L \approx 100$ mA, $I_H \approx 30$ mA. The ratio $I_L/I_H \approx 3.3$ is a common KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum dv/dt for Unintended Turn-On

**Problem:** Find the maximum permissible rate of rise of anode-cathode voltage such that the displacement current does not exceed the gate trigger current $I_{GT}$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Displacement current expression

The junction capacitance $C_j$ between anode and cathode (with depletion layer) gives a displacement current:

$$i_C = C_j \frac{dv_{AK}}{dt}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Worst-case condition

In the OFF state, the SCR can be modelled as a capacitor $C_j$ in parallel with the gate-cathode junction. The displacement current flowing through this capacitance must be less than $I_{GT}$ to avoid false turn-on:

$$C_j \left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)_{max} \leq I_{GT}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for the critical dv/dt

$$\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)_{critical} = \frac{I_{GT}}{C_j}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation

Suppose $C_j = 50$ pF and $I_{GT} = 10$ mA:

$$\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)_{critical} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-12}} = 2 \times 10^{8} \, \text{V/s} = 200 \, \text{V/┬Ąs}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Design implication

If the actual circuit dv/dt exceeds this value, an **R-C snubber** must be added. The snubber capacitor $C_s$ in parallel reduces the effective dv/dt across the device:

$$\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)_{actual} = \frac{V_{supply}}{R_s (C_j + C_s)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of GTO Turn-Off Gain

**Problem:** Show that the GTO turn-off gain $G_{TQ} = I_A / \vert I_{GQ} \vert$ is approximately equal to $\alpha_2 / (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1)$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the turn-off condition

To turn OFF the GTO, the negative gate current $I_{GQ}$ must *extract* the base charge of the equivalent PNP transistor $Q_1$ such that $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Write the base current of $Q_1$

From the two-transistor model, the base current of $Q_1$ is:

$$I_{B1} = I_A (1 - \alpha_1)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Equate to gate current

For turn-off, $I_{GQ} \approx I_{B1} / (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1)$ in the small-signal sense. Substituting:

$$I_{GQ} = \frac{I_A (1 - \alpha_1)}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $G_{TQ}$

$$G_{TQ} = \frac{I_A}{I_{GQ}} = \frac{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}{1 - \alpha_1}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Typical value

If $\alpha_1 = 0.3$ and $\alpha_2 = 0.9$, then:

$$G_{TQ} = \frac{0.3 + 0.9 - 1}{1 - 0.3} = \frac{0.2}{0.7} \approx 0.29$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reciprocal form $1/G_{TQ} \approx 3.5$ is the well-known *Knee gain* figure-of-merit ŌĆö meaning the gate drive must sink about **30%** of the anode current. This is why GTOs have been largely displaced by **IGBTs** in modern drives.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Triggering Methods vs. Devices

| Triggering Method | SCR | DIAC | TRIAC | GTO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward voltage | Ō£ō (breakover) | Ō£ō (bidirectional) | Ō£ō | Ō£ō |
| Gate pulse (positive) | Ō£ō | N/A | Ō£ō (Modes I, IV) | Ō£ō (ON) |
| Gate pulse (negative) | Ō£Ś | N/A | Ō£ō (Modes II, III) | Ō£ō (OFF) |
| dv/dt false trigger | Ō£Ś (unwanted) | Ō£Ś | Ō£Ś (unwanted) | Ō£Ś (unwanted) |
| Thermal | Ō£Ś (unwanted) | Ō£Ś | Ō£Ś | Ō£Ś |
| Light (LTT) | Ō£ō | Ō£Ś | Ō£Ś | Ō£Ś |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Transistor Equivalent Model of the SCR (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ANODE P+"] --> J1["J1 P-N Forward"]
    J1 --> N1["N Layer - Drift"]
    N1 --> J2["J2 N-P Reverse"]
    J2 --> P2["P Layer - Inner"]
    P2 --> J3["J3 P-N Forward"]
    J3 --> K["CATHODE N+"]
    G["GATE TERMINAL"] --> P2
    Q1["Equivalent PNP Q1 alpha1"] --- A
    Q2["Equivalent NPN Q2 alpha2"] --- K
    Q1 -. Coupled .- Q2
    LATCH["REGENERATIVE LATCH alpha1+alpha2 >= 1"] --- Q1
    LATCH --- Q2
```

---

## 4.2 SCR V-I Characteristics Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FORWARD["FORWARD BLOCKING REGION"]
        F1["Reverse leakage I_L rises slowly"]
        F2["IF at VBO -> BREAKOVER"]
    end
    subgraph ON["CONDUCTION REGION"]
        ON1["ON state VAK approx 1 to 2 V"]
        ON2["Current set by external load"]
    end
    subgraph REVERSE["REVERSE BLOCKING REGION"]
        R1["Leakage IR small"]
        R2["At VBR -> Avalanche breakdown"]
    end
    F2 --> ON1
    ON1 --> ON2
    ON2 --> LATCH["IA drops below IH -> returns to OFF"]
    LATCH --> F1
```

---

## 4.3 DIAC Internal Layer Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Terminal 1"] --> L1["P+ Layer"]
    L1 --> M1["N Layer"]
    M1 --> L2["P+ Layer"]
    L2 --> T2["Terminal 2"]
    NOTE1["Equivalent to two Shockley diodes in anti-parallel"] --- L1
    NOTE1 --- L2
    VBO["Breakover Voltage approx 30 to 40 V"] --- T1
    VBO --- T2
```

---

## 4.4 TRIAC Bidirectional Equivalent Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT["Positive Half Cycle MT2 positive"]
        SCR1["SCR1 forward biased P-N-P-N"]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT["Negative Half Cycle MT2 negative"]
        SCR2["SCR2 forward biased P-N-P-N"]
    end
    MT1["MT1"] --> SCR1
    SCR1 --> MT2P["MT2"]
    MT2P --> SCR2
    SCR2 --> MT1
    GATE["GATE G"] --- SCR1
    GATE --- SCR2
    MODE["Four Triggering Modes I II III IV"] --- GATE
```

---

## 4.5 GTO Turn-On / Turn-Off Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OFF["GTO OFF STATE - High impedance"] -->|"Positive gate pulse IGT"| ON["GTO ON STATE - Low impedance VAK approx 2 V"]
    ON -->|"Negative gate pulse IGQ about 0.3 IA"| TURN["TURN OFF - alpha1+alpha2 less than 1"]
    TURN --> OFF
    T1["t_on approx 1 to 5 microseconds"] --- ON
    T2["t_off approx 5 to 25 microseconds"] --- TURN
    SNUB["Snubber circuit essential for dv/dt and di/dt protection"] --- OFF
    SNUB --- ON
```

---

## 4.6 Comparative Functional Block ŌĆö Thyristor Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FAM["THYRISTOR FAMILY"]
    FAM --> SCR["SCR - Unidirectional Latching"]
    FAM --> DIAC["DIAC - Bidirectional Trigger"]
    FAM --> TRIAC["TRIAC - Bidirectional Switching"]
    FAM --> GTO["GTO - Turn-off Capable"]
    SCR --> APP1["HVDC Cycloconverters Phase controlled rectifiers"]
    DIAC --> APP2["TRIAC firing circuits Lamp dimmers"]
    TRIAC --> APP3["AC motor soft starters Heater controls"]
    GTO --> APP4["Traction drives STATCOM Large UPS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q-A1]`
**Define the term "latching current" of an SCR and state how it differs from the holding current.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Latching current ($I_L$):** It is the minimum value of anode current that *must flow through the SCR immediately after the gate pulse is removed* so that the device remains in the ON (conducting) state.

**Holding current ($I_H$):** It is the minimum value of anode current *required to maintain* the SCR in the ON state *after it has already been latched*.

**Key difference:** $I_L > I_H$ for the same device at the same temperature, because latching requires the $\alpha$'s of the two-transistor model to rise from near-zero, while holding only requires them to remain above the unity sum. Once saturated, less current suffices to maintain conduction.
*(1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for the comparison.)*

---

### Question 2 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q-A2]`
**Why is a DIAC connected in series with the gate of a TRIAC in a lamp dimmer circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. A TRIAC requires a *minimum gate trigger current* $I_{GT}$ to turn ON, and this is non-zero even at the beginning of each half-cycle. Without a DIAC, the TRIAC would fire immediately at the start of every half-cycle, resulting in full power.
2. A DIAC has a *breakover voltage* $V_{BO} \approx 30-40$ V. It blocks current until the capacitor in the dimmer RC network charges above $V_{BO}$.
3. Once $V_{BO}$ is exceeded, the DIAC *snap-toggles* into conduction and dumps the capacitor's charge into the TRIAC gate as a sharp pulse, *firing the TRIAC*. The *phase angle* of firing is varied by changing the resistance ŌĆö this is *phase-angle control*.
*(1 mark for the problem statement, 1 mark for DIAC breakover, 1 mark for the firing-pulse mechanism.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries two questions of 14 marks, and the student answers **one**. The two options provided below simulate the *internal choice* on the answer script.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q1(a)(b)]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**With a neat V-I characteristic curve, explain the working of an SCR. Clearly mark the forward breakover voltage, holding current, latching current, and reverse breakdown voltage on the curve.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Structure (2 marks):**
The SCR is a four-layer P-N-P-N, three-junction, three-terminal device with terminals **Anode (A)**, **Cathode (K)**, and **Gate (G)**.

**2. Forward blocking region (1 mark):**
With the anode positive and gate open, the middle N-P junction is reverse biased. Only a small forward leakage current (mA range) flows until the voltage reaches the **forward breakover voltage $V_{BO}$**.

**3. Conduction region (2 marks):**
At $V_{BO}$, avalanche multiplication of the reverse-biased junction causes the current to rise sharply. The $\alpha$'s of the equivalent two-transistor model reach unity sum and the device latches. The voltage drops to a small forward drop $V_T \approx 1 - 2$ V, and current is set by the external load.

**4. Reverse blocking region (1 mark):**
With the anode negative, the outer P-N junction is reverse biased. Only a small reverse leakage current $I_R$ flows until the **reverse breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$**, beyond which the device avalanches.

**5. Labelled curve (1 mark):** Draw the standard V-I curve with:
- X-axis: $V_{AK}$, Y-axis: $I_A$
- Forward region: leak, then breakover $V_{BO}$, then snap-back to $V_T$, then steep rise.
- Reverse region: leak, then breakdown $V_{BR}$.
- Mark $I_L$ (latching ŌĆö at breakover point) and $I_H$ (holding ŌĆö at snap-back point).

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Explain the two-transistor model of an SCR. Derive the condition for turn-on.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Two-transistor split (2 marks):**
The P-N-P-N stack is split into a PNP ($Q_1$, $\alpha_1$) and an NPN ($Q_2$, $\alpha_2$) transistor. The inner N-layer is the base of $Q_1$ and collector of $Q_2$. The inner P-layer is the base of $Q_2$ and collector of $Q_1$.

**2. KCL equations (2 marks):**
With $I_A$ = anode current, $I_K$ = cathode current, $I_G$ = gate current:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CO}$$

$$I_A = I_K + I_G$$

Solving the two equations:

$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**3. Latching condition (2 marks):**
For the device to turn on regeneratively, the denominator must approach zero:

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$$

When $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$, the anode current $I_A \to \infty$ mathematically (limited by external load). This is the *turn-on condition*.

**4. Role of gate (1 mark):**
A positive gate current $I_G$ raises $\alpha_2$ by injecting carriers into the base of $Q_2$. This pushes the sum $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ above 1, triggering conduction even at voltages *below* $V_{BO}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q1(a)(b) Alternative]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Explain the construction and V-I characteristics of a TRIAC. Discuss its four modes of operation with reference to gate sensitivity.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (2 marks):**
A TRIAC is essentially *two SCRs connected in anti-parallel* within a single six-layer (N-P-N-P-N-P) silicon die. It has three terminals: **MT1** (Main Terminal 1, reference), **MT2** (Main Terminal 2), and **Gate (G)**. It is bidirectional in both *blocking* and *conducting* states.

**2. V-I characteristics (2 marks):**
- In **Quadrant I** ($V_{MT2-MT1} > 0$, gate current positive), the device behaves like a forward SCR.
- In **Quadrant III** ($V_{MT2-MT1} < 0$), the same structure is triggered in reverse, behaving like the second anti-parallel SCR.
- The breakover voltage is typically **600ŌĆō800 V** and is symmetric in both polarities.

**3. Four operating modes (2 marks):**

| Mode | MT2 | Gate | $I_{GT}$ Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | + | + | **Highest** |
| II | + | ŌłÆ | Lowest |
| III | ŌłÆ | ŌłÆ | High |
| IV | ŌłÆ | + | Low |

**4. Practical implication (1 mark):**
Most dimmer circuits use Mode I or Mode III because the lower $I_{GT}$ reduces gate drive power. Mode II and IV are usually avoided in noise-sensitive designs.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**What is a GTO? Explain its turn-off mechanism and derive an expression for the turn-off gain. Why has the GTO been superseded by the IGBT in modern drives?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A GTO (Gate Turn-Off Thyristor) is a four-layer P-N-P-N latching device that, unlike a conventional SCR, can be *turned OFF by a reverse gate pulse*.

**2. Turn-off mechanism (2 marks):**
A large negative gate-cathode current $I_{GQ}$ is forced through the gate terminal. This *extracts* the excess carriers from the base region of the PNP transistor $Q_1$. The current gain $\alpha_1$ collapses, the condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ is broken, and regenerative action ceases. The device returns to the blocking state.

**3. Turn-off gain derivation (3 marks):**
From the two-transistor model during turn-off:

$$I_{GQ} = \frac{I_A (1 - \alpha_1)}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}$$

$$\Rightarrow G_{TQ} = \frac{I_A}{I_{GQ}} = \frac{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}{1 - \alpha_1}$$

For typical values $\alpha_1 \approx 0.3$, $\alpha_2 \approx 0.9$:

$$G_{TQ} \approx \frac{0.2}{0.7} \approx 0.29$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{G_{TQ}} \approx 3.5$$

This means the gate circuit must sink roughly **30% of the anode current** to force turn-off.

**4. Why superseded (1 mark):**
- The IGBT is *voltage-controlled* (no continuous gate current needed).
- Higher switching frequency (Ōēź 20 kHz vs. GTO's ~1 kHz).
- Snubberless operation possible.
- Lower switching losses.
- Simpler gate drive circuit.
- For these reasons, IGBTs and IGCTs have largely replaced GTOs in traction, UPS, and industrial drives.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Thyristor-Family Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating $I_L$ and $I_H$:** A large fraction of students state $I_L = I_H$ or even $I_L < I_H$. The correct inequality is **$I_L > I_H$** *for the same device at the same temperature*. Marks are deducted for the wrong inequality.
> 2. **Forgetting the gate signal's polarity role in TRIAC modes:** Writing "TRIAC has two modes" instead of four is a common partial-credit loss.
> 3. **dv/dt and di/dt confusion:** dv/dt limits *forward voltage rise* to prevent false turn-on; di/dt limits *initial current rise* to prevent localized hot-spot failure. Examiners reward students who can clearly distinguish these two.
> 4. **GTO turn-off gain written as $> 1$:** The *turn-off ratio* $1/G_{TQ}$ is typically > 1, but the *gain* $G_{TQ}$ itself is typically < 1. Writing $G_{TQ} > 1$ loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the anti-parallel SCR equivalent of a TRIAC:** Always draw the equivalent circuit with two SCRs in anti-parallel ŌĆö partial credit is awarded even if the prose is imperfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Thyristor Family (SCR, DIAC, TRIAC, GTO)**

- **SCR** = four-layer, three-junction, unidirectional, **latches but cannot be turned off by gate**; turn-off only by natural current zero.
- **Two-transistor model** gives the latch condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.
- **Gate triggering** is the *preferred* turn-on method ŌĆö it reduces the required $V_{AK}$ below $V_{BO}$.
- **Latching current $I_L$ > Holding current $I_H$** ŌĆö always, for the same device and temperature.
- **dv/dt triggering** is *unwanted*: it is prevented by an R-C snubber.
- **di/dt limit** is *unwanted*: it is prevented by a series inductance.
- **DIAC** is a bidirectional trigger with breakover $V_{BO} \approx 30 - 40$ V and a negative-resistance region.
- **TRIAC** = two anti-parallel SCRs; **four** triggering modes (I, II, III, IV); Modes I and III are most sensitive.
- **GTO** can be turned off by a *negative gate pulse*; turn-off gain $G_{TQ} \approx 0.3$ means the gate must sink $\sim 30\%$ of $I_A$.
- **GTO $\to$ IGBT migration:** GTOs are mostly obsolete in low-to-medium power drives due to high gate drive power, large snubbers, and slow switching.
- **LTT (Light-Triggered Thyristors)** are used in HVDC valves (e.g., RaigarhŌĆōPugalur 800 kV UHVDC, India) where electrical isolation of the gate is essential.
- **Memory hook:** *SCR ŌĆö Same-hemisphere; TRIAC ŌĆö Two-hemispheres; GTO ŌĆö Goes-Off; DIAC ŌĆö Delivers-Impulse-As-Capacitor.*
- **Numerical sense:** $I_L$ in mA, $I_H$ in mA, $V_{BO,DIAC}$ in tens of volts, $V_{BO,SCR}$ in hundreds-to-thousands of volts, $t_{off,GTO}$ in microseconds.
- **Diagrams expected in KTU answers:** Always draw the labelled V-I curve and the two-transistor model ŌĆö together they fetch 3ŌĆō4 bonus marks.
- **Pitfall:** Never use the term "thyristor" and "SCR" interchangeably in a KTU exam ŌĆö a *thyristor* is the *family*; an *SCR* is a *member*. Examiners will mark this down.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-I Characteristics of SCR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** V-I Characteristics of SCR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: V-I CHARACTERISTICS OF SCR ŌĆö SECTION 1

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR):** A four-layer (P-N-P-N), three-junction (J1, J2, J3) semiconductor power device with three terminals ŌĆö **Anode (A)**, **Cathode (K)**, and **Gate (G)** ŌĆö that conducts current only in the forward direction when triggered by a gate pulse, and remains in the OFF state once the forward current falls below the holding level.

The **V-I Characteristics of SCR** describe the graphical relationship between the **Anode-to-Cathode Voltage (VAK)** on the X-axis and the **Anode Current (IA)** on the Y-axis, traced under varying gate current (IG) conditions. The curve has **four distinct operating regions** and is **asymmetric**, i.e., behaviour in forward bias is completely different from reverse bias.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Boundary Metrics (Must be memorized):**
> - **Forward Breakover Voltage (VBO)** ŌĆö typically **200 V to 2500 V** for standard SCRs.
> - **Holding Current (IH)** ŌĆö typically **5 mA to 50 mA**.
> - **Latching Current (IL)** ŌĆö typically **10 mA to 100 mA**, always greater than IH.
> - **Reverse Breakdown Voltage (VBR)** ŌĆö slightly less than VBO.
> - **Gate Trigger Current (IGT)** ŌĆö typically **1 mA to 50 mA**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Floodgate of a Dam"

Imagine a large **water reservoir** (the anode) connected to a **lower river** (the cathode) through a **massive steel floodgate** (the SCR). Under normal conditions:

1. The floodgate is **locked shut** even though water is pushing from above (this is the **Forward Blocking Mode** ŌĆö the SCR can hold off a forward voltage without conducting).
2. To open the gate, a small **lever pulled by a worker** (the **Gate pulse IG**) is required. Once pulled, the gate **cannot be re-closed by removing the lever** ŌĆö the water pressure itself keeps it open (this is **latching** ŌĆö once anode current exceeds IL, the gate loses control).
3. The gate closes **only when water flow drops below a trickle** (current falls below IH ŌĆö **holding current**).
4. If someone tries to push water **backwards** (reverse voltage), the dam wall holds until structural failure occurs (this is **Reverse Breakdown**).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Rule for KTU Exams:** The SCR is a *"trigger-and-latch"* device. The gate is like a **finger-flick switch** ŌĆö a tiny flick starts a hurricane of current, but only nature (load current) can stop it.

---

## 1.3 Device Symbol and Terminal Identification

The standard SCR symbol is a **diode symbol with a third (gate) terminal** attached near the cathode side.

| Terminal | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Anode | **A** | Main current entry (P-layer) |
| Cathode | **K** | Main current exit (N-layer) |
| Gate | **G** | Control terminal (P-layer attached to inner P-region) |

The arrow in the symbol points from Anode to Cathode, indicating **unidirectional conduction** ŌĆö exactly like a diode, but with a controllable turn-on mechanism.

---

## 1.4 Physical Structure ŌĆö Four-Layer PNPN Stack

The SCR is fabricated as a single silicon crystal doped as:

$$
\text{P}^+ - \text{N}^- - \text{P} - \text{N}^+ \text{ (from Anode to Cathode)}
$$

Three junctions are formed:
- **J1** ŌĆö between P+ and NŌłÆ
- **J2** ŌĆö between NŌłÆ and P
- **J3** ŌĆö between P and N+

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the junction biasing conditions for each region of the V-I curve in your answers. Examiners award marks for correctly identifying that **J1 and J3 are forward-biased** while **J2 is reverse-biased** during forward blocking ŌĆö and vice versa during reverse blocking.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1: V-I CHARACTERISTICS OF SCR ŌĆö SECTION 2

## 2.1 The Two-Transistor Analogy (KTU Favourite)

The SCR can be conceptually split into **two complementary BJTs** interconnected as a **regenerative feedback pair**:

| SCR Region | Forms Transistor | Type |
|---|---|---|
| P+ (Anode) ŌĆö NŌłÆ (J1) ŌĆö P | T1 | **PNP** |
| NŌłÆ (J1) ŌĆö P (J2) ŌĆö N+ (Cathode) | T2 | **NPN** |
| Middle P-layer is **common** to both bases | ŌĆö | Cross-coupled |

The **collector of T1** feeds the **base of T2**, and the **collector of T2** feeds the **base of T1** ŌĆö this is **positive feedback**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regenerative Loop Condition:** The common-base current gain product $\alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_2$ must approach unity to trigger breakover. The forward breakover occurs when:
> $$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$$

---

## 2.2 Four Operating Regions of the V-I Curve

### Region 1 ŌĆö Forward Blocking Mode (OFF State)
- Anode is **positive** with respect to cathode, but $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$.
- Junctions J1 and J3 are **forward-biased**, J2 is **reverse-biased**.
- Only a tiny **leakage current (ID or IL)** flows ŌĆö typically in **microamperes**.
- The SCR behaves like an **open switch**.

### Region 2 ŌĆö Forward Conduction Mode (ON State)
- Once $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$ (or a gate pulse is applied), the device **switches ON** abruptly.
- The voltage across the SCR drops to a small value ŌĆö **on-state voltage drop (VT)** of about **1 V to 2 V**.
- Anode current is now limited only by the **external load**.
- Junctions J1, J2, J3 are **all forward-biased** ŌĆö the device saturates.

### Region 3 ŌĆö Reverse Blocking Mode
- Anode is **negative** with respect to cathode.
- Junctions J1 and J3 are **reverse-biased**, J2 is **forward-biased**.
- Only a small **reverse leakage current (IR)** flows.
- The SCR can sustain reverse voltages up to the **Reverse Breakdown Voltage (VBR)**.

### Region 4 ŌĆö Reverse Breakdown (Avalanche) Mode
- When reverse voltage exceeds **VBR**, avalanche multiplication occurs at J1.
- Large reverse current flows, and the device is **destroyed** due to excessive power dissipation (unless protected by an external fuse or snubber).

---

## 2.3 Effect of Gate Current on VBO

The most important practical feature: **increasing the gate current reduces VBO**.

| Gate Current IG | Forward Breakover Voltage VBO | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| IG = 0 | Maximum VBO | Hard breakover (avalanche) |
| IG = small (1 mA) | Moderate VBO | Controlled turn-on |
| IG = large (50 mA) | Very small VBO | Near-instant turn-on |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Key Sentence:** *"A positive gate current lowers the forward breakover voltage, and at a critical value of IG, the SCR turns on without exceeding VBO ŌĆö this is the normal triggering mode."*

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Anode current (two-transistor model) | $I_A = \dfrac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | Amperes |
| Breakover condition | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Emitter current relation | $I_E = I_C + I_B$ | Amperes |
| Transistor gain coupling | $I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A$, $\quad I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_A$ | Amperes |
| Latching vs Holding | $I_L > I_H$ | Always |
| On-state power loss | $P_T = V_T \cdot I_T$ | Watts |
| Reverse leakage | $I_R \approx \mu A \text{ to } mA$ | ŌĆö |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Markdown Safety Rule:** All absolute value bars and "such-that" pipes inside formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the table layout.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The V-I characteristics of SCR are **foundational** to every power electronics application:

- **Phase-controlled rectifiers** ŌĆö operate in the forward blocking-to-conduction transition (region 1 to 2).
- **AC voltage controllers** ŌĆö exploit the asymmetric V-I curve to chop AC waveforms.
- **DC circuit breakers** ŌĆö use the reverse blocking capability to interrupt fault currents.
- **Crowbar protection circuits** ŌĆö trigger on overvoltage by reaching the VBO threshold.
- **HVDC transmission** ŌĆö series-connected SCRs handle thousands of amperes, requiring precise V-I matching.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 1: V-I CHARACTERISTICS OF SCR ŌĆö SECTION 3

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Anode Current Using Two-Transistor Model

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 1 university exams. Every algebraic step must be reproduced.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Two Transistors

From the PNPN stack, the SCR is split as:

$$
\text{Transistor T}_1: \quad P^+ - N^- - P \quad \text{(PNP, common-base)}
$$

$$
\text{Transistor T}_2: \quad N^- - P - N^+ \quad \text{(NPN, common-base)}
$$

The middle P-layer acts as:
- **Collector of T1** (PNP)
- **Base of T2** (NPN)

The middle NŌłÆ layer acts as:
- **Base of T1** (PNP)
- **Collector of T2** (NPN)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Write Collector Currents

For a common-base transistor, the collector current is:

$$
I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **common-base current gain** and $I_{CBO}$ is the **leakage current** with emitter open.

Applying to T1 (PNP):

$$
I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_{E1} + I_{CBO1}
$$

The emitter of T1 is connected to the Anode, so $I_{E1} = I_A$:

$$
I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CBO1}
$$

Applying to T2 (NPN):

$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_{E2} + I_{CBO2}
$$

The emitter of T2 is connected to the Cathode, so $I_{E2} = I_K$ (cathode current):

$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CBO2}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply KCL at the Cathode Node

The total cathode current $I_K$ is the sum of all currents entering the cathode terminal:

$$
I_K = I_A + I_G
$$

This is a **critical junction** ŌĆö the gate current $I_G$ is injected into the base of T2 from outside.

> **Logic explanation:** The anode supplies $I_A$, the gate supplies $I_G$, and both these currents must exit through the cathode. This is the foundation of the entire two-transistor model.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Express I_{C2} in Terms of I_A

Substituting $I_K = I_A + I_G$ into the T2 equation:

$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CBO2}
$$

Expanding:

$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO2}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Sum the Collector Currents

The total anode current $I_A$ is the sum of the two collector currents (since the collectors of T1 and T2 both feed the anode terminal via the internal PNPN path):

$$
I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
I_A = (\alpha_1 I_A + I_{CBO1}) + (\alpha_2 I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO2})
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Group Terms

$$
I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}
$$

$$
I_A = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + (I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2})
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Combine Leakage Currents

Define the combined leakage current:

$$
I_{CO} = I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}
$$

So:

$$
I_A = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}
$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Isolate I_A

Move the $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A$ term to the left:

$$
I_A - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A = \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}
$$

Factor:

$$
I_A \left[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\right] = \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}
$$

### Step 9 ŌĆö Final Expression for I_A

$$
\boxed{\,I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}\,}
$$

> **Logic explanation of the final formula:** The numerator represents the **driving input** ŌĆö the gate current and the leakage current together try to turn the device on. The denominator is the **regenerative gain** ŌĆö when $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ approaches 1, the denominator shrinks toward zero, and $I_A$ tends to infinity (breakover).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Breakover Condition

At breakover, the denominator $1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)$ approaches zero (mathematically) before the current becomes infinite (the device physically clamps at a finite voltage).

Setting the denominator to zero:

$$
1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1\,}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** When the sum of the common-base current gains of the two internal transistors equals unity, the **regenerative feedback loop becomes self-sustaining**. Any further increase in $I_A$ increases $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ (because $\alpha$ increases with collector current in BJTs), which in turn increases $I_A$ even more ŌĆö runaway until the device saturates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Latching Current Condition

The latching current $I_L$ is the **minimum anode current required to maintain conduction** after the gate signal is removed.

When the gate signal is removed ($I_G = 0$), the anode current becomes:

$$
I_A = \dfrac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}
$$

For the device to remain latched, $I_A$ must be **at least equal to the value needed to keep $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$**. Below this current, the regenerative loop collapses and the device returns to the blocking state.

Mathematically, the **holding current $I_H$** is defined by the condition:

$$
\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1 \quad \text{at} \quad I_A = I_H
$$

And $I_L$ is slightly greater than $I_H$ because $I_L$ is the current at which the device **just latches on** during turn-on, while $I_H$ is the current at which it **just turns off** during a fall in anode current.

$$
\boxed{\,I_L > I_H\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table ŌĆö Latching vs Holding

| Parameter | Latching Current $I_L$ | Holding Current $I_H$ |
|---|---|---|
| Defined during | Turn-ON process | Turn-OFF process |
| Direction of change | $I_A$ **rising** | $I_A$ **falling** |
| Typical value | 10 mA to 100 mA | 5 mA to 50 mA |
| Relation to gate | $I_L$ measured **after gate pulse is removed** | $I_H$ measured as $I_A$ drops |
| Magnitude | Always **higher** | Always **lower** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students write $I_L = I_H$ or $I_L < I_H$. This is a **guaranteed 1-mark deduction**. Always write $I_L > I_H$ with the explanation above.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Question:** An SCR has $\alpha_1 = 0.45$ and $\alpha_2 = 0.50$ in the OFF state. The leakage current is $I_{CO} = 2 \text{ mA}$. If a gate current of $I_G = 15 \text{ mA}$ is applied, compute the anode current $I_A$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the denominator:

$$
1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 1 - (0.45 + 0.50) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

Step 2: Calculate the numerator:

$$
\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO} = (0.50)(15) + 2 = 7.5 + 2 = 9.5 \text{ mA}
$$

Step 3: Calculate the anode current:

$$
I_A = \dfrac{9.5 \text{ mA}}{0.05} = 190 \text{ mA}
$$

**Conclusion:** A small gate current of 15 mA produces an anode current of 190 mA ŌĆö demonstrating the **regenerative amplification** of the SCR.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 1: V-I CHARACTERISTICS OF SCR ŌĆö SECTION 4

## 4.1 V-I Characteristic Curve ŌĆö Detailed Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture of the V-I curve**, mapping every region, axis, transition, and gate-current effect.

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX["HORIZONTAL AXIS VAK ANODE TO CATHODE VOLTAGE V"]
    axisY["VERTICAL AXIS IA ANODE CURRENT A"]
    
    reg1["REGION 1 FORWARD BLOCKING MODE OFF STATE J1 FORWARD J2 REVERSE J3 FORWARD V AK LESS THAN VBO LEAKAGE CURRENT FLOWS"]
    reg2["REGION 2 FORWARD CONDUCTION MODE ON STATE ALL JUNCTIONS FORWARD BIASED VT APPROX 1 TO 2 VOLTS HIGH IA LIMITED BY LOAD"]
    reg3["REGION 3 REVERSE BLOCKING MODE ANODE NEGATIVE J1 REVERSE J2 FORWARD J3 REVERSE IR REVERSE LEAKAGE SMALL"]
    reg4["REGION 4 REVERSE BREAKDOWN MODE AVALANCHE AT J1 V AK EXCEEDS VBR DESTRUCTIVE UNLESS PROTECTED"]
    
    pointA["POINT A VBO FORWARD BREAKOVER VOLTAGE TRIGGERS AVALANCHE TURN ON IG ZERO"]
    pointB["POINT B VON ON STATE OPERATING POINT VT LOW IA HIGH"]
    pointC["POINT C VBR REVERSE BREAKDOWN VOLTAGE AVALANCHE FAILURE POINT"]
    pointD["POINT D IH HOLDING CURRENT MIN IA TO STAY ON"]
    pointE["POINT E IL LATCHING CURRENT MIN IA TO TURN ON"]
    
    gateE["GATE CURRENT IG EFFECT HIGHER IG LOWERS VBO CURVE SHIFTS LEFT"]
    
    trans1["TRANSITION 1 BLOCKING TO CONDUCTION VIA VBO OR GATE PULSE"]
    trans2["TRANSITION 2 CONDUCTION TO BLOCKING WHEN IA FALLS BELOW IH"]
    trans3["TRANSITION 3 REVERSE BLOCKING TO BREAKDOWN WHEN VAK EXCEEDS VBR"]
    
    axisX --> reg1
    axisX --> reg3
    axisY --> reg1
    axisY --> reg2
    
    reg1 --> trans1
    trans1 --> reg2
    reg2 --> trans2
    trans2 --> reg1
    
    reg1 --> pointA
    reg2 --> pointB
    reg3 --> pointC
    reg2 --> pointD
    reg2 --> pointE
    
    reg3 --> trans3
    trans3 --> reg4
    
    gateE --> reg1
    gateE --> pointA
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SCR Operation

The following block diagram maps the **functional flow** of how an SCR transitions between blocking and conduction states, including gate control and load interaction.

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["DC OR AC VOLTAGE SOURCE"]
    load["LOAD RESISTOR R LOAD"]
    scr["SCR ANODE TO CATHODE PATH"]
    gate["GATE DRIVE CIRCUIT IG PULSE"]
    fb["FEEDBACK LOOP REGENERATIVE T1 AND T2"]
    reg["REGION DETECTOR BLOCKING OR CONDUCTION"]
    
    src -->|"VA POSITIVE"| scr
    scr -->|"IA"| load
    load -->|"RETURN"| src
    
    gate -->|"IG TRIGGERS"| scr
    scr -->|"ALPHA1 ALPHA2 COUPLING"| fb
    fb -->|"SELF SUSTAINING"| scr
    
    scr -->|"STATUS FLAG"| reg
    reg -->|"IA GT IL ON STATE"| load
    reg -->|"IA LT IH OFF STATE"| gate
```

---

## 4.3 Two-Transistor Cross-Coupled Model ŌĆö Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    pnpA["PNP TRANSISTOR T1 EMITTER AT ANODE"]
    npnB["NPN TRANSISTOR T2 EMITTER AT CATHODE"]
    
    c1["COLLECTOR OF T1 FEEDS BASE OF T2"]
    c2["COLLECTOR OF T2 FEEDS BASE OF T1"]
    
    baseT1["BASE OF T1 N LAYER"]
    baseT2["BASE OF T2 P LAYER"]
    
    gateI["GATE CURRENT IG INJECTED AT BASE OF T2"]
    
    pnpA --> c1
    c1 --> baseT2
    npnB --> c2
    c2 --> baseT1
    baseT1 --> pnpA
    baseT2 --> npnB
    
    gateI --> baseT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **two arrows forming a loop** (T1 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T1) represent the regenerative feedback. This is the **heart of the SCR's latching behaviour**.

---

## 4.4 Decision Table ŌĆö State Transitions of SCR

| Current Condition | Gate Condition | Resulting State |
|---|---|---|
| $I_A < I_L$ | $I_G > I_{GT}$ | **Does not turn ON** (fails to latch) |
| $I_A \geq I_L$ | $I_G > I_{GT}$ | **Turns ON**, latches |
| $I_A > I_H$ (after turn-on) | $I_G = 0$ | **Stays ON** |
| $I_A$ drops below $I_H$ | $I_G = 0$ | **Turns OFF** |
| $V_{AK} > V_{BO}$ | $I_G = 0$ | **Turns ON** by avalanche |
| $V_{AK} < 0$ (reverse) | Any | **Reverse blocking** (until $V_{BR}$) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 1: V-I CHARACTERISTICS OF SCR ŌĆö SECTION 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

### PART A ŌĆö SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** Define the term "Holding Current" of an SCR and state how it differs from "Latching Current". `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Holding Current (IH):** The minimum anode current required to keep the SCR in the ON state. If the anode current falls below $I_H$, the SCR turns OFF and returns to the forward blocking state.

**Latching Current (IL):** The minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR during turn-on so that the device **continues to conduct even after the gate pulse is removed**.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Holding Current $I_H$ | Latching Current $I_L$ |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Turn-OFF | Turn-ON |
| Trend | Anode current **falling** | Anode current **rising** |
| Magnitude | Smaller | Larger |
| Relation | $I_L > I_H$ | ŌĆö |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write $I_L < I_H$ or $I_L = I_H$. This is incorrect and will attract negative marking in KTU valuation. Always write $I_L > I_H$ with the reasoning that the device must overcome additional losses during turn-on.

---

#### **Question 2:** Draw and explain the static V-I characteristics of an SCR. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The static V-I characteristics of an SCR are plotted with **anode-to-cathode voltage $V_{AK}$** on the X-axis and **anode current $I_A$** on the Y-axis. The curve has four regions:

1. **Forward Blocking Region (Quadrant I, left part):** When the anode is positive but $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$, the SCR is OFF. Only a small leakage current flows. Junctions J1 and J3 are forward-biased; J2 is reverse-biased.

2. **Forward Conduction Region (Quadrant I, right part):** Once $V_{AK}$ reaches $V_{BO}$ (or a gate pulse is applied), the SCR switches ON. The voltage drops to a low value $V_T$ (1 to 2 V), and the current is limited by the load.

3. **Reverse Blocking Region (Quadrant III, upper-left):** When the anode is negative, the SCR blocks reverse voltage. Only a small reverse leakage current $I_R$ flows.

4. **Reverse Breakdown Region (Quadrant III, lower-left):** If the reverse voltage exceeds $V_{BR}$, avalanche breakdown occurs and the device is destroyed.

(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual mapping of these regions.)

---

### PART B ŌĆö LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1):** With the help of the two-transistor model of an SCR, derive the expression for anode current. Also explain the V-I characteristics of an SCR. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(14 Marks)**

**Sub-Part (a) ŌĆö Two-Transistor Model Derivation ŌĆö 7 Marks**

The SCR (PNPN structure) is split into two cross-coupled transistors:
- **T1:** PNP transistor with emitter at the anode
- **T2:** NPN transistor with emitter at the cathode

The collector current equations in common-base configuration:

$$
I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_{E1} + I_{CBO1}
$$

Since the emitter of T1 is the anode terminal: $I_{E1} = I_A$

$$
I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CBO1}
$$

For T2:

$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_{E2} + I_{CBO2}
$$

Since the emitter of T2 is the cathode terminal: $I_{E2} = I_K = I_A + I_G$

$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CBO2}
$$

Applying KCL: $I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$

$$
I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CBO1} + \alpha_2 I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO2}
$$

$$
I_A = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + (I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2})
$$

Let $I_{CO} = I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}$. Solving:

$$
I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}
$$

**Breakover condition** occurs when $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$, making the denominator zero and $I_A \to \infty$.

**Sub-Part (b) ŌĆö V-I Characteristics Explanation ŌĆö 7 Marks**

The V-I characteristics are explained in **four regions**:

| Region | Voltage | Junctions | Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Blocking | $0 < V_{AK} < V_{BO}$ | J1, J3 forward; J2 reverse | Leakage (╬╝A) |
| Forward Conduction | $V_{AK} = V_T$ (low) | All forward | High (load-limited) |
| Reverse Blocking | $V_{AK} < 0$ but $\vert V_{AK} \vert < V_{BR}$ | J1, J3 reverse; J2 forward | Reverse leakage |
| Reverse Breakdown | $\vert V_{AK} \vert \geq V_{BR}$ | Avalanche at J1 | Destructive |

**Effect of Gate Current:** Increasing $I_G$ reduces $V_{BO}$ (the breakover curve shifts left). With sufficient $I_G$, the SCR turns on without reaching $V_{BO}$ ŌĆö this is the **normal triggering mode**.

**Holding and Latching:** Once ON, the device stays ON until $I_A < I_H$ (holding current). To turn on reliably, $I_A$ must reach $I_L$ (latching current) before the gate pulse is removed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often:
> 1. **Confuse $I_{CBO1}$ with $I_{CO}$** ŌĆö these are different ($I_{CO}$ is the sum of two leakage currents).
> 2. **Forget to mention the gate current** in the KCL equation at the cathode node.
> 3. **Skip the four-region description** in part (b) ŌĆö losing 3-4 marks easily.
> 
> **Always state both the derivation AND the operating regions** in a 14-mark question.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2):** Explain the static V-I characteristics of an SCR in detail with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss the effect of gate current on the forward breakover voltage. Also explain the terms: latching current and holding current. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**Sub-Part (a) ŌĆö V-I Characteristics and Effect of Gate Current ŌĆö 7 Marks**

The V-I characteristics of an SCR are divided into three modes of operation: **forward mode, reverse mode, and breakdown mode**. The detailed four-region description is as follows:

**Forward Blocking Mode:**
- Anode positive, $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$
- Junctions J1 and J3 are forward-biased, J2 is reverse-biased
- Only a small forward leakage current $I_F$ flows
- SCR is in OFF state

**Forward Conduction Mode:**
- At $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$ (or via gate trigger), the SCR turns ON
- The on-state voltage drop $V_T$ is small (1 to 2 V)
- The anode current is determined by the load
- All three junctions are forward-biased

**Reverse Blocking Mode:**
- Anode is negative with respect to cathode
- Junctions J1 and J3 are reverse-biased, J2 is forward-biased
- Only a small reverse leakage current $I_R$ flows
- SCR blocks reverse voltage up to $V_{BR}$

**Reverse Breakdown Mode:**
- When the reverse voltage exceeds $V_{BR}$, avalanche breakdown occurs
- Large reverse current flows ŌĆö destructive unless externally limited

**Effect of Gate Current on VBO:**

The forward breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ is **inversely proportional to the gate current $I_G$**. As $I_G$ increases:
- $V_{BO}$ decreases
- The breakover curve shifts toward the Y-axis (smaller forward voltage needed)
- At a critical value of $I_G$ (called the gate trigger current $I_{GT}$), the SCR turns on at a very small forward voltage

This is why **gate triggering** is the preferred turn-on method ŌĆö it avoids the high voltage stress associated with avalanche breakover.

**Sub-Part (b) ŌĆö Latching and Holding Currents ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Latching Current ($I_L$):**
- Defined during the turn-ON process
- The minimum anode current that must flow so that the SCR **continues to conduct** after the gate pulse is removed
- If $I_A$ does not reach $I_L$ before $I_G$ is removed, the SCR turns back OFF
- Typical value: 10 mA to 100 mA

**Holding Current ($I_H$):**
- Defined during the turn-OFF process
- The minimum anode current required to keep the SCR in the ON state
- If $I_A$ falls below $I_H$, the SCR returns to the forward blocking state
- Typical value: 5 mA to 50 mA

**Relationship:** $I_L > I_H$

**Why is $I_L > I_H$?**
- During turn-on, additional charge carriers must be supplied to establish saturation in the internal transistor pair
- During turn-off, the carriers are already present and only need to fall below the sustaining level
- Hence the device latches at a higher current than it holds

**Significance in Circuit Design:**
- Load resistance must be chosen so that the steady-state $I_A$ exceeds $I_L$ (to ensure latching)
- For forced commutation, the commutating circuit must force $I_A$ below $I_H$ (to turn off the SCR)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In this 14-mark question, students often:
> 1. **Skip the numerical typical values** of $I_L$ and $I_H$ ŌĆö examiners expect them.
> 2. **Forget to state $I_L > I_H$** with the physical reasoning ŌĆö losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Don't draw the V-I curve** ŌĆö losing 3-4 marks out of 14. Always include a labelled diagram.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for V-I Characteristics of SCR:**

- Ō£ģ SCR is a **4-layer PNPN, 3-junction, 3-terminal** unidirectional power device.
- Ō£ģ Three terminals: **Anode (A), Cathode (K), Gate (G)**.
- Ō£ģ V-I curve has **four regions**: Forward Blocking, Forward Conduction, Reverse Blocking, Reverse Breakdown.
- Ō£ģ **Forward breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$)** is the voltage at which the SCR turns ON without gate signal.
- Ō£ģ **Reverse breakdown voltage ($V_{BR}$)** is the maximum reverse voltage the SCR can withstand.
- Ō£ģ The **two-transistor model** splits the SCR into a PNP (T1) and NPN (T2) pair with regenerative feedback.
- Ō£ģ **Anode current formula:** $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$
- Ō£ģ **Breakover condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$
- Ō£ģ **Latching current $I_L$** is the minimum anode current to **stay ON** after gate pulse is removed (turn-ON parameter).
- Ō£ģ **Holding current $I_H$** is the minimum anode current to **remain ON** during current decay (turn-OFF parameter).
- Ō£ģ **Critical relationship:** $I_L > I_H$ ŌĆö always.
- Ō£ģ **Gate current reduces $V_{BO}$** ŌĆö higher $I_G$ means lower breakover voltage.
- Ō£ģ During **forward blocking**: J1, J3 forward; J2 reverse.
- Ō£ģ During **forward conduction**: All three junctions forward-biased.
- Ō£ģ During **reverse blocking**: J1, J3 reverse; J2 forward.
- Ō£ģ **On-state voltage drop $V_T$** is typically 1 V to 2 V.
- Ō£ģ **Typical values to memorize:** $V_{BO}$: 200-2500 V; $I_H$: 5-50 mA; $I_L$: 10-100 mA; $I_{GT}$: 1-50 mA.
- Ō£ģ Applications include **phase-controlled rectifiers, AC voltage controllers, DC circuit breakers, HVDC systems, and crowbar protection**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Power Electronic Circuits: AC-DC, DC-DC, DC-AC and AC-AC Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Power Electronic Circuits: AC-DC, DC-DC, DC-AC and AC-AC Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Electronics** is the branch of electrical engineering that deals with the conversion, control, and conditioning of electrical power using electronic switching devices. The core philosophy is the use of **semiconductor power switches** (operated in either fully **ON** or fully **OFF** states) to shape and transform the electrical energy from one form to another with **high efficiency** (typically > **95%**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Power Electronic circuit is a static (no moving parts) assembly of power semiconductor switches and reactive elements (L, C) that processes voltage/current attributes ŌĆö magnitude, frequency, phase, or number of phases ŌĆö to deliver controlled electrical energy to a load.

The **classification of power electronic circuits** is done based on the **type of input supply** and the **type of output delivered** to the load. There are four fundamental converter classes:

| # | Converter Class | Input | Output | Common Name |
|---|----------------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **AC ŌåÆ DC** | Fixed AC | Variable DC | **Rectifier** |
| 2 | **DC ŌåÆ DC** | Fixed DC | Variable DC | **Chopper** |
| 3 | **DC ŌåÆ AC** | Fixed DC | Variable AC | **Inverter** |
| 4 | **AC ŌåÆ AC** | Fixed AC | Variable AC | **AC-AC Converter** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water-Pipe Faucet" Model

Imagine electricity flowing through wires exactly like water flowing through pipes:

- A **Power Electronic Switch** is like a **high-speed faucet valve** that can open or close thousands of times per second, but **never partially open** (this would waste energy as heat, just like a leaky valve).
- The **input** is a pipe with water at a certain pressure (AC or DC).
- The **output** is a different pipe delivering water at a different pressure or in pulses.

| Converter | Plumbing Analogy |
|-----------|------------------|
| **AC-DC Rectifier** | Converting ocean waves (AC tide) into a steady water tank (DC reservoir) using a one-way flap valve. |
| **DC-DC Chopper** | A pressure regulator that takes a fixed water pressure and pulses it to deliver a lower/higher average pressure to a tap. |
| **DC-AC Inverter** | A reciprocating pump that pushes water back-and-forth in a pipe, simulating AC flow from a DC source. |
| **AC-AC Controller** | A variable valve that controls how much AC water pressure reaches the consumer, without changing its wave nature. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The entire field of Power Electronics is essentially the art of "chopping" electrical waveforms into segments and reassembling them into a more useful shape. The student must internalize that **switching** is the fundamental action, not linear amplification.

### 1.3 The Four Building Blocks ŌĆö Expanded View

#### A. AC-DC Converter (Rectifier)
Converts alternating voltage/current into unidirectional (DC) voltage/current. 
- **Uncontrolled Rectifier** ŌĆö uses **Power Diodes** (output is fixed, depends only on input).
- **Controlled Rectifier** ŌĆö uses **Thyristors (SCR)** (output is variable by adjusting firing angle $\alpha$).
- Applications: SMPS adapters, DC motor drives, battery chargers, HVDC transmission.

#### B. DC-DC Converter (Chopper)
Converts a fixed DC input into a **variable DC output** by varying the **duty cycle ($D$)** of the chopper switch.
- **Step-Down (Buck)**, **Step-Up (Boost)**, **Step-Down/Up (Buck-Boost)**, **Cuk**, **SEPIC**, **Zeta**.
- Applications: Electric vehicle (EV) battery management, solar MPPT, laptop chargers, drone ESCs.

#### C. DC-AC Converter (Inverter)
Converts DC input into AC output of desired **frequency** and **magnitude**.
- **VSI** (Voltage Source Inverter) ŌĆö fed from a stiff DC voltage source.
- **CSI** (Current Source Inverter) ŌĆö fed from a stiff DC current source.
- Applications: Solar PV grid-tie systems, UPS, induction motor drives, induction heating.

#### D. AC-AC Converter
Converts an AC waveform directly into another AC waveform, possibly with changed **magnitude, frequency, or both**.
- **AC Voltage Controller (AVC)** ŌĆö same frequency, variable magnitude (uses TRIAC or anti-parallel SCRs).
- **Cycloconverter** ŌĆö variable frequency (typically lower than input) and variable magnitude.
- **Matrix Converter** ŌĆö direct AC-AC using bidirectional switches; no DC link.
- Applications: Lighting dimmers, fan regulators, large AC drives (cement mills, rolling mills), soft starters.

### 1.4 The Power Semiconductor Switch Family

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four converter classes are built using a finite set of power semiconductor devices. Every KTU 2024 question implicitly assumes the student knows these devices.

| Symbol | Device | Typical Rating | Key Property |
|--------|--------|---------------|--------------|
| **D** | Power Diode | 1 kV / 1 kA | Uncontrolled, uncontrolled turn-on/off |
| **SCR / T** | Thyristor | 6 kV / 4 kA | Controlled turn-on, uncontrolled turn-off |
| **TRIAC** | Bidirectional Triode Thyristor | 1 kV / 100 A | AC switch, like two SCRs in anti-parallel |
| **GTO** | Gate Turn-Off Thyristor | 4.5 kV / 4 kA | Controlled turn-on **and** turn-off |
| **BJT / IGBT / MOSFET** | Self-Commutated Switches | 1.7 kV / 1.2 kA (IGBT) | Full control via gate/base, used in choppers & inverters |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal "Switch + Reactor" Topology

Every power electronic converter ŌĆö whether it is a tiny phone charger or a **HVDC megawatt** link ŌĆö follows the same fundamental blueprint:

1. A **switching matrix** (combination of power switches) chops the input waveform.
2. A **low-pass filter** (combination of L and C, called a *reactor*) reconstructs the chopped waveform into a smooth output.
3. A **control circuit** decides *when* to switch (turn-on angle $\alpha$, turn-off angle $\beta$, or duty cycle $D$).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Filter is the Memory:** The inductor and capacitor in the output stage "remember" the voltage and current history. Without them, the output would be a jerky pulsed waveform. The student must remember that **filter design** dictates the **ripple** of the output.

### 2.2 AC-DC Converter (Rectifier) ŌĆö Operating Principle

A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with **R-load** is the entry-level circuit:

- During the **positive half-cycle** of the AC source $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the SCR is forward-biased but does not conduct until it receives a gate pulse at **$\omega t = \alpha$**.
- The SCR conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (the natural commutation point). At $\pi$, the current naturally falls to zero, and the SCR turns OFF.
- The **average output voltage** is controlled by $\alpha$ (the firing angle).
- A **full-wave bridge** doubles the conduction interval and halves the ripple.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** For a fully controlled bridge, the output is **positive** for $\alpha < 90┬░$ (rectifier mode) and **negative** for $90┬░ < \alpha < 180┬░$ (inverter mode ŌĆö feeding power back to the AC source). The boundary at $\alpha = 90┬░$ gives $V_{dc} = 0$.

### 2.3 DC-DC Converter (Chopper) ŌĆö Operating Principle

A chopper is a high-speed ON/OFF switch connecting a DC source to a load. The duty cycle is:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

where $T$ is the switching period. The average load voltage is:

$$V_{o,avg} = D \cdot V_s$$

- **Buck (Step-Down):** Switch in series with source. $V_o = D \cdot V_s$, so $V_o < V_s$.
- **Boost (Step-Up):** Switch is in parallel with load (L stores energy). $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$, so $V_o > V_s$.
- **Buck-Boost:** Output can be either higher or lower than input, with **inverted polarity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lossless Switching Assumption:** For all chopper derivations, KTU assumes **ideal switches** (zero voltage drop when ON, zero current when OFF, instantaneous switching). Real switches have a small **conduction loss** ($V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I$) and a **switching loss** ($\frac{1}{2} V_s I (t_{on}+t_{off}) f$).

### 2.4 DC-AC Converter (Inverter) ŌĆö Operating Principle

The single-phase full-bridge VSI is the workhorse:

- Four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) are arranged in an H-bridge across a DC source $V_s$.
- S1 and S2 conduct for **half a cycle** (positive output), S3 and S4 conduct for the other half (negative output).
- The output is a **square wave** of peak $V_s$ and fundamental frequency $f_o = \frac{1}{2T}$ where $T$ is the switching period.
- **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** modulates the pulse widths within each half-cycle to vary the **fundamental amplitude** while keeping $f_o$ constant.

### 2.5 AC-AC Converter ŌĆö Operating Principle

The **single-phase AC Voltage Controller (AVC)** uses two SCRs in anti-parallel (or a TRIAC) in series with the load:

- During each half-cycle, the SCR is fired at angle $\alpha$. It conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (or $180┬░ + \alpha$).
- The **RMS output voltage** is reduced as $\alpha$ increases (similar to a lamp dimmer).
- A **Cycloconverter** synthesizes a lower-frequency output by selecting appropriate segments of the input AC supply using positive and negative converter banks.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for solving any Module 1 KTU 2024 problem. Memorize every entry.

| Converter Type | Output Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Domain |
|----------------|-----------------|---------|-------------------|
| **Half-Wave Uncontrolled Rectifier (R-load)** | $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Half-Wave Uncontrolled Rectifier (R-load)** | $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (R-load)** | $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (R-load)** | $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier** | Ripple Frequency | $2f_s$ | $f_s$ = supply frequency |
| **Full-Wave Controlled Bridge Rectifier (R-load)** | $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| **Buck Chopper** | $V_o$ | $D \cdot V_s$ | $0 < D < 1$ |
| **Boost Chopper** | $V_o$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $0 < D < 1$ |
| **Buck-Boost Chopper** | $V_o$ | $-\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ | $0 < D < 1$ |
| **Chopper Duty Cycle** | $D$ | $\dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Single-Phase VSI ŌĆö Square Wave Output** | $V_{o,rms}$ (fundamental) | $\dfrac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2} \pi} = 0.9 V_s$ | Pure square wave |
| **Single-Phase VSI ŌĆö Square Wave Output** | $V_{o,rms,total}$ | $V_s$ | True RMS of square wave |
| **Single-Phase VSI** | THD | $\dfrac{\sqrt{\pi^2 - 8}}{\sqrt{8}} \approx 48.3\%$ | Square wave |
| **AC Voltage Controller (R-load)** | $V_{o,rms}$ | $V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| **AC Voltage Controller** | Power Factor | $\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | R-load |
| **Cycloconverter** | Output Frequency | $f_o < \dfrac{f_s}{3}$ (typical) | ŌĆö |
| **Rectification Efficiency (Full-Wave)** | $\eta$ | $\dfrac{81.2\%}{}$ (R-load) | Theoretical max |
| **Ripple Factor (Full-Wave)** | $\gamma$ | $0.482$ | R-load |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
