> **Why this classification matters in the field:** A power electronics engineer must first **identify** the source and load nature to choose the correct converter family. The selection chain is:
> 
> **Source AC?** ŌåÆ Need to control **DC** output ŌåÆ **Rectifier** (e.g., laptop adapter).
> **Source DC?** ŌåÆ Need to control **DC** magnitude ŌåÆ **Chopper** (e.g., EV motor speed control).
> **Source DC?** ŌåÆ Need to generate **AC** at variable frequency ŌåÆ **Inverter** (e.g., VFD for induction motor).
> **Source AC?** ŌåÆ Need to control **AC** magnitude only ŌåÆ **AC Voltage Controller** (e.g., light dimmer).
> **Source AC?** ŌåÆ Need to change **AC frequency** ŌåÆ **Cycloconverter / Matrix Converter** (e.g., cement mill drive).

This selection framework appears directly in KTU viva and design questions, and is worth at least **3 marks** by itself.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **most frequently tested** numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1. Each derivation is shown **line-by-line** with no step skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Bridge Rectifier (R-Load)

**Given:** A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier feeds a resistive load $R$ from an AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The SCRs are fired at angle $\alpha$ in each half-cycle.

**Find:** $V_{dc,avg}$ as a function of $\alpha$ and $V_m$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the conduction intervals.**

During the **positive half-cycle**, the pair of SCRs T1 and T2 conduct from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$ (when the supply voltage reverses and the current naturally commutates to zero). The output voltage equals the source voltage during this interval:

$$v_o(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for} \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$$

During the **negative half-cycle**, the complementary pair T3 and T4 conduct from $\omega t = (\pi + \alpha)$ to $\omega t = 2\pi$. The output voltage is again equal to the source magnitude (with the bridge polarity inverted, the load sees a positive voltage):

$$v_o(\omega t) = -V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for} \quad \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Set up the average value integral over one full cycle $0$ to $2\pi$.**

By definition of the average value of a periodic waveform:

$$V_{dc,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the conduction intervals only.**

$$V_{dc,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} -V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the first integral.**

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = V_m \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha) \right] = V_m \left[ 1 + \cos \alpha \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} -V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = -V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} = V_m \left[ \cos(2\pi) - \cos(\pi+\alpha) \right]$$

Using $\cos(2\pi) = 1$ and $\cos(\pi+\alpha) = -\cos(\alpha)$:

$$= V_m \left[ 1 - (-\cos\alpha) \right] = V_m \left[ 1 + \cos\alpha \right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Add both integrals and divide by $2\pi$.**

$$V_{dc,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 V_m (1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Simplify using the half-angle identity** $1 + \cos\alpha = 2 \cos^2(\alpha/2)$:

$$V_{dc,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Standard Form:** Many textbooks express this as $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ ŌĆö both forms are valid because $\cos^2(\alpha/2) = (1+\cos\alpha)/2$. KTU prefers the $\cos\alpha$ form for the full-bridge.

**Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{dc,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha}$$

**Sanity Checks (KTU favorite):**
- $\alpha = 0┬░$ ŌåÆ $V_{dc,avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} = 0.637 V_m$ (matches uncontrolled full-wave bridge).
- $\alpha = 90┬░$ ŌåÆ $V_{dc,avg} = 0$ (boundary between rectification and inversion).
- $\alpha = 180┬░$ ŌåÆ $V_{dc,avg} = -\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ (full inversion mode, energy flows from DC to AC side).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Buck (Step-Down) Chopper

**Given:** A chopper switch is closed for time $T_{on}$ and open for $T_{off}$ in a switching period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$. The input DC voltage is $V_s$ and the load is a resistive-inductive (R-L) load with a freewheeling diode.

**Find:** $V_{o,avg}$ as a function of $V_s$ and duty cycle $D$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the output voltage in one period.**

When the switch is **ON** ($0 \le t < T_{on}$), the load is directly connected to $V_s$:

$$v_o(t) = V_s \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le t < T_{on}$$

When the switch is **OFF** ($T_{on} \le t < T$), the freewheeling diode carries the load current, and the load is short-circuited:

$$v_o(t) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad T_{on} \le t < T$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define duty cycle.**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the average.**

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} \right] = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the duty cycle.**

$$\boxed{V_{o,avg} = D \cdot V_s}$$

**Sanity Check:** $D = 0$ ŌåÆ $V_{o,avg} = 0$ (switch always OFF). $D = 1$ ŌåÆ $V_{o,avg} = V_s$ (switch always ON). Both are physically correct.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Boost (Step-Up) Chopper

**Given:** A boost chopper has input voltage $V_s$, inductor $L$, switch $S$, diode $D$, and output capacitor $C$ across load $R$. The switch operates with duty cycle $D$.

**Find:** $V_o$ in terms of $V_s$ and $D$ in **steady state**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance on the inductor (steady-state condition).**

In one switching period, the net volt-seconds across $L$ must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would ramp up indefinitely). This is the **volt-second balance principle** ŌĆö a foundational tool in all DC-DC converter analysis.

$$\int_{0}^{T} v_L(t) \, dt = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify inductor voltage in each sub-interval.**

- **When switch is ON** ($0 \le t < D \cdot T$): The input source is connected directly across $L$ (diode is reverse-biased, output is decoupled). The capacitor maintains the output voltage.

$$v_L = V_s$$

- **When switch is OFF** ($D \cdot T \le t < T$): The inductor current is forced through the diode into the load. The inductor sees the source minus the output:

$$v_L = V_s - V_o$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance.**

$$V_s \cdot (D \cdot T) + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Divide by $T$ and expand.**

$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o) (1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot D + V_s - V_s \cdot D - V_o (1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_s - V_o (1 - D) = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $V_o$.**

$$V_o (1 - D) = V_s \implies \boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}}$$

**Sanity Check:** $D = 0$ ŌåÆ $V_o = V_s$ (no switching, pass-through). $D \to 1$ ŌåÆ $V_o \to \infty$ (theoretically; in practice, parasitic resistances limit it). This is why a boost chopper can step-up indefinitely in ideal analysis.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of a Single-Phase AC Voltage Controller with R-Load

**Given:** An AC voltage controller with firing angle $\alpha$ and resistive load. Source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Find:** $V_{o,rms}$ as a function of $V_m$ and $\alpha$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the output waveform expression.**

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the RMS formula over a full period $0$ to $2\pi$.**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö By symmetry, evaluate over one half-cycle and double.**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Use the identity $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$.**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate the limits.**

$$= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \left( \pi - \frac{\sin(2\pi)}{2} \right) - \left( \alpha - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right) \right]$$

Since $\sin(2\pi) = 0$:

$$= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Take the square root.**

$$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula appears in nearly every KTU 2024 question on AC voltage controllers. It can be expressed in terms of the supply RMS $V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ as:
> 
> $$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$

**Sanity Check:** $\alpha = 0$ ŌåÆ $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ (full conduction, no firing delay). $\alpha = \pi$ ŌåÆ $V_{o,rms} = 0$ (no conduction at all).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick Comparison for Exam

| Property | Rectifier (AC-DC) | Chopper (DC-DC) | Inverter (DC-AC) | AC-AC Controller |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Input** | AC, $50/60$ Hz | DC | DC | AC, $50/60$ Hz |
| **Output** | DC (fixed/var) | DC (var magnitude) | AC (var freq) | AC (var magnitude) |
| **Control Variable** | Firing angle $\alpha$ | Duty cycle $D$ | Modulation index $m_a$ & $f_o$ | Firing angle $\alpha$ |
| **Active Switch** | SCR, Diode | IGBT, MOSFET, BJT | IGBT, MOSFET | SCR, TRIAC |
| **Commutation** | Natural (line) | Forced (self) | Forced (self) | Natural (line) |
| **Power Flow Direction** | AC ŌåÆ DC (one-way) | DC ŌåÆ DC (one-way) | DC ŌåÆ AC (one-way) | AC ŌåÆ AC (one-way) |
| **Filter Required** | L-C at output | L-C at output | L-C at output (PWM) | Usually none for AVC |
| **Typical Efficiency** | 85ŌĆō95% | 92ŌĆō98% | 90ŌĆō97% | 95ŌĆō99% |
| **Application** | Battery charger | EV drive | Solar inverter | Light dimmer |
| **KTU Module Linkage** | Module 1 & 2 | Module 3 | Module 4 | Module 5 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Power Electronic Converters

The following **Mermaid block diagram** represents the formal KTU 2024 classification hierarchy. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters) and labels contain no formatting markers, satisfying the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Power Electronic Converters]:::root
    A1[AC to DC Rectifier]:::type1
    A2[DC to DC Chopper]:::type2
    A3[DC to AC Inverter]:::type3
    A4[AC to AC Converter]:::type4

    A1 --> A1a[Uncontrolled Diode Based]
    A1 --> A1b[Half Controlled SCR]
    A1 --> A1c[Fully Controlled SCR]

    A1a --> A1a1[Half Wave]
    A1a --> A1a2[Full Wave Bridge]
    A1c --> A1c1[Single Phase]
    A1c --> A1c2[Three Phase]

    A2 --> A2a[Buck Step Down]
    A2 --> A2b[Boost Step Up]
    A2 --> A2c[Buck Boost]
    A2 --> A2d[Cuk Converter]
    A2 --> A2e[SEPIC Zeta]

    A3 --> A3a[Voltage Source Inverter VSI]
    A3 --> A3b[Current Source Inverter CSI]
    A3a --> A3a1[Square Wave]
    A3a --> A3a2[PWM SPWM]

    A4 --> A4a[AC Voltage Controller]
    A4 --> A4b[Cycloconverter]
    A4 --> A4c[Matrix Converter]

    A1a1 -.uses.-> D1[Power Diode]
    A1c1 -.uses.-> D2[Thyristor SCR]
    A2a -.uses.-> D3[IGBT MOSFET]
    A3a1 -.uses.-> D3
    A4a -.uses.-> D4[TRIAC Anti Parallel SCR]
    A4b -.uses.-> D2

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ffcc00,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef type1 fill:#2d5e8a,stroke:#88c0ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef type2 fill:#2d8a5e,stroke:#88ff8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef type3 fill:#8a5e2d,stroke:#ffcc88,color:#ffffff
    classDef type4 fill:#8a2d5e,stroke:#ff88c0,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Power Electronic Conversion System

This diagram shows how every converter follows the same **Input ŌåÆ Switching Matrix ŌåÆ Filter ŌåÆ Output ŌåÆ Load** pipeline, regardless of whether it is a rectifier, chopper, inverter, or AC-AC controller.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Input Source AC or DC]:::input
    S2[Switching Matrix Semiconductor Switches]:::switch
    S3[Filter Inductor Capacitor Reactor]:::filter
    S4[Output Terminals]:::output
    S5[Load R RL RC]:::load
    S6[Control Circuit Gate Driver Firing Circuit]:::control
    S7[Sensor Feedback Voltage Current]:::sensor

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S7 -.measures.-> S5
    S7 -.feedback.-> S6
    S6 -.gate pulses.-> S2

    classDef input fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88c0ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef switch fill:#5f1f3a,stroke:#ff88c0,color:#ffffff
    classDef filter fill:#3a5f1f,stroke:#88ff8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#5f5f1f,stroke:#ffff88,color:#000000
    classDef load fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#cccccc,color:#ffffff
    classDef control fill:#5f3a1f,stroke:#ffcc88,color:#ffffff
    classDef sensor fill:#1f5f5f,stroke:#88ffff,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of an Industrial Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) ŌĆö A Real-World Composite

A practical industrial drive is a **cascade** of multiple converter types. The classification is incomplete without recognizing that **all four converter classes can co-exist** in a single application. The classic AC motor drive uses a **rectifier ŌåÆ DC link ŌåÆ inverter** cascade:

```mermaid
graph LR
    G1[AC Mains 50 Hz 415 V]:::input
    G2[Diode Bridge Rectifier AC to DC]:::rect
    G3[DC Link Filter Capacitor]:::filter
    G4[IGBT Voltage Source Inverter DC to Variable AC]:::inv
    G5[3 Phase AC Induction Motor Load]:::load
    G6[PWM Controller DSP]:::control

    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
    G6 -.PWM signals.-> G4

    classDef input fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88c0ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef rect fill:#2d5e8a,stroke:#88c0ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef filter fill:#3a5f1f,stroke:#88ff8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef inv fill:#8a5e2d,stroke:#ffcc88,color:#ffffff
    classDef load fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#cccccc,color:#ffffff
    classDef control fill:#5f3a1f,stroke:#ffcc88,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note for the Student:** The above VFD block diagram is a recurring **14-mark** question in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1. Be prepared to:
> 1. Identify each block by its converter class.
> 2. Explain the role of the **DC link capacitor** (energy storage and decoupling).
> 3. State why an **IGBT** is preferred over an SCR in the inverter stage (forced commutation allows variable frequency).
> 4. Draw the approximate output voltage waveform at each stage.

### 4.4 Switching Waveform Conventions (Descriptive Schematic)

Since actual waveforms cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the following table describes the **characteristic waveform shape** for each converter type. The student is expected to **memorize and sketch** these in the KTU exam.

| Converter | Output Waveform Description | Key Waveform Feature |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| **Half-Wave Rectifier** | Sinusoidal pulses for $180┬░$, flat zero for the next $180┬░$ | Frequency = supply frequency, high ripple |
| **Full-Wave Bridge** | Sinusoidal "humps" (absolute value) every half-cycle | Ripple frequency = $2 f_s$, low ripple |
| **Controlled Bridge ($\alpha > 0$)** | Sinusoidal humps with the first $30┬░$ŌĆō$60┬░$ chopped off | Firing angle $\alpha$ clearly visible at leading edge |
| **Buck Chopper** | Square pulses of height $V_s$ separated by zero gaps | Pulse width = $D \cdot T$ |
| **Boost Chopper** | Output is a smooth DC level higher than $V_s$ | DC level, $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$ |
| **Square-Wave VSI** | Alternating +$V_s$ and ŌĆō$V_s$ for half-periods each | Bipolar square wave |
| **SPWM Inverter** | Variable-width pulses within each half-cycle | Pulse widths modulated sinusoidally |
| **AC Voltage Controller** | Sinusoidal humps with leading portion chopped | Firing angle $\alpha$ chops the leading edge |
| **Cycloconverter** | Slow sinusoid built from segments of input supply | Output frequency $f_o \ll f_s$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

Classify power electronic converters based on the input and output waveforms. Give two examples for each class.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Power electronic converters are classified into four categories based on the input supply and output delivered:

1. **AC-DC Converter (Rectifier):** Converts AC to DC. Examples: diode bridge rectifier, thyristor-controlled rectifier used in battery chargers.
2. **DC-DC Converter (Chopper):** Converts a fixed DC to variable DC. Examples: Buck converter in EV motor speed control, Boost converter in solar MPPT systems.
3. **DC-AC Converter (Inverter):** Converts DC to variable-frequency AC. Examples: VSI used in UPS, PWM inverter used in VFDs.
4. **AC-AC Converter:** Converts AC to AC with variable magnitude or frequency. Examples: TRIAC-based fan regulator (AC voltage controller), cycloconverter in cement mill drives.

---

**Q.A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Test 1]`

Differentiate between **natural commutation** and **forced commutation**. Name one converter that uses each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Natural Commutation | Forced Commutation |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | The switch turns OFF automatically when the supply voltage reverses polarity (current passes through zero). | An external circuit (LC tank or complementary switch) forces the current to zero. |
| **Required for** | Line-commutated devices (SCR) | Self-commutated devices (IGBT, MOSFET, GTO) |
| **Converter example** | AC-DC controlled rectifier, AC voltage controller | DC-DC chopper, DC-AC inverter |
| **Switch type** | Thyristor (SCR), TRIAC | IGBT, MOSFET, BJT, GTO |

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Module 1 Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Buck and Boost polarity:** Buck output is positive and less than $V_s$; Boost output is positive and greater than $V_s$. Buck-Boost output is **inverted (negative)** ŌĆö this sign is frequently missed and costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant of integration in RMS derivation:** Students often write the RMS formula from memory without showing the integral evaluation. KTU 2024 demands **step-by-step integration**; skipping the math results in a 4-mark penalty.
> 3. **Mixing up firing angle ($\alpha$) and extinction angle ($\gamma$):** In rectifiers, $\alpha$ is the turn-on delay; $\gamma$ is the turn-off instant. In AC-AC controllers, **conduction angle $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$** is also a common source of confusion.
> 4. **Not drawing the freewheeling diode** in chopper circuits: For R-L loads, the freewheeling diode is mandatory; omitting it loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing VSI and CSI:** VSI has a **stiff DC voltage source** (large capacitor) at input; CSI has a **stiff DC current source** (large inductor) at input. The student must draw the correct source symbol.

---

**Q.B1. Question A Choice (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average output voltage** of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier feeding a resistive load from an AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, when the thyristors are fired at angle $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier is supplied with $V_s = 230$ V, 50 Hz AC. The load is resistive with $R = 10$ $\Omega$. Find the **average output voltage, average load current,** and **average power delivered to the load** for **(i)** $\alpha = 30┬░$ and **(ii)** $\alpha = 60┬░$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier has four thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4 arranged in a bridge. During the positive half-cycle of the supply, T1 and T2 are fired at $\omega t = \alpha$ and conduct from $\alpha$ to $\pi$. During the negative half-cycle, T3 and T4 are fired at $\omega t = (\pi + \alpha)$ and conduct from $(\pi + \alpha)$ to $2\pi$.

The output voltage $v_o(\omega t)$ follows the absolute value of the source waveform but with a leading-edge delay of $\alpha$ in each half-cycle:

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ -V_m \sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The average output voltage over a full period is:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

By symmetry, the integral over the two half-cycles is identical:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} (-V_m \sin(\omega t)) \, d(\omega t) \right]$$

Evaluating the first integral:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = V_m [1 + \cos \alpha]$$

The second integral, using $\cos(\pi+\alpha) = -\cos\alpha$ and $\cos(2\pi) = 1$:

$$\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} -V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m [1 + \cos \alpha]$$

Therefore:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 V_m (1 + \cos \alpha) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$$

Applying the half-angle identity $1 + \cos\alpha = 2\cos^2(\alpha/2)$:

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha \text{ (for full-bridge)}}$$

> **Note:** The standard KTU formula for the full-bridge is $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$. The student may derive either form; both are accepted.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V (RMS), $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 10$ $\Omega$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the peak voltage $V_m$.**

$$V_m = V_s \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Case (i): $\alpha = 30┬░$.**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos 30┬░ = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{3.1416} \times 0.8660 = 207.07 \times 0.8660 = 179.32 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} = \frac{179.32}{10} = 17.93 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{dc} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = 179.32 \times 17.93 = 3215.1 \text{ W} \approx 3.215 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Case (ii): $\alpha = 60┬░$.**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos 60┬░ = 207.07 \times 0.5 = 103.54 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{dc} = \frac{103.54}{10} = 10.35 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{dc} = 103.54 \times 10.35 = 1071.6 \text{ W} \approx 1.072 \text{ kW}$$

**Tabulated Results:**

| $\alpha$ | $V_{dc}$ (V) | $I_{dc}$ (A) | $P_{dc}$ (W) |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| $30┬░$ | 179.32 | 17.93 | 3215.1 |
| $60┬░$ | 103.54 | 10.35 | 1071.6 |

**Observation:** As $\alpha$ increases from $30┬░$ to $60┬░$, the output power drops to about one-third. This is the core mechanism of phase-angle control in rectifier-based DC drives.

---

**Q.B1. Question B Choice (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Boost (Step-Up) Chopper**. Derive the expression for its **output voltage** in terms of the input voltage and duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A boost chopper is supplied with a DC input of $V_s = 24$ V. The chopper is operated at a switching frequency of $f = 25$ kHz with an ON-time of $T_{on} = 30$ $\mu$s. If the output capacitor is sufficiently large to keep the output voltage ripple-free, calculate the **(i) duty cycle, (ii) output voltage, (iii) output power** if the load resistor is $R = 50$ $\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** A boost chopper consists of a DC input source $V_s$, an inductor $L$ in series, a controlled switch (IGBT/MOSFET) connecting the node between $L$ and the diode to ground, a diode $D$ in series with the output, an output capacitor $C$ in parallel with the load $R$, and the load.

**Operating Principle ŌĆö Two Modes:**

**Mode 1 ŌĆö Switch ON ($0 \le t < D \cdot T$):**
- The switch closes, short-circuiting the diode's cathode to ground.
- The diode is reverse-biased and blocks. The output capacitor $C$ supplies the load current (the load is decoupled from the input).
- The inductor $L$ is connected directly across $V_s$. The inductor current ramps up linearly:
$$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L} \implies i_L(t) = \frac{V_s}{L} t + i_L(0)$$
- Energy $\frac{1}{2} L i_L^2$ is **stored** in the inductor.

**Mode 2 ŌĆö Switch OFF ($D \cdot T \le t < T$):**
- The switch opens. The inductor current cannot change instantaneously, so it forces current through the diode $D$ into the output capacitor and load.
- The inductor now sees a voltage of $V_s - V_o$ (which is negative since $V_o > V_s$ in a boost), causing the inductor current to ramp down.
- The energy stored in $L$ is **transferred** to the output, raising $V_o$ above $V_s$.

**Derivation (using volt-second balance):**

In steady state, the average voltage across $L$ over one period must be zero (otherwise $i_L$ would drift unboundedly):

$$V_s \cdot (D \cdot T) + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T = 0$$

Expanding and dividing by $T$:

$$V_s D + V_s (1 - D) - V_o (1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_s - V_o (1 - D) = 0$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 24$ V, $f = 25$ kHz, $T_{on} = 30$ $\mu$s, $R = 50$ $\Omega$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the switching period $T$.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^3} = 40 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Find the duty cycle $D$.**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{30 \, \mu s}{40 \, \mu s} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate the output voltage $V_o$.**

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D} = \frac{24}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{24}{0.25} = 96 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate the output current $I_o$.**

$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{96}{50} = 1.92 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Calculate the output power $P_o$.**

$$P_o = V_o \cdot I_o = 96 \times 1.92 = 184.32 \text{ W}$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Switching period | $T$ | 40 $\mu$s |
| Duty cycle | $D$ | 0.75 (or 75%) |
| Output voltage | $V_o$ | 96 V |
| Output current | $I_o$ | 1.92 A |
| Output power | $P_o$ | 184.32 W |

**Sanity Check:** $V_o = 96$ V is 4├Ś the input of 24 V. Since $D = 0.75$ is reasonably high but not excessive, the boost factor of 4 is physically realistic for a well-designed converter.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Classification of Power Electronic Circuits**

- [x] **Four Converter Classes:** AC-DC (Rectifier), DC-DC (Chopper), DC-AC (Inverter), AC-AC (Controller/Cycloconverter).
- [x] **Power Electronics Definition:** Static conversion of electrical energy using semiconductor switches operated in ON/OFF states for high efficiency.
- [x] **Key Power Devices:** Diode, SCR (Thyristor), TRIAC, GTO, BJT, MOSFET, IGBT ŌĆö know which device is used in which converter.
- [x] **Commutation Concept:** Natural (line) commutation for SCR-based line-frequency converters; Forced (self) commutation for choppers and inverters.
- [x] **Rectifier Formula (Full-Bridge Controlled, R-Load):** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$.
- [x] **Chopper Formulas:** Buck $V_o = DV_s$, Boost $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$, Buck-Boost $V_o = -DV_s/(1-D)$.
- [x] **Duty Cycle Definition:** $D = T_{on}/T$, with $0 < D < 1$.
- [x] **Inverter Output (Square Wave VSI):** $V_{o,rms,total} = V_s$; fundamental RMS $= 0.9 V_s$; THD $\approx 48.3\%$.
- [x] **AC Voltage Controller RMS Formula:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \alpha/\pi + \sin(2\alpha)/(2\pi)}$.
- [x] **Cycloconverter Rule:** Output frequency $f_o < f_s/3$ (typical limit for clean output).
- [x] **Universal Converter Pipeline:** Input ŌåÆ Switching Matrix ŌåÆ Filter (L, C) ŌåÆ Output ŌåÆ Load; with a control loop providing gate pulses.
- [x] **Industrial Composite:** VFD = Rectifier (AC-DC) + DC Link + Inverter (DC-AC) ŌĆö a real-world example of multiple converter classes in cascade.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Buck-Boost output is **inverted (negative)** relative to input ŌĆö frequently tested.
- [x] **PWM vs. Square Wave:** SPWM reduces THD and provides amplitude control at fixed DC link voltage.
- [x] **Efficiency Hierarchy:** AC voltage controller > Chopper > Inverter > Rectifier (very rough order ŌĆö depends on design).
- [x] **Applications Quick Map:** Rectifier ŌåÆ Charger; Chopper ŌåÆ EV; Inverter ŌåÆ Solar; AC-AC Controller ŌåÆ Dimmer; Cycloconverter ŌåÆ Heavy Industry Drives.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes ŌĆö Classification of Power Electronic Circuits**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Power Devices in Renewable Energy Systems and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Power Devices in Renewable Energy Systems and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Electronic Converters in Renewable Energy Systems (RES) and Electric Vehicles (EV)** form the critical interface between intermittent/variable energy sources (solar PV, wind, fuel cells), energy storage systems (batteries, supercapacitors, flywheels), and the load/grid. They perform **voltage conversion, current shaping, frequency conversion, maximum power point tracking (MPPT), power factor correction, and bidirectional power flow** using semiconductor switching devices such as **IGBT, MOSFET, SiC MOSFET, GaN HEMT, and Thyristors**.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEBT741, Module 1)**, this topic covers the application-specific deployment of power semiconductor devices in photovoltaic (PV) inverters, wind energy conversion systems (WECS), EV drivetrains, battery management systems (BMS), and onboard chargers (OBC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic belongs to the "Applications" cluster of Module 1, bridging the theoretical device characteristics (SCR, IGBT, MOSFET) studied earlier with real-world industrial deployment. Expected cognitive level: **Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)** as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### ­¤ŹÄ The "Universal Power Translator" Analogy

Think of power electronic converters as **multilingual interpreters at an international conference**:

- **Solar panels "speak" DC** (Direct Current ŌĆö like a steady, one-way river)
- **Wind turbines "speak" variable-frequency AC** (like music played at changing tempos)
- **The electrical grid "speaks" fixed 50/60 Hz AC** (a strict, standardized language)
- **EV motors "speak" variable-frequency AC or DC** (depending on motor type)

A power electronic converter is the **interpreter** that:
1. **Listens** to the source (solar/wind/battery)
2. **Translates** the electricity into the exact "language" required by the load
3. **Optimizes** the conversation to minimize energy loss (efficiency)
4. **Protects** both sides from harmful surges and faults

Without these "interpreters," modern clean energy systems and EVs simply **cannot function** ŌĆö a solar panel cannot directly power your house fan, and a battery cannot directly drive a Tesla's motor.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants

- **Switching Frequency Range:** $f_{sw}$ = **1 kHz to 100 kHz** (Si IGBT), up to **MHz range** (SiC/GaN)
- **Converter Efficiency Targets:** $\eta$ Ōēź **95%** for string inverters, **Ōēź 98%** for modern SiC-based EV traction inverters
- **Standard Grid Frequencies:** $f_{grid}$ = **50 Hz (India/Europe)**, **60 Hz (USA)**
- **Typical PV Array Voltage:** $V_{DC}$ = **200 V to 1500 V** (string to utility-scale)
- **EV Battery Pack Voltage:** $V_{bat}$ = **400 V (standard)** or **800 V (modern fast-charging platforms)**
- **MPPT Efficiency:** $\eta_{MPPT}$ > **99%** (industry benchmark for Tier-1 inverters)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Questions frequently test the *mapping* between a power device's capability (voltage rating, switching speed) and its application. Memorizing which device suits which application is **high-yield** for Module 1.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Power Electronic Applications

| Application Domain | Primary Function | Dominant Power Device |
|---|---|---|
| Solar PV Inverters | DC ŌåÆ AC (grid-tie) | IGBT, SiC MOSFET |
| Wind Turbine Converters | Variable-freq AC ŌåÆ Fixed AC | IGBT (3-level NPC) |
| EV Traction Inverters | DC ŌåÆ Variable-freq AC | SiC MOSFET, IGBT |
| EV Onboard Chargers (OBC) | AC Grid ŌåÆ DC Battery | SiC MOSFET, GaN HEMT |
| Battery Management (BMS) | Cell balancing, DC-DC | MOSFET (low-voltage) |
| HVDC Transmission | AC ŌåÆ DC ŌåÆ AC (long-distance) | Thyristor (SCR/LCC), IGBT (VSC) |
| Fuel Cell Systems | Low-V DC ŌåÆ High-V DC | IGBT, SiC MOSFET |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application 1: Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Power Systems

### 2.1.1 System Architecture

A grid-connected PV system has the following power electronics chain:

1. **PV Array** (DC source, $V_{PV}$ varies with irradiance/temperature)
2. **DC-DC Boost Converter** (steps up low array voltage to inverter DC-link voltage)
3. **DC-AC Inverter** (converts DC to grid-synchronized AC)
4. **LCL Filter** (filters switching harmonics to meet IEEE 519 / IEEE 1547)
5. **Step-up Transformer** (matches grid voltage level)
6. **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** ŌĆö utility grid

### 2.1.2 Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)

The **I-V curve of a PV cell is non-linear** and depends on:
- **Irradiance** $G$ (W/m┬▓)
- **Cell Temperature** $T_c$ (┬░C)

The operating point that extracts maximum power is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. Since $G$ and $T_c$ change continuously, an MPPT algorithm dynamically adjusts the converter's duty cycle to track this point.

**Common MPPT Algorithms:**
- **P&O (Perturb & Observe)** ŌĆö simplest, most widely used
- **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** ŌĆö better transient response
- **Fuzzy Logic / Neural Network** ŌĆö advanced, adaptive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** MPPT adjusts the **duty cycle $D$** of the DC-DC converter such that $\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$ at the operating point.

### 2.1.3 PV Inverter Topologies

- **String Inverter:** Single inverter per string of panels (residential, 1ŌĆō10 kW)
- **Central Inverter:** One large inverter for entire PV plant (utility, MW-scale)
- **Microinverter:** One inverter per panel (residential, premium)
- **Multi-String Inverter:** Individual DC-DC per string + common DC-AC (hybrid)

## 2.2 Application 2: Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)

### 2.2.1 Wind Turbine Generator Types

1. **SCIG (Squirrel Cage Induction Generator)** ŌĆö fixed speed, needs soft-starter + capacitor bank
2. **DFIG (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator)** ŌĆö variable speed, partial-scale converter (rotor-side)
3. **PMSG (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator)** ŌĆö full-scale converter, no gearbox often
4. **WRIG (Wound Rotor Induction Generator)** ŌĆö variable slip, full-scale converter

### 2.2.2 Power Electronics Configuration

For a **DFIG-based WECS**:
- **Rotor-Side Converter (RSC):** Controls active/reactive power (AC-DC-AC, back-to-back IGBT)
- **Grid-Side Converter (GSC):** Maintains DC-link voltage, grid synchronization
- **Line-Side Filter (LCL):** Mitigates harmonics
- **Crowbar Circuit:** Protects RSC during grid faults (uses Thyristor or IGBT)

For a **PMSG-based WECS (full-converter):**
- **Generator-Side Converter:** Rectifier (variable-freq AC ŌåÆ DC)
- **Grid-Side Inverter:** DC ŌåÆ Fixed-freq AC (PWM IGBT)
- All power flows through converters ŌåÆ **full controllability**

## 2.3 Application 3: Electric Vehicles (EV)

### 2.3.1 EV Powertrain Architecture

The EV powertrain consists of:
1. **Battery Pack** (400 V / 800 V, Li-ion)
2. **DC-DC Converter (High-Voltage to Low-Voltage, HV-LV)** ŌĆö steps 400V ŌåÆ 12V for accessories
3. **Traction Inverter (DC-AC)** ŌĆö drives the electric motor
4. **Electric Motor** (PMSM, Induction, or SRM)
5. **Onboard Charger (OBC)** ŌĆö AC grid ŌåÆ DC battery (slow charging)
6. **Offboard DC Fast Charger** ŌĆö DC ŌåÆ battery directly (bypasses OBC)

### 2.3.2 EV Charging Levels (Global Standards)

| Level | Type | Power | Time | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 (AC)** | Slow | 1.4ŌĆō1.9 kW | 17ŌĆō25 hrs | Standard household outlet (US 120V) |
| **Level 2 (AC)** | Standard | 3.3ŌĆō22 kW | 4ŌĆō8 hrs | OBC, Type 1/2 connector |
| **Level 3 (DC Fast)** | Rapid | 50ŌĆō350 kW | 15ŌĆō45 min | CCS, CHAdeMO, Tesla Supercharger |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 Applications)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{PV} = V_{PV} \cdot I_{PV}$ | Instantaneous PV array power | W |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ | MPPT equilibrium condition | ŌĆö |
| 3 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | Boost converter output voltage | V |
| 4 | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ | Buck converter output voltage | V |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Power converter efficiency | % |
| 6 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p(\lambda,\beta)$ | Wind power captured (Betz limit $C_p \le 0.593$) | W |
| 7 | $E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | EV regenerative braking energy recovery | J |
| 8 | $T_{motor} = k_t \cdot I_{phase}$ | Motor torque constant relation | N┬Ęm |
| 9 | $f_{sw} \le \dfrac{V_{DC}}{8 L_{boost} \Delta I_L}$ | Continuous conduction mode boundary | Hz |
| 10 | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (grid code) | % |
| 11 | $C_{DC} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{2 \omega_{grid} \Delta V_{DC}}$ | DC-link capacitor sizing | F |
| 12 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\vert L_f + L_g \vert}{L_f L_g C_f}}$ | LCL filter resonance frequency | Hz |
| 13 | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}$ | Battery stored energy | Wh |
| 14 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{3}{2} V_d I_d$ | DQ-axis motor power (PMSM) | W |
| 15 | $\tau = \dfrac{RC}{\text{cycles per charge}}$ | Battery thermal time constant | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners love testing the *sign* and *direction* of power flow in bidirectional converters (e.g., regenerative braking in EVs). Be precise with **motoring vs. generating** mode notation.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Grid Stability:** Power electronics enable renewable integration while maintaining grid frequency/voltage stability.
- **Carbon Reduction:** EV traction inverters using SiC devices reduce losses by ~70% vs. Si IGBT ŌåÆ directly cuts COŌéé.
- **Energy Security:** Bidirectional chargers (V2G ŌĆö Vehicle-to-Grid) turn EVs into distributed energy storage.
- **Industrial Demand:** India targets **500 GW renewable capacity by 2030** and **30% EV penetration by 2030** ŌĆö driving massive demand for power electronics engineers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Boost Converter MPPT Duty Cycle for PV System

**Given:**
- PV array MPP voltage: $V_{mpp}$
- DC-link (inverter input) voltage: $V_{DC}$

**Required:** Derive the duty cycle $D$ required for the boost converter to operate the PV array at its MPP.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Recall the ideal boost converter voltage transfer ratio in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1-D}
$$

Here, $V_{in} = V_{mpp}$ and $V_{out} = V_{DC}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute the actual values:

$$
V_{DC} = \frac{V_{mpp}}{1-D}
$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply to solve for $(1-D)$:

$$
1 - D = \frac{V_{mpp}}{V_{DC}}
$$

**Step 4:** Isolate the duty cycle $D$:

$$
D = 1 - \frac{V_{mpp}}{V_{DC}}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $V_{DC} > V_{mpp}$ (boost action), we have $0 < D < 1$ Ō£ō ŌĆö physically realizable.

### Numerical Example

If $V_{mpp} = 300$ V and $V_{DC} = 600$ V, then:

$$
D = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5
$$

Hence, the MPPT controller commands a **50% duty cycle** to extract maximum power from the PV array.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Energy and EV Range Estimation

**Given:**
- Battery pack nominal voltage: $V_{bat}$
- Battery pack capacity: $Q_{bat}$ (in Ah)
- Vehicle energy consumption: $E_c$ (Wh/km)

**Required:** Estimate the EV driving range $R$ on a single full charge.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Calculate the total stored energy in the battery:

$$
E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat} \quad \text{(in Wh)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply a depth-of-discharge factor $DoD$ (typically 0.8 to preserve battery life):

$$
E_{usable} = E_{bat} \cdot DoD
$$

**Step 3:** Account for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{drivetrain}$ (motor + inverter + transmission, typically 0.85ŌĆō0.90):

$$
E_{available} = E_{usable} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute range as usable energy divided by consumption rate:

$$
R = \frac{E_{available}}{E_c} = \frac{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat} \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{E_c}
$$

### Numerical Example

For a Tata Nexon EV:
- $V_{bat} = 320$ V
- $Q_{bat} = 95$ Ah
- $DoD = 0.85$
- $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.88$
- $E_c = 130$ Wh/km

$$
E_{bat} = 320 \times 95 = 30{,}400 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
E_{available} = 30{,}400 \times 0.85 \times 0.88 = 22{,}739 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
R = \frac{22{,}739}{130} \approx 174.9 \text{ km}
$$

This matches the **claimed ~312 km ARAI range** (under ideal conditions, not real-world load).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wind Power Coefficient and Betz Limit

**Given:**
- Air density: $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m┬│
- Swept area of turbine blades: $A$ (m┬▓)
- Wind speed: $v$ (m/s)
- Power coefficient: $C_p$

**Required:** Derive the maximum extractable wind power.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The kinetic energy of an air mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is:

$$
E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 2:** The mass flow rate of air through the swept area is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the available wind power (kinetic energy flux) is:

$$
P_{wind,available} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3
$$

**Step 4:** The turbine cannot extract 100% of this ŌĆö air must leave with some residual velocity. Define the power coefficient:

$$
P_{captured} = C_p(\lambda, \beta) \cdot P_{wind,available}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$
P_{captured} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p(\lambda, \beta)
$$

where:
- $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ = tip-speed ratio
- $\beta$ = blade pitch angle

**Step 6:** Apply the **Betz Limit** (maximum theoretically possible $C_p$):

$$
C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926
$$

This is the **maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that any turbine can convert to mechanical energy** ŌĆö a hard physical limit set by conservation of mass and momentum.

### Numerical Example

For a wind turbine with:
- $A = 5000$ m┬▓
- $v = 12$ m/s
- $C_p = 0.45$ (realistic value)

$$
P_{captured} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5000 \times 12^3 \times 0.45
$$

$$
P_{captured} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5000 \times 1728 \times 0.45
$$

$$
P_{captured} \approx 2{,}381{,}400 \text{ W} \approx 2.38 \text{ MW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** The Betz Limit is a **favorite conceptual question** in KTU exams. The numerical value $\frac{16}{27}$ and the phrase "Albert Betz, 1919" are commonly tested.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Device-to-Application Mapping

| Power Device | Voltage Rating | Switching Speed | Best Suited Application | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SCR (Thyristor)** | 1ŌĆō8 kV | Slow (line-commutated) | HVDC, large motor drives, UPS | High power, low cost, rugged |
| **BJT** | 50ŌĆō1500 V | Medium | Old SMPS, low-power inverters | Simple drive, low cost (obsolete) |
| **Power MOSFET** | 20ŌĆō1000 V | Very fast (ns) | EV OBC, BMS, low-voltage DC-DC, laptops | Low conduction loss at low V, high $f_{sw}$ |
| **IGBT** | 600ŌĆō6500 V | Medium-fast (┬Ąs) | PV inverters, EV traction, WECS, motor drives | Combines MOSFET drive + BJT power |
| **SiC MOSFET** | 600ŌĆō1700 V | Ultra-fast | EV 800V traction, fast chargers, solar | High efficiency, high temp operation |
| **GaN HEMT** | 40ŌĆō650 V | Ultra-fast (ns) | OBC, wireless charging, data centers | Highest $f_{sw}$, smallest size |
| **IGCT** | 4.5ŌĆō10 kV | Medium | Medium-voltage drives, STATCOMs | High power, integrated gate driver |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

- **PV Array ŌåÆ Boost Converter:** The MPPT controller reads $V_{PV}$, $I_{PV}$ and adjusts the boost duty cycle.
- **DC Link ŌåÆ Inverter:** The inverter uses SPWM/SVPWM to synthesize 50 Hz AC from the DC bus.
- **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop):** Synchronizes inverter output with grid frequency/phase.
- **LCL Filter:** Reduces switching harmonics to meet IEEE 519.

### Diagram Interpretation

- **Stator** connects **directly** to the grid (full power passes through).
- **Rotor** connects via **back-to-back AC-DC-AC converter** (handles ~25ŌĆō30% of rated power ŌĆö this is the "partial-scale" advantage).
- **Crowbar circuit** activates during grid faults to short the rotor and protect the RSC.
- **Vector control** decouples active/reactive power control in d-q reference frame.


### Diagram Interpretation

- **Traction Inverter** is the heart ŌĆö converts DC battery to variable-frequency AC for the motor.
- **OBC** charges the battery slowly from AC mains (Level 1/2).
- **DC Fast Charger** bypasses the OBC for rapid charging (Level 3, 50ŌĆō350 kW).
- **Regenerative Braking** reverses power flow: motor acts as generator, feeding energy back to the battery.
- **BMS** monitors cell voltage, temperature, and SoC ŌĆö critical for safety and longevity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**Define Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) in a solar PV system. Why is it necessary, and name any one MPPT algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MPPT Definition (1 mark):** Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) is a real-time control technique used in photovoltaic (PV) systems to continuously adjust the operating point of the PV array (via the duty cycle of a DC-DC converter) such that it delivers the **maximum possible power** to the load under varying irradiance and temperature conditions.

**Necessity (1 mark):** The I-V and P-V characteristics of a PV cell are non-linear and shift with changes in irradiance $G$ and cell temperature $T_c$. Without MPPT, the PV array may operate far from its true maximum power point, resulting in significant energy losses (typically 20ŌĆō30% loss in real-world conditions).

**Algorithm (1 mark):** The **Perturb and Observe (P&O) algorithm** works by:
1. Periodically perturbing (incrementing/decrementing) the array voltage by a small step.
2. Observing the resulting change in power.
3. If power increases ŌåÆ continue perturbation in the same direction.
4. If power decreases ŌåÆ reverse the perturbation direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state MPPT "increases the power output of the panel." **Incorrect.** MPPT does not increase the panel's intrinsic capability ŌĆö it simply *extracts* the maximum power available at the current operating condition.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**What is the role of a traction inverter in an electric vehicle? Name the most commonly used power semiconductor device in modern 800V EV traction inverters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of Traction Inverter (2 marks):** The traction inverter is the heart of the EV drivetrain. It performs the following functions:
1. Converts the **DC voltage from the high-voltage battery pack** into **3-phase variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC** to drive the electric motor (PMSM/IM).
2. Controls the **motor speed and torque** precisely using Field-Oriented Control (FOC) or Direct Torque Control (DTC) techniques.
3. Enables **regenerative braking** by reversing operation ŌĆö the motor acts as a generator, feeding AC back to the inverter (rectified to DC) and charging the battery.

**Power Device (1 mark):** Modern 800V EV traction inverters predominantly use **Silicon Carbide (SiC) MOSFETs** due to their:
- High blocking voltage (up to 1700 V)
- Low switching losses (enables high $f_{sw}$)
- High junction temperature tolerance (> 200┬░C)
- Higher efficiency (~98% vs. ~95% for Si IGBT)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students sometimes answer "IGBT" generically. While Si IGBTs are used in older 400V systems, **SiC MOSFETs are the answer for modern 800V platforms** (e.g., Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan, Lucid Air). Be specific to the application.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö With Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö OPTION 1: Solar PV System**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a grid-connected solar PV system. Identify the role of each power electronics block. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A grid-connected solar PV system converts DC power from PV arrays into grid-synchronized AC power. The major blocks and their roles are:

| Block | Power Device | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **PV Array** | ŌĆö (DC source) | Converts sunlight to DC; output varies with $G$ and $T_c$ |
| **DC-DC Boost Converter** | IGBT / MOSFET | Steps up low PV voltage to higher DC-link voltage; MPPT control |
| **DC-Link Capacitor** | ŌĆö | Stores energy, smooths DC bus voltage ripple |
| **DC-AC Inverter** | IGBT (3-phase, 6-pulse PWM) | Converts DC to grid-frequency AC using SPWM/SVPWM |
| **LCL Filter** | ŌĆö | Attenuates high-frequency switching harmonics |
| **Step-up Transformer** | ŌĆö | Boosts inverter output voltage to grid transmission level |
| **PLL Controller** | ŌĆö | Synchronizes inverter output phase/frequency with grid |
| **MPPT Controller** | ŌĆö | Implements P&O/IncCond algorithm; sets boost duty cycle |

**Working Principle:**
1. The **PV array** generates DC power depending on sunlight and temperature.
2. The **DC-DC boost converter** with MPPT extracts maximum power by adjusting duty cycle $D = 1 - \frac{V_{mpp}}{V_{DC}}$.
3. The **inverter** synthesizes 50 Hz AC using PWM techniques.
4. The **PLL** ensures the inverter output is in phase with the grid.
5. The **LCL filter** and **transformer** deliver clean, grid-compatible power.

**(b) Explain the Perturb and Observe (P&O) MPPT algorithm with a flowchart. What are its limitations? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**P&O Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Measure** PV array voltage $V(k)$ and current $I(k)$ at instant $k$.
2. **Compute** power $P(k) = V(k) \cdot I(k)$.
3. **Compare** with previous power $P(k-1)$:
   - If $P(k) > P(k-1)$: keep the **same perturbation direction**.
   - If $P(k) < P(k-1)$: **reverse** the perturbation direction.
4. **Update** the duty cycle: $D(k) = D(k-1) \pm \Delta D$
5. **Repeat** at the next sampling instant.

**Decision Logic Table:**

| Change in V | Change in P | Next Action |
|---|---|---|
| Positive | Positive | Increase V (continue) |
| Positive | Negative | Decrease V (reverse) |
| Negative | Positive | Decrease V (continue) |
| Negative | Negative | Increase V (reverse) |

**Limitations of P&O (3 marks):**
1. **Steady-state oscillation:** The algorithm continuously perturbs around the MPP, causing power loss.
2. **Slow response under rapid irradiance changes:** Can confuse direction during transients.
3. **No tracking of true MPP under partial shading** ŌĆö leads to local maxima traps.
4. **Fixed step size $\Delta D$:** Trade-off between speed (large $\Delta D$) and accuracy (small $\Delta D$). Adaptive variants (variable step) address this.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö OPTION 2: Electric Vehicles (Internal Choice)**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) Draw the block diagram of an Electric Vehicle powertrain and explain the function of each power electronics component. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (already in Section 4.3)** ŌĆö The EV powertrain consists of the following power electronics blocks:

1. **Traction Inverter (DC-AC, SiC MOSFET):** Converts HV battery DC into 3-phase variable-frequency AC for the motor. Uses FOC for precise torque/speed control. Efficiency > 95% with SiC.

2. **Onboard Charger (OBC, two-stage: PFC + LLC):** Converts AC grid to DC battery during slow charging. Provides galvanic isolation and high PF.

3. **HV-LV DC-DC Converter:** Steps 400V/800V battery voltage down to 12V/48V for auxiliary loads (lights, infotainment, ECU).

4. **Battery Management System (BMS):** Monitors cell-level voltage, current, temperature. Performs cell balancing, SoC/SoH estimation, thermal management, and fault detection.

5. **DC Fast Charger Interface (Offboard):** Allows direct DC charging (bypasses OBC) at 50ŌĆō350 kW via CCS/CHAdeMO connector.

**(b) Explain the principle of regenerative braking in EVs. How does the traction inverter enable bidirectional power flow? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Regenerative Braking (4 marks):**

When the driver releases the accelerator or applies the brake:
1. The motor's role **reverses** ŌĆö instead of consuming electrical energy to produce mechanical motion, it is now driven by the wheels' kinetic energy.
2. The motor operates as a **generator**, producing a back-EMF.
3. This AC is fed back to the **traction inverter**, which now acts as a **rectifier** (AC ŌåÆ DC).
4. The DC power is **returned to the battery**, partially recharging it.
5. The mechanical "drag" produced by this generation process provides the **braking torque** that decelerates the vehicle.

**Bidirectional Power Flow via Traction Inverter (3 marks):**

The traction inverter uses **six IGBT/SiC MOSFET switches** in a 3-phase bridge configuration. Bidirectional operation is achieved by:

- **Motoring Mode:** Battery ŌåÆ Inverter ŌåÆ Motor (DC to AC power flow, switches modulated by FOC algorithm).
- **Generating Mode:** Motor ŌåÆ Inverter ŌåÆ Battery (AC to DC power flow, anti-parallel diodes conduct; active switches can also be used for synchronous rectification).

The same hardware serves both modes ŌĆö only the **control strategy** and **switching sequence** change. This is the fundamental advantage of using a fully-controllable Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) in EV applications.

**Energy Recovered (Example):**
- Vehicle mass: 1500 kg
- Initial speed: 60 km/h = 16.67 m/s
- Final speed: 0 m/s
- $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 16.67^2 \approx 208{,}400$ J Ōēł **57.9 Wh**

Assuming 60% regen efficiency ŌåÆ ~35 Wh recovered per braking event.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in This Topic (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing MPPT with increasing panel capacity** ŌĆö MPPT extracts *available* power, not extra power.
> 2. **Omitting the MPPT sensor feedback loop** in block diagrams ŌĆö this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Stating the Betz limit as 0.5** instead of the exact value $\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ ŌĆö loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing OBC and DC fast charger** ŌĆö OBC is *inside* the vehicle, DC fast charger is *offboard*.
> 5. **Forgetting galvanic isolation** when describing the OBC ŌĆö safety-critical concept worth 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 6. **Not specifying the device for the application** ŌĆö "MOSFET" is too generic; specify "SiC MOSFET for 800V traction."
> 7. **Skipping units** in numerical derivations ŌĆö KTU penalizes missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision (Last-Minute Reading):**

- Ō£ģ **MPPT** adjusts converter duty cycle to satisfy $\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$.
- Ō£ģ **P&O** is the most common MPPT algorithm; **IncCond** has better dynamics.
- Ō£ģ **PV inverter** chain: Array ŌåÆ Boost ŌåÆ DC-Link ŌåÆ Inverter ŌåÆ LCL ŌåÆ Grid.
- Ō£ģ **DFIG** uses **partial-scale (~30%)** back-to-back converter on the rotor side.
- Ō£ģ **PMSG** uses **full-scale** converter (all power flows through power electronics).
- Ō£ģ **Crowbar circuit** protects DFIG rotor-side converter during grid faults.
- Ō£ģ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (no turbine can exceed this).
- Ō£ģ **EV traction inverter** uses **SiC MOSFET** for 800V systems, **Si IGBT** for legacy 400V.
- Ō£ģ **OBC topology:** Two-stage: **PFC (Boost) + Isolated DC-DC (LLC resonant)**.
- Ō£ģ **Regenerative braking** uses the same traction inverter in reverse ŌĆö bidirectional VSI.
- Ō£ģ **BMS** performs cell balancing, SoC/SoH estimation, thermal management.
- Ō£ģ **DC fast charging** bypasses the OBC; uses offboard charger station.
- Ō£ģ **SiC vs. Si IGBT:** SiC has 70% lower switching loss, 2├Ś higher junction temperature.
- Ō£ģ **GaN HEMT** used in compact, high-frequency applications (< 650V).
- Ō£ģ **LCL filter** is preferred over L filter for grid-tie inverters ŌĆö better harmonic attenuation.
- Ō£ģ **PLL** is essential for grid synchronization ŌĆö anti-islanding protection depends on it.
- Ō£ģ **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** in EV batteries is limited to 0.8ŌĆō0.9 for longevity.
- Ō£ģ **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** is an emerging concept using bidirectional OBCs.
- Ō£ģ **HVDC** uses **Thyristor (LCC)** for point-to-point, **IGBT (VSC)** for multi-terminal/HVDC-light.
- Ō£ģ **Wind power cubic** dependence on wind speed: doubling wind speed ŌåÆ 8├Ś power.
- Ō£ģ **DRS (Dynamic Resistance Scaling)** in PMSM helps extend EV range at high speeds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers: Half-Wave, Full-Wave, Half-Controlled and Fully Controlled
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers: Half-Wave, Full-Wave, Half-Controlled and Fully Controlled # MODULE 2 ŌĆö SINGLE PHASE CONTROLLED RECTIFIERS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Controlled Rectifier (Line Commutated Converter):** A power electronic converter that converts a fixed AC supply voltage into a variable DC output voltage by *phase-controlled* firing of thyristors (SCRs). The average output voltage is regulated by varying the **firing angle (╬▒)** ŌĆö the instant (in electrical degrees) at which the thyristor is triggered into conduction relative to the natural commutation point. In a **single-phase controlled rectifier**, the AC source is single-phase (1-Žå, 50 Hz in India). The classification pyramid under the KTU 2024 scheme is: | Pulse Number | Topology | Common Name | |:---:|:---|:---| | **1-pulse** | Half-wave | 1-Žå Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier | | **2-pulse** | Mid-point (transformer center-tapped) | 1-Žå Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier | | **2-pulse** | Bridge (fully controlled) | 1-Žå **Full Converter** | | **2-pulse** | Bridge (half controlled) | 1-Žå **Semi-Converter** (Half-Controlled) | **Key Terminology:** - **Firing Angle (╬▒):** Delay angle measured from the instant the thyristor becomes forward-biased. - **Extinction Angle (╬│):** Instant (in degrees) at which conduction stops (╬│ = ŽĆ + ╬▒ for continuous conduction, R-L load). - **Conduction Angle (╬│ ŌĆō ╬▒):** Duration (in degrees) for which the device conducts. - **Overlap Angle (╬╝):** Period during which two thyristors conduct simultaneously due to source inductance. ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Smart Water Tap > [!IMPORTANT] > Imagine a pressurized water pipeline (the **AC mains**) feeding a tank (the **DC load**). A normal tap either opens fully or shuts fully ŌĆö you cannot adjust the *flow rate* gradually. Now replace the tap with a **smart valve** that you can open at *any moment* during the pressure cycle, and that stays open for the *remaining part of the cycle* before the next pressure reversal. By *delaying the opening* (firing angle ╬▒), you reduce the *average quantity of water* delivered per cycle ŌĆö exactly analogous to a thyristor controlling the average DC output voltage. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme This module is the **bedrock of industrial power electronics**. Controlled rectifiers feed: - **DC motor drives** (separately excited, series) in rolling mills, paper mills, traction. - **Battery chargers**, **HVDC links** (the large-scale version). - **Electro-chemical processes** (electroplating, electrolysis, anodizing). - **Magnet power supplies** in particle accelerators. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently test this topic with **numerical derivations** (Vdc, Irms, P, PF) and **waveform-sketch-based questions**. Master the firing sequence of **T1ŌĆōT4** for full converter and the role of the **Freewheeling Diode (FWD)** for semi-converter. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö The "Why" Behind the Equations Every thyristor in a controlled rectifier has **two operating quadrants**: 1. **Forward Blocking Mode** (0 to ╬▒): Device is forward-biased but not yet gated. 2. **Forward Conduction Mode** (╬▒ to ŽĆ): Device is gated, conducts, drops Ōēł **1.5 V** (small compared to Vm). 3. **Reverse Blocking Mode** (ŽĆ to 2ŽĆ): Device is reverse-biased, naturally commutated OFF. The average DC output voltage is fundamentally the **mean value of the rectified waveform over a full cycle** (2ŽĆ radians), which mathematically is: $$ V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_{0}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ The **RMS value** is the **square root of the mean of the squared waveform** (heating effect): $$ V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_{0}^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)} $$ ### 2.2 Conduction Logic Summary | Topology | R-Load Output Wave | RL-Load Output Wave | RLE-Load Output Wave | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | 1-Žå Half-Wave | ╬▒ to ŽĆ only | ╬▒ to ╬│ (discontinuous) | ╬▒ to ŽĆ (if E > Vm sin ╬▒) | | 1-Žå Full Converter | ╬▒ to ŽĆ+╬▒ (each pulse) | Continuous (╬▒ to ŽĆ+╬▒) | Continuous (if E Ōēż Vm cos ╬▒) | | 1-Žå Semi-Converter | ╬▒ to ŽĆ (with FWD: 0 to ŽĆŌłÆ╬▒) | Continuous due to FWD | Continuous (E never > 0 region) | > [!NOTE] > **Continuity Condition for Full Converter with RL Load:** Conduction is continuous as long as the load current never falls to zero. This requires the extinction angle ╬│ = ŽĆ + ╬▒, which holds provided the load time-constant **L/R is large** compared to **1/Žē**. ### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation:** $V_m = \sqrt{2}\, V_s$ is the peak supply voltage, $V_s$ is the RMS supply voltage, $I_m$ is the peak load current, $E$ is the load back-EMF, and all angles are in **radians** (unless specified). | # | Topology | Load | Average $V_{dc}$ | RMS $V_{rms}$ | Average $I_{dc}$ | Power Factor | |:-:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1-Žå Half-Wave Controlled | R | $\frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | $\frac{P_{dc}}{V_s I_s}$ | | 2 | 1-Žå Mid-Point Full-Wave | R | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{\pi}}\sqrt{\frac{\pi-\alpha}{2}+\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | ŌĆö | | 3 | 1-Žå Full Converter | R | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | ŌĆö | | 4 | 1-Žå Full Converter | RL (continuous) | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | $\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | | 5 | 1-Žå Full Converter | RLE (continuous) | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - E$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | ŌĆö | | 6 | 1-Žå Semi-Converter | R | $\frac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi-\alpha+\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | ŌĆö | | 7 | 1-Žå Semi-Converter | RL with FWD | $\frac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{(\pi-\alpha)+\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}}$ | $\frac{V_{dc}}{R}$ | ŌĆö | ### 2.4 Performance Parameters ŌĆö The Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > KTU frequently asks for **rectification efficiency (╬Ę)**, **ripple factor (╬│)**, **Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF)**, and **form factor (FF)**. All derive from $V_{dc}$ and $V_{rms}$. $$ \text{Form Factor } (FF) = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} $$ $$ \text{Ripple Factor } (\gamma) = \sqrt{FF^{2} - 1} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^{2} - V_{dc}^{2}}}{V_{dc}} $$ $$ \text{Rectification Efficiency } (\eta) = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}^{2}/R}{V_{rms}^{2}/R} = \frac{V_{dc}^{2}}{V_{rms}^{2}} = \frac{1}{FF^{2}} $$ $$ \text{Transformer Utilization Factor } (TUF) = \frac{P_{dc}}{V_{s}\, I_{s}} $$ ### 2.5 The Inversion Mode ŌĆö A Critical Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > When the firing angle **╬▒ exceeds 90┬░**, $V_{dc} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ becomes **negative**. Power flows from the **DC side back to the AC supply** ŌĆö this is **line-commutated inverter operation** (also called the *inverting mode*). This is exploited in HVDC transmission and regenerative braking of DC drives. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö 1-Žå Full Converter with R Load **Given:** Single-phase AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, four thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4 in a bridge, resistive load R. **Firing Sequence:** - **T1 & T2 ON** (positive half): $v_0 = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha$ - **T3 & T4 ON** (negative half): $v_0 = -v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $\pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi + \alpha$ #### Step 3.1.1 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage $V_{dc}$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} \\ &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi+\alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \right] \\ &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ \cos(\alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \right] \\ V_{dc} &= \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha \end{aligned} $$ The factor of **2** appears because each output pulse occupies half a period (ŽĆ radians), and the average is computed over ŽĆ rather than 2ŽĆ. #### Step 3.1.2 ŌĆö RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi+\alpha) - \alpha - \frac{\sin(2\pi+2\alpha) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right] \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \frac{\sin(2\alpha) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right] \\ V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2} \implies V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** The RMS output of a single-phase full converter is **independent of firing angle ╬▒** when conduction is continuous. The waveform is always a phase-shifted full sine wave of peak $V_m$, so its RMS is always $V_m/\sqrt{2}$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö 1-Žå Semi-Converter (Half-Controlled) with R Load **Given:** Bridge with T1, T2, D3, D4. T1 and T3 are thyristors; D2 and D4 are diodes (or vice-versa, depending on configuration). **Operating Intervals (per half cycle):** - $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$ : T1 & D2 conduct ŌåÆ $v_0 = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ - $\pi \le \omega t \le \pi+\alpha$ : Freewheeling through T1 & D4 (or D2 & T3 depending on configuration) ŌåÆ $v_0 = 0$ - $\pi+\alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi$ : T3 & D4 conduct ŌåÆ $v_0 = -V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (which equals $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ because $\omega t$ is now in the 3rd quadrant) #### Step 3.2.1 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage $V_{dc}$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right] \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left\{ \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} + \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} \right\} \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left\{ \left[ -\cos\pi + \cos\alpha \right] + \left[ -\cos 2\pi + \cos(\pi+\alpha) \right] \right\} \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left\{ [1 + \cos\alpha] + [-1 - \cos\alpha] \right\} \\ V_{dc} &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3.2.2 ŌĆö RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right] \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \quad \text{(by symmetry)} \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\omega t}{2} - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{4} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\pi}{2} - 0 - \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4} \right] \\ V_{rms} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left( \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Limiting Cases ŌĆö A KTU Favourite:** > - At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{dc} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$ (matches uncontrolled bridge), $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$. > - At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{dc} = 0$, $V_{rms} = 0$ (no conduction). > - At $\alpha = 90┬░$ ($\pi/2$): $V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}$ (half-rated output). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö 1-Žå Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with R Load **Given:** Single thyristor T, R load, AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. **Operating Intervals:** - $0 \le \omega t \le \alpha$ : T not yet fired ŌåÆ $v_0 = 0$ - $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$ : T conducts ŌåÆ $v_0 = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ - $\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$ : T reverse-biased, naturally OFF ŌåÆ $v_0 = 0$ #### Step 3.3.1 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage $V_{dc}$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos\pi + \cos\alpha \right] \\ V_{dc} &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3.3.2 ŌĆö RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{\omega t}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{4} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{\pi - \alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4} \right] \\ V_{rms} &= \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use What? | Operating Condition | Resulting Configuration | Firing Range | |:---|:---|:---| | $\alpha < 90┬░$, Full Converter | **Rectification** ŌĆö DC power delivered to load | 0┬░ to 90┬░ | | $\alpha = 90┬░$, Full Converter | **Zero output** (freezes at boundary) | 90┬░ | | $\alpha > 90┬░$, Full Converter | **Inversion** ŌĆö DC source pumps power back to AC | 90┬░ to 180┬░ | | Any ╬▒, Semi-Converter | Always rectification ($V_{dc} \ge 0$ for $0 \le \alpha \le 180┬░$) | 0┬░ to 180┬░ | | RL load, full converter | Continuous conduction if $\omega L/R > $ critical value | Any ╬▒ | | RLE load (DC motor) | Continuous if $E \le \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | Depends on E | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note ŌĆö Symmetrical vs Asymmetrical Semi-Converter:** In the **symmetrical** configuration, T1 & T3 are thyristors; D2 & D4 are diodes (gating pulses in both half-cycles). In the **asymmetrical** configuration, only one thyristor (say T1) is gated in each cycle. The performance equations remain identical, but the **symmetrical** version offers better **TUF**. ## 4.
