KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define firing angle and extinction angle. Why is the firing angle the primary control variable in a controlled rectifier?** **Model Answer:** The **firing angle (╬▒)** is the angle (in electrical degrees/radians) measured from the instant the thyristor becomes forward-biased to the instant it is triggered by the gate pulse. The **extinction angle (╬│)** is the angle at which conduction ceases. For a full converter with a highly inductive load, $\gamma = \pi + \alpha$, so the **conduction angle** is $\gamma - \alpha = \pi$ (always 180┬░). The firing angle is the primary control variable because $V_{dc} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ is a monotonically decreasing cosine function of ╬▒ ŌĆö varying ╬▒ continuously from 0┬░ to 180┬░ smoothly regulates $V_{dc}$ from its maximum positive value through zero to its maximum negative value (inversion mode). **(3 marks)** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** **Q2. Compare the 1-Žå full converter and the 1-Žå semi-converter in terms of inversion capability and freewheeling action.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Full Converter | Semi-Converter | |:---|:---|:---| | Thyristors | 4 | 2 | | Inversion Mode | Possible ($\alpha > 90┬░$) | Not possible (always $V_{dc} \ge 0$) | | Freewheeling | Needs external FWD | Built-in (via T1 & T3 or D2 & D4) | | $V_{dc}$ Range | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ (positive & negative) | $\frac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ (always $\ge 0$) | | Control Range | 0┬░ to 180┬░ | 0┬░ to 180┬░ | | Application | DC drives, HVDC | Battery chargers, drives needing uni-directional control | **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** #### Ō£ģ OPTION A ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the expressions for average and RMS output voltages of a single-phase full converter feeding a **highly inductive load** under continuous conduction. State the necessary conditions for continuous conduction. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Firing Logic:** For a 1-Žå full converter with RL load in continuous conduction, the pair T1-T2 conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$, and the pair T3-T4 conducts from $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ to $\omega t = 2\pi + \alpha$. The load current is **continuous** and never falls to zero because the inductive EMF sustains conduction. **Step 2 ŌĆö Average Voltage Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} \\ &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi+\alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \right] \\ &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ \cos\alpha + \cos\alpha \right] \\ \boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö RMS Voltage Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} \\ &= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \cdot \pi \\ \boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Condition for Continuous Conduction:** Conduction is continuous if the load time constant $\tau = L/R \gg T/2$ (i.e., $\omega L \gg R$). Quantitatively, continuous conduction is maintained as long as $\alpha \le \pi - \tan^{-1}(\omega L/R)$. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz full converter feeds a DC motor (RLE load) with $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$, $L_a = 10\,mH$, and $E_b = 100\,V$. Compute the firing angle required for a motor speed such that $I_{dc} = 50\,A$. Find also the input power factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V, $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$, $E_b = 100$ V, $I_{dc} = 50$ A. **Step 1 ŌĆö KVL on DC side:** $$ V_{dc} = E_b + I_{dc} R_a = 100 + 50 \times 0.5 = 125 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for ╬▒:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha \\ 125 &= \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi}\cos\alpha \\ \cos\alpha &= \frac{125 \times \pi}{650.54} = 0.6034 \\ \boxed{\alpha = 52.87┬░} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Source RMS Current:** For a square-wave-like load current in continuous conduction, $I_s = I_{dc}$ (assuming ideal flat-topped current). Therefore $I_s = 50$ A. **Step 4 ŌĆö Power Factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dc} &= V_{dc} \times I_{dc} = 125 \times 50 = 6250 \text{ W} \\ P_{ac} &= V_s \times I_s = 230 \times 50 = 11500 \text{ W} \\ \text{PF} &= \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{6250}{11500} = 0.5435 \text{ (lagging)} \\ \boxed{\text{PF} = 0.5435 \text{ lagging}} \end{aligned} $$ **(7 marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ OPTION B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram and waveform sketches, explain the operation of a single-phase **half-controlled bridge rectifier (semi-converter)** with **R load**. Derive the expression for the average output voltage. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Circuit & Components:** The semi-converter consists of two thyristors (T1, T3) and two diodes (D2, D4) in a bridge. For an **asymmetric configuration**, only T1 and T3 are gated once per cycle. T1 and D2 conduct from $\alpha$ to $\pi$; T3 and D4 conduct from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$. The freewheeling action is inherent ŌĆö during $\pi$ to $\pi + \alpha$, both T1 and T3 conduct (one commutating the other), clamping the output to zero. **Step 2 ŌĆö Operating Intervals (one full cycle = 2ŽĆ):** - **Interval 1 ($\alpha \to \pi$):** T1 and D2 forward-biased and ON ŌåÆ $v_0 = +V_m \sin(\omega t)$ - **Interval 2 ($\pi \to \pi + \alpha$):** T1 continues to conduct; D2 reverse-biased, D4 forward-biased ŌåÆ freewheeling through T1 and D4 ŌåÆ $v_0 = 0$ - **Interval 3 ($\pi + \alpha \to 2\pi$):** T3 fired, T1 reverse-biased OFF; T3 and D4 conduct ŌåÆ $v_0 = +V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (which is positive since $\omega t$ is in 3rd/4th quadrant) **Step 3 ŌĆö Average Voltage Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right] \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left\{ \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} + \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} \right\} \\ &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left\{ [1 + \cos\alpha] + [-1 - \cos\alpha] \right\} \\ \boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Limiting Cases:** - $\alpha = 0$: $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$ (uncontrolled bridge value) - $\alpha = 180┬░$: $V_{dc} = 0$ (no conduction) **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** A 1-Žå semi-converter is supplied from a 120 V, 50 Hz source and delivers power to an R load of $R = 10\,\Omega$. The firing angle is $\alpha = 60┬░$. Determine: (i) Average output voltage (ii) RMS output voltage (iii) Average and RMS load currents (iv) Rectification efficiency (v) Ripple factor **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V_s = 120$ V, $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 120 = 169.71$ V, $\alpha = 60┬░ = \pi/3$ rad, $R = 10\,\Omega$. **(i) Average Output Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{dc} &= \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{169.71}{\pi}(1 + \cos 60┬░) \\ &= 54.02 \times (1 + 0.5) = 54.02 \times 1.5 \\ \boxed{V_{dc} = 81.03 \text{ V}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) RMS Output Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rms} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)} \\ &= \frac{169.71}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{\sin 120┬░}{2}\right)} \\ &= 120 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(2.094 + 0.4330\right)} \\ &= 120 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.527}{3.1416}} \\ &= 120 \times \sqrt{0.8044} = 120 \times 0.897 \\ \boxed{V_{rms} = 107.6 \text{ V}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Load Currents:** $$ \boxed{I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} = \frac{81.03}{10} = 8.10 \text{ A}} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R} = \frac{107.6}{10} = 10.76 \text{ A}} $$ **(iv) Rectification Efficiency:** $$ \boxed{\eta = \frac{V_{dc}^{2}}{V_{rms}^{2}} = \frac{81.03^{2}}{107.6^{2}} = \frac{6566}{11578} = 0.567 = 56.7\%} $$ **(v) Ripple Factor:** $$ \boxed{\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{107.6}{81.03}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{1.763 - 1} = 0.873} $$ **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Forgot the factor of 2 in $V_{dc}$:** Students often write $V_{dc} = (V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ for the full converter ŌĆö this loses 2 marks immediately. The factor of 2 is because there are **2 pulses per cycle** (each of width ŽĆ) and the integration is over **ŽĆ**, not $2\pi$. > 2. **Wrong integration limits:** Many students integrate from 0 to ŽĆ instead of $\alpha$ to $\pi+\alpha$ in the full-converter R-load derivation. Always align the limits with the **conduction interval** of the thyristor pair. > 3. **Confusing Semi and Full Converter equations:** Remember: full converter has $\cos\alpha$ in $V_{dc}$, semi-converter has $(1+\cos\alpha)$. These are different. > 4. **Ignoring load inductance effect on RMS:** In continuous RL conduction, the load current is assumed constant (square wave), simplifying RMS computations. If the current is assumed sinusoidal, you **must** integrate over the actual current waveform. > 5. **Direction of $E_b$ in motor load:** The back-EMF opposes the current. The correct KVL is $V_{dc} = E_b + I_{dc} R_a$ (not the reverse). > 6. **Inversion mode boundary:** $V_{dc}$ becomes negative only for the **full converter** at $\alpha > 90┬░$. The semi-converter **cannot** invert ŌĆö it always gives $V_{dc} \ge 0$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 2** - ŌŁÉ **Firing angle ╬▒** = delay from natural commutation point. **Primary control variable.** - ŌŁÉ **Average output voltage** for full converter: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ - ŌŁÉ **Average output voltage** for semi-converter: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ - ŌŁÉ **Average output voltage** for half-wave: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ - ŌŁÉ **RMS output voltage** for full converter with continuous conduction: $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ (**independent of ╬▒**) - ŌŁÉ **RMS output voltage** for semi-converter: $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ - ŌŁÉ **Inversion mode:** Full converter with $\alpha \in (90┬░, 180┬░)$ ŌåÆ $V_{dc} < 0$ ŌåÆ power flows DC ŌåÆ AC. - ŌŁÉ **Continuous conduction condition (RL load):** $\omega L \gg R$ or $\tau \gg T/2$. - ŌŁÉ **RLE load condition:** Conduction blocked if $E_b > \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$. - ŌŁÉ **Freewheeling action** is **inherent in semi-converter**; **external FWD** is required in full converter for RL loads. - ŌŁÉ **Form factor (FF)** = $V_{rms}/V_{dc}$; **Ripple factor** = $\sqrt{FF^2 - 1}$; **Efficiency** = $1/FF^2$. - ŌŁÉ **TUF** ranking: Full Converter (0.81) > Semi-Converter (0.69) > Mid-Point (0.57) > Half-Wave (0.29). - ŌŁÉ **Pulse number p** = 2 for all single-phase full-bridge and mid-point circuits ŌåÆ fundamental ripple frequency = $2 \times 50 = 100$ Hz. - ŌŁÉ **Source current waveform** for full converter (continuous RL): quasi-square wave of amplitude $I_{dc}$ with phase shift of ╬▒ from the supply voltage. - ŌŁÉ **KTU Hot Tip:** Always check the load type (R, RL, RLE) **first** before selecting the formula ŌĆö this is the #1 reason students lose marks. > **End of Module 2 Notes ŌĆö Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers** > *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme PEEBT741 | Bloom Levels: L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate)*

topics.title: Typical Waveforms with R, RL with Diode and RL with Voltage Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Typical Waveforms with R, RL with Diode and RL with Voltage Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **phase-controlled rectifier**, the load behavior critically determines the shape of the output voltage and current waveforms. A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeding different load configurations produces distinctively different output signatures. The three canonical load conditions examined in Module 2 are:

1. **Purely Resistive (R) Load** ŌĆō The simplest case where the load current follows the same shape as the load voltage.
2. **R-L Load (without freewheeling)** ŌĆō The load inductance stores energy and forces the current to continue even after the source voltage reverses polarity, extending conduction into the negative half-cycle.
3. **R-L Load with Freewheeling Diode (FWD)** ŌĆō A diode placed across the load provides a path for the inductive current, clamping the output voltage to zero during the freewheeling interval.
4. **R-L Load with a DC Voltage Source (E)** ŌĆō Models a back-EMF load such as a **DC motor armature** or a **battery being charged**, where conduction begins only when the instantaneous source voltage exceeds E.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The examination places strong emphasis on drawing the **source voltage $v_s$, output voltage $v_o$, load current $i_o$, and thyristor/diode voltage $v_T$** waveforms for each load type. Marks are awarded for correct identification of **conduction angles ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$)** and the **polarity reversals** of $v_o$ when inductance is present.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆō The Water Tap Model

Imagine a water tap (the **thyristor**) that you can open, but it will only **close by itself** when you turn the handle past a certain point (the **firing angle $\alpha$**):

- **R Load (Open Drain):** Water flows out instantly when you open the tap, and the moment the tap closes, flow stops. Current and pressure (voltage) rise and fall together.
- **R-L Load (Swimming Pool Inlet):** The pool has a "memory" (the **inductor**). Even after you turn off the supply, water keeps swirling around the pool for a while ŌĆō the thyristor keeps conducting past the zero-crossing of the source voltage.
- **R-L with Freewheeling Diode (Bypass Pipe):** A parallel drainage pipe (the **FWD**) opens automatically when the main supply goes negative, safely returning the stored water back to the source side and clamping the output pressure to zero.
- **R-L with Voltage Source (Charging a Battery):** A battery (E) sits at the load end. The tap will only allow current to flow when the incoming water pressure is **higher** than the battery's internal pressure. Below that threshold, no current flows at all.

> [!NOTE]
> The **average output voltage $V_{o(avg)}$** is the most frequently tested quantity in KTU board examinations, as it directly determines the power delivered to a DC load (motors, batteries, heaters).

---

## 1.3 Circuit Topology and Component Roles

| Symbol | Component | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | AC Source (50 Hz, India) | Supplies sinusoidal input |
| $T$ | Thyristor (SCR) | Unidirectional controlled switch |
| $D_F$ | Freewheeling Diode | Provides path for inductive current when $v_s < 0$ |
| $R$, $L$ | Load resistance & inductance | Defines the load time constant $\tau = L/R$ |
| $E$ | DC back-EMF source | Represents battery or motor armature EMF |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is to **forget the freewheeling diode** when sketching waveforms. Always check the question statement: if an FWD is mentioned, $v_o$ becomes **non-negative**; if not, $v_o$ will have a **negative lobe** during the inductive extension interval.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case 1: Half-Wave Rectifier with Pure R Load

### Operating Principle
- The thyristor **T** is fired at angle $\alpha$ in every positive half-cycle.
- It conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$, after which the source voltage reverses and the thyristor naturally turns off (commutates).
- The load current $i_o = v_s / R$ is **in phase** with the source voltage during conduction.
- During the negative half-cycle, $T$ is OFF and the entire source voltage appears across the thyristor as $v_T$.

### Waveform Characteristics
- **Conduction angle** $= \pi - \alpha$
- **Output voltage** is **never negative**
- **No overlap**, no extension beyond $\pi$

---

## 2.2 Case 2: Half-Wave Rectifier with R-L Load (No Freewheeling Diode)

### Operating Principle
- At $\omega t = \alpha$, thyristor is fired; the load current $i_o$ begins to build up.
- At $\omega t = \pi$, $v_s$ becomes negative, but $i_o$ **cannot fall to zero instantly** due to $L$.
- The thyristor continues to conduct until $i_o$ finally reaches zero at $\omega t = \beta$, where $\beta > \pi$.
- During $\alpha \to \beta$: $v_o = v_s$ (may be negative from $\pi$ to $\beta$).
- During $\beta \to (2\pi + \alpha)$: $T$ is OFF, $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$.

### The Conduction Equation (Critical KTU Derivation)
The instantaneous current is governed by the differential equation:

$$L \frac{di_o}{dt} + R i_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

Solving with initial condition $i_o(\omega t = \alpha) = 0$:

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z} \left[ \sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi) \, e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)} \right]$$

where:
- $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$
- $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$
- $\tau = L / R$

The extinction angle $\beta$ is found by setting $i_o(\beta) = 0$:

$$\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}$$

This is a **transcendental equation** and must be solved iteratively for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 Case 3: Half-Wave Rectifier with R-L Load and Freewheeling Diode

### Operating Principle
- For $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$: thyristor **T** conducts, $v_o = v_s$, $i_o = i_T$.
- At $\omega t = \pi$: $v_s$ becomes negative. Since $i_o > 0$, the FWD becomes forward-biased and turns ON.
- For $\pi \le \omega t \le (\pi + \gamma)$: FWD conducts, $v_o = 0$ (clamped!), $i_o = i_{DF}$.
- At $\omega t = (\pi + \gamma)$: $i_o$ decays to zero, FWD turns OFF.
- For the rest of the negative half-cycle: all switches are OFF, $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> The freewheeling diode **prevents the negative lobe** in $v_o$ and **improves the average output voltage**. It also **protects the thyristor** from high reverse voltage transients.

### Average Output Voltage (KTU's Most Tested Result)

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$$

---

## 2.4 Case 4: Half-Wave Rectifier with R-L Load and Voltage Source E (e.g., DC Motor)

### Operating Principle
- The thyristor can only conduct when $v_s > E$. Conduction begins at $\omega t = \alpha$ where $V_m \sin \alpha = E$, so:

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{E}{V_m}\right)$$

- The thyristor naturally turns off at $\omega t = \beta$ where $v_s = E$ again on the descending side: $\beta = \pi - \alpha$.
- With inductance added, $\beta$ extends beyond $(\pi - \alpha)$ and is found by setting $i_o(\beta) = 0$ in the full R-L-E differential equation.
- **Discontinuous conduction** is common in this configuration.

### Differential Equation
$$L \frac{di_o}{dt} + R i_o + E = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

### Average Output Voltage
$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} (V_m \sin \omega t) \, d\omega t + \int_{\beta}^{2\pi + \alpha} 0 \, d\omega t \right] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos \alpha - \cos \beta) - \frac{E \cdot (\beta - \alpha)}{2\pi}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Load Type | Conduction Interval | $V_{o(avg)}$ | $V_{o(rms)}$ | Key Waveform Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R (Resistive)** | $\alpha \to \pi$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\sqrt{\pi}}\sqrt{(\pi-\alpha)+\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}}$ | Always $\ge 0$, no negative lobe |
| **R-L (No FWD)** | $\alpha \to \beta$ (where $\beta > \pi$) | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ | Complex integral (transcendental $\beta$) | $v_o$ becomes **negative** from $\pi$ to $\beta$ |
| **R-L + Freewheeling Diode** | T: $\alpha \to \pi$; FWD: $\pi \to (\pi+\gamma)$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | Depends on $\gamma$ | $v_o$ clamped to $0$ during freewheeling |
| **R-L + Voltage Source E** | $\alpha \to \beta$ (discontinuous) | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta) - \dfrac{E(\beta-\alpha)}{2\pi}$ | Discontinuous | Conduction only when $v_s > E$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** R-L loads model **field windings** of machines; R-L with FWD models **shunt motor armatures**; R-L with E models **separately excited DC motors** during regenerative braking and **battery charging circuits** in EV chargers.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **R Load:** Incandescent lamp dimmers, resistance welding control, heater temperature controllers.
- **R-L Load:** DC motor field circuits, solenoid actuators, relay coils.
- **R-L + FWD:** Variable-speed DC drives, chopper-fed DC motors, switched-mode power supplies.
- **R-L + E:** Battery chargers (two-wheeler EVs, UPS battery banks), DC traction motors, electroplating rectifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage for R-L Load with Freewheeling Diode (Half-Wave)

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation**. Below is the complete, exhaustive solution.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the conduction intervals.**

The thyristor **T** is forward-biased only during the positive half-cycle of $v_s$. The FWD is forward-biased when $v_o$ would otherwise become negative (i.e., when $v_s < 0$ but $i_o > 0$).

Therefore:
- $T$ conducts for: $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$
- $D_F$ conducts for: $\pi \le \omega t \le (\pi + \gamma)$
- Both OFF for: $(\pi + \gamma) \le \omega t \le (2\pi + \alpha)$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the expression for $v_o$ in each interval.**

During $T$ conduction: $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$

During $D_F$ conduction: $v_o = 0$ (the diode short-circuits the load)

During OFF interval: $v_o = 0$ (no source, no current)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the definition of average value.**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Since $v_o$ is non-zero only during $\alpha \to \pi$:

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral.**

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos\alpha) = 1 + \cos\alpha$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final result.**

$$\boxed{V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $\alpha = 0$: $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ (maximum, uncontrolled diode equivalent). When $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{o(avg)} = 0$ (no conduction).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Extinction Angle $\beta$ for R-L Load (No FWD)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differential equation of the loop during conduction.**

Applying KVL around the source-thyristor-load loop during $\alpha \le \omega t \le \beta$:

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = R \cdot i_o + L \frac{di_o}{dt}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recognize the standard first-order ODE form.**

This is a linear non-homogeneous ODE. The solution has two parts: the **steady-state (sinusoidal)** and the **transient (exponential)**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Steady-state (forced) response.**

In steady state, the current is sinusoidal with a phase lag of $\phi$ behind the voltage:

$$i_{ss}(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Transient (natural) response.**

The characteristic equation of the homogeneous part is $L p + R = 0$, giving $p = -R/L$. Therefore:

$$i_{tr}(\omega t) = A \cdot e^{-(R/L)t} = A \cdot e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}$$

where $\tau = L / R$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply initial condition $i_o(\omega t = \alpha) = 0$.**

At $\omega t = \alpha$:

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z} \sin(\alpha - \phi) + A \cdot e^{0} \implies A = -\frac{V_m}{Z} \sin(\alpha - \phi)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Complete solution.**

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}\right]$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Apply final condition $i_o(\omega t = \beta) = 0$.**

$$0 = \sin(\beta - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}$$

$$\boxed{\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}}$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively (Newton-Raphson or trial-and-error) for $\beta$.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A single-phase half-wave rectifier feeds an R-L load with $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 31.83\,\text{mH}$, $V_s = 230\,\text{V (RMS)}$, $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$. The thyristor is fired at $\alpha = 30┬░$. Calculate (a) $\beta$ and (b) $V_{o(avg)}$ (no freewheeling diode).

**Given:**
- $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\,\text{V}$
- $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.16\,\text{rad/s}$
- $\omega L = 314.16 \times 0.03183 = 10\,\Omega$
- $Z = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = 14.14\,\Omega$
- $\phi = \tan^{-1}(10/10) = 45┬░$
- $\omega \tau = \omega L / R = 10/10 = 1\,\text{rad}$

**Step (a) ŌĆö Solve transcendental equation for $\beta$.**

$$\sin(\beta - 45┬░) = \sin(30┬░ - 45┬░) \cdot e^{-(\beta - 30┬░)\pi/180┬░}$$

Note: $(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau) = (\beta - 30┬░)\cdot(\pi/180┬░)$ (since $\omega \tau = 1$ rad).

Try $\beta = 180┬░$:

$$\sin(135┬░) = 0.707 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sin(-15┬░) \cdot e^{-(150┬░)\pi/180┬░} = (-0.259) \cdot e^{-2.618} = -0.0193$$

LHS > RHS, so $\beta < 180┬░$. Try $\beta = 170┬░$:

$$\sin(125┬░) = 0.819 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sin(-15┬░) \cdot e^{-(140┬░)\pi/180┬░} = -0.259 \cdot e^{-2.443} = -0.0223$$

LHS still > RHS. Try $\beta = 165┬░$:

$$\sin(120┬░) = 0.866 \quad \text{vs.} \quad (-0.259) \cdot e^{-(135┬░)\pi/180┬░} = -0.259 \cdot e^{-2.356} = -0.0234$$

LHS still > RHS. Try $\beta = 210┬░$:

$$\sin(165┬░) = 0.259 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sin(-15┬░) \cdot e^{-(180┬░)\pi/180┬░} = -0.259 \cdot e^{-\pi} = -0.0107$$

LHS = 0.259, RHS magnitude = 0.0107. LHS > |RHS|. Try $\beta = 220┬░$:

$$\sin(175┬░) = 0.0872 \quad \text{vs.} \quad (-0.259) \cdot e^{-(190┬░)\pi/180┬░} = -0.259 \cdot e^{-3.316} = -0.00902$$

LHS (0.087) Ōēł RHS magnitude (0.009), but signs: LHS positive, RHS negative. Solution where both transition. Try $\beta = 230┬░$:

$$\sin(185┬░) = -0.0872 \quad \text{vs.} \quad (-0.259) \cdot e^{-(200┬░)\pi/180┬░} = -0.00998$$

Now LHS and RHS both negative. Between $220┬░$ and $230┬░$, LHS changes sign. Iterating finely gives $\beta \approx 218┬░$.

**Step (b) ŌĆö Calculate $V_{o(avg)}$.**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(\cos 30┬░ - \cos 218┬░)$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(0.866 - (-0.788)) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1.654)$$

$$\boxed{V_{o(avg)} \approx 85.7\,\text{V}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Half-Wave Rectifier State Machine)

The following Mermaid state diagram captures the **operational state transitions** of a half-wave controlled rectifier as it processes one full AC cycle. This is the precise equivalent of the physical waveform segmentation required in KTU answer scripts.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> WaitTrigger
    WaitTrigger --> ThyristorON : Gate pulse at alpha
    ThyristorON --> SourceNegative : omega t reaches pi
    SourceNegative --> FreewheelActive : FWD present AND current nonzero
    SourceNegative --> ThyristorOFF : No FWD AND current reaches zero at beta
    FreewheelActive --> AllOFF : Current decays to zero at pi + gamma
    AllOFF --> WaitTrigger : Next positive half cycle begins
    ThyristorOFF --> WaitTrigger : Next positive half cycle begins
    AllOFF --> [*]
    ThyristorOFF --> [*]

    note right of ThyristorON
        v_o equals v_s
        i_o builds up through R and L
    end note

    note right of FreewheelActive
        v_o is clamped to zero
        Load current freewheels through diode
    end note

    note right of SourceNegative
        v_s is negative
        Without FWD, v_o follows v_s into negative region
    end note
```

## 4.2 Comparative Waveform Matrix (Block Schematic of Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputSource["AC Source vs"]
        A1["Sinusoidal voltage with peak Vm"]
    end

    subgraph LoadBlock["Load Configuration Selector"]
        L1["R Load"]
        L2["R-L Load"]
        L3["R-L with Freewheeling Diode"]
        L4["R-L with Voltage Source E"]
    end

    subgraph OutputWaveforms["Resulting Waveform Signatures"]
        W1["vo: positive half sinusoid only"]
        W2["vo: positive lobe plus negative extension to beta"]
        W3["vo: positive half sinusoid then clamped zero"]
        W4["vo: partial lobe where vs exceeds E"]
    end

    A1 --> L1
    A1 --> L2
    A1 --> L3
    A1 --> L4

    L1 --> W1
    L2 --> W2
    L3 --> W3
    L4 --> W4
```

## 4.3 Conduction Timeline (Half-Wave Rectifier with FWD)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Conduction Timeline for R-L Load with Freewheeling Diode
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S deg

    section Source Voltage
    Positive Half   :a1, 0, 180
    Negative Half   :a2, 180, 360

    section Thyristor T
    OFF before alpha    :crit, 0, 30
    Conducting          :active, 30, 180
    OFF after pi        :a3, 180, 360

    section Diode DF
    OFF before pi       :b1, 0, 180
    Conducting          :active2, 180, 280
    OFF after gamma     :b2, 280, 360

    section Output Voltage
    Follows source      :done, 30, 180
    Clamped at zero     :crit2, 180, 360

    section Load Current
    Builds up and decays  :active3, 30, 280
    Zero                 :a4, 280, 360
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** What is the role of a freewheeling diode in a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeding an R-L load? How does it affect the output voltage waveform?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A freewheeling diode (FWD) is connected in **anti-parallel** with the R-L load, with its cathode at the positive terminal of the load and anode at the negative terminal. Its roles are:

1. **Prevents negative output voltage:** When the source voltage $v_s$ becomes negative at $\omega t = \pi$, the FWD becomes forward-biased (since the inductive current cannot change instantaneously) and clamps the output voltage to zero, eliminating the negative lobe.

2. **Allows stored inductive energy to freewheel:** The load current $i_o$ continues to flow through the FWD and load, dissipating the stored magnetic energy in $R$.

3. **Improves the input power factor** by reducing the conduction period of the thyristor.

The output voltage waveform transforms from having a negative extension (from $\pi$ to $\beta$) to a clean positive lobe followed by a zero interval, with $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** Why does the load current in a half-wave controlled rectifier with R-L load (without FWD) become discontinuous beyond a certain firing angle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The load current is **naturally commutated** only when it falls to zero. In an R-L load, the inductor $L$ opposes any sudden change in current. When the source voltage $v_s$ becomes negative (at $\omega t = \pi$), the inductor's stored energy sustains the current, causing the thyristor to continue conducting past $\pi$ into the negative half-cycle until the energy is fully dissipated in $R$.

**Discontinuity occurs** because the thyristor requires a minimum positive voltage across it (i.e., $v_s$ must be positive) for current to begin building up. If $\alpha$ is large, the available energy in the short positive half-cycle is insufficient to sustain current through the entire negative half-cycle. The current falls to zero, the thyristor turns OFF, and remains OFF for the rest of the cycle. The next pulse of current only begins when the next gate trigger is applied at $\omega t = (2\pi + \alpha)$, resulting in **discontinuous conduction**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆō Internal Choice Model)

### Question A: 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeds an R-L load with $R = 5\,\Omega$, $L = 20\,\text{mH}$ from a $230\,\text{V}$, $50\,\text{Hz}$ supply. The firing angle is $\alpha = 45┬░$.

**(a)** Derive the expression for the average output voltage of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R-L load and freewheeling diode, and calculate its value for the given parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the waveforms of source voltage $v_s$, output voltage $v_o$, thyristor current $i_T$, freewheeling diode current $i_{DF}$, load current $i_o$, and thyristor voltage $v_T$ for the above case. Label all conduction angles. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the firing angle and peak voltage.**

$$\alpha = 45┬░ = \pi/4 \text{ rad}, \quad V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Confirm FWD operation.**

Since the load is R-L (inductive), and a freewheeling diode is present, the output voltage is clamped to zero whenever the source goes negative. Conduction:
- Thyristor T: $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$ (i.e., $45┬░$ to $180┬░$)
- FWD: $\pi \le \omega t \le (\pi + \gamma)$ (extends into the negative half-cycle)

**Step 3 ŌĆö State the formula for $V_{o(avg)}$.**

For a half-wave rectifier with R-L load and FWD, the average output voltage is:

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral.**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha) \right]$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute numerical values.**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{2\pi} (1 + \cos 45┬░) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi} (1 + 0.7071)$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27 \times 1.7071}{6.2832} = \frac{555.3}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{V_{o(avg)} \approx 88.4\,\text{V}}$$

#### Model Solution (b) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Waveform Sketch (textual description for the answer script):**

The student must draw a single full AC cycle (0 to $2\pi$) with the following aligned waveforms (all sharing the same time axis):

1. **$v_s$ (Source Voltage):** Pure sinusoid $V_m \sin(\omega t)$, crossing zero at $0$, $\pi$, $2\pi$.
2. **$v_o$ (Output Voltage):** Identical to $v_s$ from $\alpha$ to $\pi$, then **zero** from $\pi$ to $2\pi + \alpha$ (because FWD clamps it).
3. **$i_T$ (Thyristor Current):** Non-zero only from $\alpha$ to $\pi$; rises and falls with the inductor; zero elsewhere.
4. **$i_{DF}$ (Diode Current):** Zero until $\pi$; takes over from $\pi$ to $(\pi + \gamma)$; decays exponentially to zero.
5. **$i_o$ (Load Current):** Equals $i_T$ from $\alpha$ to $\pi$; equals $i_{DF}$ from $\pi$ to $(\pi + \gamma)$; zero thereafter. Note the **continuous but piecewise-defined** nature.
6. **$v_T$ (Thyristor Voltage):** Zero from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (T is ON). From $\pi$ to $2\pi + \alpha$, $v_T = v_s$ (entire source voltage blocked by T). At $2\pi + \alpha$, a new gate pulse fires T and $v_T$ returns to zero.

**Critical markings to include:**
- Firing angle $\alpha = 45┬░$ marked on the time axis.
- Extinction of thyristor at $\omega t = \pi$.
- Freewheeling interval from $\pi$ to $(\pi + \gamma)$.
- Note that $\gamma$ depends on $L/R$ time constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (b part):**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw $v_T$ waveform**: 2 marks are reserved for the thyristor voltage showing the blocked negative half-cycle.
> 2. **Drawing $v_o$ with a negative lobe**: This is wrong when FWD is present. The output voltage **must** be $\ge 0$.
> 3. **Not marking the angle $\gamma$**: Examiners specifically look for the freewheeling duration.
> 4. **Showing $i_o$ discontinuous** (returning to zero before $\pi$): Incorrect ŌĆö $i_o$ flows continuously through the FWD.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is used to charge a $48\,\text{V}$ battery from a $230\,\text{V}$, $50\,\text{Hz}$ AC supply through a series resistance of $R = 2\,\Omega$ and inductance $L = 10\,\text{mH}$. The firing angle is $\alpha = 30┬░$.

**(a)** Determine the extinction angle $\beta$ and the average charging current. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the operation of this circuit with the case where a freewheeling diode is added across the battery. Sketch the $v_o$ and $i_o$ waveforms for both cases. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify circuit parameters.**

$V_m = 325.27\,\text{V}$, $E = 48\,\text{V}$, $R = 2\,\Omega$, $L = 10\,\text{mH}$, $\omega = 314.16\,\text{rad/s}$, $\alpha = 30┬░$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute circuit constants.**

$\omega L = 314.16 \times 0.010 = 3.1416\,\Omega$

$$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9.87} = 3.73\,\Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R) = \tan^{-1}(3.1416/2) = 57.5┬░$$

$$\omega \tau = \omega L / R = 1.5708\,\text{rad} = 90┬░$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Note the assumption.**

In an R-L-E circuit, the thyristor can only conduct when $v_s > E$. We assume that $V_m \gg E$ (true here: $325 > 48$), so the firing angle is approximately equal to the given $\alpha$ (i.e., we ignore the offset). A more rigorous approach uses:

$$\alpha_{true} = \sin^{-1}(E/V_m) + \text{trigger delay}$$

For simplicity (and as expected in KTU problems), we proceed with $\alpha = 30┬░$ as the **effective** firing angle.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve the transcendental equation for $\beta$.**

The current during conduction satisfies:

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}\right] - \frac{E}{R}\left[1 - e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}\right]$$

Setting $i_o(\beta) = 0$ and solving iteratively (similar to the worked example in Section 3.3), the extinction angle is found to be approximately:

$$\beta \approx 215┬░ \text{ to } 225┬░$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the average charging current.**

$$I_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} i_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

For an approximate estimate, assuming a roughly triangular current pulse with peak $\approx (V_m - E)/Z \approx 75\,\text{A}$ and base $(\beta - \alpha) \approx 185┬░$:

$$I_{o(avg)} \approx \frac{1}{2} \times 75 \times \frac{185┬░}{360┬░} \approx 19.3\,\text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{o(avg)} \approx 19\,\text{A}, \quad \beta \approx 220┬░}$$

#### Model Solution (b) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Without FWD | With FWD |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output voltage $v_o$ | Has a negative lobe (from $\pi$ to $\beta$) | Clamped to 0 once $v_s < 0$ |
| Conduction of T | $\alpha \to \beta$ (long, $\beta > \pi$) | $\alpha \to \pi$ (short) |
| Average charging current | Lower (some current flows in reverse during negative lobe) | Higher (no reverse current) |
| Waveform of $i_o$ | Single continuous pulse, possibly with positive and negative portions | Two-part: T portion + FWD portion, all $\ge 0$ |
| Power dissipation in $R$ | Higher (RMS current higher) | Lower (no negative excursion) |
| Harmonic content | Higher | Lower (rectangular-ish shape) |

**Waveform Sketch Instructions:**

*Without FWD:*
- $v_o$ follows $v_s$ from $\alpha$ to $\beta$; positive from $\alpha$ to $\pi$, **negative from $\pi$ to $\beta$**, then zero.
- $i_o$ rises from zero at $\alpha$, reaches a peak, falls back to zero at $\beta$, and remains zero until the next cycle.

*With FWD:*
- $v_o$ follows $v_s$ from $\alpha$ to $\pi$, then **clamped to 0** from $\pi$ to $(2\pi + \alpha)$.
- $i_o$ is always $\ge 0$: T supplies current from $\alpha$ to $\pi$, FWD freewheels from $\pi$ to $(\pi + \gamma)$, then zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Confusing the firing angle and extinction angle definitions** for R-L-E loads. Make sure to define both clearly.
> 2. **Neglecting $E$ in the current expression**: $i_o$ must include a constant offset of $-E/R$ shifted by the transient term.
> 3. **Failing to state assumptions** explicitly (e.g., constant $E$, no source impedance).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- **Pure R Load:** Thyristor conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi$. $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$. **No negative lobe, no extension.**
- **R-L Load (No FWD):** Conduction extends to $\beta > \pi$. $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$. **Negative lobe present in $v_o$.**
- **R-L + FWD:** Thyristor OFF at $\pi$; FWD conducts from $\pi$ to $(\pi+\gamma)$. $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ (same formula as R load, because FWD eliminates negative lobe).
- **R-L + E (Battery/Motor):** Conduction only when $v_s > E$. **Discontinuous** for large $E$ or large $\alpha$. $\alpha_{true} = \sin^{-1}(E/V_m)$ (for R-E load, no L).
- **Conduction equation (R-L):** $\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}$ ŌĆö transcendental, solved iteratively.
- **Waveform drawing order (KTU marks):** $v_s \to v_o \to i_T \to i_{DF} \to i_o \to v_T$, all on the **same time axis**, with **angles labeled**.
- **Sign conventions:** $v_o$ positive at top, $i_o$ positive when flowing from top to bottom of the load, $v_T$ positive when anode is positive w.r.t. cathode.
- **Common formulas to memorize:** $V_{o(avg)}^{R,FWD} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$; $V_{o(avg)}^{RL} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$.
- **Why FWD is essential:** Prevents reversal of load voltage, improves PF, reduces commutation voltage stress, allows discontinuous operation in DC drives.
- **Time constant effect:** Larger $L/R$ ŌåÆ larger $\gamma$ and $\beta$ ŌåÆ more overlap ŌåÆ more negative area ŌåÆ lower average $V_o$ (in RL case).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** For 14-mark waveform questions, dedicate roughly **3 marks' worth of effort** to clean, labeled sketches. Examiners award partial marks even if the analytical portion is incomplete, provided the waveforms are accurate. Always mark $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, $\pi$, and $2\pi + \alpha$ on the time axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage and Current Equations for Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage and Current Equations for Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier** is a phase-controlled power electronic converter that uses a **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR / Thyristor)** to convert a sinusoidal AC supply voltage into a unidirectional, variable DC output voltage. The output is controlled by varying the **firing angle (╬▒)**, which is the angular instant (measured from the zero-crossing of the supply voltage) at which the SCR is triggered into conduction by applying a gate pulse.

For a purely resistive (R) load:
- The SCR conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$ (the natural commutation point).
- The average and RMS output voltages are functions of $\alpha$ and the peak supply voltage $V_m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The derivation of average output voltage ($V_{dc}$), RMS output voltage ($V_{rms}$), and the corresponding current expressions for a **single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier supplying an R load and an R-L load** (without and with a Freewheeling Diode) are the principal learning outcomes of this topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **firefighter's water hose connected to a pressurized water main**:

- The water main is your **AC source** ŌĆö pressure constantly rises, falls, and reverses direction.
- The firefighter holds a **squeeze-grip nozzle** ŌĆö this is your **SCR**. Water (current) cannot flow until the grip is squeezed.
- Squeezing the grip at a *delayed* moment in the pressure cycle is equivalent to **delaying the firing angle $\alpha$**.
- The hose sprays water on a patch of grass (**R load**) for only part of each pressure cycle. The longer you delay squeezing, the less water reaches the grass.
- The **average amount of water** landing on the grass corresponds to the **average DC output voltage**.
- The **effective heating power** of the spray corresponds to the **RMS output voltage**.

> [!NOTE]
> For an **inductive (R-L) load** (like a sprinkler with a heavy rotor that keeps spinning after the water stops), the SCR refuses to turn off exactly when the AC voltage hits zero ŌĆö the load inductance "drags" the current along. The SCR conducts beyond $\pi$ until the load current naturally falls to zero at an angle $\beta > \pi$.

### 1.3 Circuit Topology (Textual Schematic)

The converter comprises **four essential elements** connected in a single series loop:

1. **AC Source:** $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (transformer secondary, single-phase).
2. **Thyristor (SCR):** $T$ ŌĆö unidirectional current flow, gate-controlled turn-on.
3. **Load:** Purely resistive $R$, or inductive $R-L$ (and optionally a Freewheeling Diode $D_F$ across the load).
4. **Gate Trigger Circuit:** Provides $i_g$ pulses at $\omega t = \alpha, \alpha + 2\pi, \alpha + 4\pi, \ldots$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard assumed value for a **50 Hz Indian/KTU supply** is $f = 50$ Hz, hence $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ rad/s. The peak voltage $V_m$ and RMS source voltage $V_s$ are related by $V_m = \sqrt{2}\, V_s$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence (R Load ŌĆö Case 1)

The single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with a **purely resistive load** operates in three distinct intervals per supply cycle:

**Interval I ŌĆö Blocking State ($\omega t \in [0, \alpha]$):**
- The SCR is forward-biased, but no gate pulse is applied.
- Thyristor acts as an open switch: $i_o = 0$, $v_o = 0$.

**Interval II ŌĆö Conduction State ($\omega t \in [\alpha, \pi]$):**
- A gate pulse at $\omega t = \alpha$ latches the SCR.
- $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- $i_o = v_o / R = (V_m / R)\sin(\omega t)$.

**Interval III ŌĆö Reverse Blocking ($\omega t \in [\pi, 2\pi]$):**
- The supply voltage reverses polarity; the SCR is reverse-biased and turns off at $\pi$ (natural commutation).
- $i_o = 0$, $v_o = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Why**: For an R-load, the current and voltage are in phase, so the current falls to zero exactly when the voltage does at $\omega t = \pi$. This is the *extinction angle* $\beta = \pi$ for an R-load. This is the foundation for deriving all quantitative equations.

### 2.2 Operational Sequence (R-L Load ŌĆö Case 2)

**Without Freewheeling Diode (FWD):**
- Conduction extends beyond $\pi$ because the inductor $L$ releases stored energy, sustaining current flow in the positive direction.
- Conduction is from $\alpha$ to $\beta$, where $\beta$ is found from the transcendental equation $\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\tan\phi}$.
- Here $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$ is the **load impedance angle**.

**With Freewheeling Diode (FWD):**
- During the negative half-cycle, the FWD becomes forward-biased and short-circuits the load.
- The SCR turns off at $\omega t = \pi$ (forced commutation by FWD).
- The energy stored in $L$ freewheels through $D_F$ instead of returning to the source.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations constitute the complete quantitative toolset for this topic.

| # | Quantity | Equation (R Load) | Equation (R-L Load, No FWD) | Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Instantaneous Output Voltage | $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$ | $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $\alpha \le \omega t \le \beta$ | V |
| 2 | Instantaneous Load Current | $i_o = (V_m / R)\sin(\omega t)$ | Solved via differential equation (see ┬¦3.3) | A |
| 3 | **Average (DC) Output Voltage** $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\,(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\,(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ | V |
| 4 | **RMS Output Voltage** $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi-\alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\beta-\alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}$ | V |
| 5 | Average Load Current $I_{dc}$ | $V_{dc} / R$ | $V_{dc} / R$ | A |
| 6 | RMS Load Current $I_{rms}$ | $V_{rms} / R$ | $V_{rms} / Z$, $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ | A |
| 7 | Output DC Power $P_{dc}$ | $V_{dc}^2 / R$ | $V_{dc}^2 / R$ | W |
| 8 | Output AC Power $P_{ac}$ | $V_{rms}^2 / R$ | $I_{rms}^2 \cdot R$ | W |
| 9 | Rectification Efficiency $\eta$ | $P_{dc} / P_{ac}$ | $P_{dc} / P_{ac}$ | ŌĆö |
| 10 | Form Factor $FF$ | $V_{rms} / V_{dc}$ | $V_{rms} / V_{dc}$ | ŌĆö |
| 11 | Ripple Factor $RF$ | $\sqrt{FF^2 - 1}$ | $\sqrt{FF^2 - 1}$ | ŌĆö |
| 12 | TUF (Half-Wave) | $1/\pi$ (constant, supply transformer secondary) | $1/\pi$ (constant, supply transformer secondary) | ŌĆö |
| 13 | Extinction Angle $\beta$ | $\beta = \pi$ | $\beta$ from transcendental eq. (or $\beta = \pi$ with FWD) | rad |
| 14 | Peak Inverse Voltage $PIV$ | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are foundational for designing **battery chargers (R-type)**, **DC motor armature drives (R-L with FWD)**, **electroplating rectifiers**, **HVDC link inner stages**, and **static VAR compensators** in modern renewable energy systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations follow the **KTU board examiner's expected valuation pattern** ŌĆö every integral is expanded, every substitution is shown explicitly, and no algebraic transition is skipped. Adopt this exact step-by-step style in your answer scripts.

### 3.1 Derivation of Average Output Voltage ($V_{dc}$) ŌĆö R Load

The average value of the rectified output voltage over one full supply cycle ($0$ to $2\pi$) is defined as:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Split the integral** according to the conduction intervals (0 to ╬▒: zero; ╬▒ to ŽĆ: supply; ŽĆ to 2ŽĆ: zero):

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\alpha} 0\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} 0\, d(\omega t)\right]
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Drop the zero integrals** for compactness:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Perform the integration** using $\int \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = -\cos(\omega t)$:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the limits** ($\cos\pi = -1$ and $\cos\alpha$ stays as is):

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-\cos\pi - (-\cos\alpha)\Big] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-(-1) + \cos\alpha\Big]
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify** the double negative:

$$
\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\,(1 + \cos\alpha)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick sanity check:** When $\alpha = 0$ (uncontrolled diode equivalent), $V_{dc} = V_m/\pi$, which is the standard half-wave rectifier average ŌĆö matches. When $\alpha = \pi$, $V_{dc} = 0$ ŌĆö also matches (SCR never fires).

### 3.2 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage ($V_{rms}$) ŌĆö R Load

The RMS value is defined as the square root of the mean of the square:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the squared output voltage** (zero outside conduction, $V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$ during):

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor out the constant** $V_m^2$:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the half-angle identity** $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Split the integral**:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} 1\, d(\omega t) - \frac{1}{2}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate the first integral** (constant 1 over interval of length $\pi - \alpha$):

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} 1\, d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Evaluate the second integral** using $\int \cos(2\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}$:

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin 2\pi - \sin 2\alpha}{2} = \frac{0 - \sin 2\alpha}{2} = -\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute** the two evaluated integrals back:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\pi - \alpha) - \left(-\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)\right]}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Simplify the bracket**:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}
$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Pull the ┬Į out as $1/\sqrt{2}$**:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}} = \frac{V_m}{2\sqrt{\pi}}\sqrt{(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}}
$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Move $\sqrt{\pi}$ inside the root as $1/\pi$** for the standard KTU textbook form:

$$
\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** At $\alpha = 0$, $V_{rms} = (V_m/2)\sqrt{(1/\pi)(\pi + 0)} = V_m/2$ ŌĆö the standard half-wave RMS, correct.

### 3.3 Derivation of Load Current ŌĆö R-L Load (No FWD)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply KVL** during conduction ($\alpha \le \omega t \le \beta$):

$$
V_m \sin(\omega t) = R\, i_o + L\,\frac{di_o}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recognize this as a first-order linear ODE** in $i_o$. The general solution is the sum of the forced (steady-state) response and the natural (transient) response:

$$
i_o(\omega t) = i_{forced}(\omega t) + i_{natural}(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the forced response** (steady-state sinusoidal current lagging by $\phi$):

$$
i_{forced} = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi), \quad \text{where } Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2},\ \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write the natural response** (decaying exponential with time constant $\tau = L/R$):

$$
i_{natural} = A\, e^{-(R/L)\, t} = A\, e^{-(\omega t)/\tan\phi}
$$

(Here, $A$ is a constant determined by the initial condition.)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the boundary condition** that the load current is zero at the instant of triggering ($\omega t = \alpha$):

$$
i_o(\alpha) = 0 \implies \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi) + A\, e^{-\alpha/\tan\phi} = 0
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for the constant $A$**:

$$
A = -\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{\alpha/\tan\phi}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute $A$ back** into the natural response:

$$
i_{natural} = -\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{\alpha/\tan\phi}\, e^{-(\omega t)/\tan\phi} = -\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\tan\phi}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Combine forced and natural parts** to get the complete solution:

$$
\boxed{i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\Big[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\tan\phi}\Big], \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \beta}
$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Apply the second boundary condition** to find the extinction angle $\beta$: the current returns to zero at $\omega t = \beta$:

$$
\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\tan\phi}
$$

This transcendental equation must be solved iteratively (Newton-Raphson) or graphically. For **$\alpha = \phi$** (firing exactly at the load impedance angle), the natural-response term vanishes, the current is purely sinusoidal, and the SCR naturally commutates at $\beta = \pi + \alpha$.

### 3.4 Average Output Voltage ŌĆö R-L Load

The derivation follows the identical procedure as ┬¦3.1, but the upper limit is $\beta$ instead of $\pi$:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\beta}
$$

Applying limits and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\,(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an R-load, $\beta = \pi \implies \cos\beta = -1$, and this reduces to $V_{dc} = (V_m/2\pi)(1 + \cos\alpha)$ ŌĆö confirming internal consistency between the two cases.

### 3.5 RMS Output Voltage ŌĆö R-L Load

Following the same procedure as ┬¦3.2 with upper limit $\beta$:

$$
\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}}
$$

### 3.6 Numerical Evaluation Example (R Load)

Given: $V_s = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 10$ $\Omega$, $\alpha = 60┬░ = \pi/3$ rad.

**Compute $V_m$**:

$$
V_m = \sqrt{2}\, V_s = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.269 \text{ V}
$$

**Compute $V_{dc}$**:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{325.269}{2\pi}\,(1 + \cos 60┬░) = \frac{325.269}{2\pi}\,(1 + 0.5) = \frac{325.269 \times 1.5}{6.2832} = 77.65 \text{ V}
$$

**Compute $V_{rms}$**:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{325.269}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \pi/3) + \frac{\sin(2\pi/3)}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= 162.635 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[2.0944 + \frac{0.8660}{2}\right]} = 162.635 \sqrt{\frac{2.5274}{\pi}} = 162.635 \sqrt{0.8045} = 162.635 \times 0.8970 = 145.88 \text{ V}
$$

**Compute currents and derived quantities**:

$$
I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} = \frac{77.65}{10} = 7.765 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R} = \frac{145.88}{10} = 14.588 \text{ A}
$$

$$
P_{dc} = V_{dc}\, I_{dc} = 77.65 \times 7.765 = 602.95 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{ac} = I_{rms}^2\, R = 14.588^2 \times 10 = 2128.13 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{602.95}{2128.13} = 28.33\%
$$

$$
FF = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{145.88}{77.65} = 1.879
$$

$$
RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.879^2 - 1} = \sqrt{2.531} = 1.591
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Topology ŌĆö Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier (R and R-L Loads)

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional power-flow architecture** of the converter, mapping every element and signal path as a directed graph. This is the recommended adaptation for power-electronics schematics that involve physical layouts which are difficult to draw natively in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Source Vs equals Vm sin omega t] --> B[Thyristor SCR T]
    B --> C{Load Selection}
    C -- R Load --> D[Resistor R]
    C -- R-L Load --> E[Inductor L in series with R]
    E -. Optional Freewheeling .-> F[Diode DF across R-L]
    D --> G[Output Terminal DC plus]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Output Terminal DC minus]
    H --> A
    I[Gate Trigger Pulse at omega t equals alpha] --> B
    J[Gate Trigger Pulse at omega t equals alpha plus 2pi] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> Node labels are deliberately rendered in plain uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `Vs equals Vm sin omega t`) to comply with the Mermaid label-formatting restriction. No markdown bold or italics are used inside the node bodies.

### 4.2 Waveform Timing Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

The following block diagram conveys the **temporal evolution of the SCR's operational states** across one complete supply cycle. The SCR remains in the **Blocking State** for the first interval, transitions to **Conduction State** at the firing angle, and naturally returns to blocking at the commutation point.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Time equals 0 to alpha] --> A1[SCR is forward biased BUT no gate pulse]
    A1 --> A2[State: Blocking - io equals 0 - vo equals 0]
    A2 --> B0[Firing event at omega t equals alpha]
    B0 --> B1[Gate pulse applied - SCR latches ON]
    B1 --> B2[Conduction interval: alpha to pi for R load or alpha to beta for R-L load]
    B2 --> C0[Supply voltage crosses zero at omega t equals pi]
    C0 --> C1{Load type check}
    C1 -- R Load --> C2[SCR turns OFF naturally at beta equals pi]
    C1 -- R-L Load No FWD --> C3[SCR continues conducting until beta where io reaches zero]
    C1 -- R-L Load With FWD --> C4[Freewheeling diode DF conducts and forces SCR OFF at pi]
    C2 --> D0[Blocking interval resumes: pi to 2pi]
    C3 --> D0
    C4 --> D0
    D0 --> E0[Cycle repeats at omega t equals 2pi plus alpha]
```

### 4.3 Analytical Reference Matrix ŌĆö Conduction Boundaries

| Operating Mode | Conduction Interval | Lower Limit ($\omega t_{on}$) | Upper Limit ($\omega t_{off}$) | Discontinuity Trigger |
|----------------|---------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|-----------------------|
| R Load | $\alpha \to \pi$ | $\alpha$ (firing) | $\pi$ (natural commutation) | Supply voltage zero-crossing |
| R-L Load (no FWD) | $\alpha \to \beta$ | $\alpha$ (firing) | $\beta > \pi$ (current zero) | Load current natural decay |
| R-L Load (with FWD) | $\alpha \to \pi$ | $\alpha$ (firing) | $\pi$ (forced commutation) | FWD forward-bias onset |
| Discontinuous Boundary | $\alpha = \phi$ | $\alpha$ | $\pi + \alpha$ | Critical condition $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\omega L/R)$ |
| Maximum Output | $\alpha \to 0$ | $0$ | $\pi$ | Uncontrolled diode behavior |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] ŌĆö Q1 (3 Marks)**
*Define firing angle and conduction angle for a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier. How are they related for a purely resistive load?*

**Model Answer:**

The **firing angle ($\alpha$)** is the angular delay (measured from the instant the supply voltage becomes positive) after which a gate pulse is applied to the thyristor to initiate conduction. It is expressed in degrees or radians.

The **conduction angle ($\gamma$)** is the angular duration (in degrees or radians) for which the thyristor remains in the ON state during one half-cycle of the supply.

For a purely resistive load, the thyristor conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$ (the natural commutation point where the supply voltage reverses). Therefore:

$$
\gamma = \pi - \alpha
$$

The **extinction angle** $\beta$ (the instant the SCR turns OFF) for an R load is $\beta = \pi$, confirming that $\gamma = \beta - \alpha = \pi - \alpha$.

---

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] ŌĆö Q2 (3 Marks)**
*Write the expression for the average output voltage of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R load. State the value of $V_{dc}$ at $\alpha = 0$ and $\alpha = 180┬░$.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

**At $\alpha = 0$:** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + 1) = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ (maximum; identical to an uncontrolled diode rectifier).

**At $\alpha = 180┬░$:** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos 180┬░) = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 - 1) = 0$ V (no output; SCR never fires).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] ŌĆö Module 2, Q-A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average output voltage** of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier supplying a **resistive (R) load**. State the conditions for maximum and minimum output.

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **RMS output voltage** of the same converter. Hence compute the **rectification efficiency, form factor, and ripple factor** for $\alpha = 30┬░$ and $V_s = 230$ V.

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

The instantaneous output voltage is:

$$
v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi < \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}
$$

The average value over one cycle is:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Integrating:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\big[-\cos\pi + \cos\alpha\big]
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)}
$$

- **Maximum** at $\alpha = 0$: $V_{dc,\max} = V_m/\pi$.
- **Minimum** at $\alpha = 180┬░$: $V_{dc,\min} = 0$.

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

From ┬¦3.2:

$$
\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}}
$$

For $\alpha = 30┬░ = \pi/6$ rad, $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V:

**Average voltage:**

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}\Big(1 + \cos\frac{\pi}{6}\Big) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1 + 0.8660) = \frac{325.27 \times 1.8660}{6.2832} = 96.59 \text{ V}
$$

**RMS voltage:**

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{325.27}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \pi/6) + \frac{\sin(\pi/3)}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= 162.635\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[2.6180 + \frac{0.8660}{2}\right]} = 162.635\sqrt{\frac{3.0510}{\pi}} = 162.635 \times 0.9854 = 160.27 \text{ V}
$$

**Form Factor:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{160.27}{96.59} = 1.659
$$

**Ripple Factor:**

$$
RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.659^2 - 1} = \sqrt{2.752 - 1} = \sqrt{1.752} = 1.324
$$

**Rectification Efficiency:**

Assuming R-load ($I_{rms} = V_{rms}/R$, $I_{dc} = V_{dc}/R$):

$$
\eta = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{V_{rms}^2 / R} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} = \frac{96.59^2}{160.27^2} = \frac{9329.6}{25686.4} = 0.3632 = 36.32\%
$$

---

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] ŌĆö Module 2, Q-B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeds an **R-L load** ($R = 5$ $\Omega$, $L = 20$ mH, $V_s = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $\alpha = 45┬░$). Determine whether the load current is **continuous or discontinuous** and find the **average output voltage** assuming continuous conduction.

**(b)** Explain the role of a **Freewheeling Diode (FWD)** in an R-L loaded half-wave rectifier. With a neat sketch, show the change in output voltage waveform and write the modified $V_{dc}$ expression.

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the load impedance angle $\phi$**:

$$
\omega L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.020 = 6.2832 \text{ }\Omega
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{6.2832}{5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.2566) = 51.49┬░ = 0.8987 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compare $\alpha$ and $\phi$**: $\alpha = 45┬░ < \phi = 51.49┬░$, so $\alpha < \phi$. This means the **load current is discontinuous** (the SCR cannot latch because the natural response term has the wrong polarity at $\omega t = \alpha$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find $\beta$ using the transcendental equation** (iterative solution). Initial guess $\beta_0 = \pi + \alpha = 3.927 + 0.785 = 4.712$ rad (225┬░). For discontinuous conduction, $\beta$ is solved numerically.

For a **first-order estimate** assuming $\beta = \pi + \alpha$:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(\cos 45┬░ - \cos 225┬░)
$$

$$
= \frac{325.27}{6.2832}(0.7071 - (-0.7071)) = 51.76 \times 1.4142 = 73.20 \text{ V}
$$

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Function of FWD:** The Freewheeling Diode (connected in anti-parallel with the R-L load) becomes forward-biased whenever the load voltage attempts to reverse. It provides a path for the load current during the negative half-cycle of the supply, thereby:

1. **Preventing negative voltage** across the load.
2. **Improving the average output voltage** (because the negative half-cycles are clipped to zero).
3. **Reducing commutation stress** on the SCR (turn-off is forced at $\omega t = \pi$ instead of $\beta$).
4. **Eliminating the discontinuous-conduction problem** at high firing angles.

**Modified $V_{dc}$ expression:** With FWD, conduction of the SCR is restricted to $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$. The negative area from $\pi$ to $2\pi$ is replaced by zero (the FWD shorts the load). Therefore:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

This is the **same expression as the R-load case** ŌĆö meaning the FWD effectively transforms an R-L load into an R-equivalent for voltage-averaging purposes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently write $V_{dc} = (V_m/\pi)(1 + \cos\alpha)$ (forgetting the factor of **2 in the denominator**). The full cycle is $2\pi$, not $\pi$. This single error will cost you 2ŌĆō3 marks.
>
> **Common Mistake 2:** When asked for $V_{rms}$ with R-L load, students often plug in $\beta = \pi$ instead of computing the correct extinction angle. The transcendental equation $\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\tan\phi}$ **must** be solved first; only then can you substitute $\beta$ into the $V_{rms}$ formula.
>
> **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **RMS load current** with **average load current** in efficiency calculations. Always use $P_{ac} = I_{rms}^2 R$ (heating power) and $P_{dc} = V_{dc} I_{dc}$ (DC power). Mixing them is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting the conversion between $\alpha$ in **degrees** and **radians** when calling `cos()`, `sin()`, or `tan()` in calculator evaluations. The KTU paper typically gives $\alpha$ in degrees; convert before integrating.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö Module 2**

- ŌśÉ **Half-wave controlled rectifier** uses a single SCR; conducts from $\alpha$ to extinction.
- ŌśÉ For **R-load**: $\beta = \pi$ (fixed); **R-L load**: $\beta > \pi$ (solved from transcendental equation).
- ŌśÉ **With FWD**: $\beta$ is forced back to $\pi$ regardless of $L$; negative half-cycle of $v_o$ is clipped to zero.
- ŌśÉ **Key average voltage formulas**:
   - R load: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
   - R-L load (no FWD): $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$.
   - R-L load (with FWD): $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ (same as R-load).
- ŌśÉ **Key RMS voltage formula**: $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\text{upper} - \text{lower}) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}$.
- ŌśÉ **Conduction angle** for R-load: $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$.
- ŌśÉ **PIV** of the SCR: $V_m$ (half-wave); $2V_m$ (full-wave bridge).
- ŌśÉ **Rectification efficiency** of half-wave is **always below 40.6%** (theoretical max at $\alpha = 0$).
- ŌśÉ **Form factor** $FF$ for half-wave: ranges from $1.11$ (full-wave equivalent) to $1.879$ ($\alpha = 60┬░$).
- ŌśÉ **Ripple factor** is high for half-wave; full-wave is preferred for low-ripple DC applications.
- ŌśÉ **Freewheeling diode** is mandatory for high-power DC motor drives to prevent inversion-mode operation.
- ŌśÉ The **load impedance angle** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L/R)$ determines the boundary between continuous and discontinuous conduction.
- ŌśÉ **Numerical conversions**: $V_m = \sqrt{2}\, V_s$ (single-phase RMS supply); $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- ŌśÉ **Average current**: $I_{dc} = V_{dc}/R$ (R-load) ŌĆö same formula for R-L because the inductor averages to zero over a cycle.
- ŌśÉ **Examiner tip**: Always draw the **conduction intervals** explicitly on the time axis before starting any derivation. This secures 1ŌĆō2 easy marks even if the integration has minor errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 3 ŌĆö Basic Chopper Operation Using Semiconductor Switches

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **DC Chopper** is a static power electronic device that converts a **fixed DC input voltage** into a **variable DC output voltage** by periodically connecting and disconnecting the source using a high-speed **semiconductor switching element**. The output magnitude is controlled by varying the **ON-time (T_on)** and **OFF-time (T_off)** intervals of the switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Nomenclature:**
> A chopper that uses a controlled semiconductor switch (BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, GTO, or Thyristor with forced commutation) is formally termed a **"DCŌĆōDC Converter"**. In KTU papers, both terms ŌĆö *Chopper* and *DCŌĆōDC Converter* ŌĆö are accepted and interchangeable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine your kitchen washbasin. The inlet pipe carries water at a **constant high pressure** (this is your **DC source V_s**). The outlet you actually want should deliver water at **adjustable pressure** to fill different buckets (this is your **load R_L**). You cannot easily install a mechanical pressure-reducer, so what do you do?

You turn the **tap on for a short while**, then **close it**, then **open again** ŌĆö repeatedly. If you open the tap for **more time**, more water flows per cycle, raising the *average* pressure on the outlet side. If you open it for **less time**, the *average* pressure falls.

That tap is your **semiconductor switch (BJT/MOSFET/IGBT)**. The *duration ratio* of open-to-closed is your **duty cycle (K)**. The chopper is exactly this ŌĆö a high-speed electronic tap that switches at **kHz to tens of kHz** to chop a steady DC into a *controllable average DC*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Quantities

The following standard metrics govern every chopper analysis under the KTU 2024 scheme:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **V_s** | Source (DC Input) Voltage | **V (Volts)** |
| **V_o** | Average Output Voltage | **V (Volts)** |
| **I_o** | Average Load Current | **A (Amperes)** |
| **K** | Duty Cycle / Time Ratio | dimensionless (0 Ōēż K Ōēż 1) |
| **T** | Chopping Period (T_on + T_off) | **s (Seconds)** |
| **f** | Chopping Frequency (1/T) | **Hz / kHz** |
| **╬Ę** | Converter Efficiency | typically **85% to 95%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Ratio (K)** is defined as:
> $$K = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = f \cdot T_{on}$$
> where $T_{on}$ is the ON interval and $T_{off}$ is the OFF interval of the semiconductor switch.
