## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Use Semiconductor Switches (and not mechanical switches)?

A mechanical switch cannot operate reliably beyond **~50 Hz** without contact wear, arcing, and slow response. Semiconductor switches solve this by switching at **10 kHz to 100 kHz** with no moving parts, enabling:
- **Reduced filter size** (smaller L and C)
- **Faster dynamic response** to load changes
- **Higher efficiency** (low conduction loss)
- **Compact, silent, and reliable** operation

The commonly used semiconductor switches in chopper circuits (per the KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

| Switch | Typical Rating | Trigger Mechanism | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BJT** (Bipolar Junction Transistor) | Up to **1500 V / 800 A** | Continuous base current | Older textbooks |
| **Power MOSFET** | Up to **1000 V / 50 A** | GateŌĆōsource voltage (V_GS) | High-frequency choppers |
| **IGBT** (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor) | Up to **6500 V / 3600 A** | GateŌĆōemitter voltage (V_GE) | Modern traction & drives |
| **GTO Thyristor** | Up to **6000 V / 6000 A** | Gate pulse (turn-on & turn-off) | High-power choppers |
| **SCR with forced commutation** | Very high power | Gate pulse + commutation circuit | Classical design |

### 2.2 Operating Principle ŌĆö How a Chopper Works

A chopper circuit has **four essential functional blocks**:

1. **Source (V_s):** Provides the unregulated DC supply (often a rectified AC or battery).
2. **Semiconductor Switch (S):** Periodically connects V_s to the load (transistors in modern design).
3. **Freewheeling Diode (D_F):** Provides a path for the inductive load current when the switch is OFF, preventing dangerous voltage spikes.
4. **Load (R-L or R-L-E):** The DC motor armature, battery, or resistive element being driven.

**Logical Operating Sequence:**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Switch ON (interval T_on):**
  The semiconductor switch S is gated ON. Source voltage V_s is applied across the load. Load current rises (because of inductance L), storing energy in the magnetic field. The freewheeling diode D_F is reverse-biased and plays no role.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Switch OFF (interval T_off):**
  The semiconductor switch is gated OFF. The source is disconnected from the load. However, the inductor opposes the sudden interruption of current and forces current to freewheel through diode D_F in a closed loop. Load current decays gradually.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Cycle Repeats:**
  After T_off, the switch is gated ON again. The cycle repeats at chopping frequency f.

### 2.3 Classification of Basic Choppers (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **three primary chopper topologies**:

#### (A) Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter)
- Output voltage is **less than** input voltage.
- Switch is in **series** with the load.
- Applications: DC motor speed control (below base speed), battery chargers.

#### (B) Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter)
- Output voltage is **greater than** input voltage.
- Switch is in **parallel** with the load (in the inductor branch).
- Applications: Regenerative braking of DC motors, photovoltaic systems.

#### (C) Step-Up / Step-Down Chopper (Buck-Boost Converter)
- Output can be either **greater or less** than input.
- Output voltage is **inverted** in polarity.
- Applications: Switched-mode power supplies (SMPS).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam verified and frequently tested in Part A & Part B.**

| # | Chopper Type | Output Voltage V_o (Average) | Output Current I_o (Average) | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step-Down (Buck) | $V_o = K \cdot V_s$ | $I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R}$ | $0 \leq K \leq 1$, hence $V_o \leq V_s$ |
| 2 | Step-Up (Boost) | $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - K}$ | $I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R}$ | $0 \leq K < 1$, hence $V_o > V_s$ |
| 3 | Step-Up/Down (Buck-Boost) | $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{K}{1 - K}$ | $I_o = \dfrac{\vert V_o \vert}{R}$ | Polarity is **inverted** relative to V_s |
| 4 | RMS Output (Step-Down) | $V_{rms} = V_s \cdot \sqrt{K}$ | ŌĆö | Used for ripple/THD calculations |
| 5 | Chopping Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | ŌĆö | Typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz** |
| 6 | Time Ratio | $K = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ | ŌĆö | Always between **0 and 1** |
| 7 | Input Current (Step-Down) | $I_s = K \cdot I_o$ | ŌĆö | Source supplies only during T_on |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_o}{P_o + P_{loss}}$ | ŌĆö | Modern choppers achieve **> 90%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:**
> $T_{on} = K \cdot T$ and $T_{off} = (1 - K) \cdot T$
> These two identities unlock every numerical problem ŌĆö memorize them on day one.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

The chopper is the **workhorse of modern DC drives** and is found in:

- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction:** IGBT choppers control battery-fed DC motors with regenerative braking.
- **Suburban & Metro Trains:** DC choppers (DCŌĆōDC converters) step down 750 V catenary to variable motor voltage.
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Every laptop charger is a high-frequency chopper feeding a high-frequency transformer.
- **Solar PV Systems:** Boost choppers lift low panel voltages (~17 V) to usable DC bus levels (~400 V).
- **Battery-Operated Tools:** Cordless drills use buck choppers to vary motor speed.
- **Robotics:** Servo motor drivers use choppers for precise torque/speed control.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations follow the **exact logical sequence expected by KTU board examiners**. Each step is laid out so that even a first-time reader can follow without backtracking.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage of a Step-Down (Buck) Chopper

**Given:** Source voltage V_s (constant DC), Switch ON-time T_on, Switch OFF-time T_off, Chopping period T = T_on + T_off, Resistive load R.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the instantaneous output voltage v_o(t):**

When the switch S is ON (during 0 Ōēż t < T_on), the source is directly connected across the load, so:
$$v_o(t) = V_s$$

When the switch S is OFF (during T_on Ōēż t < T), the load is disconnected from the source and freewheels through D_F, so:
$$v_o(t) = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the average value over one full chopping period:**

The average (DC) value of any periodic waveform v_o(t) over one period T is:
$$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Split the integral over the two sub-intervals:**

Since v_o(t) takes two distinct constant values, split the integral at t = T_on:
$$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integrals:**

The first integral contributes V_s multiplied by T_on; the second integral vanishes:
$$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify to obtain the final result:**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute the duty cycle K = T_on / T:**

$$\boxed{V_o = K \cdot V_s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Because K is bounded between 0 and 1, V_o is always less than or equal to V_s. This is precisely why it is called a **step-down** (buck) chopper.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage of a Step-Up (Boost) Chopper

**Given:** Source V_s, Inductor L, Switch S in series with V_s, Diode D, Capacitor C, Load R. Switch S is in **parallel** with the load through the diode.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Analyze the circuit when S is ON (interval T_on):**

The switch shorts the input side. The source V_s is applied across the inductor L (the diode D is reverse-biased because the load capacitor holds the output voltage). The inductor stores energy:
$$V_s = L \cdot \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

Current builds up at rate:
$$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L}$$

The **load** is isolated from the source and supplied solely by the capacitor C, which discharges slowly through R.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Analyze the circuit when S is OFF (interval T_off):**

The inductor releases its stored energy. Now V_s and the inductor's EMF add up because they are in series. The diode D becomes forward-biased and conducts:
$$V_s + V_L = V_o$$

In steady state, applying **volt-second balance** on the inductor over one full cycle (the average inductor voltage in periodic steady state is zero):
$$\int_{0}^{T} v_L(t) \, dt = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express v_L(t) in the two intervals:**

During T_on (switch ON): $v_L = V_s$ (the inductor sees only the source).
During T_off (switch OFF): $v_L = V_s - V_o$ (the inductor sees source minus output).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance:**

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Expand and collect terms:**

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + V_s \cdot T_{off} - V_o \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot (T_{on} + T_{off}) = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

$$V_s \cdot T = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for V_o:**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T_{off}}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Express T_off in terms of K:**

Recall that T_off = T - T_on = T(1 - K), so:
$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T(1 - K)} = \frac{V_s}{1 - K}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - K}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** As K ŌåÆ 1 (switch almost always ON), V_o ŌåÆ Ōł× in theory (limited in practice by parasitic resistances). Thus the **step-up (boost)** chopper can produce an output **greater than the input**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage of a Step-Up/Down (Buck-Boost) Chopper

**Given:** Source V_s, Inductor L, Switch S, Diode D, Load R. The output is taken across R with **opposite polarity** to the input.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Switch ON (interval T_on):**

The source charges the inductor through the switch; the diode is reverse-biased; the load is supported by the output capacitor.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Switch OFF (interval T_off):**

The inductor discharges through the diode into the load and capacitor, transferring stored energy.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance on inductor:**

During T_on: $v_L = V_s$ (source across inductor).
During T_off: $v_L = -V_o$ (because the output polarity is inverted relative to the source, the inductor sees V_o of opposite sign).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write the volt-second balance equation:**

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (-V_o) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for V_o:**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T_{off}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute T_on = K┬ĘT and T_off = (1-K)┬ĘT:**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{K \cdot T}{(1 - K) \cdot T}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{K}{1 - K}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** When K < 0.5, the ratio K/(1-K) < 1 ŌåÆ output less than input (step-down). When K > 0.5, the ratio > 1 ŌåÆ output greater than input (step-up). Hence the **buck-boost chopper** adapts to both ranges, with the bonus of **polarity inversion**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Step-Down (Buck) | Step-Up (Boost) | Step-Up/Down (Buck-Boost) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switch position | Series with load | Series with inductor | Series with inductor |
| V_o formula | $K \cdot V_s$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-K}$ | $V_s \cdot \dfrac{K}{1-K}$ |
| Output range | $0$ to $V_s$ | $V_s$ to $\infty$ | $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ |
| Output polarity | Same as input | Same as input | **Inverted** |
| Energy storage | Inductor L | Inductor L | Inductor L |
| Freewheeling path | Diode D_F | Diode D | Diode D |
| Application | DC motor below base speed | DC motor above base speed, PV | SMPS, polarity-converting drives |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Generic Chopper

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional signal flow** of a generic DC chopper, mapping the energy path from source through the semiconductor switch to the load, with all associated control signals.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[DC Source Vs]
    sw1[Semiconductor Switch S BJT MOSFET IGBT GTO]
    ctl1[Gate Drive Circuit PWM Controller]
    fw1[Freewheeling Diode DF]
    lf1[Load RL or RLE]
    fb1[Output Filter L C optional]
    sen1[Current Sensor]
    out1[Variable DC Output Vo]

    src1 -->|continuous DC| sw1
    ctl1 -->|PWM gate pulses| sw1
    sw1 -->|ON interval Ton| lf1
    fw1 -->|OFF interval Toff| lf1
    lf1 --> out1
    sen1 -->|feedback signal| ctl1
    fb1 --> out1
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **DC Source Vs** provides the unregulated input.
- **Gate Drive Circuit** generates the PWM signal that controls the duty cycle K.
- **Semiconductor Switch S** opens and closes at the chopping frequency.
- **Freewheeling Diode DF** provides the inductive current path during OFF intervals.
- **Load RL or RLE** represents a resistive, inductive, or back-EMF load (e.g., DC motor).
- **Current Sensor** closes the loop, feeding back to the PWM controller for closed-loop regulation.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Step-Down (Buck) Chopper

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vs_node[Vs DC Source]
    s_node[S Semiconductor Switch]
    d_node[DF Freewheeling Diode]
    l_node[L Load Inductance]
    r_node[R Load Resistance]
    pwm_node[PWM Gate Signal]

    vs_node --- s_node
    s_node --- d_node
    d_node --- l_node
    l_node --- r_node
    pwm_node -.controls.-> s_node
```

**Operating Cycle Summary (Buck):**
- **Ton:** S closes ŌåÆ current flows Vs ŌåÆ S ŌåÆ L ŌåÆ R ŌåÆ back to Vs.
- **Toff:** S opens ŌåÆ inductor current freewheels through DF ŌåÆ L ŌåÆ R ŌåÆ DF.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Step-Up (Boost) Chopper

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vs_nodeB[Vs DC Source]
    l_nodeB[L Boost Inductor]
    s_nodeB[S Boost Switch]
    d_nodeB[D Boost Diode]
    c_nodeB[C Output Capacitor]
    r_nodeB[R Load Resistance]
    pwm_nodeB[PWM Gate Signal]

    vs_nodeB --- l_nodeB
    l_nodeB --- s_nodeB
    s_nodeB --- d_nodeB
    d_nodeB --- c_nodeB
    c_nodeB --- r_nodeB
    pwm_nodeB -.controls.-> s_nodeB
```

**Operating Cycle Summary (Boost):**
- **Ton:** S closes ŌåÆ inductor L charges from Vs (current builds); diode D is reverse-biased; capacitor C alone supplies load.
- **Toff:** S opens ŌåÆ inductor releases energy, diode D conducts, current flows into capacitor and load. V_load = Vs + V_inductor.

---

### 4.4 Output Waveform (Step-Down Chopper, R-L Load)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisT[Time axis t]
    v_high[Vo equals Vs during Ton]
    v_zero[Vo equals 0 during Toff]

    axisT --> v_high
    v_high --> v_zero
    v_zero --> v_high
```

This pattern repeats every period T, producing an **average DC value** of $V_o = K \cdot V_s$. The chopper converts a constant DC into a *pulsed* waveform whose **average** is the desired output.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks Total)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]`

**Define a DC chopper. List any two semiconductor switches used in chopper circuits.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A DC chopper is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by rapid ONŌĆōOFF switching of a semiconductor element. The output is controlled by varying the ON-time (Ton) and OFF-time (Toff), giving a variable average output.

Two semiconductor switches used in choppers (2 marks):
1. **Power BJT / Power MOSFET / IGBT** ŌĆö fully controlled, self-commutating, used in modern choppers.
2. **GTO Thyristor / SCR with forced commutation** ŌĆö used in classical high-power chopper designs.

(Any two correctly named devices with 1 mark each, plus 1 mark for the definition.)

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by duty cycle in a chopper? Write the formula for the average output voltage of a step-down chopper.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **duty cycle (K)** is defined as the ratio of the switch ON-time to the total chopping period:
$$K = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity lying between **0 and 1**. (2 marks)

The average output voltage of a step-down chopper is:
$$V_o = K \cdot V_s$$

(1 mark for the formula.)

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (ESE Style: 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the **operating principle of a step-down (buck) chopper** feeding a resistive load. Derive the expression for its average output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A step-down chopper has a DC source voltage V_s = **220 V**, ON-time T_on = **2 ms**, and OFF-time T_off = **3 ms**. Calculate:
   - (i) The duty cycle K
   - (ii) The chopping frequency f
   - (iii) The average output voltage V_o
   - (iv) The RMS output voltage V_rms
   **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B-A

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Step-Down Chopper Operation (7 Marks)

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A step-down chopper consists of a DC source V_s feeding a load R through a semiconductor switch S (BJT/MOSFET/IGBT). A freewheeling diode D_F is connected across the load in reverse-bias configuration. When the switch S is ON, D_F is reverse-biased; when S is OFF, D_F conducts to provide a path for the inductive current.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
- **Interval 0 to T_on (Switch ON):** The switch S is gated ON by a control signal. The source voltage V_s is applied directly across the load R. The output voltage v_o = V_s, and load current i_o = V_s / R flows.
- **Interval T_on to T (Switch OFF):** The switch S is gated OFF. The source is disconnected. For a resistive load, the current simply becomes zero (no inductive storage). For an R-L load, the inductor forces current to freewheel through diode D_F, and v_o Ōēł 0.
- The cycle repeats at frequency f = 1/T.

**Derivation of Average Output Voltage (2 marks):**
$$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T} = K \cdot V_s$$

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** V_s = 220 V, T_on = 2 ms = 2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ s, T_off = 3 ms = 3 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ s

**(i) Duty Cycle K (1 Mark):**
$$T = T_{on} + T_{off} = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ ms}$$
$$K = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

**(ii) Chopping Frequency f (2 Marks):**
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Average Output Voltage V_o (2 Marks):**
$$V_o = K \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 220 = 88 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) RMS Output Voltage V_rms (2 Marks):**
$$V_{rms} = V_s \cdot \sqrt{K} = 220 \times \sqrt{0.4} = 220 \times 0.6325 \approx 139.14 \text{ V}$$

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **operating principle of a step-up (boost) chopper** with a neat functional block diagram. Derive the expression for its average output voltage in terms of duty cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A step-up chopper supplies a load of R = **20 ╬®** from a DC source of V_s = **48 V**. The chopper operates at a switching frequency of f = **2 kHz** with a duty cycle of K = **0.6**. Calculate:
   - (i) The ON-time T_on and OFF-time T_off
   - (ii) The average output voltage V_o
   - (iii) The average load current I_o
   - (iv) The average source current I_s
   **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B-B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Step-Up Chopper Operation (7 Marks)

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A step-up chopper consists of a DC source V_s in series with an inductor L and a semiconductor switch S (MOSFET/IGBT) connected across the source. A diode D is in series with the load branch, and a capacitor C smooths the output voltage across load R.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
- **Interval 0 to T_on (Switch ON):** The switch S is closed. Current from V_s builds up through L (energy stored in the magnetic field). Diode D is reverse-biased because the output capacitor holds V_o across the load. The capacitor alone supplies the load.
- **Interval T_on to T (Switch OFF):** The switch S opens. The inductor's collapsing magnetic field reverses its polarity, and its EMF now **adds** to V_s. The combined voltage forward-biases diode D, charging the capacitor and supplying the load at a **higher voltage** V_o > V_s.

**Derivation of Average Output Voltage (2 marks):**
Applying volt-second balance on the inductor L (average inductor voltage over one cycle = 0 in steady state):

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

Solving:
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - K}$$

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** R = 20 ╬®, V_s = 48 V, f = 2 kHz = 2 ├Ś 10┬│ Hz, K = 0.6

**(i) ON-time and OFF-time (2 Marks):**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^3} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{on} = K \cdot T = 0.6 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 0.3 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{off} = (1 - K) \cdot T = 0.4 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Average Output Voltage V_o (2 Marks):**

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - K} = \frac{48}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{48}{0.4} = 120 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Average Load Current I_o (1.5 Marks):**

$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{120}{20} = 6 \text{ A}$$

**(iv) Average Source Current I_s (1.5 Marks):**

By power balance (assuming lossless converter):
$$P_s = P_o \implies V_s \cdot I_s = V_o \cdot I_o$$

$$I_s = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s} = \frac{120 \times 6}{48} = \frac{720}{48} = 15 \text{ A}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the polarity inversion** in buck-boost choppers ŌåÆ up to **ŌłÆ2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up K and (1-K)** in the boost chopper formula ŌåÆ the boost is $V_s/(1-K)$, NOT $V_s/(K)$.
> 3. **Ignoring the freewheeling diode** in the circuit diagram ŌåÆ ŌłÆ1 mark. Always draw D_F even if the load is purely resistive.
> 4. **Not stating the boundary** of K (i.e., 0 Ōēż K Ōēż 1) when defining duty cycle ŌåÆ ŌłÆ1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing RMS with Average** in numerical problems ŌåÆ RMS uses $\sqrt{K}$, not K.
> 6. **Unit conversion errors** in numericals (ms to s, kHz to Hz) ŌåÆ ŌłÆ1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping volt-second balance** derivation in boost chopper ŌåÆ examiners expect the integration step explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it beside you while writing the exam.**

- Ō£ģ A **chopper** = DC-to-DC converter using a semiconductor switch (BJT/MOSFET/IGBT/GTO).
- Ō£ģ **Duty cycle (K)** = T_on / T, always between **0 and 1**.
- Ō£ģ **Step-down (Buck)** output: $V_o = K \cdot V_s$, $V_o \leq V_s$.
- Ō£ģ **Step-up (Boost)** output: $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-K}$, $V_o \geq V_s$.
- Ō£ģ **Step-up/Down (Buck-Boost)** output: $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{K}{1-K}$, **polarity inverted**.
- Ō£ģ **Freewheeling diode** is essential for R-L loads; it carries current during T_off.
- Ō£ģ **Chopping frequency** typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz**; higher ŌåÆ smaller filters but more switching loss.
- Ō£ģ **RMS output voltage** (step-down) = $V_s \sqrt{K}$; this is what heating/lighting loads respond to.
- Ō£ģ **Switch S ON** ŌåÆ source delivers energy; **Switch S OFF** ŌåÆ inductor delivers stored energy.
- Ō£ģ Applications: **EV traction, SMPS, DC motor drives, solar PV systems, robotics**.
- Ō£ģ Higher **K** ŌåÆ higher V_o in buck; higher K ŌåÆ higher V_o in boost and buck-boost.
- Ō£ģ Memorize: $T_{on} = K \cdot T$, $T_{off} = (1-K) \cdot T$, $f = 1/T$.
- Ō£ģ The **semiconductor switch choice** depends on rating: BJT for low power, MOSFET for high frequency, IGBT for high power & frequency, GTO for very high power.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buck, Boost and Buck-Boost Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Buck, Boost and Buck-Boost Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 ŌĆö BUCK, BOOST & BUCK-BOOST CONVERTERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Power Electronics ŌĆö Module 3):**
> *Buck, Boost and Buck-Boost converters* are **non-isolated, switch-mode DC-DC regulators** that use a controlled semiconductor switch (BJT / MOSFET / IGBT), an inductor, a capacitor, and a freewheeling diode to convert a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by varying the **duty ratio (D)** of the switch.

### 1.1 The Family of DC-DC Choppers (Switching Regulators)

A DC-DC converter is essentially a **DC equivalent of a transformer** ŌĆö but instead of using magnetic turns-ratio to alter voltage, it uses **high-frequency switching (PWM)** plus an energy-storage element (L or C) to "average" the chopped waveform into a smooth DC level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Switching Mode"?**
> The transistor operates only in **cut-off (OFF)** or **saturation (ON)** ŌĆö never in the linear (active) region. This is why efficiency is so high (often **> 90 %**) ŌĆö *P = V ├Ś I* in the switch is virtually zero in both states.

The three basic topologies covered in this module are:

| Converter Type | Alternate Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Buck Converter** | Step-Down Chopper | $V_o < V_s$ |
| **Boost Converter** | Step-Up Chopper | $V_o > V_s$ |
| **Buck-Boost Converter** | Step-Up/Down Chopper (with polarity inversion) | $V_o = \pm \, f(V_s, D)$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water-Tap" Picture

Imagine a water tank (the **inductor L**) that fills when a valve (the **MOSFET switch S**) is opened, and drains into a delivery pipe (the **load R**) when a one-way valve (the **diode D**) allows it.

- **Buck converter** ŌåÆ A pressure regulator placed **after** the tank. Output pressure is *less* than the mains pressure.
- **Boost converter** ŌåÆ A pump that is *in series* with the tank, pushing water **harder** than the incoming pressure.
- **Buck-Boost converter** ŌåÆ A pump installed on a **separate discharge line** that flows *backwards* through the consumer's gauge, so the meter reads a *negative* pressure ŌĆö but the magnitude can be either higher or lower.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:**
> *Buck = "Down"*, *Boost = "Up"*, *Buck-Boost = "Upside-Down"* (the output polarity is inverted with respect to the input).

### 1.3 Common Building Blocks

Every converter in this module is built from the **same four sub-blocks**:

1. **Controlled Switch (S)** ŌĆö MOSFET / IGBT / BJT, driven by a PWM gate signal of frequency $f_s$ and duty cycle $D$.
2. **Inductor (L)** ŌĆö Energy-storage element; never allows its current to change instantaneously.
3. **Freewheeling / Steering Diode (D)** ŌĆö Provides a unidirectional path for inductor current when the switch is OFF.
4. **Output Filter Capacitor (C)** ŌĆö Smooths the chopped voltage; holds the output nearly constant between switching cycles.

### 1.4 Operating Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** ŌĆö Inductor current $i_L$ is **always > 0** (never falls to zero). This is the **default mode of analysis** required in KTU exam problems.
>
> **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)** ŌĆö Inductor current **falls to zero** for some portion of the switching period. Occurs at **light loads** (high R).

The transition boundary between CCM and DCM is governed by the **critical inductance $L_c$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Universal Principle ŌĆö Volt-Second Balance

For any DC-DC converter operating in steady-state, the **average voltage across the inductor over one switching period must be zero** (otherwise the inductor current would ramp up indefinitely):

$$\int_{0}^{T_s} v_L(t) \, dt = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{L,\text{ON}} \cdot D + V_{L,\text{OFF}} \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

This single rule, applied to the three circuits below, gives **all** the conversion-ratio equations in this module.

### 2.2 The Buck (Step-Down) Converter ŌĆö Operation

**Circuit**: Input $V_s$ ŌĆö Switch S ŌĆö Inductor L ŌĆö Capacitor C $\parallel$ Load R ŌĆö Diode D (cathode at $V_s$, anode at switch-L node).

**Mode 1: Switch ON (duration $DT_s$)**
- Diode is reverse-biased.
- Inductor sees $v_L = V_s - V_o > 0$, so $i_L$ **ramps up linearly**.
- Capacitor charges; load is supplied by both source and inductor.

**Mode 2: Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$)**
- Inductor maintains current direction; diode becomes forward-biased (freewheels).
- Inductor sees $v_L = -V_o < 0$, so $i_L$ **ramps down linearly**.
- Capacitor now supplies the load.

**Volt-second balance:**

$$(V_s - V_o) D + (-V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

Solving:

$$V_o = D \cdot V_s$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $0 \le D \le 1$, output is always $V_o \le V_s$. **Step-down verified.**

### 2.3 The Boost (Step-Up) Converter ŌĆö Operation

**Circuit**: Input $V_s$ ŌĆö Switch S (to ground) ŌĆö Inductor L (in series with source) ŌĆö Diode D (forward to output) ŌĆö Capacitor C $\parallel$ Load R.

**Mode 1: Switch ON ($DT_s$)**
- Diode reverse-biased; output is isolated from input.
- Inductor charges from source: $v_L = V_s$, $i_L$ ramps up.
- Capacitor alone supplies load.

**Mode 2: Switch OFF ($(1-D)T_s$)**
- Diode forward-biased; source and inductor deliver energy to load.
- Inductor sees $v_L = V_s - V_o < 0$, $i_L$ ramps down.

**Volt-second balance:**

$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $0 \le D \le 1$, output is always $V_o \ge V_s$. **Step-up verified.**

### 2.4 The Buck-Boost Converter ŌĆö Operation

**Circuit**: Input $V_s$ ŌĆö Inductor L ŌĆö Switch S (to ground) ŌĆö Diode D (anode at L-switch node, cathode at output) ŌĆö Capacitor C $\parallel$ Load R.

**Mode 1: Switch ON ($DT_s$)**
- Diode reverse-biased; output isolated.
- Inductor charges: $v_L = V_s$, $i_L$ ramps up.

**Mode 2: Switch OFF ($(1-D)T_s$)**
- Inductor polarity reverses, forward-biases diode.
- Inductor delivers energy to load and capacitor: $v_L = -V_o$, $i_L$ ramps down.
- **Output polarity is inverted** (negative w.r.t. input ground).

**Volt-second balance:**

$$V_s \cdot D + (-V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_o = - \frac{D}{1-D} \, V_s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **negative sign** in $V_o$ is **not optional** ŌĆö it represents polarity inversion. Dropping it costs **2 marks** in derivation problems.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (CCM Operation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation:** $V_s$ = input, $V_o$ = output, $D$ = duty cycle, $f_s$ = switching frequency, $T_s = 1/f_s$, $R$ = load, $I_o$ = output current, $\Delta i_L$ = peak-to-peak inductor ripple current.

| # | Parameter | Buck Converter | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Conversion Ratio | $V_o = D \, V_s$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{D}{1-D} \, V_s$ |
| 2 | Average Inductor Current $I_L$ | $I_L = I_o$ | $I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ | $I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ |
| 3 | Average Source Current $I_s$ | $I_s = D \, I_o$ | $I_s = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ | $I_s = \dfrac{D \, I_o}{1-D}$ |
| 4 | Inductor Ripple $\Delta i_L$ | $\dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{L f_s}$ | $\dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{L f_s}$ *(expressed via $V_o$)* | $\dfrac{V_s D}{L f_s}$ |
| 5 | Critical Inductance $L_c$ | $\dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ |
| 6 | Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_o$ | $\dfrac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^{\,2}}$ | $\dfrac{I_o D}{C f_s} = \dfrac{V_o D}{R C f_s}$ | $\dfrac{I_o D}{C f_s} = \dfrac{\vert V_o \vert D}{R C f_s}$ |
| 7 | Critical (Boundary) Resistance $R_c$ | $\dfrac{2 f_s L}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{2 f_s L}{D(1-D)^2}$ | $\dfrac{2 f_s L}{(1-D)^2}$ |
| 8 | Switch Voltage Stress $V_{T,\text{max}}$ | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + \vert V_o \vert$ |
| 9 | Diode Voltage Stress $V_{D,\text{max}}$ | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + \vert V_o \vert$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Question 4ŌĆō6 above (ripple and $L_c$) are *favourite* 14-mark topics. Memorise row 5 ŌĆö it is asked **every year**.

### 2.6 Continuous vs Discontinuous Conduction ŌĆö Boundary Condition

A converter operates at the **CCM / DCM boundary** when the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF interval:

$$I_{L,\text{min}} = I_L - \frac{\Delta i_L}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_L = \frac{\Delta i_L}{2}$$

Substituting the appropriate $I_L$ and $\Delta i_L$ expressions yields the **critical inductance** $L_c$ in row 5 of the table above.

**Engineering interpretation:** If the actual inductance $L < L_c$, the converter enters DCM ŌĆö output voltage then depends on $D$, $L$, $f_s$, and $R$ (not on $D$ alone).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, board-exam-grade derivations** for every formula in the table above. **No step is skipped.**

---

### 3.1 Derivation of Buck Converter Output Voltage $V_o$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify inductor voltage in each sub-interval.**

During ON: $v_L = V_s - V_o$

During OFF: $v_L = -V_o$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance.**

$$(V_s - V_o)(DT_s) + (-V_o)\big((1-D)T_s\big) = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cancel $T_s$.**

$$(V_s - V_o) D - V_o (1-D) = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand and collect terms.**

$$V_s D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D = 0$$

$$V_s D - V_o = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_o = D \, V_s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Boost Converter Output Voltage $V_o$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify inductor voltage in each sub-interval.**

During ON: $v_L = V_s$

During OFF: $v_L = V_s - V_o$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance.**

$$V_s (DT_s) + (V_s - V_o)\big((1-D)T_s\big) = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cancel $T_s$.**

$$V_s D + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand.**

$$V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o + V_o D = 0$$

$$V_s - V_o + V_o D = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $V_o$.**

$$V_s = V_o (1 - D)$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Buck-Boost Converter Output Voltage $V_o$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify inductor voltage in each sub-interval.**

During ON: $v_L = V_s$

During OFF: $v_L = -V_o$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply volt-second balance.**

$$V_s (DT_s) + (-V_o)\big((1-D)T_s\big) = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cancel $T_s$ and solve.**

$$V_s D - V_o (1-D) = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_o = - \frac{D}{1-D} \, V_s}$$

The **minus sign** confirms output polarity inversion.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Critical Inductance $L_c$ ŌĆö Buck Converter

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expression for inductor ripple current.**

During ON, the inductor sees a constant voltage $(V_s - V_o)$ for time $DT_s$:

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{(V_s - V_o) D T_s}{L} = \frac{V_s (1-D) D}{L f_s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö At CCM/DCM boundary, $I_L = \dfrac{\Delta i_L}{2}$.**

In a buck converter, by charge balance on the output capacitor, $I_L = I_o$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and equate.**

$$I_o = \frac{V_s D (1-D)}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Replace $V_s$ using $V_o = D V_s \Rightarrow V_s = V_o / D$.**

$$I_o = \frac{(V_o / D) \cdot D (1-D)}{2 L f_s} = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Use Ohm's law $V_o = I_o R$.**

$$I_o = \frac{I_o R (1-D)}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Cancel $I_o$ and solve.**

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Critical Inductance $L_c$ ŌĆö Boost Converter

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inductor ripple (during ON).**

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \, D}{L f_s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Average inductor current via power balance.**

In a boost converter, only during ON does the source deliver current equal to $i_L$. Hence:

$$I_s = D \cdot I_L$$

Power balance: $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$

$$V_s \cdot D \cdot I_L = V_o \cdot I_o = \frac{V_s \, I_o}{1-D}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad I_L = \frac{I_o}{D(1-D)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Boundary condition $I_L = \dfrac{\Delta i_L}{2}$.**

$$\frac{I_o}{D(1-D)} = \frac{V_s D}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $I_o = V_o / R = V_s / \big[R(1-D)\big]$.**

$$\frac{V_s}{D(1-D)^2 R} = \frac{V_s D}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Cancel $V_s$ and solve.**

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{D (1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation of Critical Inductance $L_c$ ŌĆö Buck-Boost Converter

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inductor ripple (during ON).**

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s D}{L f_s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Average inductor current via power balance.**

During ON, source current = $i_L$; average source current $I_s = D \cdot I_L$.

Power balance $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$:

$$V_s \cdot D \cdot I_L = V_o I_o = -\frac{D}{1-D} V_s \cdot I_o$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad I_L = \frac{I_o}{1-D}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Boundary condition.**

$$\frac{I_o}{1-D} = \frac{V_s D}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $I_o = \vert V_o \vert / R = \dfrac{V_s D}{(1-D) R}$.**

$$\frac{V_s D}{(1-D)^2 R} = \frac{V_s D}{2 L f_s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Cancel $V_s D$.**

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}$$

---

### 3.7 Derivation of Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_o$

#### (a) Buck Converter

Output capacitor supplies the *difference* between inductor current and load current. For small ripple:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s C}$$

Substituting $\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{L f_s}$:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{V_s D (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^{\,2}} = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^{\,2}}$$

#### (b) Boost / Buck-Boost Converter

In both, the output diode conducts **only during $(1-D)T_s$**. So the load is *disconnected* from the inductor for $DT_s$ each cycle, and the capacitor alone supplies the load:

Charge lost by capacitor per cycle: $\Delta Q = I_o \cdot D T_s$

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta Q}{C} = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s} = \frac{V_o D}{R C f_s}$$

---

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem.** A buck converter has $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$, $D = 0.4$, $L = 200 \, \mu H$, $C = 100 \, \mu F$, $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz}$, and supplies a load $R = 10 \, \Omega$. Determine $V_o$, $I_o$, $\Delta i_L$, and $\Delta V_o$.

**Solution.**

Step 1: Output voltage:
$$V_o = D V_s = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ V}$$

Step 2: Output current:
$$I_o = V_o / R = 9.6 / 10 = 0.96 \text{ A}$$

Step 3: Inductor ripple:
$$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s D (1-D)}{L f_s} = \frac{24 \times 0.4 \times 0.6}{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{3}} = \frac{5.76}{5} = 1.152 \text{ A}$$

Step 4: Output voltage ripple:
$$\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^{\,2}} = \frac{9.6 \times 0.6}{8 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (25 \times 10^{3})^2}$$

Compute denominator:
$$8 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{8} = 1 \times 10^{2} = 100$$

So:
$$\Delta V_o = 5.76 / 100 = 0.0576 \text{ V} \approx 57.6 \text{ mV}$$

Step 5: Check CCM. Critical inductance:
$$L_c = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.6 \times 10}{2 \times 25 \times 10^{3}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} = 120 \, \mu H$$

Since $L = 200 \, \mu H > L_c = 120 \, \mu H$, the converter operates in **CCM** Ō£ō.

> [!TIP]
> Always verify CCM at the end of any numerical. This shows the examiner "full understanding" ŌĆö easy **2 bonus marks**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered as **functional / topological flowcharts** because the physical circuits (with their four discrete components) are most clearly represented as Mermaid block diagrams. Each block corresponds to a sub-component, and arrows indicate the **direction of current flow** in the two operating states.

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö All Three Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputNode["DC SOURCE Vs"]:::src
    BuckBlock["BUCK CONVERTER\nSwitch S in series with L\nDiode across L to ground\nOutput V_o less than Vs"]:::buck
    BoostBlock["BOOST CONVERTER\nL in series with source\nSwitch S to ground after L\nDiode forward to output\nOutput V_o greater than Vs"]:::boost
    BuckBoostBlock["BUCK-BOOST CONVERTER\nL in series with source\nSwitch S to ground after L\nDiode from L-switch node to output\nOutput inverted polarity\nMagnitude up or down"]:::bb
    LoadNode["DC LOAD R"]:::load

    InputNode --> BuckBlock
    InputNode --> BoostBlock
    InputNode --> BuckBoostBlock
    BuckBlock --> LoadNode
    BoostBlock --> LoadNode
    BuckBoostBlock --> LoadNode

    classDef src fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buck fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef boost fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bb fill:#FAE8FF,stroke:#7E22CE,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Operating-State Flowchart ŌĆö Buck Converter

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Start
    Start --> S_ON : t = 0  PWM gate HIGH
    S_ON : Diode reverse biased
    S_ON : Inductor charges  vL = Vs minus Vo
    S_ON : iL ramps UP linearly
    S_ON --> S_OFF : t = D Ts
    S_OFF : Diode forward biased  freewheel
    S_OFF : Inductor discharges  vL = minus Vo
    S_OFF : iL ramps DOWN linearly
    S_OFF --> S_ON : t = Ts  PWM gate HIGH again
    S_OFF --> [*] : DCM if iL reaches zero
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Boost Converter Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StateA["STATE A  SWITCH ON"]:::on
    StateB["STATE B  SWITCH OFF"]:::off

    subgraph OnMode["During D Ts"]
        Vs_A["Source Vs"] -->|current rises| L_A["Inductor L\nstores energy"]
        C_A["Capacitor C"] -->|discharges into| Load_A["Load R"]
    end

    subgraph OffMode["During 1 minus D Ts"]
        Vs_B["Source Vs"] --> L_B["Inductor L\nreleases energy"]
        L_B -->|via diode D| C_B["Capacitor C\ncharges"]
        C_B --> Load_B["Load R"]
    end

    StateA --- OnMode
    StateB --- OffMode

    classDef on fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef off fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Buck-Boost Energy Flow (with Polarity Inversion)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InNode["INPUT Vs\npositive terminal top"]:::src
    L1["INDUCTOR L\nstores energy in DTs"]:::l
    SW1["SWITCH S\nto ground"]:::sw
    D1["DIODE D\nforward to output"]:::d
    CoNode["OUTPUT NODE\npolarity INVERTED\nminus sign on Vo"]:::out
    Cn["CAPACITOR C parallel R"]:::cap

    InNode --> L1
    L1 --> SW1
    L1 --> D1
    D1 --> CoNode
    CoNode --> Cn
    CoNode -.->|"Vo is NEGATIVE w.r.t. ground"| LoadPol["Load R sees -D by 1 minus D times Vs"]

    classDef src fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef l fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FAE8FF,stroke:#7E22CE,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree ŌĆö CCM vs DCM (Examination Heuristic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is actual L\ngreater than Lc ?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> CCM["CCM\nVo depends only on D and Vs"]
    Q1 -- NO  --> DCM["DCM\nVo depends on D L R and fs"]
    Q1 -- EQUAL --> BC["BOUNDARY CONDITION\ninductor current just touches zero"]

    CCM -.-> Note1["Use formula sheet row 1 only"]
    DCM -.-> Note2["Use modified transfer function"]
    BC -.-> Note3["Use Lc formula from row 5"]

    classDef ccm fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dcm fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef note2 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class DCM dcm

    class Note2 note2
    class Note3 note3
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In 14-mark problems, always draw the **two current paths** (one for switch ON, one for switch OFF) ŌĆö this alone is worth **3-4 marks** even before any calculation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Set A]**
>
> **Q1.** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a buck converter in CCM. Derive the expression for its output voltage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A buck converter consists of a controlled switch S (MOSFET), inductor L, freewheeling diode D, filter capacitor C, and load R connected across a DC source $V_s$.

- **Switch ON (duration $DT_s$):** Diode D is reverse-biased. Inductor stores energy; current rises linearly. Both source and inductor supply the load; capacitor charges.
- **Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):** Inductor current freewheels through D. Capacitor maintains load voltage.

Applying volt-second balance to the inductor:

$$(V_s - V_o) D - V_o (1-D) = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$

Since $0 \le D \le 1$, $V_o \le V_s$ ŌĆö hence *step-down*. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Set B]**
>
> **Q2.** Compare buck, boost and buck-boost converters on the basis of (i) output voltage expression, (ii) polarity of output, and (iii) switch stress voltage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Buck | Boost | Buck-Boost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_o$ expression | $D \, V_s$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D} V_s$ |
| Output polarity | Same as input | Same as input | **Inverted** |
| Switch stress $V_{T,\text{max}}$ | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + \vert V_o \vert$ |

Buck and boost are non-inverting; buck-boost **inverts** the output polarity. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**
>
> ### QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a circuit diagram and steady-state waveforms, explain the operation of a **boost converter** in continuous conduction mode (CCM). Derive the expression for its output voltage $V_o$ in terms of input voltage $V_s$ and duty cycle $D$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the boost converter in part (a), a load $R = 20 \, \Omega$ is supplied from $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$ at switching frequency $f_s = 20 \text{ kHz}$ and duty cycle $D = 0.5$. Determine: (i) output voltage $V_o$, (ii) average inductor current $I_L$, (iii) critical inductance $L_c$, and (iv) minimum capacitance $C_{\min}$ if the allowable output voltage ripple is $\Delta V_o / V_o \le 5\%$.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Operation & Derivation [7 marks]**

The boost converter consists of an inductor L in series with the source $V_s$, a controlled switch S (MOSFET) connecting the L-switch node to ground, a diode D with anode at the L-switch node and cathode at the output, and an output capacitor C across the load.

**Mode 1 ŌĆö Switch ON ($DT_s$):**
- Diode D is reverse-biased (output node sees negative voltage).
- Inductor is connected directly across $V_s$; current builds up linearly.
- Capacitor C discharges into the load; load is isolated from the source.

**Mode 2 ŌĆö Switch OFF ($(1-D)T_s$):**
- Inductor current cannot change instantaneously; it forward-biases diode D.
- Source and inductor together charge capacitor C and supply the load.
- Inductor current ramps down.

**Derivation of $V_o$:**

Applying volt-second balance across the inductor:

$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o + V_o D = 0$$

$$V_s = V_o (1-D)$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}}$$

Since $0 < D < 1$, we have $V_o > V_s$, confirming the **step-up** action. **[7 marks]**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$, $R = 20 \, \Omega$, $f_s = 20 \text{ kHz}$, $D = 0.5$, ripple ratio $K = 0.05$.

**(i) Output voltage $V_o$:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D} = \frac{24}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{24}{0.5} = 48 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Average inductor current $I_L$:**

For a boost converter, $I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{D(1-D)}$, where $I_o = V_o / R$:

$$I_o = \frac{48}{20} = 2.4 \text{ A}$$

$$I_L = \frac{2.4}{0.5 \times 0.5} = \frac{2.4}{0.25} = 9.6 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Critical inductance $L_c$:**

$$L_c = \frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.5 \times (0.5)^2 \times 20}{2 \times 20 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$L_c = \frac{0.5 \times 0.25 \times 20}{4 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{2.5}{40000} = 62.5 \, \mu H$$

**(iv) Minimum capacitance $C_{\min}$:**

Output ripple formula for boost converter:

$$\frac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \frac{D}{R C f_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{\min} = \frac{D}{R f_s \cdot K}$$

$$C_{\min} = \frac{0.5}{20 \times 20 \times 10^{3} \times 0.05} = \frac{0.5}{20000} = 25 \, \mu F$$

**Final answers:** $V_o = 48 \text{ V}$, $I_L = 9.6 \text{ A}$, $L_c = 62.5 \, \mu H$, $C_{\min} = 25 \, \mu F$. **[7 marks]**

---

> ### QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **buck-boost converter**. Show that the output voltage is given by $V_o = -D/(1-D) \cdot V_s$. Comment on the polarity of the output.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A buck-boost converter operates at $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz}$ with $D = 0.4$, $V_s = 30 \text{ V}$, $L = 250 \, \mu H$, $C = 80 \, \mu F$, and $R = 15 \, \Omega$. Determine: (i) output voltage $V_o$, (ii) load current $I_o$, (iii) average inductor current $I_L$, (iv) ripple in inductor current $\Delta i_L$, and (v) ripple in output voltage $\Delta V_o$.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Operation & Derivation [7 marks]**

The buck-boost converter has the inductor L in series with the source $V_s$. The switch S connects the L-switch node to ground. The diode D has its anode at the L-switch node and cathode at the output node, where C and R are connected in parallel to ground.

**Mode 1 ŌĆö Switch ON ($DT_s$):**
- Diode D is reverse-biased; load is isolated from the source.
- Inductor charges from the source: $v_L = V_s$, $i_L$ ramps up.

**Mode 2 ŌĆö Switch OFF ($(1-D)T_s$):**
- Inductor voltage reverses polarity, forward-biasing diode D.
- Inductor transfers stored energy to the capacitor and load.
- The output node is driven *negative* with respect to ground ŌĆö **polarity inversion**.

**Derivation:**

Applying volt-second balance across the inductor:

$$V_s \cdot D + (-V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_o = -\frac{D}{1-D} V_s}$$

The **negative sign** confirms that the output polarity is **inverted** with respect to the input. The magnitude can be either greater than or less than $V_s$, depending on the duty cycle D. **[7 marks]**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_s = 30 \text{ V}$, $D = 0.4$, $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz}$, $L = 250 \, \mu H$, $C = 80 \, \mu F$, $R = 15 \, \Omega$.

**(i) Output voltage $V_o$:**

$$V_o = -\frac{D}{1-D} V_s = -\frac{0.4}{0.6} \times 30 = -20 \text{ V}$$

Magnitude: $\vert V_o \vert = 20 \text{ V}$ (negative polarity).

**(ii) Load current $I_o$:**

$$I_o = \frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R} = \frac{20}{15} = 1.333 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Average inductor current $I_L$:**

$$I_L = \frac{I_o}{1-D} = \frac{1.333}{0.6} = 2.222 \text{ A}$$

**(iv) Inductor ripple current $\Delta i_L$:**

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s D}{L f_s} = \frac{30 \times 0.4}{250 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$= \frac{12}{6.25} = 1.92 \text{ A}$$

**(v) Output voltage ripple $\Delta V_o$:**

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{\vert V_o \vert D}{R C f_s} = \frac{20 \times 0.4}{15 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{3}}$$

Denominator: $15 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{3} = 15 \times 2 = 30$

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{8}{30} = 0.267 \text{ V}$$

**Final answers:** $V_o = -20 \text{ V}$, $I_o = 1.333 \text{ A}$, $I_L = 2.222 \text{ A}$, $\Delta i_L = 1.92 \text{ A}$, $\Delta V_o = 0.267 \text{ V}$. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Dropping the negative sign** in buck-boost $V_o$ ŌåÆ loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the inductor current expression** $I_L$ between buck and boost ŌĆö for buck, $I_L = I_o$; for boost / buck-boost, $I_L = I_o / (1-D)$ or $I_o / [D(1-D)]$ ŌåÆ lose **1 mark per error**.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify CCM** at the end of a numerical (compare $L$ with $L_c$) ŌåÆ lose **1-2 marks** even if final answer is correct.
> 4. **Wrong ripple formula** ŌĆö buck uses $\Delta V_o = \Delta i_L / (8 f C)$, but boost / buck-boost use $\Delta V_o = I_o D / (C f)$. **Memorising the wrong one is a 3-mark loss.**
> 5. **Not drawing the two current paths** (ON and OFF) in descriptive questions ŌåÆ lose **up to 3 marks** even if derivations are perfect.
> 6. **Units mismatch** ŌĆö converting $kHz$ to $Hz$ and $\mu H$ to $H$ inconsistently is a classic slip; **always write units in every step**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Buck, Boost & Buck-Boost Converters**

**I. Core Conversion Ratios (must memorise verbatim):**

- **Buck:** $V_o = D V_s$
- **Boost:** $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$
- **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = -D V_s / (1-D)$ *(minus sign mandatory)*

**II. Critical Inductance Formulas:**

- **Buck:** $L_c = (1-D) R / (2 f_s)$
- **Boost:** $L_c = D (1-D)^2 R / (2 f_s)$
- **Buck-Boost:** $L_c = (1-D)^2 R / (2 f_s)$

**III. Key Operating Principles:**

- **Volt-second balance** across L: average $v_L = 0$ in steady state.
- **Charge balance** on C: average $i_C = 0$ in steady state.
- Inductor current is **continuous** in CCM; output current is **discontinuous** for boost and buck-boost (because diode conducts only during OFF).

**IV. Polarity & Stress Summary:**

- Buck and Boost ŌåÆ output **same polarity** as input.
- Buck-Boost ŌåÆ output **inverted polarity**.
- Switch & diode see peak inverse voltage $V_s$ (buck), $V_o$ (boost), $V_s + \vert V_o \vert$ (buck-boost).

**V. Energy Flow During Modes:**

| Mode | Buck | Boost | Buck-Boost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switch ON | L charges from $V_s$ | L charges from $V_s$ | L charges from $V_s$ |
| Switch OFF | L discharges to load | L + $V_s$ discharge to load | L discharges (inverted) to load |

**VI. Exam-Day Heuristics:**

- For numerical problems, always compute **$V_o$ first**, then **$I_o = V_o/R$**, then **$L_c$**, then check **$L > L_c$** to confirm CCM.
- For descriptive problems, always include: **(1) circuit, (2) two operating modes, (3) waveforms, (4) volt-second balance, (5) derivation, (6) polarity comment**.
- Switching frequency $f_s$ for IGBT-based converters is typically **5-25 kHz**; for MOSFET-based converters it can go up to **several hundred kHz**.
- A *practical* design always chooses $L > L_c$ (typically 1.25 to 2 times) to ensure CCM under load transients.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Devices, Electrical and Thermal Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Devices, Electrical and Thermal Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Selection of Power Semiconductor Devices** is the systematic engineering process of choosing an appropriate power switching device (SCR, BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO, etc.) for a specific power electronic converter application, based on rigorous evaluation of **electrical ratings, thermal limits, switching characteristics, and reliability constraints**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Stress** refers to the instantaneous or sustained electrical loading (voltage, current, $dV/dt$, $di/dt$) imposed on a power device that approaches or exceeds its rated blocking/forward limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Stress** refers to the cumulative heat generation within a semiconductor junction due to conduction and switching losses, which elevates the **junction temperature ($T_j$)** beyond safe operating limits if not properly managed via a heat sink and cooling system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a power electronic device like a **heavy-duty water tap (faucet)** connected to a high-pressure water pipeline:
- The **electrical rating** is the maximum pressure (voltage) and flow rate (current) the tap walls can withstand before bursting.
- **Electrical stress** is the sudden "water hammer" effect when the tap is slammed shut too fast ($dV/dt$ or $di/dt$ surge).
- **Thermal stress** is like the tap becoming **hot** after continuous use ŌĆö you need a **heat sink (radiator fins)** to dissipate the heat into the surrounding air, or else the tap melts.
- **Selection of devices** is like choosing the correct tap size, material (brass vs. plastic), and pressure rating for your specific pipeline.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

The following **standard metrics** must be considered during device selection:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Rated Voltage** | $V_{RRM}$, $V_{DRM}$ | **600 V ŌĆō 6500 V** |
| **Rated Current (RMS/Avg)** | $I_{T(AVG)}$, $I_{D}$ | **10 A ŌĆō 3600 A** |
| **Junction Temperature** | $T_j$ | **ŌĆō40 ┬░C to +150 ┬░C** (Si), **+175 ┬░C** (SiC) |
| **Thermal Resistance** | $R_{th}$ | **0.02 ŌĆō 3.0 ┬░C/W** |
| **Switching Frequency** | $f_{sw}$ | **1 kHz (SCR) ŌĆō 1 MHz (GaN)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** A device is *never* operated at 100% of its rating. Industrial practice demands a **derating factor of 70%ŌĆō80%** on voltage and **50%ŌĆō60%** on current to ensure long-term reliability.

### 1.4 Categories of Stress in Power Devices

```
Electrical Stress
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Over-Voltage Stress (Transient surges, lightning)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Over-Current Stress (Short circuit, inrush)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ dv/dt Stress (False triggering of thyristors)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ di/dt Stress (Localized hot-spot heating)
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Reverse Recovery Stress

Thermal Stress
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Steady-State Thermal Stress (Continuous conduction)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Transient/Cyclic Thermal Stress (Power cycling)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Localized Hot Spots (Non-uniform current density)
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Secondary Breakdown (In BJT)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Device Selection ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Identify Application Category**
- **AC-DC Converter (Rectifier)** ŌåÆ SCR / Thyristor family
- **DC-DC Chopper** ŌåÆ BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT
- **DC-AC Inverter** ŌåÆ IGBT, MOSFET (low power)
- **High-Frequency SMPS** ŌåÆ MOSFET, GaN HEMT, SiC MOSFET
- **HVDC/FACTS** ŌåÆ Thyristor, GTO, IGCT

**Step 2: Determine Blocking Voltage Requirement**
- For a single-phase **full-bridge rectifier** with $V_s = 230$ V RMS, the peak is $V_m = 325$ V.
- PIV of each device = $2V_m = 650$ V.
- Apply a **safety factor of 2 to 3** ŌåÆ select device with $V_{RRM} \geq 1300$ V.

**Step 3: Determine Current Rating**
- Compute average and RMS currents for the converter.
- Apply a **derating factor of 1.5 to 2** on the calculated current.

**Step 4: Evaluate Thermal Budget**
- Compute total power loss: $P_{loss} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$.
- Calculate required thermal resistance from junction to ambient.
- Select appropriate heat sink.

**Step 5: Check Switching Frequency**
- For $f_{sw} > 20$ kHz ŌåÆ prefer MOSFET or IGBT.
- For $f_{sw} < 1$ kHz, high power ŌåÆ SCR/Thyristor.

### 2.2 Why Electrical Stress Matters ŌĆö The Physics

When a power device is subjected to voltage or current beyond its **breakdown voltage ($V_{BR}$)** or **surge current ($I_{TSM}$)** rating, one of the following failure mechanisms occurs:

1. **Avalanche Breakdown** ŌĆö High reverse voltage accelerates carriers, creating a chain reaction.
2. **Second Breakdown (in BJT)** ŌĆö Localized hot spot causes thermal runaway.
3. **dv/dt False Triggering (in thyristors)** ŌĆö Capacitive coupling through junction capacitances injects current, turning the device ON spuriously.
4. **di/dt Failure** ŌĆö Initial turn-on concentrates current near the gate, melting the silicon.

### 2.3 Why Thermal Stress Matters ŌĆö The Physics

The **junction temperature $T_j$** of a semiconductor is governed by the **thermal Ohm's law**:

$$T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \times R_{th(ja)}$$

Where the total thermal resistance from junction to ambient is:

$$R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In an EV traction inverter (Toyota Prius, Tesla Model 3), an IGBT module dissipating 200 W per switch must keep $T_j < 150$ ┬░C. A 1 ┬░C/W error in heat sink calculation reduces the **inverter lifetime by ~10%** for every 10 ┬░C rise (Arrhenius law).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{loss} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Total power dissipation | **W** |
| 2 | $P_{cond} = V_{T(0)} \cdot I_{T(AVG)} + r_T \cdot I_{T(RMS)}^2$ | Conduction loss (thyristor) | **W** |
| 3 | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{cc} I_L (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching loss | **W** |
| 4 | $T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(ja)}$ | Junction temperature | **┬░C** |
| 5 | $R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$ | Total thermal resistance | **┬░C/W** |
| 6 | $P_{max} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_a}{R_{th(ja)}}$ | Max allowable dissipation | **W** |
| 7 | $R_{th(sa)} = \frac{1}{h A_s}$ | Heat sink thermal resistance | **┬░C/W** |
| 8 | $h = 1.42 \left(\frac{\Delta T}{L}\right)^{0.25}$ | Convection coefficient (vertical fin) | **W/m┬▓K** |
| 9 | $\tau_{th} = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ | Thermal time constant | **s** |
| 10 | $V_{RRM} \geq 2 \text{ to } 3 \times V_{m}$ | Voltage derating rule | **V** |
| 11 | $I_{T(AVG)} \geq 1.5 \text{ to } 2 \times I_{calc}$ | Current derating rule | **A** |
| 12 | $MTTF \propto e^{\frac{E_a}{kT_j}}$ | Arrhenius reliability equation | **hours** |
| 13 | $SOA: V \cdot I \leq P_{max, transient}$ | Safe Operating Area limit | **W** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Never confuse $R_{th(jc)}$ (junction-to-case, given in datasheet) with $R_{th(ja)}$ (junction-to-ambient, system-level). Many students write $T_j = T_a + P \cdot R_{th(jc)}$, which is **wrong** unless a perfect infinite heat sink is assumed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Heat Sink Thermal Resistance

**Given:**
- Load: $I_L = 50$ A, $V_s = 230$ V RMS, full-wave controlled bridge.
- Device: SCR with $V_T = 1.8$ V, $R_{th(jc)} = 0.15$ ┬░C/W.
- Ambient: $T_a = 40$ ┬░C, Max $T_j = 125$ ┬░C.
- Switching frequency negligible (line frequency, 50 Hz).

**Find:** Required heat sink resistance $R_{th(sa)}$ and surface area $A_s$.

---

**Step 1: Compute average and RMS thyristor current**

For a full-bridge rectifier with $\alpha = 30┬░$ feeding a highly inductive load, each SCR conducts for $180┬░$. The average and RMS currents per device are:

$$I_{T(AVG)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{2}, \quad I_{T(RMS)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

With $I_{dc} = 50$ A (assumed constant due to freewheeling):

$$I_{T(AVG)} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{T(RMS)} = \frac{50}{\sqrt{2}} = 35.36 \text{ A}$$

---

**Step 2: Compute conduction power loss**

$$P_{loss} = V_T \cdot I_{T(AVG)} + r_T \cdot I_{T(RMS)}^2$$

Assuming $r_T$ is small (idealized) and using $V_T = 1.8$ V:

$$P_{loss} = 1.8 \times 25 = 45 \text{ W}$$

---

**Step 3: Apply thermal Ohm's law for total thermal resistance**

We require $T_j \leq 125$ ┬░C, so:

$$R_{th(ja)} \leq \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_a}{P_{loss}} = \frac{125 - 40}{45} = \frac{85}{45}$$

$$R_{th(ja)} \leq 1.889 \text{ ┬░C/W}$$

---

**Step 4: Decompose the total resistance**

$$R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$$

With $R_{th(jc)} = 0.15$ ┬░C/W and $R_{th(cs)} = 0.05$ ┬░C/W (mica insulator with thermal grease):

$$1.889 = 0.15 + 0.05 + R_{th(sa)}$$

$$R_{th(sa)} = 1.889 - 0.20 = 1.689 \text{ ┬░C/W}$$

---

**Step 5: Calculate heat sink surface area**

For natural convection with vertical fins:

$$R_{th(sa)} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A_s} \implies A_s = \frac{1}{h \cdot R_{th(sa)}}$$

With $h = 8$ W/m┬▓K (typical for natural convection):

$$A_s = \frac{1}{8 \times 1.689} = \frac{1}{13.512}$$

$$A_s \approx 0.074 \text{ m}^2 = 740 \text{ cm}^2$$

> **Engineering Insight:** If forced air cooling is used with $h = 25$ W/m┬▓K, the surface area drops to **237 cm┬▓**, allowing a much smaller heat sink.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: SOA (Safe Operating Area) Verification

For a BJT/MOSFET in a switching application, the SOA is bounded by four limits:

1. **Maximum RMS current limit** ŌĆö bounded by lead bonding.
2. **Maximum peak current limit** ŌĆö bounded by second breakdown.
3. **Maximum voltage limit** ŌĆö bounded by avalanche breakdown.
4. **Thermal limit** ŌĆö $P_D = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_c}{R_{th(jc)}}$

The **thermal-limited DC SOA** equation is derived as follows:

$$P_{D(max,DC)} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_c}{R_{th(jc)}}$$

For a **pulsed** operation with pulse width $t_p$ and duty cycle $D$:

$$P_{D(max,pulse)} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_c}{R_{th(jc)} \cdot Z_{th(t)}}$$

Where $Z_{th(t)}$ is the **transient thermal impedance** (from the datasheet curve). For a single pulse:

$$Z_{th(t)} = R_{th(jc)} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)$$

Therefore, the **SOA pulse limit** is:

$$V_{DS} \cdot I_{D} \leq \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_c}{R_{th(jc)} \left(1 - e^{-t_p/\tau_{th}}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: dv/dt Triggering Limit for SCRs

A thyristor can be falsely triggered if the rate of rise of anode-to-cathode voltage exceeds a critical value. The dv/dt triggers a displacement current through the junction capacitance $C_j$:

$$i_{disp} = C_j \frac{dV}{dt}$$

For false triggering, this displacement current must equal the **latching current** $I_L$:

$$C_j \frac{dV}{dt} \geq I_L \implies \left(\frac{dV}{dt}\right)_{critical} = \frac{I_L}{C_j}$$

> **Example:** If $C_j = 100$ pF and $I_L = 10$ mA:

$$\left(\frac{dV}{dt}\right)_{critical} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = 100 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$$

This is why **RC snubber circuits** are mandatory across the SCR terminals in high-dV/dt applications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Device Selection Comparison

| Criterion | SCR | BJT | MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage rating | **Up to 12 kV** | Up to 1.2 kV | Up to 1.2 kV | **Up to 6.5 kV** |
| Current rating | **Up to 6000 A** | Up to 800 A | Up to 200 A | Up to 3600 A |
| Switching freq. | **< 400 Hz** | < 10 kHz | **Up to 1 MHz** | Up to 100 kHz |
| Conduction drop | Medium (1.5ŌĆō2.5 V) | High (2ŌĆō3 V) | High ($I \cdot R_{DS}$) | Medium (1.5ŌĆō2.5 V) |
| dv/dt rating | **Low (needs snubber)** | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| Drive complexity | **Low (pulse gate)** | Complex (Baker clamp) | **Simple (V_GS)** | Simple (V_GE) |
| Cost per kVA | **Lowest** | Low | High (low power) | Medium |
| Typical application | HVDC, DC drives, heaters | Old inverters | SMPS, low-power drives | **VFDs, EV, solar inverters** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Thermal Equivalent Circuit (Cauer Network)

graph TD
    Pdiss["Power Dissipation P_loss in W"] -->|"Heat flow"| Rjc["R_th_jc Junction to Case"]
    Rjc -->|"Heat flow"| Rcs["R_th_cs Case to Sink (Insulator + Grease)"]

```

**Interpretation:** The diagram models heat flow analogously to current flow in an electrical circuit, where **temperature is analogous to voltage** and **heat flow is analogous to current**.

---

### 4.2 Device Selection Decision Flowchart

    H --> J

    E --> J

    K --> L["Select Heat Sink and Cooling Method"]
    L --> M["Verify SOA at Worst Case"]

```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Protected Power Module
