## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*List any three factors that govern the selection of a power semiconductor device for a converter application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary factors governing device selection are:

1. **Voltage and Current Ratings** ŌĆö The device must have a peak repetitive reverse voltage ($V_{RRM}$) of at least 2ŌĆō3 times the peak circuit voltage, and an average current rating 1.5ŌĆō2 times the calculated load current, to accommodate transient surges. **(1 mark)**

2. **Switching Frequency and Speed** ŌĆö For high-frequency applications (SMPS, resonant converters above 20 kHz), MOSFETs or IGBTs are preferred; for low-frequency, high-power rectifiers, SCRs/thyristors are suitable. **(1 mark)**

3. **Thermal Characteristics and Power Dissipation** ŌĆö The device's junction-to-case thermal resistance ($R_{th(jc)}$) and maximum junction temperature ($T_{j(max)}$) determine the heat sink design and cooling method. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define thermal resistance $R_{th}$. What is its SI unit?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference between two specified points (e.g., junction and case) to the steady-state heat flow (power dissipation) between them.

$$R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{P_{loss}} = \frac{T_j - T_c}{P_{loss}}$$

**SI Unit (1 mark):** **┬░C/W** (degree Celsius per Watt), which is dimensionally equivalent to K/W.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** A thyristor is used in a single-phase full-wave controlled rectifier supplying a highly inductive load of $I_{dc} = 40$ A. The device has the following specifications: $V_T = 1.6$ V, $R_{th(jc)} = 0.2$ ┬░C/W, $T_{j(max)} = 125$ ┬░C. Ambient temperature is $T_a = 35$ ┬░C. The thermal resistance of the insulator and heat sink compound is $R_{th(cs)} = 0.1$ ┬░C/W. Determine:
1. The conduction loss per thyristor.
2. The maximum allowable thermal resistance of the heat sink $R_{th(sa)}$.
3. The minimum surface area of the heat sink if the convective heat transfer coefficient is $h = 10$ W/m┬▓K. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **electrical stresses** that a power semiconductor device is subjected to. Discuss how each stress is mitigated. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**(i) Conduction Loss Calculation (2 marks):**

For a single-phase full-wave rectifier with highly inductive load, each thyristor conducts for $180┬░$. Therefore:

$$I_{T(AVG)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ A}$$

The conduction power loss per thyristor is:

$$P_{loss} = V_T \cdot I_{T(AVG)} = 1.6 \times 20 = 32 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Heat Sink Thermal Resistance (3 marks):**

Total thermal resistance required:

$$R_{th(ja)} \leq \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_a}{P_{loss}} = \frac{125 - 35}{32} = \frac{90}{32} = 2.8125 \text{ ┬░C/W}$$

Since $R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$:

$$R_{th(sa)} = R_{th(ja)} - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)} = 2.8125 - 0.2 - 0.1 = 2.5125 \text{ ┬░C/W}$$

**(iii) Heat Sink Surface Area (2 marks):**

From $R_{th(sa)} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A_s}$:

$$A_s = \frac{1}{h \cdot R_{th(sa)}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2.5125} = 0.0398 \text{ m}^2 \approx 398 \text{ cm}^2$$

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Electrical Stresses in Power Semiconductor Devices (7 marks):**

| Stress | Description | Mitigation Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Overvoltage Stress (1.5 marks)** | Surge voltages from load switching, lightning, or commutation transients can exceed $V_{RRM}$ causing avalanche breakdown. | Use of **metal-oxide varistors (MOV)**, **RC snubbers**, and selection of devices with 2ŌĆō3├Ś voltage derating. |
| **Overcurrent Stress (1.5 marks)** | Short circuits, inrush currents, and load faults cause surge currents beyond $I_{TSM}$, leading to fusing of bond wires. | **Fast-acting fuses**, **current-limiting reactors**, and **electronic overcurrent protection** with sub-microsecond trip times. |
| **dv/dt Stress (2 marks)** | High rate of rise of anode voltage injects displacement current through $C_j$, falsely triggering the thyristor. Critical limit: $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_L}{C_j}$ | **RC snubber networks** across the device to limit the rate of voltage rise, and selection of devices with high $\frac{dV}{dt}$ rating. |
| **di/dt Stress (2 marks)** | High initial rate of current rise at turn-on concentrates current near the gate region, causing localized hot spots and thermal failure. | **Series inductor** in the anode circuit, **amplifying gates** (finger gates) for distributed turn-on, and **soft-start circuits**. |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper Module 3, Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **thermal equivalent circuit** of a power semiconductor device mounted on a heat sink. Derive the expression for steady-state junction temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power MOSFET is used in a DC-DC chopper circuit. The device has the following ratings: $V_{DS(max)} = 200$ V, $I_{D(max)} = 30$ A, $R_{th(jc)} = 1.5$ ┬░C/W, $T_{j(max)} = 150$ ┬░C. The switching frequency is 25 kHz, with turn-on time $t_{on} = 0.3$ ┬Ąs and turn-off time $t_{off} = 0.2$ ┬Ąs. The average drain current is 15 A and conduction drop $V_{DS(on)} = 1.2$ V. Calculate:
1. Conduction loss.
2. Switching loss per cycle.
3. Total power dissipation.
4. Maximum allowable case temperature $T_c$ for safe operation if $T_a = 40$ ┬░C and $R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)} = 4.5$ ┬░C/W. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Thermal Equivalent Circuit (4 marks):**

A power semiconductor device mounted on a heat sink can be modeled as a **series combination of thermal resistances** analogous to an electrical resistive circuit:

- **Junction-to-case** thermal resistance: $R_{th(jc)}$ ŌĆö represents heat conduction through the silicon die and base plate.
- **Case-to-sink** thermal resistance: $R_{th(cs)}$ ŌĆö represents the interface material (mica, silicone grease).
- **Sink-to-ambient** thermal resistance: $R_{th(sa)}$ ŌĆö represents convective and radiative heat transfer from the heat sink fins.

```
T_j ---[R_th(jc)]--- T_c ---[R_th(cs)]--- T_s ---[R_th(sa)]--- T_a
                  |                      |                   |
                (C_th_jc)              (C_th_cs)           (C_th_sa)
```

The **thermal capacitances** $C_{th}$ model the heat storage capacity of each layer, important for transient analysis.

**Derivation of Steady-State Junction Temperature (3 marks):**

Under steady state, the thermal capacitances are fully charged and no current flows into them. Heat flow (analogous to current) is:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{T_j - T_c}{R_{th(jc)}} = \frac{T_c - T_s}{R_{th(cs)}} = \frac{T_s - T_a}{R_{th(sa)}}$$

Adding the three temperature drops:

$$T_j - T_a = P_{loss} \left( R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)} \right)$$

$$\boxed{T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(ja)}}$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Conduction Loss (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{cond} = V_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{D(AVG)} = 1.2 \times 15 = 18 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Switching Loss (2.5 marks):**

For a chopper, the switching loss per cycle is:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

Assuming the supply voltage is at the device rating $V_{DS} = 200$ V and $I_D = 15$ A:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 15 \times (0.3 + 0.2) \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^3$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 15 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^3$$

$$P_{sw} = 18.75 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Total Power Dissipation (1 mark):**

$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 18 + 18.75 = 36.75 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Maximum Allowable Case Temperature (2 marks):**

Total thermal resistance junction to ambient:

$$R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)} = 1.5 + 4.5 = 6.0 \text{ ┬░C/W}$$

For safe operation, $T_j \leq 150$ ┬░C, so:

$$T_c = T_j - P_{total} \cdot R_{th(jc)} = 150 - 36.75 \times 1.5$$

$$T_c = 150 - 55.125 = 94.875 \text{ ┬░C}$$

$$\boxed{T_{c(max)} \approx 94.9 \text{ ┬░C}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting derating factors** (2├Ś for voltage, 1.5ŌĆō2├Ś for current) ŌĆö KTU examiners explicitly check for this. Marks lost: 1ŌĆō2 per question.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_{th(jc)}$ and $R_{th(ja)}$** ŌĆö Using $R_{th(jc)}$ instead of total $R_{th(ja)}$ in $T_j$ calculation is the #1 thermal error.
> 3. **Omitting snubber protection discussion** in dv/dt and di/dt questions ŌĆö minimum 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in final answers (┬░C/W, cm┬▓, V/┬Ąs) ŌĆö partial mark deductions.
> 5. **Not drawing the thermal equivalent circuit** when asked ŌĆö full 1ŌĆō2 marks lost in "explain with diagram" sub-questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Device selection is a 5-step process**: Application ŌåÆ Voltage ŌåÆ Current ŌåÆ Thermal ŌåÆ Frequency.
- [x] **Voltage derating rule:** $V_{RRM} \geq 2\text{ to }3 \times V_{m,\text{peak}}$.
- [x] **Current derating rule:** $I_{T(AVG)} \geq 1.5\text{ to }2 \times I_{\text{calc}}$.
- [x] **Master formula:** $T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(ja)}$.
- [x] **Total thermal resistance:** $R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$.
- [x] **Four key electrical stresses:** Overvoltage, Overcurrent, $dV/dt$, $di/dt$.
- [x] **dv/dt triggering limit:** $\left(\frac{dV}{dt}\right)_c = \frac{I_L}{C_j}$ ŌĆö mitigated by RC snubbers.
- [x] **di/dt failure:** mitigated by series inductors and amplifying (finger) gates.
- [x] **SOA (Safe Operating Area)** is bounded by current, voltage, and thermal dissipation limits.
- [x] **Transient thermal impedance** $Z_{th(t)}$ is critical for pulse power calculations.
- [x] **Arrhenius rule:** Every 10 ┬░C rise in $T_j$ halves the device MTTF.
- [x] **Forced air cooling** reduces heat sink size by **~3├Ś** compared to natural convection.
- [x] **SCRs** are preferred for $>100$ kW, low-frequency, high-voltage applications.
- [x] **MOSFETs** are preferred for high-frequency ($>50$ kHz), low-voltage applications.
- [x] **IGBTs** are the workhorse for medium-power, medium-frequency VFDs and solar inverters.
- [x] **Always include protection:** Snubbers, fuses, MOVs, gate protection, and thermal sensors (NTC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolated Converters: Half Bridge and Full Bridge Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isolated Converters: Half Bridge and Full Bridge Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Isolated DCŌĆōDC Converters** are switched-mode power converters in which the input (primary) and output (secondary) sides are **galvanically isolated** by a **high-frequency (HF) transformer**. This isolation provides:
1. **Safety** (protection from high-voltage DC bus faults on the user side).
2. **Voltage scaling** (step-up or step-down via the turns ratio $n = N_1/N_2$).
3. **Multiple outputs** (different secondaries for different voltage rails).

The **Half-Bridge** and **Full-Bridge** converters are *pulse-width modulated (PWM)* isolated converters that apply a **symmetric alternating square wave** (AC) of amplitude $\pm V_s/2$ (half-bridge) or $\pm V_s$ (full-bridge) to the primary of the HF transformer. A diode rectifier and LC filter on the secondary deliver a regulated DC output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō PEEBT741, Module 3):**
> The Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge isolated converters are part of the *family of square-wave / PWM isolated DCŌĆōDC converters*. They are the **building blocks of Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)**, UPS front-ends, and the DCŌĆōDC stage of solar micro-inverters and EV chargers.

## 1.2 Why Isolation? The "Why" Behind the Transformer

In a non-isolated buck/boost converter, the input and output share a common ground. This is unacceptable when:
- The DC bus is at **400 V / 800 V** (e.g., EV traction, solar PV string) and the load must be touch-safe (Ōēż **60 V** for SELV ŌĆö Safety Extra-Low Voltage).
- Multiple isolated rails are required from a single bus.
- Regulatory standards (IEC 60950, IEC 62368) mandate **galvanic isolation** between mains-derived HV and user-accessible circuits.

A **low-frequency (50/60 Hz) line transformer** is far too bulky. Hence, the **DC is first chopped** at high frequency (typically **20 kHz ŌĆō 500 kHz**), passed through a compact HF transformer, then rectified and filtered. The transformer's volume scales as $V_{\text{core}} \propto 1/f$, so higher frequency ŌåÆ smaller, lighter, cheaper magnetics.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "AC Sandwich" Concept

Think of an isolated DCŌĆōDC converter as an **"AC sandwich"**:

| Layer | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Input Chopper (Inverter) | DC ŌåÆ high-frequency AC square wave | Your voice (DC hum) is **chopped** into a high-frequency whistle |
| 2. HF Transformer | Step up/down + isolate | The whistle is **filtered/amplified** through a small "translator" device |
| 3. Output Rectifier + Filter | HF AC ŌåÆ smooth DC | The whistle is **decoded back** into a clean, amplified voice |

- The **Half-Bridge** is like a **2-switch pendulum** ŌĆö it swings the transformer primary between $+V_s/2$ and $-V_s/2$ using a *capacitive voltage divider*.
- The **Full-Bridge** is like a **4-switch H-bridge** ŌĆö it swings the primary between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ using a full H-leg arrangement, delivering **twice the volt-second product** for the same duty cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition ŌĆö Duty Ratio $D$:**
> For both isolated bridge converters, each switch (or diagonal pair) is ON for a duration $DT$ in a switching period $T$. In continuous conduction mode (CCM), the constraint is $0 < D \leq 0.5$ to prevent **shoot-through** (simultaneous conduction of series switches that would short the source).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Half-Bridge Isolated DCŌĆōDC Converter

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology

The half-bridge isolated converter consists of:
- Two equal **energy-storage / DC-blocking capacitors** $C_1$ and $C_2$ forming a capacitive divider (midpoint at $V_s/2$).
- Two controllable switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ (MOSFETs / IGBTs) in a half-bridge leg.
- An **HF transformer** with turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$ (or $n$, used interchangeably).
- A **center-tapped secondary** with two diodes $D_1, D_2$ forming a full-wave rectifier.
- An **LC output filter** ($L_f, C_f$) feeding the load $R_L$.

### 2.1.2 Modes of Operation (CCM)

**Mode 1 ŌĆö Switch $S_1$ ON (interval $0 < t < DT$):**
- $S_1$ connects the top of the primary to $+V_s$; the primary bottom sits at the $C_1$-$C_2$ midpoint ($V_s/2$).
- Voltage across the primary: $v_{p}(t) = +V_s/2$.
- The upper half of the secondary ($N_2$ turns) is forward-biased; diode $D_1$ conducts. $v_s = +V_s/(2a)$.
- Energy flows: $V_s \rightarrow S_1 \rightarrow$ primary $\rightarrow D_1 \rightarrow L_f \rightarrow R_L \rightarrow$ center-tap $\rightarrow C_1$.

**Mode 2 ŌĆö Switch $S_2$ ON (interval $DT < t < 2DT$, symmetric):**
- $S_2$ connects the primary bottom to ground; primary top is at the capacitor midpoint ($V_s/2$).
- $v_p(t) = -V_s/2$.
- The lower half of the secondary is forward-biased; $D_2$ conducts. $v_s = +V_s/(2a)$.
- Freewheeling current circulates via $D_2$.

**Mode 3 ŌĆö Both switches OFF (dead time, $2DT < t < T$):**
- Both $D_1$ and $D_2$ are OFF; the load current freewheels through the filter inductor and the output capacitor.

### 2.1.3 Voltage Transfer Ratio ŌĆö Derivation Logic

In CCM, the filter inductor $L_f$ sustains a (nearly) constant output current. By **volt-second balance on the primary** (the average voltage across the primary over one full period must be zero, else the transformer saturates):

$$
\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_p(t)\,dt = 0
$$

Since $v_p = +V_s/2$ for $DT$ and $v_p = -V_s/2$ for $DT$ in symmetric operation, this is automatically satisfied.

The **secondary voltage** (full secondary) is:

$$
v_{s,\text{full}}(t) = \frac{1}{a} v_p(t) = \pm \frac{V_s}{2a}
$$

After full-wave rectification (center-tapped, each half-secondary used for one half-cycle), the average output voltage is:

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{D \, V_s}{a}}, \quad 0 < D \leq 0.5
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric Switch Drive:** In a half-bridge, **each switch is on for $DT$**, and the total active interval is $2DT$ per period. This means the **converter delivers power only during $2D$ fraction** of the period. Maximum output occurs at $D = 0.5$ giving $V_{o,\max} = V_s/(2a)$.

## 2.2 Full-Bridge Isolated DCŌĆōDC Converter

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

The full-bridge uses **four switches** $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ arranged in an **H-bridge**:
- Left leg: $S_1$ (top) and $S_3$ (bottom)
- Right leg: $S_2$ (top) and $S_4$ (bottom)
- The transformer primary is connected between the two leg midpoints.

### 2.2.2 Modes of Operation (CCM)

**Mode 1 ŌĆö Diagonal Pair ON ($S_1, S_4$):** $0 < t < DT$
- Current path: $+V_s \rightarrow S_1 \rightarrow$ primary $\rightarrow S_4 \rightarrow$ return.
- $v_p(t) = +V_s$
- One half of the secondary conducts via $D_1$.

**Mode 2 ŌĆö Other Diagonal Pair ON ($S_2, S_3$):** $DT < t < 2DT$
- $v_p(t) = -V_s$
- The other half of the secondary conducts via $D_2$.

**Mode 3 ŌĆö All switches OFF (dead time):** $2DT < t < T$
- Freewheeling through output filter and capacitor.

### 2.2.3 Voltage Transfer Ratio

The peak primary voltage is now the **full DC bus** $V_s$ (twice that of the half-bridge). Thus:

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 D \, V_s}{a}}, \quad 0 < D \leq 0.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Comparison Insight:**
> The full-bridge has **twice the voltage conversion ratio** of the half-bridge for the same duty ratio. Equivalently, for the same $V_o$, the full-bridge needs only **half the duty cycle**, which reduces switch stress and improves efficiency at the cost of **4 switches + 4 gate drivers** instead of 2.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Half-Bridge Formula | Full-Bridge Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak primary voltage $V_{p,\text{peak}}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | $V_s$ | V |
| 2 | Secondary voltage (each half-cycle) | $\dfrac{V_s}{2a}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{a}$ | V |
| 3 | Output voltage $V_o$ (CCM, ideal) | $\dfrac{D \, V_s}{a}$ | $\dfrac{2 D \, V_s}{a}$ | V |
| 4 | Maximum output $V_{o,\max}$ (at $D=0.5$) | $\dfrac{V_s}{2a}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{a}$ | V |
| 5 | Switch count | 2 | 4 | ŌĆö |
| 6 | Switch voltage stress $V_{T,\text{max}}$ | $V_s$ | $V_s$ | V |
| 7 | Switch RMS current $I_{T,\text{rms}}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D}$ | A |
| 8 | Transformer primary volt-second $\vert \lambda \vert$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{2} \cdot DT$ | $V_s \cdot DT$ | V┬Ęs |
| 9 | Operating duty range (CCM) | $0 < D \leq 0.5$ | $0 < D \leq 0.5$ | ŌĆö |
| 10 | Power handling capability | Medium (Ōēż $\sim$ 500 W) | High (Ōēź $\sim$ 500 W to MW) | W |

**Where:**
- $V_s$ ŌĆö DC input supply voltage
- $D$ ŌĆö Duty ratio of each switch (or each diagonal pair)
- $a = N_1 / N_2$ ŌĆö Primary-to-secondary turns ratio
- $I_o$ ŌĆö DC load (output) current

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Preferred Topology | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Laptop / mobile chargers (Ōēż 100 W)** | Half-bridge | Lower switch count, cost-effective |
| **Server SMPS, telecom rectifiers (1ŌĆō5 kW)** | Full-bridge | Higher power density, lower primary current |
| **EV onboard chargers (3ŌĆō22 kW)** | Full-bridge (often with phase-shift modulation) | Bidirectional capability, high efficiency |
| **Solar PV micro-inverters** | Full-bridge (with HF transformer) | Galvanic isolation mandatory for grid-tie |
| **Welding / induction heating inverters** | Full-bridge | Very high power, low harmonic distortion |
| **Aerospace DCŌĆōDC converters** | Full-bridge (resonant variant) | High reliability, soft-switching reduces EMI |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Half-Bridge Output Voltage

**Given:**
- DC input: $V_s$
- Turns ratio: $a = N_1/N_2$
- Each switch ON for $DT$, OFF for the remainder of the half-period
- Symmetric operation (both halves of the AC cycle have equal duration $DT$)
- CCM, ideal components

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the primary voltage waveform $v_p(t)$.**

The primary is connected between the switch-leg midpoint and the capacitor midpoint. When $S_1$ is ON, the leg midpoint is at $+V_s$ and the cap midpoint is at $V_s/2$. Hence:

$$
v_p(t) = V_s - \frac{V_s}{2} = +\frac{V_s}{2} \quad \text{for } 0 < t < DT
$$

When $S_2$ is ON, the leg midpoint is at $0$ V and the cap midpoint is at $V_s/2$:

$$
v_p(t) = 0 - \frac{V_s}{2} = -\frac{V_s}{2} \quad \text{for } DT < t < 2DT
$$

For the remaining dead time $2DT < t < T$ (or equivalently, between half-cycles if we define the period as $2DT$ for the symmetric chopper), $v_p = 0$ (no current source, transformer magnetizing current circulates elsewhere).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Verify volt-second balance on the primary.**

$$
\int_0^{2DT} v_p(t)\, dt = \left(\frac{V_s}{2}\right)(DT) + \left(-\frac{V_s}{2}\right)(DT) = 0
$$

This confirms **no DC bias** on the transformer ŌĆö essential to prevent saturation.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Transform to secondary (each half of the center-tap).**

With turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$, the half-secondary voltage (one half of the center-tap) is:

$$
v_{s,\text{half}}(t) = \frac{1}{a} v_p(t) = \pm \frac{V_s}{2a}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply full-wave rectification.**

The two diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ alternately conduct, both delivering the **same polarity** to the output. Hence the rectified voltage seen by the LC filter is:

$$
v_{\text{rect}}(t) = \left\vert v_{s,\text{half}}(t) \right\vert = \frac{V_s}{2a} \quad \text{during active intervals}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Average over one switching period $T_s$ (where active interval is $2DT$):**

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_0^{T_s} v_{\text{rect}}(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot \frac{V_s}{2a} \cdot 2DT
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{D \, V_s}{a}}
$$

This is the canonical **half-bridge isolated converter voltage transfer ratio** in CCM.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Full-Bridge Output Voltage

**Step 1 ŌĆö Primary voltage waveform.**

In the full-bridge, when $S_1, S_4$ are ON:

$$
v_p(t) = +V_s \quad \text{for } 0 < t < DT
$$

When $S_2, S_3$ are ON:

$$
v_p(t) = -V_s \quad \text{for } DT < t < 2DT
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Volt-second balance check:**

$$
\int_0^{2DT} v_p(t)\, dt = (V_s)(DT) + (-V_s)(DT) = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Secondary voltage (full secondary, $2N_2$ turns):**

$$
v_{s,\text{full}}(t) = \frac{1}{a} v_p(t) = \pm \frac{V_s}{a}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rectified secondary voltage:**

$$
v_{\text{rect}}(t) = \left\vert v_{s,\text{full}}(t) \right\vert = \frac{V_s}{a} \quad \text{during active intervals}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Average over the period $T_s$:**

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot \frac{V_s}{a} \cdot 2DT
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 D \, V_s}{a}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** A full-bridge isolated DCŌĆōDC converter operates from a 400 V DC bus. The HF transformer has a turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2 = 4$. The switching frequency is $f_s = 50$ kHz, and the duty ratio is $D = 0.4$. The load draws $I_o = 20$ A in CCM. Compute:
(a) The output voltage $V_o$.
(b) The average and RMS current through each switch.
(c) The transformer primary RMS current.

**Solution:**

**(a) Output voltage:**

$$
V_o = \frac{2 D \, V_s}{a} = \frac{2 \times 0.4 \times 400}{4} = \frac{320}{4} = 80 \text{ V}
$$

**(b) Switch currents:**

The secondary RMS current (assuming CCM with negligible ripple):

$$
I_{s,\text{rms}} \approx I_o = 20 \text{ A}
$$

Referred to the primary:

$$
I_{p,\text{rms}} = \frac{I_o}{a} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ A}
$$

Each switch conducts for $DT$ per period. The **average switch current**:

$$
I_{T,\text{avg}} = D \cdot \frac{I_o}{a} = 0.4 \times 5 = 2 \text{ A}
$$

The **RMS switch current** (for a rectangular pulse of magnitude $I_p$ for $DT$):

$$
I_{T,\text{rms}} = \frac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D} = 5 \times \sqrt{0.4} = 5 \times 0.6325 \approx 3.16 \text{ A}
$$

**(c) Primary RMS current:**

$$
I_{p,\text{rms}} = \frac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{2D} = 5 \times \sqrt{0.8} = 5 \times 0.894 \approx 4.47 \text{ A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $\sqrt{2D}$ factor in (c)?** The primary carries current for *both* half-cycles (each diagonal pair conducts for $DT$, total active time $2DT$). The RMS over the full period is therefore $\sqrt{2D}$ times the secondary-referred DC current. This distinction between **switch** (one pair only) and **transformer primary** (both pairs) is a classic KTU exam trap.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge | KTU Exam Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output voltage (same $D, a, V_s$) | $D V_s / a$ | $2 D V_s / a$ | Full-bridge has **2├Ś gain** |
| Switch count | 2 | 4 | Cost vs. performance trade-off |
| Switch voltage stress | $V_s$ | $V_s$ | Same blocking voltage needed |
| Switch RMS current | $\dfrac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D}$ | Same per switch |
| Primary RMS current | $\dfrac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{2D}$ | Higher in full-bridge due to both halves active |
| Capacitor voltage ripple | High (capacitive divider sees charging/discharging) | None (no divider) | Full-bridge is gentler on input caps |
| Transformer utilization | Lower (only $\pm V_s/2$ swing) | Higher (full $\pm V_s$ swing) | Full-bridge ŌåÆ smaller transformer |
| Typical power level | Ōēż 500 W | 500 W ŌĆō several MW | Topology choice depends on $P_o$ |
| Complexity / cost | Low | High (more drivers, isolation) | Half-bridge preferred in low-cost SMPS |
| Shoot-through risk | Medium (1 leg) | Higher (2 legs must not cross-conduct) | Requires careful dead-time insertion |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**"List any three advantages of providing galvanic isolation in a DCŌĆōDC converter. Mention the role of the high-frequency transformer in achieving this."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Safety** (1 mark): Galvanic isolation prevents direct conduction of fault currents from the high-voltage DC bus to the low-voltage load side, protecting users and downstream electronics. This is mandatory in **EV chargers, medical equipment, and grid-tied PV** systems as per IEC standards.

2. **Voltage scaling flexibility** (1 mark): By choosing the turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$, the designer can step up or step down the voltage. A single $400$ V bus can be converted to $12$ V, $24$ V, or $48$ V rails using different secondaries on the same transformer.

3. **Multiple isolated outputs** (1 mark): Multiple secondary windings on the same HF transformer can simultaneously generate different DC voltages (e.g., $\pm 15$ V, $5$ V, $3.3$ V) for analog and digital circuitry, all from a single primary chopper.

> The **HF transformer** operates at switching frequencies (typically $20$ kHz ŌĆō $500$ kHz) ŌĆö about **1000├Ś higher** than line frequency ŌĆö drastically reducing the core size and weight compared to a 50/60 Hz line transformer of the same power rating.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**"Compare the Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge isolated DCŌĆōDC converters with respect to (i) number of switches, (ii) switch voltage stress, and (iii) voltage transfer ratio."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Number of switches | **2** (one leg) | **4** (two legs) |
| (ii) Switch voltage stress | $V_s$ (each switch blocks full bus) | $V_s$ (each switch blocks full bus) |
| (iii) Voltage transfer ratio | $\dfrac{D V_s}{a}$ | $\dfrac{2 D V_s}{a}$ |

**Concluding statement (1 mark):** The full-bridge delivers **twice the output voltage** for the same $D$ and $a$, but at the cost of **2 additional switches and 2 additional gate drivers**. Hence, half-bridge is preferred for low-to-medium power, and full-bridge for medium-to-high power applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Attempt **either (a) or (b)** from each module question. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. Internal choice is **within** the same module, not across modules.

---

### ŌŁÉ Question Choice A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**"With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Half-Bridge isolated DCŌĆōDC converter. Derive the expression for its output voltage in CCM. Compare it with the Full-Bridge converter."**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Circuit, Operation, and Waveforms (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** A half-bridge isolated DCŌĆōDC converter consists of a DC source $V_s$ feeding two equal capacitors $C_1, C_2$ in series (forming a divider with midpoint at $V_s/2$) and two switches $S_1, S_2$ in a half-bridge leg. The primary of the HF transformer is connected between the switch-leg midpoint and the capacitor midpoint. The center-tapped secondary connects to two diodes $D_1, D_2$ in a full-wave rectifier, followed by an LC filter ($L_f, C_f$) and load $R_L$.

**Operation in CCM (3 marks):**

- **Mode 1** ($S_1$ ON, $S_2$ OFF, duration $DT$): Current flows from $V_s$ through $S_1$, down the primary (top to bottom), through the upper half of the secondary, through $D_1$, into $L_f$, to the load, and back via the center tap to $C_1$. The primary voltage is $v_p = +V_s/2$. Energy is delivered to the load; $L_f$ stores energy.

- **Mode 2** ($S_2$ ON, $S_1$ OFF, duration $DT$): Current flows from $C_2$ through the primary (bottom to top), through the lower half of the secondary, through $D_2$, into $L_f$ and load. The primary voltage is $v_p = -V_s/2$. The transformer core is reset (flux returns to zero). Energy continues to flow to the load.

- **Mode 3** (Both OFF, duration $T - 2DT$): The load current freewheels through $L_f$, $C_f$, and the load. Both diodes are reverse-biased. The magnetizing current of the transformer circulates within the primary winding (or via a snubber, in non-ideal designs).

**Waveforms (2 marks):** The primary voltage is a symmetric square wave of amplitude $\pm V_s/2$ with $50\%$ active duty. The secondary-rectified voltage has amplitude $V_s/(2a)$ during the active interval. The filter inductor current has a small triangular ripple superimposed on its DC value $I_o$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Derivation of Output Voltage + Comparison (7 marks)

**Derivation of $V_o$ (5 marks):**

The average voltage across the primary over one full period must be zero (volt-second balance, prevents saturation):

$$
\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_p(t)\, dt = 0
$$

The rectified secondary voltage (center-tapped) has magnitude $V_s/(2a)$ for a total active duration of $2DT$ per period. In CCM with a large $L_f$, the output voltage equals the average of the rectified waveform:

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_0^{T_s} v_{\text{rect}}(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot \frac{V_s}{2a} \cdot 2DT
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{D\,V_s}{a}}
$$

**Comparison with Full-Bridge (2 marks):**

| Feature | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| Number of switches | 2 | 4 |
| $V_o$ expression | $D V_s / a$ | $2 D V_s / a$ |
| Peak $V_p$ | $V_s/2$ | $V_s$ |
| Power level | Ōēż 500 W | Ōēź 500 W to MW |
| Transformer utilization | Lower | Higher |
| Cost / complexity | Low | High |

**Conclusion:** The full-bridge achieves twice the voltage transfer ratio for the same duty cycle, but requires twice the switch count.

---

### ŌŁÉ Question Choice B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024]

**"Describe the operation of a Full-Bridge isolated DCŌĆōDC converter with a circuit diagram and relevant waveforms. Derive its output voltage in CCM. A full-bridge converter operates from $V_s = 200$ V, $a = 5$, $f_s = 100$ kHz, $D = 0.35$, supplying a load of $R_L = 5\ \Omega$. Compute (i) the output voltage, (ii) the load current, and (iii) the average and RMS current through each switch."**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Operation, Waveforms, and Derivation (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Circuit and Operation (3 marks):** A full-bridge converter has four switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ arranged in two legs. The transformer primary is connected between the midpoints of the two legs. The diagonal pairs $(S_1, S_4)$ and $(S_2, S_3)$ conduct alternately, each for $DT$, applying $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ across the primary respectively. The center-tapped secondary is rectified by $D_1, D_2$ and filtered by $L_f, C_f$.

**Waveforms (2 marks):** The primary voltage is a symmetric square wave of amplitude $\pm V_s$. The secondary-rectified waveform has amplitude $V_s/a$ for $2DT$ per period, zero for the remaining $(T - 2DT)$ freewheeling interval. The output inductor current has small ripple around $I_o$.

**Derivation of $V_o$ (2 marks):**

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot \frac{V_s}{a} \cdot 2DT
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 D\,V_s}{a}}
$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_s = 200$ V, $a = 5$, $f_s = 100$ kHz, $D = 0.35$, $R_L = 5\ \Omega$.

**(i) Output voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_o = \frac{2 D V_s}{a} = \frac{2 \times 0.35 \times 200}{5} = \frac{140}{5} = 28 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Load current (2 marks):**

$$
I_o = \frac{V_o}{R_L} = \frac{28}{5} = 5.6 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Switch currents (3 marks):**

The secondary-referred DC current referred to the primary:

$$
I_p = \frac{I_o}{a} = \frac{5.6}{5} = 1.12 \text{ A}
$$

Each switch conducts for $DT$ per period.

**Average switch current:**

$$
I_{T,\text{avg}} = D \cdot \frac{I_o}{a} = 0.35 \times 1.12 = 0.392 \text{ A}
$$

**RMS switch current:**

$$
I_{T,\text{rms}} = \frac{I_o}{a}\sqrt{D} = 1.12 \times \sqrt{0.35} = 1.12 \times 0.5916 \approx 0.663 \text{ A}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Mixing up primary and secondary currents:** The transformer turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2 > 1$ means the *primary* current is **smaller** than the secondary by a factor of $a$. Many students divide by $a$ in the wrong direction and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the full-bridge transfer ratio:** The half-bridge formula is $D V_s / a$; the full-bridge is $2 D V_s / a$. This factor arises because the full-bridge applies the **full bus** $V_s$ (not $V_s/2$) to the primary.
> 3. **Switch vs. primary RMS confusion:** Each **switch** sees current for only $DT$ (one diagonal pair), so $I_{T,\text{rms}} = (I_o/a)\sqrt{D}$. The **transformer primary** sees current for $2DT$ (both pairs), so $I_{p,\text{rms}} = (I_o/a)\sqrt{2D}$. This is a **favourite 2-mark sub-question** in KTU ESE.
> 4. **Duty cycle limit:** Both converters must operate with $D \leq 0.5$ to prevent shoot-through. If a numerical problem yields $D > 0.5$, the assumption is invalid and the student's answer is wrong.
> 5. **Forgetting the dead time $T - 2DT$:** Inductor current continues to flow during the dead time via the freewheeling diodes/load. The output voltage is the average over the *full* period $T$, not just the active interval $2DT$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Galvanic isolation** is provided by an **HF transformer** (operates at $20$ kHz ŌĆō $500$ kHz) ŌĆö reduces size/weight drastically compared to 50/60 Hz mains transformers.
- Ō£ģ **Half-bridge** uses **2 switches** + capacitive divider; primary voltage swing is $\pm V_s/2$.
- Ō£ģ **Full-bridge** uses **4 switches** in H-configuration; primary voltage swing is $\pm V_s$.
- Ō£ģ **Voltage transfer ratios (CCM, ideal):**
    - Half-Bridge: $V_o = D V_s / a$
    - Full-Bridge: $V_o = 2 D V_s / a$
- Ō£ģ **Duty cycle limit:** $0 < D \leq 0.5$ for both topologies (to prevent shoot-through).
- Ō£ģ **Maximum output** occurs at $D = 0.5$: $V_{o,\max} = V_s/(2a)$ (half-bridge) and $V_{o,\max} = V_s/a$ (full-bridge).
- Ō£ģ **Switch voltage stress** = $V_s$ (each switch blocks the full DC bus) ŌĆö **same for both** topologies.
- Ō£ģ **Switch RMS current** (per switch) = $(I_o/a)\sqrt{D}$ ŌĆö **same for both** topologies.
- Ō£ģ **Transformer primary RMS current** = $(I_o/a)\sqrt{D}$ (half-bridge) or $(I_o/a)\sqrt{2D}$ (full-bridge).
- Ō£ģ **Half-bridge** is preferred for **low-to-medium power** (Ōēż 500 W) due to switch count economy.
- Ō£ģ **Full-bridge** is preferred for **medium-to-high power** (Ōēź 500 W) due to better transformer utilization and full DC bus exploitation.
- Ō£ģ **Shoot-through prevention** is critical ŌĆö dead time of $100$ ns ŌĆō $500$ ns is inserted between complementary switch transitions.
- Ō£ģ The **secondary is always center-tapped** with a full-wave rectifier (2 diodes) ŌĆö this gives **twice the rectified output** compared to a half-wave rectifier for the same secondary winding.
- Ō£ģ **DC blocking capacitor** divider ($C_1, C_2$) in the half-bridge must be sized to handle the **magnetizing current ripple**; otherwise, the midpoint voltage drifts from $V_s/2$ and causes flux imbalance.
- Ō£ģ **Pulse-width modulation (PWM)** at fixed $f_s$ with variable $D$ regulates $V_o$; **phase-shift modulation (PSM)** is a popular variant for full-bridge used in **zero-voltage switching (ZVS)** applications.
- Ō£ģ KTU 2024 Scheme tag: this topic maps to **CO3** (Analyze and design isolated DCŌĆōDC converter topologies) and **CO4** (Apply power electronics to real-world SMPS / EV / renewable systems).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Governing Equations and Switching Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Governing Equations and Switching Waveforms # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Governing Equations and Switching Waveforms"? In Power Electronics, every DC-DC converter (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost) operates by **rapidly switching** a semiconductor device (MOSFET/IGBT) between ON and OFF states. The **Governing Equations** are the mathematical relationships that describe how the inductor current, capacitor voltage, load voltage, and load current evolve during each switching interval, while the **Switching Waveforms** are the time-domain plots of these quantities across one complete switching period $T_s$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Governing equations are the differential equations governing the steady-state behavior of power electronic switching converters in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM), derived using the **Volt-Second Balance** of the inductor and the **Charge Balance (Amp-Second Balance)** of the capacitor. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Water Bucket Analogy ­¤Æ¦ Imagine you have a **leaky bucket** (the inductor) connected to a tap (input source). Every second, you turn the tap **ON** for time $t_{on}$ to fill the bucket, and **OFF** for time $t_{off}$ during which the bucket leaks water to the load. - **Filling phase (Switch ON):** Water flows in faster than it leaks ŌåÆ bucket level rises. - **Emptying phase (Switch OFF):** No inflow, but bucket keeps leaking ŌåÆ bucket level falls. - In **steady state**, the average level stays the same: what comes in must come out over one cycle. This is exactly how a switching regulator behaves. The switch controls the **average energy** delivered to the load by varying the **duty ratio** $D$. ### 1.3 Standard Definitions and Constants > [!NOTE] > **Critical Quantities Used in Governing Equations:** > - **Duty Cycle ($D$)**: Ratio of ON-time to total switching period, **$D = t_{on}/T_s$**. Range: **0 < D < 1**. > - **Switching Frequency ($f_s$)**: Number of switching cycles per second. **$f_s = 1/T_s$**. > - **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)**: The inductor current never falls to zero during the switching cycle. > - **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)**: The inductor current falls to zero and remains zero for some interval during the cycle. > - **Ripple**: The peak-to-peak variation in a waveform about its DC average value. For KTU 2024, the emphasis is on **CCM operation**, which is assumed unless stated otherwise. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Principles of Converter Steady-State Analysis Any DC-DC switching converter (Buck, Boost, or Buck-Boost) can be completely analyzed using **two foundational principles**: ### Principle 1: Inductor Volt-Second Balance In **steady state**, the net voltage impressed across an inductor over one switching period must equal zero. Otherwise, the inductor current would grow unboundedly. $$\int_0^{T_s} v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$ ### Principle 2: Capacitor Charge (Amp-Second) Balance In **steady state**, the net current flowing into a capacitor over one switching period must equal zero. Otherwise, the capacitor voltage would accumulate without bound. $$\int_0^{T_s} i_C(t)\, dt = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > These two principles are the **bedrock** of every KTU Power Electronics problem on converter analysis. Memorize them ŌĆö they unlock the conversion ratio, ripple, and boundary condition derivations for **every** converter topology. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic ŌĆö The Generic PWM Switching Cycle During one switching period $T_s$, the converter alternates between two sub-intervals: **Interval 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le DT_s$):** - The switch is closed; the diode is reverse-biased. - The inductor is connected to a "charging" voltage source $V_{x,on}$. - Inductor current rises linearly: $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{x,on}}{L}$. - Capacitor charges/discharges depending on whether $i_L$ exceeds the load current. **Interval 2 (Switch OFF, $DT_s \le t \le T_s$):** - The switch is open; the diode conducts (freewheeling). - The inductor sees a different voltage $V_{x,off}$. - Inductor current falls linearly: $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{x,off}}{L}$. - Capacitor supplies/accepts the imbalance current. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles **all high-yield equations** for Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost converters in CCM. These equations appear in nearly every KTU 2024 Module 3 numerical. | Parameter | Buck Converter | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter | |---|---|---|---| | **Conversion Ratio $\frac{V_o}{V_s}$** | $D$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | | **Inductor Voltage ON ($V_{L,on}$)** | $V_s - V_o$ | $V_s$ | $V_s$ | | **Inductor Voltage OFF ($V_{L,off}$)** | $-V_o$ | $V_s - V_o$ | $V_o$ | | **Inductor Current Ripple $\Delta i_L$** | $\dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$ | | **Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_o / V_o$** | $\dfrac{1-D}{8 L C f_s^2}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ | | **Critical Inductance $L_c$** | $\dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D (1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | | **Critical Capacitance $C_c$** | $\dfrac{1-D}{16 L f_s^2}$ | $\dfrac{D V_o}{R \Delta V_o f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D V_o}{R \Delta V_o f_s}$ | | **Switch Voltage Stress** | $V_s$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | | **Diode Voltage Stress** | $V_s$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | > [!WARNING] > In KTU board exams, students often forget that the **Buck-Boost output voltage is negative**. Mark this carefully ŌĆö a sign error here loses **2 to 3 marks**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These equations are not merely academic. They directly drive the design of: - **Smartphone and laptop battery chargers** (Buck topology dominates). - **Solar PV MPPT systems** (Boost topology raises the panel voltage to the inverter DC bus). - **Hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) DC-DC converters** (Buck-Boost interfaces the battery and 48V bus). - **Server SMPS** (where synchronous Buck regulators at $f_s > 500$ kHz are now standard). Every time you plug in a laptop, a high-frequency switching converter is performing the exact computations these equations describe ŌĆö at **hundreds of thousands of cycles per second**. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Conversion Ratio of the Buck Converter **Given:** A Buck converter with input $V_s$, output $V_o$, switching period $T_s$, duty ratio $D = t_{on}/T_s$, operating in CCM. **Step 1: Define inductor voltage during each interval.** During switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$), KVL around the inputŌĆōswitchŌĆōinductorŌĆōoutput loop gives: $$v_{L,on} = V_s - V_o$$ During switch OFF ($DT_s \le t \le T_s$), the diode freewheels and KVL yields: $$v_{L,off} = -V_o$$ **Step 2: Apply Volt-Second Balance.** In steady state, the average voltage across the inductor must be zero: $$\int_0^{T_s} v_L(t)\, dt = V_{L,on} \cdot D T_s + V_{L,off} \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$ Substituting: $$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D T_s + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$ Dividing through by $T_s$ (it cancels on both sides): $$(V_s - V_o) D - V_o (1-D) = 0$$ Expanding: $$V_s D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D = 0$$ $$V_s D - V_o = 0$$ **Step 3: Solve for $V_o$.** $$\boxed{\,V_o = D \cdot V_s\,}$$ This proves the Buck converter is a **step-down** converter since $0 < D < 1$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Conversion Ratio of the Boost Converter **Given:** A Boost converter with input $V_s$, output $V_o$, CCM operation. **Step 1: Inductor voltage during each interval.** When the switch is ON, the inductor is connected directly across the source, so: $$v_{L,on} = V_s$$ When the switch is OFF, the diode conducts and KVL around the sourceŌĆōinductorŌĆōdiodeŌĆōoutput loop gives: $$v_{L,off} = V_s - V_o$$ **Step 2: Apply Volt-Second Balance.** $$V_s \cdot D T_s + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) T_s = 0$$ Divide by $T_s$: $$V_s D + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) = 0$$ Expanding: $$V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o (1-D) = 0$$ $$V_s - V_o (1-D) = 0$$ **Step 3: Solve for $V_o$.** $$\boxed{\,V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}\,}$$ Since $0 < (1-D) < 1$ in CCM, $V_o > V_s$ ŌĆö hence the name **Boost**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Conversion Ratio of the Buck-Boost Converter **Given:** A Buck-Boost converter with input $V_s$, output $V_o$, CCM operation. **Step 1: Inductor voltage during each interval.** When the switch is ON, the diode is reverse-biased; the inductor is again connected to the source: $$v_{L,on} = V_s$$ When the switch is OFF, the inductor discharges through the diode into the output (with inverted polarity). The reference direction of the output voltage makes $v_{L,off} = V_o$. **Step 2: Apply Volt-Second Balance.** $$V_s \cdot D T_s + V_o \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$ Dividing by $T_s$: $$V_s D + V_o (1-D) = 0$$ **Step 3: Solve for $V_o$.** $$V_o = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$$ The negative sign confirms the output is **inverted** with respect to the input. $$\boxed{\,V_o = -\dfrac{D}{1-D} V_s\,}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Inductor Current Ripple (Common to All Topologies) **Step 1: Use the defining equation of an inductor.** $$v_L(t) = L \dfrac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 2: Rearrange to express the current change.** $$\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = \dfrac{v_L}{L}$$ Over the switch-ON interval of duration $D T_s$: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_{L,on}}{L} \cdot D T_s$$ **Step 3: Substitute the appropriate $V_{L,on}$ for each converter.** For the **Buck** converter, $V_{L,on} = V_s - V_o = V_s (1-D)$, so: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s (1-D)}{L} \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$$ For the **Boost** and **Buck-Boost** converters, $V_{L,on} = V_s$, giving: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s}{L} \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L}$$ (Wait ŌĆö let me re-verify.) In Boost CCM, $V_{L,on} = V_s$ and $V_{L,off} = V_s - V_o$. The voltage across the inductor for the ON interval is $V_s$: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L}$$ But using the OFF interval yields the equivalent result: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{(V_s - V_o)(1-D) T_s}{L}$$ Since $V_s - V_o = -V_s D / (1-D)$, the magnitude becomes: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **unified expression** $\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$ applies to **all three basic converters** when written in terms of $V_s$, $D$, $f_s$, and $L$. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Output Voltage Ripple for the Buck Converter **Step 1: Capacitor current waveform.** During switch ON, the diode is off and the capacitor supplies the entire load current. So: $$i_C = -I_o \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le D T_s$$ During switch OFF, $i_C = i_L - I_o$, where $i_L$ is decreasing. **Step 2: Apply Charge Balance.** The average capacitor current must be zero. Therefore, the area of the negative rectangle during ON must equal the area of the positive triangle during OFF: $$\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta i_L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{T_s}{2} = I_o \cdot D T_s$$ The peak capacitor current is $\Delta i_L / 2$, and it occurs at $t = D T_s$. **Step 3: Solve for $\Delta i_L$.** $$\dfrac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s} = I_o D$$ $$\Delta i_L = 8 I_o D f_s$$ Substituting $I_o = V_o / R$ and equating to the earlier ripple expression $\Delta i_L = V_s D (1-D) / (f_s L) = V_o (1-D) / (f_s L)$: $$\dfrac{V_o (1-D)}{f_s L} = \dfrac{8 V_o D f_s}{1}$$ Hmm, this is incorrect. The standard derivation proceeds as follows: **Corrected Step 3:** The peak-to-peak voltage ripple across the output capacitor is the integral of capacitor current during the time the current is most negative: $$\Delta V_o = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{1}{C} \int_0^{DT_s} (-I_o)\, dt = \dfrac{I_o D T_s}{C}$$ Substituting $I_o = V_o / R$ and $T_s = 1/f_s$: $$\Delta V_o = \dfrac{V_o D}{R C f_s}$$ Dividing by $V_o$: $$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$$ $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R C f_s}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note the slight difference: for the **Buck** converter, a more refined derivation that accounts for the ESR (equivalent series resistance) and the exact waveform shape yields $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{1-D}{8 L C f_s^2}$, which is the form most often expected in KTU examinations for design problems. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Boundary Between CCM and DCM The boundary between CCM and DCM occurs when the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the switching period. The load resistance at this boundary is: | Converter | Critical Resistance $R_{crit}$ | |---|---| | Buck | $R_{crit} = \dfrac{2 f_s L}{1-D}$ | | Boost | $R_{crit} = \dfrac{2 f_s L}{D (1-D)^2}$ | | Buck-Boost | $R_{crit} = \dfrac{2 f_s L}{(1-D)^2}$ | **Operating rule:** - If $R > R_{crit}$ ŌåÆ converter operates in **DCM**. - If $R < R_{crit}$ ŌåÆ converter operates in **CCM**. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Generic PWM Switching Cycle Analyzer ```mermaid flowchart TD A["DC Source Vs"] --> B["Switching Network MOSFET IGBT"] B --> C{"Switch State Selector"} C -->|Switch ON
Duration DTs| D["Inductor Charging Phase
vL is positive
iL rises linearly"] C -->|Switch OFF
Duration 1-DTs| E["Inductor Discharging Phase
Diode freewheels
iL falls linearly"] D --> F["LC Low Pass Filter"] E --> F F --> G["DC Output Vo with small ripple"] F --> H["Capacitor Charging Discharging"] H --> G G --> I["Load Resistor R"] I -.feedback.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Switching Waveform Timing Diagram (Buck Converter, CCM) ```mermaid gantt title Switching Waveform Phases in One Period Ts (Buck CCM) dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Switch Gate Signal Switch ON (High) :a1, 0, 6 Switch OFF (Low) :a2, 6, 10 section Inductor Voltage vL vL = Vs minus Vo (Positive) :b1, 0, 6 vL = minus Vo (Negative) :b2, 6, 10 section Inductor Current iL iL Rising Linearly :c1, 0, 6 iL Falling Linearly :c2, 6, 10 section Diode Current iD iD equals Zero :d1, 0, 6 iD equals iL :d2, 6, 10 section Output Voltage Vo Vo Ripple Around Average :e1, 0, 10 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR Input[Input DC Source Vs] --> PWMGen[PWM Controller IC] PWMGen --> GateDrv[Gate Driver Circuit] GateDrv --> Switch[Power Switch MOSFET or IGBT] Switch --> Inductor[Inductor L] Inductor --> OutNode[Output Node] OutNode --> Capacitor[Filter Capacitor C] OutNode --> Load[Load R] Load -.feedback voltage.-> PWMGen Load -.feedback current.-> PWMGen Switch -.parallel diode.-> FreewheelDiode[Freewheeling Diode] FreewheelDiode --> Inductor ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this architecture**: The PWM controller IC compares the output voltage with an internal reference and generates a duty-cycle modulated gate pulse. The gate driver level-shifts this low-power signal to drive the MOSFET. The L-C network filters the chopped waveform to recover a near-DC output voltage. The freewheeling diode provides a path for the inductor current when the switch is OFF, ensuring **continuous conduction**. ## 4.4 Mermaid Safe-Reference Cheat Sheet The following table summarizes the labels used in the diagrams above for clarity: | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | Vs | Source DC input voltage | | Vo | Output DC voltage | | D | Duty cycle | | Ts | Switching period | | fs | Switching frequency | | L | Inductance value | | C | Filter capacitance | | R | Load resistance | | iL | Inductor current | | vL | Inductor voltage | | iD | Diode current | # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] **State and explain the Volt-Second Balance principle used in DC-DC converter analysis.** **Model Answer:** The Volt-Second Balance principle states that in **steady-state** operation of a switching converter, the average voltage across an inductor over one complete switching cycle $T_s$ must be zero. Mathematically: $$\int_0^{T_s} v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$ This arises because any non-zero average inductor voltage would cause the inductor current to either increase or decrease unboundedly, violating the steady-state assumption. It is the **fundamental tool** used to derive the conversion ratios $V_o/V_s$ for Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost converters in CCM. *(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for physical justification)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM).** **Model Answer:** | Feature | CCM | DCM | |---|---|---| | Inductor current at end of cycle | $i_L > 0$ | $i_L = 0$ for some interval | | Switching losses | Lower per cycle | Higher per cycle | | Conversion ratio | Depends only on $D$ | Depends on $D$ and load | | Conduction losses | Higher | Lower | | Typical application | High-power converters | Light-load / standby | In CCM, the inductor never fully discharges during the OFF interval, while in DCM the inductor current reaches zero and stays at zero for a finite portion of the cycle. *(3 marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for comparison)* --- ## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] **(a)** For a Buck converter operating in CCM, derive the expression for the output voltage $V_o$ in terms of input voltage $V_s$ and duty cycle $D$ using the volt-second balance principle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Boost converter has $V_s = 24$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100$ ┬ĄH, $f_s = 50$ kHz, and load $R = 20$ ╬®. Calculate: **(i)** output voltage, **(ii)** inductor current ripple, and **(iii)** verify CCM operation. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A **(a) Derivation of Buck converter output voltage:** **Step 1:** During switch ON interval $0 \le t \le D T_s$, applying KVL: $$v_L = V_s - V_o$$ **Step 2:** During switch OFF interval $D T_s \le t \le T_s$, applying KVL around the freewheeling loop: $$v_L = -V_o$$ **Step 3:** Apply volt-second balance: $$(V_s - V_o) D T_s + (-V_o)(1-D) T_s = 0$$ **Step 4:** Divide by $T_s$: $$(V_s - V_o) D - V_o (1-D) = 0$$ **Step 5:** Expand and simplify: $$V_s D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D = 0$$ $$V_s D - V_o = 0$$ $$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$ Since $0 < D < 1$, we have $V_o < V_s$, confirming step-down operation. *(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1-5, 2 for final boxed result with physical interpretation)* --- **(b) Boost converter calculations:** **Given:** $V_s = 24$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100$ ┬ĄH, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $R = 20$ ╬®. **(i) Output voltage:** $$V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D} = \dfrac{24}{1-0.4} = \dfrac{24}{0.6} = 40 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) Inductor current ripple:** First, compute average inductor current. In a Boost converter, by power balance (assuming lossless), $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$, and $I_L = I_s = V_o I_o / V_s$. Alternatively: $$I_L = \dfrac{V_o}{R(1-D)} = \dfrac{40}{20 \times 0.6} = 3.333 \text{ A}$$ Now apply ripple formula: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L} = \dfrac{24 \times 0.4}{50 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{9.6}{5} = 1.92 \text{ A}$$ **(iii) Verify CCM operation:** For CCM, we need $I_L > \Delta i_L / 2$: $$\dfrac{\Delta i_L}{2} = \dfrac{1.92}{2} = 0.96 \text{ A}$$ Since $I_L = 3.333$ A $\gg 0.96$ A, the inductor current never reaches zero. **Operation is in CCM**. Ō£ō *(7 marks: 2 for (i), 3 for (ii), 2 for (iii) with proper justification)* --- ### Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice) **(a)** For a Buck-Boost converter operating in CCM, derive the conversion ratio and explain why the output voltage is inverted with respect to the input. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Buck converter feeds a load $R = 10$ ╬® from a 48 V source. The switching frequency is 100 kHz and the duty cycle is 0.45. Design the inductor $L$ such that the peak-to-peak inductor current ripple is limited to 20% of the average inductor current. Also calculate the output voltage ripple if $C = 47$ ┬ĄF. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B **(a) Derivation of Buck-Boost conversion ratio:** **Step 1:** When the switch is ON (interval $0 \le t \le D T_s$), the inductor is connected directly across the source, so: $$v_L = V_s$$ **Step 2:** When the switch is OFF (interval $D T_s \le t \le T_s$), the inductor discharges through the diode into the output. With the conventional polarity reference: $$v_L = V_o$$ **Step 3:** Apply volt-second balance: $$V_s \cdot D T_s + V_o \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$ Dividing by $T_s$: $$V_s D + V_o (1-D) = 0$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $V_o$: $$V_o = -\dfrac{D V_s}{1-D}$$ The **negative sign** arises because of the polarity convention adopted for the output voltage. Physically, when the switch turns OFF, the inductor forces current out of its dotted terminal through the diode into the output capacitor. With the polarity of $V_o$ defined with the positive terminal at the cathode of the diode, the voltage is naturally negative with respect to the input common terminal. *(7 marks: 2 each for steps 1-3, 1 for the final derivation)* --- **(b) Buck converter inductor design:** **Given:** $V_s = 48$ V, $D = 0.45$, $f_s = 100$ kHz, $R = 10$ ╬®, ripple = 20% of average current. **Step 1: Output voltage.** $$V_o = D V_s = 0.45 \times 48 = 21.6 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Average load current.** $$I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = \dfrac{21.6}{10} = 2.16 \text{ A}$$ For a Buck converter in CCM, $I_L = I_o = 2.16$ A. **Step 3: Required ripple.** $$\Delta i_L = 0.20 \times I_L = 0.20 \times 2.16 = 0.432 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4: Solve for L.** From the ripple formula: $$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$$ Rearranging: $$L = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s \Delta i_L} = \dfrac{48 \times 0.45 \times 0.55}{100 \times 10^3 \times 0.432}$$ $$L = \dfrac{11.88}{43{,}200} = 2.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ H} = 275 \text{ ┬ĄH}$$ **Step 5: Output voltage ripple.** Using the standard approximation: $$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{1-D}{8 L C f_s^2}$$ $$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{1-0.45}{8 \times 275 \times 10^{-6} \times 47 \times 10^{-6} \times (10^5)^2}$$ $$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{0.55}{8 \times 275 \times 10^{-6} \times 47 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{10}}$$ $$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{0.55}{103{,}400} = 5.32 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\Delta V_o = 5.32 \times 10^{-6} \times 21.6 = 1.149 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.115 \text{ mV}$$ This ripple is exceptionally small ŌĆö well within typical SMPS design specifications. *(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1-4, 3 for step 5 including ripple calculation and interpretation)* --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Sign Error in Buck-Boost:** Forgetting the negative sign on $V_o = -D V_s / (1-D)$ costs **2 to 3 marks**. Examiners frequently test this. > 2. **Confusing $V_{L,on}$ Across Topologies:** In Buck, $V_{L,on} = V_s - V_o$; in Boost and Buck-Boost, $V_{L,on} = V_s$. Always derive from KVL, never memorize blindly. > 3. **Not Specifying CCM Assumption:** Many answers lose a mark because the student fails to explicitly state "assuming continuous conduction mode." > 4. **Wrong Ripple Formula:** Mixing the Buck ripple formula $\dfrac{1-D}{8 L C f_s^2}$ with the Boost ripple formula $\dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ is a common error. **Choose the correct one based on the converter topology.** > 5. **Skipping Units:** Final answers without units (V, A, H, F) lose a mark each. Always carry units through every calculation. > 6. **Critical Inductance Calculation:** When asked to find "minimum $L$ for CCM," students often forget to substitute the **maximum** $R$ (i.e., minimum load) at which CCM must still be maintained. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Volt-Second Balance** is the master equation: $\int_0^{T_s} v_L dt = 0$. - [x] **Charge (Amp-Second) Balance**: $\int_0^{T_s} i_C dt = 0$. - [x] **Buck** converter: $V_o = D V_s$ (step-down, output in phase with input). - [x] **Boost** converter: $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$ (step-up, output in phase with input). - [x] **Buck-Boost** converter: $V_o = -D V_s / (1-D)$ (step-up or step-down, output inverted). - [x] **Inductor ripple**: $\Delta i_L = V_s D (1-D) / (f_s L)$ ŌĆö same form for all three basic converters. - [x] **CCM/DCM boundary** is set by $L_c$ or equivalently $R_{crit}$. - [x] **Switching waveforms** to draw in exam answers: gate pulse, $v_L$, $i_L$, $i_D$, $i_C$, $v_o$ ŌĆö always label axes with units. - [x] In steady state, $\Delta i_L$ ripple is symmetric about the average current $I_L$. - [x] The freewheeling diode carries the inductor current only during the OFF interval. - [x] KTU problems typically assume ideal switches, ideal diodes, and CCM unless stated otherwise. - [x] Always specify the polarity reference when defining $V_o$ ŌĆö especially critical for Buck-Boost. - [x] Frequency $f_s$ appears **squared** in some ripple expressions (e.g., output voltage ripple of Buck) ŌĆö read carefully.

topics.title: DC-AC Inverters: Square Wave Operations, Half Bridge and Full Bridge Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** DC-AC Inverters: Square Wave Operations, Half Bridge and Full Bridge Inverters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > A **DC-AC Inverter** is a power electronic converter that converts a **DC input voltage source** into a **symmetrical AC output voltage** of desired magnitude and frequency. The basic objectives of an inverter are: > 1. To produce a sinusoidal AC output waveform from a fixed or variable DC source. > 2. To control the magnitude and frequency of the produced AC output waveform. > 3. To provide a regulated AC power to various loads (inductive, resistive, or non-linear). Inverters operating in **square wave mode** generate an output that alternates between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ for equal half-cycles, producing a non-sinusoidal but periodic waveform whose fundamental component is sinusoidal. ### 1.2 Types of Inverters (KTU 2024 Scheme Classification) > [!NOTE] KTU Classification Highlight > **Based on Configuration:** > - **Half-Bridge (Single-Phase) Inverter** ŌĆö 2 switches, 1 leg, 2 DC sources (or split capacitor). > - **Full-Bridge (Single-Phase) Inverter** ŌĆö 4 switches, 2 legs, 1 DC source. > > **Based on Output Waveform:** > - **Square-Wave Inverter** ŌĆö Output switches between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ symmetrically. > - **Quasi-Square Wave (Modified Sine Wave)** ŌĆö Includes a zero-voltage interval ($\mu$) to reduce harmonics. > - **PWM Inverter** ŌĆö Pulse-width modulation for sinusoidal synthesis (covered later). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Water Tap of Electricity" Imagine a **DC battery** as a one-way water tap that only pushes water (current) in one direction. A DC-AC inverter is like a **smart plumber** who: - Connects two tanks of water (positive and negative voltages) to a common pipe (the AC output). - Uses **valves (transistors like MOSFETs/IGBTs)** to alternately direct positive water and negative water to your home tap. - A **square wave inverter** is the most basic plumber ŌĆö it slams the valve fully open in one direction, then slams it fully in the opposite direction, creating abrupt, jarring pressure changes (the abrupt voltage edges). - A **half-bridge** is a 2-valve setup needing **two water tanks** ($V_s/2$ each). - A **full-bridge** is a 4-valve setup that cleverly uses **one large tank** ($V_s$) to produce the same swing ŌĆö a more efficient plumber! ### 1.4 Ideal Switches & Key Assumptions (Standard KTU Assumptions) > [!IMPORTANT] Standard Assumptions for Analysis > 1. All switches are **ideal** ŌĆö zero ON-state voltage drop, infinite OFF-state impedance. > 2. The output load is **purely resistive** ($R$) for initial analysis (or R-L in advanced problems). > 3. The DC source $V_s$ is **constant and ripple-free**. > 4. The switching frequency $f_o$ is the fundamental output frequency. > 5. Switching is **instantaneous** (no rise/fall time). ### 1.5 Need & Applications | Application Domain | Use Case | |---|---| | **Renewable Energy** | Solar PV inverters, wind turbine grid-tie conversion | | **UPS Systems** | Uninterruptible Power Supplies for computers/hospitals | | **Motor Drives** | Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for induction motors | | **HVDC Transmission** | High-voltage DC-to-AC conversion at grid level | | **Induction Heating** | High-frequency AC generation for heating | **Physical Constants Used:** - $V_s = $ DC Source Voltage (in Volts) - $f_o = $ Fundamental Output Frequency (in Hz, typically **50 Hz / 60 Hz**) - $T = 1/f_o = $ Time Period of one cycle - $\omega_o = 2\pi f_o = $ Angular Frequency ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Half-Bridge Square Wave Inverter #### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology The half-bridge inverter consists of: - **Two equal DC sources** $V_s/2$ each (or a split capacitor creating a virtual midpoint $N$). - **Two controlled switches** $S_1$ and $S_2$ (e.g., IGBT/MOSFET with anti-parallel diodes for inductive loads). - A **load** connected between the midpoint $A$ of the switch leg and the neutral point $N$. > [!NOTE] Switch Pairing Rule > $S_1$ and $S_2$ operate **180┬░ out of phase**. When $S_1$ is ON, $S_2$ is OFF, and vice versa. This prevents a short-circuit across the DC bus (shoot-through fault). #### 2.1.2 Mode-by-Mode Operation **Mode 1 ($0 < t < T/2$):** - $S_1$ is ON, $S_2$ is OFF. - Point $A$ is connected to the positive rail ($+V_s/2$). - Load voltage $v_o = +V_s/2$. - Load current $i_o = V_s / (2R)$ (positive direction). **Mode 2 ($T/2 < t < T$):** - $S_1$ is OFF, $S_2$ is ON. - Point $A$ is connected to the negative rail ($-V_s/2$). - Load voltage $v_o = -V_s/2$. - Load current $i_o = -V_s / (2R)$ (reverses direction). #### 2.1.3 Why Peak Output is $V_s/2$ ŌĆö The Intuitive Explanation In a half-bridge, the load only "sees" **half** of the total DC bus voltage because one DC source is on one side and the other on the other side. The load is essentially connected between the +terminal of the upper source and the ŌłÆterminal of the lower source through the neutral. Hence, peak voltage $= V_s/2$ (not $V_s$). ### 2.2 Full-Bridge Square Wave Inverter #### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology The full-bridge (H-bridge) inverter consists of: - **One DC source** $V_s$. - **Four controlled switches** $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ arranged as two legs. - A **load** connected between the midpoints $A$ and $B$ of the two legs. > [!IMPORTANT] Switch Pairing Logic > - $S_1$ and $S_2$ are diagonal pairs operating **simultaneously** (180┬░ apart in each half-cycle). > - $S_3$ and $S_4$ form the other diagonal pair. > - When $S_1, S_2$ ON: $v_o = +V_s$. > - When $S_3, S_4$ ON: $v_o = -V_s$. #### 2.2.2 Mode-by-Mode Operation **Mode 1 ($0 < t < T/2$):** - Switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ are ON; $S_3$ and $S_4$ are OFF. - Current path: $V_s(+) \rightarrow S_1 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow V_s(-)$. - Load voltage $v_o = V_s$ (full bus voltage). **Mode 2 ($T/2 < t < T$):** - Switches $S_3$ and $S_4$ are ON; $S_1$ and $S_2$ are OFF. - Current path: $V_s(+) \rightarrow S_3 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow S_4 \rightarrow V_s(-)$ (current now flows in opposite direction). - Load voltage $v_o = -V_s$. ### 2.3 Fourier Analysis of the Square Wave The output is an **odd, half-wave symmetric** square wave. By Fourier theorem: $$v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5...}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega_o t)$$ The Fourier coefficient is computed as: $$B_n = \frac{4}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} v_o(t) \sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt$$ For a half-bridge (peak $= V_s/2$): $$B_n = \frac{4}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \frac{V_s}{2} \sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt = \frac{2V_s}{n\pi} \quad \text{for odd } n$$ For a full-bridge (peak $= V_s$): $$B_n = \frac{4V_s}{n\pi} \quad \text{for odd } n$$ **Fundamental Component ($n = 1$):** - Half-bridge: $\hat{V}_{o1} = \dfrac{2 V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \, V_s$ - Full-bridge: $\hat{V}_{o1} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.2732 \, V_s$ **Third Harmonic ($n = 3$):** - Half-bridge: $\hat{V}_{o3} = \dfrac{2 V_s}{3\pi}$ - Full-bridge: $\hat{V}_{o3} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{3\pi}$ ### 2.4 RMS Output Voltage The RMS value of a square wave with peak $\hat{V}_o$ is simply $\hat{V}_o$ itself, because the waveform magnitude is constant in each half-cycle: $$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o^2(t)\, dt} = \hat{V}_o$$ Therefore: - **Half-bridge:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s/2$ - **Full-bridge:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ ### 2.5 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}}$$ For an ideal square wave (regardless of half/full bridge), **THD Ōēł 48.34 %**. This high harmonic content is the **major drawback** of square wave inverters ŌĆö it causes heating in motors and interference with sensitive electronics. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] Quick Reference Table for KTU Examinations | Parameter | Half-Bridge Inverter | Full-Bridge Inverter | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Number of switches | 2 | 4 | ŌĆö | | DC source requirement | 2 (each $V_s/2$) or split cap | 1 (full $V_s$) | ŌĆö | | Peak Output Voltage $\hat{V}_o$ | $V_s / 2$ | $V_s$ | Volts | | RMS Output Voltage $V_{o,rms}$ | $V_s / 2$ | $V_s$ | Volts | | Fundamental Peak $V_{o1,m}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_s}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi}$ | Volts | | Fundamental RMS $V_{o1,rms}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}$ | Volts | | $n$-th Harmonic Peak $V_{on,m}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_s}{n\pi}$ | $\dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi}$ | Volts | | RMS Load Current (resistive) | $\dfrac{V_s}{2R}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{R}$ | Amperes | | Peak Load Current | $\dfrac{V_s}{2R}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{R}$ | Amperes | | THD | $\approx 48.34\,\%$ | $\approx 48.34\,\%$ | ŌĆö | | Switching Frequency | $f_o$ | $f_o$ | Hz | | Diagonal Pair ON Duration | $T/2$ | $T/2$ | seconds | **Quasi-Square Wave Modification:** If the output remains at $0$ for an interval $\mu$ (in radians) in each half-cycle before flipping: $$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\mu}{\pi}}$$ $$\hat{V}_{o1,m} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{2}\right)$$ This control of $\mu$ (called **pulse-width modulation at fundamental frequency**) is the simplest way to **regulate the fundamental output amplitude** in square-wave inverters ŌĆö a very common KTU question! ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Fourier Coefficients for a Full-Bridge Square Wave **Given:** $v_o(t) = +V_s$ for $0 \leq t < T/2$ and $v_o(t) = -V_s$ for $T/2 \leq t < T$. **Step 1:** Identify symmetry. The waveform is **odd-symmetric** ($v_o(-t) = -v_o(t)$) and **half-wave symmetric** ($v_o(t + T/2) = -v_o(t)$). Hence, only **odd sine harmonics** exist: $n = 1, 3, 5, ...$ **Step 2:** Use the Fourier sine coefficient formula: $$B_n = \frac{4}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} v_o(t) \sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $v_o(t) = +V_s$ over $[0, T/2]$: $$B_n = \frac{4}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} V_s \sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt$$ **Step 4:** Perform the integration. Let $\theta = n\omega_o t$, so $d\theta = n\omega_o\, dt$, meaning $dt = \dfrac{d\theta}{n\omega_o}$: $$B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{n\omega_o} \int_{0}^{n\omega_o T/2} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate the integral $\int_0^{n\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta = [-\cos\theta]_0^{n\pi} = -\cos(n\pi) + \cos(0) = 1 - \cos(n\pi)$. **Step 6:** Since $n$ is odd, $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, so the integral evaluates to $1 - (-1) = 2$. **Step 7:** Substitute back. Note that $\omega_o T = 2\pi$, so $\dfrac{1}{T\,n\omega_o} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi n}$: $$B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{n\omega_o} \cdot 2 = \frac{8 V_s}{T \cdot n \omega_o} = \frac{8 V_s}{n \cdot 2\pi} = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}$$ **Step 8:** Final expression: $$\boxed{B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \quad \text{for } n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of RMS Value for Half-Bridge **Step 1:** The RMS definition: $$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o^2(t)\, dt}$$ **Step 2:** Split the integral into two half-cycles: $$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{2}\right)^2\!dt + \int_{T/2}^{T}\!\left(-\frac{V_s}{2}\right)^2\!dt\right]}$$ **Step 3:** Each integral simplifies: $$\int_0^{T/2}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{2}\right)^2 dt = \frac{V_s^2}{4} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = \frac{V_s^2 T}{8}$$ **Step 4:** Both halves give the same contribution: $$\int_0^T v_o^2(t)\, dt = 2 \cdot \frac{V_s^2 T}{8} = \frac{V_s^2 T}{4}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\cdot\frac{V_s^2 T}{4}} = \frac{V_s}{2}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of THD for Square Wave **Step 1:** We have $V_{o,rms} = \hat{V}_o$ and $V_{1,rms} = \hat{V}_o/\sqrt{2}$ for any square wave. **Step 2:** For the full-bridge, $\hat{V}_o = V_s$, so $V_{1,rms} = V_s/\sqrt{2}$ and $V_{o,rms} = V_s$. **Step 3:** Plug into THD formula: $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_s^2 - V_s^2/2}}{V_s/\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_s/\sqrt{2}}{V_s/\sqrt{2}} = 1$$ Wait ŌĆö this is wrong! Let's redo carefully: $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_s^2 - V_s^2/2}}{V_s/\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_s \sqrt{1/2}}{V_s/\sqrt{2}} = 1 = 100\,\%$$ This means total harmonic power equals fundamental power. The THD formula often uses the **fundamental peak** in the denominator for some textbook conventions. Using the standard IEEE definition (where $V_{1,rms}$ is denominator and harmonics are summed): $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5...} V_{n,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}}$$ Using Parseval's theorem and exact series sum: $$\sum_{n=3,5...} \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\frac{16V_s^2}{\pi^2}\left(\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\right)}}{\frac{4V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2/8 - 1}{\pi^2/8 \cdot \frac{1}{2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{(\pi^2 - 8)/8}{1/2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi/4}$$ Simplifying: $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2-8}{4}} \approx 0.4834 = \boxed{48.34\,\%}$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Worked Numerical Examples #### Example 1: Half-Bridge Output Calculation > Given: $V_s = 200\,\text{V}$, $R = 10\,\Omega$, $f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$, square-wave operation. **Step 1:** Peak output $\hat{V}_o = V_s/2 = 100\,\text{V}$. **Step 2:** RMS output $V_{o,rms} = V_s/2 = 100\,\text{V}$. **Step 3:** Fundamental peak $V_{o1,m} = 2V_s/\pi = 400/\pi \approx 127.32\,\text{V}$. **Step 4:** Fundamental RMS $V_{o1,rms} = 127.32/\sqrt{2} \approx 90.03\,\text{V}$. **Step 5:** 3rd harmonic peak $V_{o3,m} = 2V_s/(3\pi) \approx 42.44\,\text{V}$. **Step 6:** Load RMS current $I_{o,rms} = V_{o,rms}/R = 100/10 = 10\,\text{A}$. **Step 7:** Peak current $I_{o,peak} = 100/10 = 10\,\text{A}$. #### Example 2: Full-Bridge Comparison > Given: Same parameters ($V_s = 200\,\text{V}$, $R = 10\,\Omega$). | Quantity | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge | Ratio | |---|---|---|---| | $\hat{V}_o$ | $100\,\text{V}$ | $200\,\text{V}$ | $1:2$ | | $V_{o,rms}$ | $100\,\text{V}$ | $200\,\text{V}$ | $1:2$ | | $V_{o1,m}$ | $127.32\,\text{V}$ | $254.64\,\text{V}$ | $1:2$ | | $I_{o,rms}$ | $10\,\text{A}$ | $20\,\text{A}$ | $1:2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] Conclusion: For the same DC source voltage, a full-bridge inverter delivers **twice the output power** of a half-bridge. This is why full-bridge is preferred for higher power applications. ### 3.5 Quasi-Square Wave Operation Derivation **Setup:** Output $= +V_s$ for $0 \leq \omega t < (\pi - \mu)/2$, then $= 0$ for $(\pi - \mu)/2 \leq \omega t < (\pi + \mu)/2$, then $= -V_s$ for $(\pi + \mu)/2 \leq \omega t < \pi$, then back to zero for $\pi - (\pi+\mu)/2$ etc. **Step 1:** The Fourier series for a quasi-square wave (odd symmetric): $$v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5...} \frac{4V_s}{n\pi} \cos\!\left(\frac{n\mu}{2}\right) \sin(n\omega_o t)$$ **Step 2:** Fundamental peak ($n=1$): $$\hat{V}_{o1} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{\mu}{2}\right)$$ **Step 3:** For $\mu = 0$: $\hat{V}_{o1} = 4V_s/\pi$ (pure square wave ŌĆö maximum fundamental). **Step 4:** For $\mu = \pi$: $\hat{V}_{o1} = 0$ (no output ŌĆö extreme case). **Step 5:** RMS value: $$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\mu}{\pi}}$$ **Step 6:** This proves $\mu$ is the **control parameter** to regulate output amplitude from 0 to $4V_s/\pi$. ### 3.6 Comparison Table ŌĆö Half Bridge vs Full Bridge | Feature | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge | |---|---|---| | **DC Source Requirement** | Two (or split capacitor) | Single | | **Switch Count** | 2 | 4 | | **Output Voltage (for same $V_s$)** | $V_s/2$ | $V_s$ | | **Output Power (for same $V_s$, $R$)** | $V_s^2/4R$ | $V_s^2/R$ | | **Switch Voltage Rating** | $V_s$ | $V_s$ | | **Switch Current Rating** | $V_s/2R$ | $V_s/R$ | | **Complexity & Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Typical Application** | Low-power (<1 kW) | Medium-to-high power | | **Switch Utilization** | Same | Same | | **3rd Harmonic** | $\dfrac{2V_s}{3\pi}$ | $\dfrac{4V_s}{3\pi}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Half-Bridge Inverter ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR DC1["DC Source V_s/2
Upper Rail"] DC2["DC Source V_s/2
Lower Rail"] N["Neutral Point N
Virtual Midpoint"] A["Midpoint A
Output Node"] S1["Switch S1
IGBT/MOSFET"] S2["Switch S2
IGBT/MOSFET"] D1["Freewheel D1
Anti-Parallel Diode"] D2["Freewheel D2
Anti-Parallel Diode"] LOAD["Load R
Resistive"] OUT["v_o(t)
Square Wave Output"] DC1 --- S1 S1 --- A A --- S2 S2 --- DC2 DC1 --- N DC2 --- N A --- D1 D2 --- A LOAD --- OUT A --- LOAD N --- LOAD ``` ### 4.2 Full-Bridge Inverter ŌĆö H-Bridge Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB VS["DC Source V_s
Single Bus"] S1["S1
Upper Left"] S2["S2
Lower Left"] S3["S3
Upper Right"] S4["S4
