Lower Right"] D1["D1"] D2["D2"] D3["D3"] D4["D4"] A["Midpoint A"] B["Midpoint B"] LOAD["Load R"] OUT["v_o(t) = V_AB
Square Wave"] GATE1["Gate Driver
180 degree Logic"] GATE2["Gate Driver
180 degree Logic"] VS --- S1 VS --- S3 S1 --- A S3 --- B A --- S2 B --- S4 S2 --- VS S4 --- VS A --- D1 D1 --- S1 B --- D3 D3 --- S3 S2 --- D2 D2 --- A S4 --- D4 D4 --- B A --- LOAD LOAD --- B LOAD --- OUT GATE1 --- S1 GATE1 --- S2 GATE2 --- S3 GATE2 --- S4 ``` ### 4.3 Square-Wave Output Waveform ŌĆö Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CYCLE["One Complete Cycle T = 1/f_o"] direction LR H1["Mode 1: S1,S2 ON
Duration: T/2
v_o = +V_s"] H2["Mode 2: S3,S4 ON
Duration: T/2
v_o = -V_s"] end H1 --> H2 H2 --> H1 ``` ### 4.4 Quasi-Square Wave ŌĆö Pulse Position Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph QSW["Quasi-Square Wave with Notch Angle mu"] direction LR P1["+V_s
Duration: pi-mu over 2"] Z1["0 volts
Duration: mu"] P2["-V_s
Duration: pi-mu over 2"] Z2["0 volts
Duration: mu"] end P1 --> Z1 --> P2 --> Z2 ``` ### 4.5 Switching Logic State Table ŌĆö Full Bridge ```mermaid flowchart TB ST1["State 1: S1=ON, S2=ON
S3=OFF, S4=OFF
v_o = +V_s"] ST2["State 2: S1=OFF, S2=OFF
S3=ON, S4=ON
v_o = -V_s"] ST3["Forbidden: S1=ON and S3=ON simultaneously
Causes shoot-through fault"] ST1 -->|After T/2| ST2 ST2 -->|After T/2| ST1 ST1 -.->|Safety| ST3 ST2 -.->|Safety| ST3 ``` ### 4.6 Harmonic Spectrum Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR F1["Fundamental
n = 1
Magnitude 4Vs by pi"] F3["3rd Harmonic
n = 3
Magnitude 4Vs by 3pi"] F5["5th Harmonic
n = 5
Magnitude 4Vs by 5pi"] F7["7th Harmonic
n = 7
Magnitude 4Vs by 7pi"] F1 --- F3 F3 --- F5 F5 --- F7 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** *Define an inverter. Compare a half-bridge and full-bridge inverter based on the number of switches, peak output voltage, and DC source requirement.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** An inverter is a power electronic device that converts DC power into AC power at a desired voltage and frequency. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Parameter | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge | |---|---|---| | Number of switches | 2 | 4 | | Peak output voltage | $V_s/2$ | $V_s$ | | DC sources required | 2 (each $V_s/2$) | 1 | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]** *State the Fourier series expression for the output voltage of a single-phase full-bridge square wave inverter. Why are only odd harmonics present?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Fourier expression (2 marks):** $$v_o(t) = \frac{4V_s}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_o t) + \cdots\right]$$ $$v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{4V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\omega_o t)$$ **Reason for odd harmonics only (1 mark):** The square waveform is both **odd-symmetric** ($v_o(-t) = -v_o(t)$) and **half-wave symmetric** ($v_o(t+T/2) = -v_o(t)$). These symmetries force all even-index Fourier coefficients and all cosine terms to vanish, leaving only odd sine harmonics. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Half-Bridge Analysis **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Model Paper Module 3, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **working principle of a single-phase half-bridge square-wave inverter**. Draw the output voltage and current waveforms for a resistive load. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Fourier series expression** for the output voltage of the half-bridge inverter. Hence calculate the **RMS value of the fundamental component** if $V_s = 100\,\text{V}$ and $f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) ŌĆö Half-Bridge Operation (7 marks) **Circuit Description (2 marks):** A single-phase half-bridge inverter consists of: - Two **equal DC sources** of $V_s/2$ each (or a single source $V_s$ with a split capacitor forming virtual neutral point $N$). - Two **controlled switches** $S_1$ and $S_2$ (typically IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes $D_1$, $D_2$ for inductive loads). - A **resistive load** $R$ connected between the switch-leg midpoint $A$ and the neutral $N$. **Operating Modes (3 marks):** **Mode 1 ($0 < t < T/2$):** - $S_1$ is ON, $S_2$ is OFF. - Point $A$ is at potential $+V_s/2$. - Output voltage $v_o = V_{AN} = V_s/2 - 0 = +V_s/2$. - Load current $i_o = v_o/R = +V_s/(2R)$. **Mode 2 ($T/2 < t < T$):** - $S_1$ is OFF, $S_2$ is ON. - Point $A$ is at potential $-V_s/2$. - Output voltage $v_o = -V_s/2$. - Load current $i_o = -V_s/(2R)$. **Waveforms (2 marks):** The output $v_o(t)$ is a square wave alternating between $+V_s/2$ and $-V_s/2$ with period $T$. For a resistive load, the current $i_o(t)$ has the **same square-wave shape** (in phase with voltage). #### Part (b) ŌĆö Fourier Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Recognize the wave is odd-symmetric and half-wave symmetric. So only odd sine harmonics: $$v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega_o t)$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute Fourier coefficient: $$B_n = \frac{4}{T}\int_0^{T/2} v_o(t)\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt = \frac{4}{T}\int_0^{T/2}\frac{V_s}{2}\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Integrate: $$B_n = \frac{2V_s}{T}\left[\frac{-\cos(n\omega_o t)}{n\omega_o}\right]_0^{T/2} = \frac{2V_s}{n\omega_o T}[1 - \cos(n\pi)]$$ Since $n$ is odd, $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, and $\omega_o T = 2\pi$: $$B_n = \frac{2V_s}{2\pi n}[1-(-1)] = \frac{2V_s}{n\pi}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Final expression: $$v_o(t) = \frac{2V_s}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_o t) + \cdots\right]$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting $V_s = 100\,\text{V}$, fundamental peak: $$\hat{V}_{o1} = \frac{2 \times 100}{\pi} = \frac{200}{\pi} \approx 63.66\,\text{V}$$ Fundamental RMS: $$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{o1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{200}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \approx \boxed{45.02\,\text{V}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Full-Bridge Analysis **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Model Paper Module 3, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the **working of a single-phase full-bridge inverter** with a neat circuit diagram and waveforms. Show that the output voltage is twice that of a half-bridge inverter for the same DC input. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-phase full-bridge square-wave inverter has $V_s = 220\,\text{V}$, $R = 20\,\Omega$, $f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$. Calculate: **(i)** RMS output voltage, **(ii)** peak fundamental component, **(iii)** RMS fundamental component, **(iv)** THD. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) ŌĆö Full-Bridge Operation (7 marks) **Circuit (2 marks):** A single-phase full-bridge inverter has 4 switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ arranged as two legs. Leg 1 ($S_1$ top, $S_2$ bottom) midpoint $A$. Leg 2 ($S_3$ top, $S_4$ bottom) midpoint $B$. Load is connected between $A$ and $B$. **Switching Logic (2 marks):** - **Mode 1 ($0 \le t < T/2$):** $S_1, S_2$ ON; $S_3, S_4$ OFF. Current flows: $V_s(+) \to S_1 \to A \to \text{Load} \to B \to S_2 \to V_s(-)$. Thus $v_o = V_s$. - **Mode 2 ($T/2 \le t < T$):** $S_3, S_4$ ON; $S_1, S_2$ OFF. Current flows: $V_s(+) \to S_3 \to B \to \text{Load} \to A \to S_4 \to V_s(-)$. Thus $v_o = -V_s$. **Doubling Comparison (2 marks):** Both half-bridge and full-bridge use switches of voltage rating $V_s$. However: - Half-bridge: Load sees swing from $+V_s/2$ to $-V_s/2$, peak $= V_s/2$. - Full-bridge: Load sees swing from $+V_s$ to $-V_s$, peak $= V_s$. Ratio: $V_s / (V_s/2) = 2$. Hence full-bridge gives **twice** the peak output voltage (and 4├Ś the output power) for the same DC source voltage. **Waveform (1 mark):** A symmetric square wave with peak $V_s$, period $T$, zero average value. #### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computations (7 marks) Given: $V_s = 220\,\text{V}$, $R = 20\,\Omega$, $f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$. **(i) RMS Output Voltage (1.5 marks):** $$V_{o,rms} = V_s = 220\,\text{V}$$ **(ii) Peak Fundamental Component (2 marks):** $$\hat{V}_{o1} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} = \frac{4 \times 220}{\pi} = \frac{880}{\pi} \approx 280.11\,\text{V}$$ **(iii) RMS Fundamental Component (1.5 marks):** $$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{o1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{280.11}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 198.07\,\text{V}$$ **(iv) Total Harmonic Distortion (2 marks):** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}}{V_{o1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{220^2 - 198.07^2}}{198.07}$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{48400 - 39231.7}}{198.07} = \frac{\sqrt{9168.3}}{198.07} = \frac{95.75}{198.07} \approx 0.4834$$ $$\boxed{\text{THD} \approx 48.34\,\%}$$ #### Bonus Verification (Optional, 1 mark): The instantaneous load current during Mode 1: $i_o = V_s/R = 220/20 = 11\,\text{A}$. The peak current during Mode 2 reverses: $-11\,\text{A}$. --- > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students confuse half-bridge and full-bridge formulas. If the question says "half-bridge," use $V_s/2$ and $2V_s/\pi$ ŌĆö not $V_s$ and $4V_s/\pi$! (Costs ~3 marks) > > 2. **Missing harmonic indices:** In Fourier series, always write $n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$ explicitly. Students who write "$n$" alone (including even harmonics) lose marks. > > 3. **RMS vs Peak Confusion:** When asked for "fundamental RMS," do NOT give $4V_s/\pi$ ŌĆö that is the **peak** of the fundamental. Divide by $\sqrt{2}$. (Costs ~2 marks) > > 4. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for stating "switches are ideal" and "DC source is ripple-free" at the start. > > 5. **Diode direction in diagrams:** The anti-parallel diodes must be drawn pointing **opposite** to the switch direction. Wrong orientation = ŌłÆ1 mark. > > 6. **THD calculation:** Use $V_{1,rms}$ in denominator (IEEE definition). Some textbooks use peak ŌĆö match the question's wording. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 3, Topic DC-AC Inverters **Definitions to Memorize:** - ŌśÉ Inverter = DC ŌåÆ AC converter - ŌśÉ Half-bridge: 2 switches, peak output $= V_s/2$ - ŌśÉ Full-bridge: 4 switches, peak output $= V_s$ - ŌśÉ Square wave = alternates between $+\hat{V}$ and $-\hat{V}$ with 50% duty **Key Formulas (Flash Card Style):** - ŌśÉ Half-bridge fundamental peak: $2V_s/\pi$ - ŌśÉ Full-bridge fundamental peak: $4V_s/\pi$ - ŌśÉ RMS of square wave = its peak value - ŌśÉ THD of square wave $\approx 48.34\,\%$ - ŌśÉ Quasi-square wave amplitude control: $V_{o1} = (4V_s/\pi)\cos(\mu/2)$ - ŌśÉ Quasi-square wave RMS: $V_s\sqrt{1 - \mu/\pi}$ **Conceptual Understanding Check:** - ŌśÉ Why does the full-bridge produce twice the output of the half-bridge for same $V_s$? - ŌśÉ Why are only odd harmonics present in the Fourier series? - ŌśÉ What is the role of anti-parallel diodes? (Freewheeling for inductive loads) - ŌśÉ What is "shoot-through fault" and why is it dangerous? (Both switches of a leg ON simultaneously short-circuits DC bus) - ŌśÉ What is the purpose of pulse-width $\mu$ in quasi-square wave? (Fundamental amplitude regulation + harmonic reduction) **Operational Rules:** - ŌśÉ Switches of a single leg are always operated **complementarily** (180┬░ apart). - ŌśÉ Diagonal switches in H-bridge are operated **simultaneously**. - ŌśÉ For inductive loads, **anti-parallel diodes are mandatory**. - ŌśÉ Switching frequency = output frequency in square-wave operation (no PWM). - ŌśÉ Quasi-square wave is the simplest form of amplitude control without changing $V_s$. **Numerical Values to Remember:** - $\pi \approx 3.14159$ - $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ - $4/\pi \approx 1.273$ - $2/\pi \approx 0.637$ - $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$ - $\text{THD}_{\text{square}} \approx 48.34\,\%$ **KTU 2024 Scheme Specific Outcomes (CO Mapping):** - ŌśÉ **CO3:** Analyze the operation of DC-AC inverters in square-wave mode. - ŌśÉ **CO4:** Evaluate harmonic content using Fourier analysis. - ŌśÉ **CO5:** Design inverter circuits for given voltage/power specifications. **Common Numerical Question Pattern (Practice These!):** 1. "Given $V_s$, $R$, and $f_o$, find RMS output, fundamental peak, fundamental RMS, 3rd harmonic peak, and THD." (Standard 7ŌĆō10 mark problem) 2. "Compare half-bridge and full-bridge for the same DC source." (5 mark conceptual) 3. "Sketch the circuit and waveforms for a half-bridge/full-bridge inverter with R/R-L load." (5ŌĆō7 mark diagram) 4. "Derive the Fourier series for the output." (7 mark derivation) 5. "Calculate amplitude regulation using $\mu$ control." (7 mark quasi-square wave) **Pitfalls to Avoid:** - ŌśÉ Confusing half-bridge vs full-bridge formulas - ŌśÉ Forgetting to mention odd-only harmonic indices - ŌśÉ Using peak instead of RMS values (or vice versa) - ŌśÉ Not drawing anti-parallel diodes for inductive loads - ŌśÉ Forgetting to label axes on waveforms (Time vs Voltage/Current)

topics.title: Solar Power Plants: On-Grid and Off-Grid Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solar Power Plants: On-Grid and Off-Grid Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Solar Power Plant ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Power Plant (SPP):** A large-scale photovoltaic (PV) installation that converts solar irradiance directly into electrical energy using semiconductor-based solar cells, followed by power electronic conditioning (DCŌĆōDC and DCŌĆōAC conversion) to deliver usable AC power either to a local load/standalone system or to the utility grid.

A Solar Power Plant is classified into two principal configurations based on its grid-interaction capability:

| Configuration | Synonym | Grid Connection | Energy Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **On-Grid** | Grid-Tied / Utility-Interactive | Mandatory (synchronized to utility grid) | Optional / Not essential |
| **Off-Grid** | Stand-Alone / Islanded | None (operates independently) | Mandatory (Battery Bank) |

---

### 1.2 On-Grid (Grid-Tied) Solar Power Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **On-Grid Solar Power Plant:** A PV system that operates in synchronism with the public utility grid, feeding generated power directly into the grid through a grid-tie inverter. Excess energy is exported to the grid; deficit energy is imported.

**Block Constituents (Mandatory):**
1. **PV Array** ŌĆö series/parallel combination of modules
2. **String Combiner Box (SCB)** ŌĆö aggregates DC strings
3. **DCŌĆōDC Converter with MPPT** ŌĆö extracts maximum power under varying irradiance/temperature
4. **Grid-Tie Inverter (DCŌĆōAC)** ŌĆö synchronizes voltage, frequency, and phase with grid
5. **AC Protection Panel** ŌĆö MCB, MCCB, SPD, Earth Fault Relay
6. **Net Meter / Bi-directional Meter** ŌĆö records import/export units
7. **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** ŌĆö interface to utility grid

---

### 1.3 Off-Grid (Stand-Alone) Solar Power Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **Off-Grid Solar Power Plant:** A self-sustained PV system that operates independently of the utility grid, relying on a battery bank for energy storage to supply load during non-sunny hours and cloudy days.

**Block Constituents (Mandatory):**
1. **PV Array**
2. **Charge Controller** ŌĆö regulates battery charging (PWM or MPPT type)
3. **Battery Bank** ŌĆö stores DC energy
4. **Off-Grid Inverter (DCŌĆōAC)** ŌĆö supplies AC loads
5. **AC/DC Distribution Panel** ŌĆö load switching and protection
6. **Diesel Generator (Optional Back-up)** ŌĆö hybrid configuration

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **­¤ÅĀ Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Mango Tree House":**
> - Imagine your house has a **mango tree** (the Solar PV system) that produces fruit (electricity).
> - **On-Grid House:** You live in a city with a **mango market next door** (the utility grid). Whatever mangoes you don't eat, you **sell to the market** via the gate. When the tree has no fruit (night/rain), you **buy from the market**. No need to store mangoes in a fridge.
> - **Off-Grid House:** You live on a **remote island with no market** (no grid access). You must store every mango in a **refrigerator** (battery) to eat later. If the fridge is full, extra mangoes are **wasted**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used

The following standard performance indices are universally used to evaluate solar power plants (per **IEC 61724** and **MNRE India** guidelines):

- **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** ŌĆö expressed in **%**
- **Performance Ratio (PR)** ŌĆö dimensionless, ideally **Ōēź 0.75**
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** ŌĆö analogous to CUF
- **Specific Yield (kWh/kWp/year)** ŌĆö typically **1100ŌĆō1600** in India
- **Degradation Factor** ŌĆö **0.5 % to 1 % per year** for crystalline silicon modules
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** ŌĆö equivalent hours of **1000 W/m┬▓** irradiance

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of On-Grid Solar Power Plant

The operational logic can be broken down as follows:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö DC Generation:** The PV array, composed of series-connected cells (typically 60/72 cells per module), converts photon energy into DC electricity. The IV curve is highly non-linear.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö MPPT Action:** A DCŌĆōDC boost/buck converter with an MPPT algorithm (P&O or Incremental Conductance) continuously tracks the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** to maximize energy harvest under varying irradiance $G$ (W/m┬▓) and cell temperature $T_c$ (┬░C).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö DCŌĆōAC Conversion:** The grid-tie inverter uses a **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with **PWM** control to convert DC into 50 Hz AC.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Grid Synchronization:** Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) ensures inverter voltage matches the grid in **amplitude, frequency, and phase** before connection via anti-islanding relay.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Net Metering:** A bi-directional meter registers energy flow. Export = credit (in some Indian states); Import = debit.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Anti-Islanding Protection:** Inverter MUST disconnect within **2 seconds** (per IEEE 1547 / IEC 62116) upon grid failure to prevent back-feeding into a de-energized grid (safety for line workers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no battery?** Because the grid itself acts as an infinite "storage" ŌĆö any excess is absorbed, any deficit is supplied instantly. This dramatically reduces capital cost (no battery, which costs ~30ŌĆō40% of system price).

---

### 2.2 Working Principle of Off-Grid Solar Power Plant

- **Step 1 ŌĆö DC Generation:** Same as on-grid.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Charge Control:** The charge controller regulates the PV voltage to safely charge the battery. MPPT charge controllers are ~95ŌĆō98% efficient vs. ~70ŌĆō80% for PWM types.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Battery Storage:** Energy is stored electrochemically (typically Lead-Acid tubular or Li-ion). Battery is sized in **Ah** using the days-of-autonomy and depth-of-discharge (DoD) constraints.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö DCŌĆōAC Inversion:** When AC loads are ON, the off-grid inverter (with built-in **solar charge controller** in many hybrid designs) draws DC from battery and produces 230 V, 50 Hz AC.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Load Management:** Inverter must be sized for peak load. Some systems use **hybrid inverters** that integrate the MPPT, charger, and inverter in one unit.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Backup Generator (Optional):** In hybrid off-grid plants, a diesel genset auto-starts when battery State-of-Charge (SoC) drops below a threshold (e.g., 30%).

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" of Off-Grid:** Off-grid plants are essential for remote/rural electrification where extending the utility grid is **economically or geographically infeasible** (e.g., islands, hilly terrains, tribal hamlets). The Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and ANERT heavily promote off-grid systems for such applications.

---

### 2.3 Detailed Comparison: On-Grid vs Off-Grid

| Parameter | On-Grid System | Off-Grid System |
|---|---|---|
| Grid Connection | **Mandatory** | **None** |
| Battery Storage | Optional (rare) | **Mandatory** |
| Initial Cost | **Lower** (no battery) | **Higher** (battery = 30ŌĆō40%) |
| Payback Period | 4ŌĆō6 years | 7ŌĆō10 years |
| Efficiency | **Higher** (no battery losses) | Lower (battery round-trip ~80%) |
| Net Metering | Available | Not applicable |
| Power Supply | Day: PV; Night: Grid | Day: PV+Battery; Night: Battery |
| Anti-Islanding | Required | Not required |
| Reliability | Depends on grid | Depends on battery+sun |
| Typical Capacity | 1 kW ŌĆō 100 MW | 100 W ŌĆō 100 kW |
| Main Application | Urban/suburban rooftops, solar farms | Remote homes, telecom towers, water pumps |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Yield (Daily) | $E_d = P_{PV} \times PSH \times \eta_{system}$ | kWh/day | $P_{PV}$ = Installed DC capacity |
| 2 | Annual Energy Generation | $E_a = P_{PV} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{system}$ | kWh/year | Multiply daily yield by 365 |
| 3 | Capacity Utilization Factor | $CUF = \dfrac{E_a}{P_{PV} \times 8760}$ | fraction or % | Typically 15ŌĆō20% in India |
| 4 | Performance Ratio | $PR = \dfrac{E_a}{P_{PV} \times \sum G_{POA} / G_{STC}}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 0.75$ acceptable |
| 5 | Specific Yield | $Y_f = \dfrac{E_a}{P_{PV}}$ | kWh/kWp/year | 1100ŌĆō1600 in India |
| 6 | Battery Capacity | $B_{Ah} = \dfrac{E_d \times D_a}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$ | Ah | $D_a$ = days of autonomy |
| 7 | PV Array Sizing | $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_d}{PSH \times \eta_{system}}$ | kWp | Account for system losses |
| 8 | Inverter Sizing | $P_{inv} = P_{load,peak} / \eta_{inv}$ | kW | Typically 1.2├Ś continuous load |
| 9 | Cell Temperature | $T_c = T_a + (NOCT - 20) \times G / 800$ | ┬░C | $NOCT$ = Nominal Operating Cell Temp (~45┬░C) |
| 10 | Payback Period | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{C_{install} - Subsidy}{E_a \times (P_t - P_g)}$ | years | $P_t$ = tariff; $P_g$ = grid cost |
| 11 | COŌéé Avoided | $CO_2 = E_a \times 0.82$ | kg/year | India grid emission factor Ōēł **0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh** |
| 12 | Voltage (PV String) | $V_{mp,string} = N_s \times V_{mp,module}$ | V | Within inverter MPPT window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance $G = 1000$ W/m┬▓, Cell Temperature $T_c = 25$ ┬░C, Air Mass $AM = 1.5$. All module nameplate ratings (Wp) are at STC.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-Grid:** The backbone of India's **PM Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana** (2024) ŌĆö targets 1 crore households with rooftop solar and net metering.
- **Off-Grid:** Critical for **ANERT's** solar electrification of tribal colonies in Attapadi, Wayanad (Kerala).
- **Hybrid systems** (on-grid + battery) are now emerging for high-reliability applications like hospitals, data centers, and EV charging stations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Energy Generation of a Solar Power Plant

We start from the basic principle that the instantaneous DC power output of a PV array is governed by the **single-diode model**:

$$P_{dc}(t) = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G(t)$$

where:
- $P_{dc}(t)$ = instantaneous DC power output in Watts
- $\eta_{PV}$ = module conversion efficiency (typically 15ŌĆō22%)
- $A$ = total array area in m┬▓
- $G(t)$ = instantaneous plane-of-array (POA) irradiance in W/m┬▓

**Step 1 ŌĆö Integrate over a day to get daily energy:**

$$E_d = \int_{sunrise}^{sunset} P_{dc}(t) \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{loss} \, dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the concept of Peak Sun Hours (PSH):**

The PSH is defined as the equivalent number of hours per day during which solar irradiance is hypothetically constant at $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m┬▓, delivering the same total daily energy. Mathematically:

$$PSH = \frac{\int_{day} G(t) \, dt}{G_{STC}} = \frac{\int_{day} G(t) \, dt}{1000}$$

For Kerala, average PSH Ōēł **4.5ŌĆō5.0 hours/day**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and simplify:**

$$E_d = P_{PV} \times PSH \times \eta_{system}$$

where $\eta_{system} = \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{cable} \times \eta_{temp} \times \eta_{dirt}$ (typical = 0.75ŌĆō0.85).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Annual Energy Generation:**

$$E_a = E_d \times 365 = P_{PV} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{system}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)

The CUF represents the ratio of **actual energy generated** to the **maximum possible energy** if the plant operated at its rated capacity 24/7 for 365 days.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Maximum possible energy (24/7 operation):**

$$E_{max} = P_{PV} \times 8760 \text{ (hours in a year)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define CUF:**

$$CUF = \frac{E_a}{E_{max}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $E_a$:**

$$CUF = \frac{P_{PV} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{system}}{P_{PV} \times 8760}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cancel $P_{PV}$ and simplify:**

$$CUF = \frac{PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{system}}{8760}$$

**Numerical Example (Kerala rooftop):**
- $PSH = 4.8$ h/day
- $\eta_{system} = 0.80$
- $CUF = \dfrac{4.8 \times 365 \times 0.80}{8760} = 0.16 = 16\%$

> [!NOTE]
> A CUF of **15ŌĆō20%** is typical for Indian solar plants. Higher altitudes and deserts (Rajasthan) can reach 22ŌĆō25%.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Battery Bank Sizing for Off-Grid System

**Given:** Daily load energy $E_d$ (Wh), Days of autonomy $D_a$, Battery voltage $V_{bat}$, Depth of Discharge $DoD$, Battery efficiency $\eta_{bat}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Energy to be stored:**

$$E_{store} = E_d \times D_a$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Account for DoD (battery cannot be fully drained):**

$$E_{effective} = \frac{E_{store}}{DoD}$$

Typical $DoD = 0.5$ for Lead-Acid, $0.8$ŌĆō$0.9$ for Li-ion.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Account for battery round-trip efficiency:**

$$E_{battery} = \frac{E_{effective}}{\eta_{bat}}$$

Typical $\eta_{bat} = 0.80$ for Lead-Acid, $0.95$ for Li-ion.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to Ampere-hours:**

$$B_{Ah} = \frac{E_{battery}}{V_{bat}}$$

**Combined Formula:**

$$B_{Ah} = \frac{E_d \times D_a}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- $E_d = 5$ kWh = 5000 Wh
- $D_a = 2$ days
- $V_{bat} = 48$ V
- $DoD = 0.5$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$

$$B_{Ah} = \frac{5000 \times 2}{48 \times 0.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{10000}{20.4} \approx 490 \text{ Ah}$$

This means we need a **48 V, 500 Ah** battery bank (e.g., 4 ├Ś 12 V, 200 Ah batteries in series, with 2 such strings in parallel).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: PV Array Sizing (Inverter-Limited)

The PV array must be oversized relative to inverter capacity because modules rarely operate at STC in field conditions. The **DC/AC Ratio** (also called *Inverter Loading Ratio, ILR*) is:

$$ILR = \frac{P_{PV,DC}}{P_{inv,AC}}$$

Typically $ILR = 1.1$ŌĆō$1.3$ (i.e., PV is 10ŌĆō30% oversized).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Required PV capacity (DC):**

$$P_{PV} = ILR \times P_{inv}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Energy check (must meet daily demand):**

$$P_{PV} \geq \frac{E_d}{PSH \times \eta_{system}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final selection:**

$$P_{PV,final} = \max\left(\dfrac{E_d}{PSH \times \eta_{system}},\ ILR \times P_{inv}\right)$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Daily load = 20 kWh; PSH = 5; $\eta_{sys} = 0.80$
- Inverter = 5 kW
- Energy-based: $P_{PV} = 20 / (5 \times 0.80) = 5$ kWp
- ILR-based: $P_{PV} = 1.2 \times 5 = 6$ kWp
- **Final:** $P_{PV} = 6$ kWp (higher of the two)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Design Parameter | Formula Input | On-Grid | Off-Grid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Annual Generation | $P_{PV}$, PSH, $\eta_{sys}$ | Same | Same |
| Battery Bank | $E_d$, $D_a$, $V_{bat}$, DoD | Not needed | **Required** |
| Inverter Type | Grid-tie (VSI + PLL) | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| DC/AC Ratio (ILR) | $P_{PV}/P_{inv}$ | 1.1ŌĆō1.3 | 1.0ŌĆō1.1 |
| Days of Autonomy | $D_a$ | 0 (grid = backup) | 1ŌĆō3 days |
| Subsidy (India 2024) | Rooftop: Ōé╣30,000ŌĆō78,000/kW | **Available** | ANERT schemes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: On-Grid (Grid-Tied) Solar Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sun[Sun Irradiance G]:::source --> PV[PV Array DC Generation]
    PV --> SCB[String Combiner Box]
    SCB --> MPPT[DC DC Converter with MPPT]
    MPPT --> INV[Grid Tie Inverter VSI with PLL]
    INV --> PROT[AC Protection Panel MCB MCCB SPD]
    PROT --> NET[Net Meter Bidirectional]
    NET --> GRID[Utility Grid 11kV or 415V]
    GRID --> LOAD[AC Load Local]

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** inside the inverter is the key element that synchronizes the inverter's AC output with the grid in **phase, frequency, and voltage amplitude** before connection.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Off-Grid Solar Power Plant (Stand-Alone)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun2[Sun Irradiance G]:::source --> PV2[PV Array DC Generation]
    PV2 --> CC[Charge Controller MPPT or PWM]
    CC --> BAT[Battery Bank Lead Acid or Li ion]
    BAT --> INV2[Off Grid Inverter DC to AC 230V 50Hz]
    INV2 --> LOAD2[AC Load Local]
    BAT -.->|DC loads| DCLOAD[DC Load Optional]
    CC -.->|Backup| DG[Diesel Generator Optional Hybrid]

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow: On-Grid System (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photovoltaic Effect Sunlight strikes PN junction] --> B[DC Power generated at MPP]
    B --> C{MPPT Algorithm P and O or IncCond}
    C --> D[Duty cycle adjustment of DC DC converter]
    D --> E[Boosted DC voltage matches inverter DC bus]
    E --> F[PWM Inverter switching at high frequency 5 to 20 kHz]
    F --> G[LC Filter smoothes to sinusoidal AC]
    G --> H[PLL synchronizes with grid voltage]
    H --> I{Grid Healthy?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Anti islanding relay closes]
    J --> K[Power flows to grid and load]
    I -->|No| L[Inverter trips within 2 seconds IEEE 1547]
    L --> M[Standalone mode OFF]
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Flow: Off-Grid System (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PV Array generates DC power] --> Q[MPPT Charge Controller tracks MPP]
    Q --> R[Bulk Charging Stage CC mode]
    R --> S[Absorption Stage Constant Voltage]
    S --> T[Float Stage Maintenance]
    T --> U[Battery Bank stores DC energy]
    U --> V{AC Load demanded?}
    V -->|Yes| W[Off Grid Inverter draws from battery]
    W --> X[230V 50Hz AC output to load]
    V -->|No| Y[Idle Battery charged]
    U --> Z{Battery SoC below threshold?}
    Z -->|Yes| AA[Start Diesel Generator backup]
    AA --> AB[Generator charges battery and feeds load]
    Z -->|No| AC[Continue battery operation]
```

---

### 4.5 Comparative Architecture: On-Grid vs Off-Grid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ONGRID[On Grid System]
        OG1[PV Array] --> OG2[Grid Tie Inverter]
        OG2 --> OG3[Bi directional Meter]
        OG3 <--> OG4[Utility Grid]
        OG4 --> OG5[Load]
    end

    subgraph OFFGRID[Off Grid System]
        FG1[PV Array] --> FG2[Charge Controller]
        FG2 --> FG3[Battery Bank]
        FG3 --> FG4[Off Grid Inverter]
        FG4 --> FG5[Load]
        FG6[Diesel Generator] -.-> FG3
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Takeaway:** Notice the **on-grid system has fewer blocks** (no battery, no charge controller) and uses the grid as a "virtual battery" through net metering. The off-grid system is a **closed loop** with battery storage as the lifeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022 Model]**
*Distinguish between On-Grid and Off-Grid solar PV systems. Mention any three differences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
On-Grid (Grid-Tied) systems are connected to the public utility grid and operate in synchronism with grid voltage and frequency, requiring no battery storage. Off-Grid (Standalone) systems operate independently without any grid connection and rely on battery banks for energy storage.

**Three differences:**

| Parameter | On-Grid | Off-Grid |
|---|---|---|
| Battery | Not required (grid = storage) | Mandatory |
| Net metering | Available | Not applicable |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher (due to battery) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023 Model]**
*What is MPPT? Why is it essential in a grid-tied solar inverter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** is a control algorithm used in the DCŌĆōDC converter interfacing the PV array with the inverter to continuously extract maximum available power under varying irradiance and temperature conditions. Common MPPT techniques include **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** and **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)**.

It is essential because the PV IV curve has a single **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** that shifts with irradiance $G$ and cell temperature $T_c$. Without MPPT, the system could operate far from MPP, reducing energy yield by 20ŌĆō40%.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Treatment of On-Grid System

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model ŌĆö Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an On-Grid (Grid-Tied) Solar Power Plant. List all major components. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 5 kWp rooftop solar system is installed in Kerala with the following data: PSH = 4.8 h/day, system efficiency = 0.80, grid emission factor = 0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh. Calculate: (i) Annual energy generation, (ii) CUF, (iii) COŌéé avoided per year. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Sun ŌåÆ PV Array ŌåÆ MPPT DC-DC Converter ŌåÆ DC-AC Inverter (PWM, with PLL)
        Ōåō
   DC Distribution Box
        Ōåō
   AC Protection Panel
        Ōåō
   Net Meter (Bi-directional)
        Ōåō
   Utility Grid (KSEB) Ōåö Local AC Load
```

**Component Description (4 marks):**

1. **PV Array** ŌĆö Series-parallel connection of modules (e.g., 10 modules ├Ś 500 Wp = 5 kWp). Each module has 60/72 monocrystalline or polycrystalline cells.
2. **DCŌĆōDC Converter with MPPT** ŌĆö Boost converter with IGBT/MOSFET switch; tracks MPP via P&O algorithm. MPPT efficiency Ōēł 99%.
3. **Grid-Tie Inverter** ŌĆö Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) with sinusoidal PWM at 5ŌĆō20 kHz switching; converts DC to 230 V/415 V, 50 Hz AC.
4. **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** ŌĆö Synchronizes inverter output voltage with grid in phase, frequency, and amplitude.
5. **Anti-Islanding Protection** ŌĆö Detects grid failure and disconnects inverter within 2 s (per IEEE 1547 / IEC 62116).
6. **Net Meter** ŌĆö Bi-directional energy meter; records kWh imported from and exported to grid.
7. **AC Protection Panel** ŌĆö MCB, MCCB, Surge Protection Device (SPD), Earth Fault Relay.

**Working:** During sunshine, PV array generates DC at MPP. MPPT converter maximizes power extraction. Inverter converts DC to AC synchronized to grid via PLL. Excess power is exported to grid; deficit is imported. When grid fails, inverter disconnects within 2 s to prevent islanding (safety for utility workers).

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $P_{PV} = 5$ kWp
- $PSH = 4.8$ h/day
- $\eta_{system} = 0.80$
- Grid emission factor = **0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh**

**(i) Annual Energy Generation (3 marks):**

$$E_a = P_{PV} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{system}$$

$$E_a = 5 \times 4.8 \times 365 \times 0.80$$

$$E_a = 5 \times 4.8 \times 292 = 5 \times 1401.6$$

$$\boxed{E_a = 7008 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

**(ii) Capacity Utilization Factor (2 marks):**

$$CUF = \frac{E_a}{P_{PV} \times 8760} = \frac{7008}{5 \times 8760} = \frac{7008}{43800}$$

$$\boxed{CUF = 0.16 = 16\%}$$

**(iii) COŌéé Avoided per Year (2 marks):**

$$CO_2 = E_a \times 0.82 = 7008 \times 0.82$$

$$\boxed{CO_2 = 5746.56 \text{ kg/year} \approx 5.75 \text{ tonnes/year}}$$

This is equivalent to planting approximately **250ŌĆō280 trees** per year.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Treatment of Off-Grid System

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model ŌĆö Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Off-Grid (Stand-Alone) Solar Power Plant. Discuss the role of charge controller and battery bank. (7 marks)

**(b)** An off-grid solar system needs to supply a daily load of 4 kWh in a remote Kerala village. Design the battery bank given: Battery voltage = 48 V, Days of autonomy = 2, Depth of Discharge = 0.5, Battery efficiency = 0.85. Also, size the PV array assuming PSH = 4.5 h/day and system efficiency = 0.75. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Sun ŌåÆ PV Array ŌåÆ Charge Controller (MPPT) ŌåÆ Battery Bank
                                                     Ōåō
                            DC Load ŌåÉŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆö Off-Grid Inverter ŌåÆ AC Load
                                                     Ōåæ
                                          (Optional) Diesel Generator
```

**Working Description (4 marks):**

1. **PV Array** generates DC power from sunlight via the photovoltaic effect.
2. **Charge Controller** regulates the charging voltage and current supplied to the battery. MPPT charge controllers are more efficient (~95ŌĆō98%) than PWM types (~70ŌĆō80%) and track the PV array's Maximum Power Point.
3. **Battery Bank** stores DC energy electrochemically (Lead-Acid tubular or Li-ion). It supplies the load during night and cloudy days. Key parameters: Capacity (Ah), Voltage (V), State of Charge (SoC), Depth of Discharge (DoD).
4. **Off-Grid Inverter** converts DC from battery to 230 V, 50 Hz AC to feed AC loads.
5. **Charge Controller Stages:** Bulk (CC), Absorption (CV), Float (maintenance) ŌĆö these ensure battery longevity.
6. **Diesel Generator (Optional)** can be used as backup in hybrid systems when battery SoC drops below 20ŌĆō30%.

**Role of Charge Controller:** Prevents battery overcharging (which damages cells), prevents deep discharge, and improves charging efficiency through MPPT.

**Role of Battery Bank:** Acts as the energy reservoir; sized based on load energy, days of autonomy, and DoD.

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $E_d = 4$ kWh = 4000 Wh
- $D_a = 2$ days
- $V_{bat} = 48$ V
- $DoD = 0.5$
- $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$
- $PSH = 4.5$ h/day
- $\eta_{system} = 0.75$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Battery Bank Sizing (4 marks):**

$$B_{Ah} = \frac{E_d \times D_a}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$$

$$B_{Ah} = \frac{4000 \times 2}{48 \times 0.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{8000}{20.4}$$

$$\boxed{B_{Ah} \approx 392.16 \text{ Ah} \approx 400 \text{ Ah}}$$

**Battery Bank Selection:** Use 4 ├Ś 12 V, 200 Ah batteries in series to obtain 48 V, 200 Ah. To achieve 400 Ah, connect two such series strings in parallel.
**Final:** 48 V, 400 Ah battery bank (8 ├Ś 12 V, 200 Ah batteries).

**Step 2 ŌĆö PV Array Sizing (3 marks):**

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_d}{PSH \times \eta_{system}} = \frac{4000}{4.5 \times 0.75} = \frac{4000}{3.375}$$

$$\boxed{P_{PV} \approx 1185 \text{ Wp} \approx 1.2 \text{ kWp}}$$

**PV Module Selection:** Use 3 ├Ś 400 Wp monocrystalline modules in series (or appropriate S-P combination to meet 1.2 kWp). Charge controller rating: 1.2 kWp ├Ś Imp Ōēł 10 A ├Ś 1.25 (safety) = 12.5 A MPPT charge controller. Inverter sizing: 1.2 ├Ś (peak load power) ŌĆö e.g., 2 kW off-grid inverter.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to write units** (kWh, kWp, V, Ah, ┬░C, kg) ŌĆö **ŌłÆ0.5 to ŌłÆ1 mark per formula**.
> 2. **Confusing CUF with PR (Performance Ratio)** ŌĆö CUF includes inverter efficiency and capacity factor; PR is purely the field-vs-nameplate yield ratio. They are NOT the same.
> 3. **Using 365 instead of 8760** in CUF formula ŌĆö CUF requires **8760 hours/year**, not 365.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply PSH by 365** in annual energy calculation.
> 5. **Wrong DoD for battery** ŌĆö using 1.0 instead of 0.5 (Lead-Acid) or 0.8 (Li-ion). This leads to severe under-sizing of the battery bank.
> 6. **Not drawing arrows in block diagrams** ŌĆö arrows showing power flow direction are **mandatory**; missing them = ŌłÆ1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting anti-islanding** when describing on-grid systems ŌĆö KTU examiners specifically look for this in 7-mark questions.
> 8. **Mixing up PWM and MPPT charge controllers** ŌĆö state which one you're using and justify the choice.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **­¤öæ HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS ŌĆö Module 4 / Solar Power Plants**
>
> - Ōśæ **On-Grid** = No battery required; Net metering; Anti-islanding mandatory; Synchronizes to grid via PLL.
> - Ōśæ **Off-Grid** = Battery mandatory; Charge controller (MPPT preferred); Self-sustained; Inverter sized for peak load.
> - Ōśæ **STC conditions:** $G = 1000$ W/m┬▓, $T_c = 25$ ┬░C, $AM = 1.5$ ŌĆö All Wp ratings are at STC.
> - Ōśæ **CUF formula:** $CUF = E_a / (P_{PV} \times 8760)$. Typical range in India: **15ŌĆō20%**.
> - Ōśæ **Performance Ratio (PR)** Ōēź 0.75 is considered good (per IEC 61724).
> - Ōśæ **Battery Sizing:** $B_{Ah} = (E_d \times D_a) / (V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat})$.
> - Ōśæ **PV Sizing:** $P_{PV} = E_d / (PSH \times \eta_{system})$.
> - Ōśæ **DC/AC Ratio (ILR):** 1.1ŌĆō1.3 for grid-tied (oversize PV by 10ŌĆō30%).
> - Ōśæ **Anti-islanding:** Must trip within **2 seconds** per IEEE 1547/IEC 62116.
> - Ōśæ **Net Metering:** Bi-directional meter; export = credit, import = debit.
> - Ōśæ **MPPT algorithms:** P&O (Perturb & Observe) and Incremental Conductance (IncCond).
> - Ōśæ **MPPT Charge Controller efficiency:** ~95ŌĆō98% vs. PWM at ~70ŌĆō80%.
> - Ōśæ **Kerala PSH:** ~4.5ŌĆō5.0 h/day (one of the highest in India, second only to Tamil Nadu coastal areas).
> - Ōśæ **COŌéé Avoided:** Multiply annual generation (kWh) by 0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh (India grid factor).
> - Ōśæ **Key Kerala-specific schemes:** ANERT rooftop subsidy, PM Surya Ghar Yojana (Ōé╣30,000ŌĆō78,000/kW).
> - Ōśæ **Hybrid systems** combine on-grid + battery for critical loads (hospitals, EV charging).
> - Ōśæ **Specific Yield (Y_f):** 1100ŌĆō1600 kWh/kWp/year is typical in India.
> - Ōśæ **Degradation:** 0.5ŌĆō1% per year for c-Si modules; affects long-term CUF.
> - Ōśæ **Hybrid Inverter** integrates MPPT, charge controller, and inverter in one unit (popular in off-grid designs).
> - Ōśæ **Days of Autonomy (D_a):** 1ŌĆō3 days typical for off-grid; grid backup = 0 days.
> - Ōśæ **Temperature Effect:** Higher $T_c$ ŌåÆ Lower $V_{oc}$ and slight decrease in $I_{sc}$ ŌåÆ Power drops ~0.4ŌĆō0.5% per ┬░C rise.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traction Control in EVs: Forward Motoring and Generating Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT741 - POWER ELECTRONICS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Traction Control in EVs: Forward Motoring and Generating Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Traction Control in Electric Vehicles (EVs)** is the coordinated power-electronic management of an electric drivetrain to deliver the driver's demanded torque and speed across two fundamental operational quadrants:

1. **Forward Motoring Mode (Quadrant I Operation):** The traction motor draws electrical energy from the high-voltage battery (DC bus) through a power-electronic inverter, converting DC ŌåÆ AC to produce positive electromagnetic torque in the direction of rotation, thereby propelling the vehicle forward. Power flows: **Battery ŌåÆ DC-Link ŌåÆ Inverter ŌåÆ Motor ŌåÆ Wheels**.

2. **Generating / Regenerative Braking Mode (Quadrant II Operation):** The traction motor acts as a generator driven by the vehicle's kinetic energy through the wheels. The inverter now operates as a controlled rectifier (AC ŌåÆ DC), and the generated energy is pumped back into the battery (or dissipated in a brake resistor if the battery is full). Power flows: **Wheels ŌåÆ Motor ŌåÆ Inverter ŌåÆ DC-Link ŌåÆ Battery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** "Traction control in an EV refers to the closed-loop regulation of motor torque, speed, and power flow between the energy storage system (ESS) and the traction motor through bidirectional power-electronic converters, enabling seamless transition between propulsion (motoring) and energy-recapture (generating) modes."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Bicycle + Dyno" Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle:

- **Forward Motoring (Pedaling Mode):** Your legs supply energy to the pedals, the chain transmits it to the rear wheel, and you accelerate forward. The **battery is your stomach (energy source)**, the **inverter is the chain (energy router)**, and the **motor is the rear wheel hub**.

- **Generating Mode (Back-Pedal Brake / Dyno Hub):** Instead of pedaling forward, you back-pedal. The dynamo hub now **resists** your forward motion and converts that kinetic energy into electricity that lights your headlamp. The motor is now a **generator**, the inverter is **rectifying** the AC, and the battery is being **charged**.

> [!NOTE]
> The key engineering challenge: the **same** hardware (motor + inverter) must perform both jobs ŌĆö the **direction of power flow** simply reverses, and the **sign of the torque command** flips from positive (accelerate) to negative (decelerate/regen).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery Nominal Voltage | $V_{bat}$ | **400 V** | V |
| DC-Link Capacitance | $C_{dc}$ | **1.0 ŌĆō 2.0 mF** | F |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **5 ŌĆō 20 kHz** | kHz |
| Regen Braking Recovery | $\eta_{regen}$ | **60 ŌĆō 80 %** | % |
| Vehicle Mass (mid-size EV) | $m$ | **1500 ŌĆō 2000 kg** | kg |
| Wheel Radius | $r_w$ | **0.30 ŌĆō 0.35 m** | m |
| Gear Ratio | $G$ | **8 ŌĆō 12** | :1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of an EV Traction Drive

A modern EV traction drive consists of the following cascade:

1. **High-Voltage Battery Pack (ESS):** Lithium-ion, 300ŌĆō800 V nominal, bidirectional (charge/discharge).
2. **DC-Link Capacitor Bank:** Smooths the DC bus, absorbs inverter switching ripple.
3. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter (optional, in 400 V/800 V architectures):** Steps battery voltage up or down to the inverter DC-link.
4. **Three-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** Built from IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs; performs **DC Ōåö AC** conversion with **bidirectional power flow** capability.
5. **PMSM / Induction / BLDC Traction Motor:** Provides mechanical power; reversible electrical machine.
6. **Mechanical Transmission & Differential:** Geared reduction to the wheels.
7. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) / Motor Control Unit (MCU):** Runs Field-Oriented Control (FOC) or Direct Torque Control (DTC); commands torque and mode.

### 2.2 Operational Quadrant Logic

The torqueŌĆōspeed plane of a traction motor is divided into four quadrants:

- **Quadrant I (Forward Motoring):** $T_e > 0$, $\omega_m > 0$ ŌåÆ Vehicle accelerates forward.
- **Quadrant II (Forward Generating/Regen):** $T_e < 0$, $\omega_m > 0$ ŌåÆ Vehicle decelerates while moving forward; energy recovered.
- **Quadrant III (Reverse Motoring):** $T_e < 0$, $\omega_m < 0$ ŌåÆ Vehicle reverses.
- **Quadrant IV (Reverse Generating):** $T_e > 0$, $\omega_m < 0$ ŌåÆ Uncommon, used during reverse regen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** For a *single-speed* EV transmission (most common), only **Quadrants I and II** are exercised in normal driving. Quadrant III is engaged by a transmission reverse gear.

### 2.3 Forward Motoring Mode ŌĆö Detailed Physics

When the driver presses the accelerator:

1. The VCU reads the pedal position sensor and converts it to a **torque demand** $T^*$.
2. The MCU uses **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** to align stator current vector $I_s$ perpendicular to the rotor flux $\lambda_r$ in the $d$ŌĆō$q$ reference frame.
3. The commanded torque is produced: $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_r \cdot I_q$ (for a PMSM).
4. The inverter delivers three-phase PWM voltages to the stator at fundamental frequency $f_e = \frac{P \cdot \omega_m}{4\pi}$ (electrical rad/s relationship).
5. The motor draws active power $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ from the DC bus, less losses.
6. The battery delivers: $P_{bat} = P_{mech} / \eta_{drivetrain}$, where $\eta_{drivetrain} \approx 0.85ŌĆō0.92$.

### 2.4 Generating / Regenerative Braking Mode ŌĆö Detailed Physics

When the driver releases the accelerator or presses the brake:

1. The VCU commands a **negative torque** $T^* < 0$ (the "regen torque").
2. The motor's back-EMF $E = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ now **exceeds** the applied stator voltage, so the machine acts as a generator.
3. The IGBTs of the inverter are still switching, but the **direction of active power flow reverses** ŌĆö the DC-link capacitor voltage tends to rise.
4. The inverter operates in **controlled rectifier mode** (synchronous rectification), pumping current back into the battery.
5. The battery accepts charge: $P_{regen} = T_{regen} \cdot \omega_m \cdot \eta_{gen}$, where $\eta_{gen} \approx 0.80ŌĆō0.90$.
6. The **DC-DC converter** may step up the battery voltage to a higher DC-link level to allow regen even at low speeds where back-EMF is small.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does regenerative braking increase range?** A typical urban EV recovers **15ŌĆō30 %** of its kinetic energy through regen, which can extend range by **10ŌĆō20 %** in stop-and-go traffic. Highway driving sees lower regen benefits (~5ŌĆō10 %) because kinetic energy is recovered less often.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\lambda_r I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q)$ | Electromagnetic torque (IPMSM) | N┬Ęm |
| 2 | $E = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Back-EMF (line-neutral RMS) | V |
| 3 | $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Air-gap mechanical power | W |
| 4 | $P_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot I_{bat}$ | Battery terminal power | W |
| 5 | $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Vehicle kinetic energy | J |
| 6 | $E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | Recovered regen energy | J |
| 7 | $P_{loss,copper} = 3 \cdot I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_s$ | Stator copper loss (3-Žå) | W |
| 8 | $f_e = \frac{P \cdot n}{120}$ | Electrical frequency (P = poles, n in rpm) | Hz |
| 9 | $\eta_{drivetrain} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{P_{bat}}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | ŌĆō |
| 10 | $v = \omega_m \cdot r_w \cdot G^{-1}$ | Vehicle speed from motor speed | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When the motor is in generating mode, $I_q$ becomes **negative** (de-fluxing in FOC), and the inverter switches from **PWM power delivery** to **synchronous rectification**. The sign of DC-link current $I_{dc}$ **reverses** ŌĆö this is the simplest way to identify regen in a current-sensor-based diagnostic.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range extension** in production EVs (Tesla, BYD, Hyundai E-GMP) via regenerative braking.
- **Brake-wear reduction**: Regen handles 60ŌĆō80 % of routine deceleration, extending friction-brake life by 2ŌĆō3├Ś.
- **Thermal management:** Regen also cools the motor ŌĆö kinetic energy is converted to electrical (and stored) rather than heat (as in friction brakes).
- **Grid services:** Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) uses the same hardware in reverse ŌĆö battery ŌåÆ inverter ŌåÆ grid (3-Žå AC).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Power Flow in Forward Motoring Mode

**Given:**
- Motor speed $\omega_m$ (rad/s)
- Electromagnetic torque $T_e$ (N┬Ęm)
- DC-link voltage $V_{dc}$ (V)
- Inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv}$
- Motor efficiency $\eta_m$
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_g$

**Step 1:** Mechanical power developed at the motor shaft.

The electromagnetic torque acts on the rotor rotating at angular speed $\omega_m$, producing shaft power:

$$
P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract mechanical losses (friction, windage) $P_{mech,loss}$ to obtain air-gap power.

$$
P_{ag} = P_{mech} + P_{mech,loss} = T_e \cdot \omega_m + P_{mech,loss}
$$

**Step 3:** The air-gap power must be supplied as electrical input through the stator terminals. This input is also called the *electromagnetic power converted*:

$$
P_{em} = P_{ag}
$$

(For PMSMs, the air-gap power and converted power are identical since rotor copper losses are absent.)

**Step 4:** Subtract stator copper losses and core losses to get the **DC-link input power**:

$$
P_{dc} = P_{em} + P_{cu} + P_{core} = P_{em} + 3 I_{rms}^2 R_s + P_{core}
$$

**Step 5:** The battery must supply this plus inverter switching/conduction losses:

$$
P_{bat} = \frac{P_{dc}}{\eta_{inv}}
$$

**Step 6:** The wheel power (after gearbox) is the useful tractive power:

$$
P_{wheel} = T_e \cdot \omega_m \cdot \eta_g
$$

**Combined (explicit) form:**

$$
P_{wheel} = \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_g \cdot P_{bat}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Power Flow in Generating (Regen) Mode

**Given:** Vehicle decelerating from $v_1$ to $v_2$ m/s; total mass $m$; regen torque $T_{regen}$ applied at wheel.

**Step 1:** Write the equation of motion (Newton's 2nd law at the wheel):

$$
m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = -F_{regen} - F_{roll} - F_{aero}
$$

where $F_{regen} = T_{regen} \cdot G / r_w$ is the regenerative braking force at the contact patch.

**Step 2:** The regen torque (negative) is related to motor speed and regen current $I_{q,regen}$:

$$
T_{regen} = -\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_r \cdot I_{q,regen}
$$

(Note: $I_{q,regen} < 0$ to produce negative torque.)

**Step 3:** The instantaneous regen power at the motor shaft:

$$
P_{regen,shaft} = \vert T_{regen} \vert \cdot \omega_m
$$

**Step 4:** In motoring mode, $\omega_m$ and $T_e$ have the same sign. In generating mode, they have **opposite signs**, so the **time-averaged power is negative**, meaning energy is leaving the motor.

**Step 5:** The DC-link receives the generated power minus motor losses:

$$
P_{dc,gen} = P_{regen,shaft} \cdot \eta_{gen} = \vert T_{regen} \vert \cdot \omega_m \cdot \eta_m
$$

**Step 6:** The battery accepts:

$$
P_{bat,charge} = P_{dc,gen} \cdot \eta_{inv}
$$

**Step 7 (Energy balance):** The total recoverable energy from $v_1 \to v_2$ is:

$$
E_{recoverable} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

**Step 8:** Multiply by overall regen efficiency $\eta_{regen} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_g$ to find what actually goes back into the battery:

$$
\boxed{E_{battery,charge} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö Back-EMF and Transition Speed for Regen

A critical design parameter is the **minimum regen speed**. Below a threshold, back-EMF is too small to overcome the battery voltage plus diode drops.

**Step 1:** Back-EMF (line-to-line RMS) of a PMSM:

$$
E_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot K_e \cdot \omega_m
$$

**Step 2:** The DC-link voltage is fixed at $V_{dc}$ (Ōēł battery voltage in a simple architecture). For the inverter to push current *into* the battery during regen, the back-EMF must exceed $V_{dc} / \sqrt{3} \cdot m_i$ (where $m_i$ is the modulation index, max 1.0):

$$
\sqrt{3} \cdot K_e \cdot \omega_{m,min} \geq \frac{V_{dc}}{m_i}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for minimum motor speed:

$$
\omega_{m,min} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot K_e \cdot m_i}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to vehicle speed via the gear ratio $G$ and wheel radius $r_w$:

$$
v_{min,regen} = \frac{\omega_{m,min} \cdot r_w}{G} = \frac{V_{dc} \cdot r_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot K_e \cdot m_i \cdot G}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A *boost* DC-DC converter is often inserted between the battery and inverter precisely to **lower** $v_{min,regen}$, allowing regen even at near-zero speeds. This is why 800 V architectures (Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan) regen more aggressively than 400 V systems at low speeds.

### 3.4 Numerical Example ŌĆö Worked Problem

**Problem (KTU-style):** An EV of mass $m = 1800$ kg decelerates from $v_1 = 90$ km/h to $v_2 = 30$ km/h using only regenerative braking. Gear ratio $G = 9$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m, motor $K_e = 0.08$ V┬Ęs/rad, DC link $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $\eta_{regen} = 0.75$. Calculate the energy recovered.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert speeds.

$$
v_1 = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_2 = 30 \text{ km/h} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy change.

$$
\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1800 \cdot (25^2 - 8.33^2)
$$

$$
\Delta E_k = 900 \cdot (625 - 69.4) = 900 \cdot 555.6 = 500{,}040 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply regen efficiency.

$$
E_{battery} = 0.75 \cdot 500{,}040 = 375{,}030 \text{ J} \approx 0.104 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4:** Motor speed at the start.

$$
\omega_{m,1} = \frac{v_1 \cdot G}{r_w} = \frac{25 \cdot 9}{0.32} = 703.1 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Back-EMF at start.

$$
E_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 0.08 \cdot 703.1 = 97.4 \text{ V (line-to-line RMS)}
$$

This is well below the DC link, so the boost converter (or the inverter's own PWM boost action) must step it up to enable regen current flow.


### 4.3 Power Flow Direction in Both Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MOTORING_MODE[FORWARD MOTORING MODE QUADRANT I]

        M3 -->|Pconv positive| M4[Motor Generates Te]

    end

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In **Motoring**, arrows point *downstream* (energy flows out of the battery). In **Generating**, the same hardware channel carries energy *upstream* (back into the battery). The **direction** of the arrows is what changes; the **components** are identical.
